"I was younger than you, six, maybe seven. A woodcarver set up shop in the village under my father's keep, and to buy favor he sent us gifts. The old man made marvelous toys. I don't remember what I got, but it was Gregor's gift I wanted. A wooden knight, all painted up, every joint pegged separate and fixed with strings, so you could make him fight. Gregor is five years older than me, the toy was nothing to him, he was already a squire, near six foot tall and muscled like an ox. So I took his knight, but there was no joy to it, I tell you. I was scared all the while, and true enough, he found me. There was a brazier in the room. Gregor never said a word, just picked me up under his arm and shoved the side of my face down in the burning coals and held me there while I screamed and screamed. You saw how strong he is. Even then, it took three grown men to drag him off me. The septons preach about the seven hells. What do they know? Only a man who's been burned knows what hell is truly like.
"My father told everyone my bedding had caught fire, and our maester gave me ointments. Ointments! Gregor got his ointments too. Four years later, they anointed him with the seven oils and he recited his knightly vows and Rhaegar Targaryen tapped him on the shoulder and said, 'Arise, Ser Gregor.'"

> ‚ÄúWhy do you let people call you a dog? You won‚Äôt let anyone call you a knight.‚Äù ‚ÄúI like dogs better than knights. My father‚Äôs father was kennelmaster at the Rock. One autumn year, Lord Tytos came between a lioness and her prey. The lioness didn‚Äôt give a shit that she was Lannister‚Äôs own sigil. Bitch tore into my lord‚Äôs horse and would have done for my lord too, but my grandfather came up with the hounds. Three of his dogs died running her off. My grandfather lost a leg, so Lannister paid him for it with lands and a towerhouse, and took his son to squire. The three dogs on our banner are the three that died, in the yellow of autumn grass. A hound will die for you, but never lie to you. And he‚Äôll look you straight in the face.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúBrave?‚Äù His laugh was half a snarl. ‚ÄúA dog doesn‚Äôt need courage to chase off rats. They had me thirty to one, and not a man of them dared face me.‚Äù She hated the way he talked, always so harsh and angry. ‚ÄúDoes it give you joy to scare people?‚Äù ‚ÄúNo, it gives me joy to kill people.‚Äù His mouth twitched. ‚ÄúWrinkle up your face all you like, but spare me this false piety. You were a high lord‚Äôs get. Don‚Äôt tell me Lord Eddard Stark of Winterfell never killed a man.‚Äù ‚ÄúThat was his duty. He never liked it.‚Äù ‚ÄúIs that what he told you?‚Äù Clegane laughed again. ‚ÄúYour father lied. Killing is the sweetest thing there is.‚Äù He drew his longsword. ‚ÄúHere‚Äôs your truth. Your precious father found that out on Baelor‚Äôs steps. Lord of Winterfell, Hand of the King, Warden of the North, the mighty Eddard Stark, of a line eight thousand years old¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut Ilyn Payne‚Äôs blade went through his neck all the same, didn‚Äôt it? Do you remember the dance he did when his head came off his shoulders?‚Äù Sansa hugged herself, suddenly cold. ‚ÄúWhy are you always so hateful? I was thanking you¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúJust as if I was one of those true knights you love so well, yes. What do you think a knight is for, girl? You think it‚Äôs all taking favors from ladies and looking fine in gold plate? Knights are for killing.‚Äù He laid the edge of his longsword against her neck, just under her ear. Sansa could feel the sharpness of the steel. ‚ÄúI killed my first man at twelve. I‚Äôve lost count of how many I‚Äôve killed since then. High lords with old names, fat rich men dressed in velvet, knights puffed up like bladders with their honors, yes, and women and children too‚Äîthey‚Äôre all meat, and I‚Äôm the butcher. Let them have their lands and their gods and their gold. Let them have their sers.‚Äù Sandor Clegane spat at her feet to show what he thought of that. ‚ÄúSo long as I have this,‚Äù he said, lifting the sword from her throat, ‚Äúthere‚Äôs no man on earth I need fear.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúWhat will you do when he crosses?‚Äù ‚ÄúFight. Kill. Die, maybe.‚Äù ‚ÄúAren‚Äôt you afraid? The gods might send you down to some terrible hell for all the evil you‚Äôve done.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhat evil?‚Äù He laughed. ‚ÄúWhat gods?‚Äù ‚ÄúThe gods who made us all.‚Äù ‚ÄúAll?‚Äù he mocked. ‚ÄúTell me, little bird, what kind of god makes a monster like the Imp, or a halfwit like Lady Tanda‚Äôs daughter? If there are gods, they made sheep so wolves could eat mutton, and they made the weak for the strong to play with.‚Äù ‚ÄúTrue knights protect the weak.‚Äù He snorted. ‚ÄúThere are no true knights, no more than there are gods. If you can‚Äôt protect yourself, die and get out of the way of those who can. Sharp steel and strong arms rule this world, don‚Äôt ever believe any different.‚Äù Sansa backed away from him. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôre awful.‚Äù ‚ÄúI‚Äôm honest. It‚Äôs the world that‚Äôs awful. Now fly away, little bird, I‚Äôm sick of you peeping at me.‚Äù

{:#Renly-Baratheon-Quotes}
**Renly Baratheon**

> ‚ÄúI broke bread with Gulian Swann and old Penrose, and the Tarths consented to a midnight meeting in a grove. The others‚Äîwell, Beric Dondarrion is gone missing, some say dead, and Lord Caron is with Renly. Bryce the Orange, of the Rainbow Guard.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe Rainbow Guard?‚Äù ‚ÄúRenly‚Äôs made his own Kingsguard,‚Äù the onetime smuggler explained, ‚Äúbut these seven don‚Äôt wear white. Each one has his own color. Loras Tyrell‚Äôs their Lord Commander.‚Äù It was just the sort of notion that would appeal to Renly Baratheon; a splendid new order of knighthood, with gorgeous new raiment to proclaim it. Even as a boy, Renly had loved bright colors and rich fabrics, and he had loved his games as well. ‚ÄúLook at me!‚Äù he would shout as he ran laughing through the halls of Storm‚Äôs End. ‚ÄúLook at me, I‚Äôm a dragon,‚Äù or ‚ÄúLook at me, I‚Äôm a wizard,‚Äù or ‚ÄúLook at me, look at me, I‚Äôm the rain god.‚Äù The bold little boy with wild black hair and laughing eyes was a man grown now, one-and-twenty, and still he played his games. Look at me, I‚Äôm a king, Cressen thought sadly. Oh, Renly, Renly, dear sweet child, do you know what you are doing? And would you care if you did? Is there anyone who cares for him but me?

> Near all the chivalry of the south had come to Renly‚Äôs call, it seemed. The golden rose of Highgarden was seen everywhere: sewn on the right breast of armsmen and servants, flapping and fluttering from the green silk banners that adorned lance and pike, painted upon the shields hung outside the pavilions of the sons and brothers and cousins and uncles of House Tyrell. As well Catelyn spied the fox-and-flowers of House Florent, Fossoway apples red and green, Lord Tarly‚Äôs striding huntsman, oak leaves for Oakheart, cranes for Crane, a cloud of black-and-orange butterflies for the Mullendores. Across the Mander, the storm lords had raised their standards‚ÄîRenly‚Äôs own bannermen, sworn to House Baratheon and Storm‚Äôs End. Catelyn recognized Bryce Caron‚Äôs nightingales, the Penrose quills, and Lord Estermont‚Äôs sea turtle, green on green. Yet for every shield she knew, there were a dozen strange to her, borne by the small lords sworn to the bannermen, and by hedge knights and freeriders who had come swarming to make Renly Baratheon a king in fact as well as name. Renly‚Äôs own standard flew high over all. From the top of his tallest siege tower, a wheeled oaken immensity covered with rawhides, streamed the largest war banner that Catelyn had ever seen‚Äîa cloth big enough to carpet many a hall, shimmering gold, with the crowned stag of Baratheon black upon it, prancing proud and tall.

> The lords and ladies in the gallery were as engrossed in the melee as the men on the ground. Catelyn marked them well. Her father had oft treated with the southron lords, and not a few had been guests at Riverrun. She recognized Lord Mathis Rowan, stouter and more florid than ever, the golden tree of his House spread across his white doublet. Below him sat Lady Oakheart, tiny and delicate, and to her left Lord Randyll Tarly of Horn Hill, his greatsword Heartsbane propped up against the back of his seat. Others she knew only by their sigils, and some not at all. In their midst, watching and laughing with his young queen by his side, sat a ghost in a golden crown. Small wonder the lords gather around him with such fervor, she thought, he is Robert come again. Renly was handsome as Robert had been handsome; long of limb and broad of shoulder, with the same coal-black hair, fine and straight, the same deep blue eyes, the same easy smile. The slender circlet around his brows seemed to suit him well. It was soft gold, a ring of roses exquisitely wrought; at the front lifted a stag‚Äôs head of dark green jade, adorned with golden eyes and golden antlers. The crowned stag decorated the king‚Äôs green velvet tunic as well, worked in gold thread upon his chest; the Baratheon sigil in the colors of Highgarden. The girl who shared the high seat with him was also of Highgarden: his young queen, Margaery, daughter to Lord Mace Tyrell. Their marriage was the mortar that held the great southron alliance together, Catelyn knew. Renly was one-and-twenty, the girl no older than Robb, very pretty, with a doe‚Äôs soft eyes and a mane of curling brown hair that fell about her shoulders in lazy ringlets. Her smile was shy and sweet.

> Catelyn could scarcely imagine what she might need that had not already been provided. The pavilion was larger than the common rooms of many an inn and furnished with every comfort: feather mattress and sleeping furs, a wood-and-copper tub large enough for two, braziers to keep off the night‚Äôs chill, slung leather camp chairs, a writing table with quills and inkpot, bowls of peaches, plums, and pears, a flagon of wine with a set of matched silver cups, cedar chests packed full of Renly‚Äôs clothing, books, maps, game boards, a high harp, a tall bow and a quiver of arrows, a pair of red-tailed hunting hawks, a vertible armory of fine weapons. He does not stint himself, this Renly, she thought as she looked about. Small wonder this host moves so slowly. Beside the entrance, the king‚Äôs armor stood sentry; a suit of forest-green plate, its fittings chased with gold, the helm crowned by a great rack of golden antlers. The steel was polished to such a high sheen that she could see her reflection in the breastplate, gazing back at her as if from the bottom of a deep green pond. The face of a drowned woman, Catelyn thought. Can you drown in grief? She turned away sharply, angry with her own frailty.

> Renly sat with his young bride on his left hand and her brother on the right. Apart from the white linen bandage around his brow, Ser Loras seemed none the worse for the day‚Äôs misadventures. He was indeed as comely as Catelyn had suspected he might be. When not glazed, his eyes were lively and intelligent, his hair an artless tumble of brown locks that many a maid might have envied. He had replaced his tattered tourney cloak with a new one; the same brilliantly striped silk of Renly‚Äôs Rainbow Guard, clasped with the golden rose of Highgarden. From time to time, King Renly would feed Margaery some choice morsel off the point of his dagger, or lean over to plant the lightest of kisses on her cheek, but it was Ser Loras who shared most of his jests and confidences. The king enjoyed his food and drink, that was plain to see, yet he seemed neither glutton nor drunkard. He laughed often, and well, and spoke amiably to highborn lords and lowly serving wenches alike.

> "And what should I do with a hundred swords, my lord?" "Strike! Now, while the castle sleeps." Renly looked back at Ser Boros again and dropped his voice to an urgent whisper. "We must get Joffrey away from his mother and take him in hand. Protector or no, the man who holds the king holds the kingdom. We should seize Myrcella and Tommen as well. Once we have her children, Cersei will not dare oppose us. The council will confirm you as Lord Protector and make Joffrey your ward." Ned regarded him coldly. "Robert is not dead yet. The gods may spare him. If not, I shall convene the council to hear his final words and consider the matter of the succession, but I will not dishonor his last hours on earth by shedding blood in his halls and dragging frightened children from their beds. Lord Renly took a step back, taut as a bowstring. "Every moment you delay gives Cersei another moment to prepare. By the time Robert dies, it may be too late ‚Ä¶ for both of us."

> Varys gave him a sorrowful look. "I fear Lord Renly has left the city." "Left the city?" Ned had counted on Renly's support. "He took his leave through a postern gate an hour before dawn, accompanied by Ser Loras Tyrell and some fifty retainers," Varys told them. "When last seen, they were galloping south in some haste, no doubt bound for Storm's End or Highgarden."

> ‚ÄúOn the night of Robert‚Äôs death, I offered your husband a hundred swords and urged him to take Joffrey into his power. Had he listened, he would be regent today, and there would have been no need for me to claim the throne.‚Äù ‚ÄúNed refused you.‚Äù She did not have to be told. ‚ÄúHe had sworn to protect Robert‚Äôs children,‚Äù Renly said. ‚ÄúI lacked the strength to act alone, so when Lord Eddard turned me away, I had no choice but to flee. Had I stayed, I knew the queen would see to it that I did not long outlive my brother.‚Äù Had you stayed, and lent your support to Ned, he might still be alive, Catelyn thought bitterly.

> ‚ÄúI‚Äôm told your son crossed the Neck with twenty thousand swords at his back,‚Äù Renly went on. ‚ÄúNow that the lords of the Trident are with him, perhaps he commands forty thousand.‚Äù No, she thought, not near so many, we have lost men in battle, and others to the harvest. ‚ÄúI have twice that number here,‚Äù Renly said, ‚Äúand this is only part of my strength. Mace Tyrell remains at Highgarden with another ten thousand, I have a strong garrison holding Storm‚Äôs End, and soon enough the Dornishmen will join me with all their power. And never forget my brother Stannis, who holds Dragonstone and commands the lords of the narrow sea.‚Äù ‚ÄúIt would seem that you are the one who has forgotten Stannis,‚Äù Catelyn said, more sharply than she‚Äôd intended. ‚ÄúHis claim, you mean?‚Äù Renly laughed. ‚ÄúLet us be blunt, my lady. Stannis would make an appalling king. Nor is he like to become one. Men respect Stannis, even fear him, but precious few have ever loved him.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe is still your elder brother. If either of you can be said to have a right to the Iron Throne, it must be Lord Stannis.‚Äù Renly shrugged. ‚ÄúTell me, what right did my brother Robert ever have to the Iron Throne?‚Äù He did not wait for an answer. ‚ÄúOh, there was talk of the blood ties between Baratheon and Targaryen, of weddings a hundred years past, of second sons and elder daughters. No one but the maesters care about any of it. Robert won the throne with his warhammer.‚Äù He swept a hand across the campfires that burned from horizon to horizon. ‚ÄúWell, there is my claim, as good as Robert‚Äôs ever was. If your son supports me as his father supported Robert, he‚Äôll not find me ungenerous. I will gladly confirm him in all his lands, titles, and honors. He can rule in Winterfell as he pleases. He can even go on calling himself King in the North if he likes, so long as he bends the knee and does me homage as his overlord. King is only a word, but fealty, loyalty, service¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthose I must have.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúAll this of snakes and incest is droll, but it changes nothing. You may well have the better claim, Stannis, but I still have the larger army.‚Äù Renly‚Äôs hand slid inside his cloak. Stannis saw, and reached at once for the hilt of his sword, but before he could draw steel his brother produced¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýa peach. ‚ÄúWould you like one, brother?‚Äù Renly asked, smiling. ‚ÄúFrom Highgarden. You‚Äôve never tasted anything so sweet, I promise you.‚Äù He took a bite. Juice ran from the corner of his mouth. ‚ÄúI did not come here to eat fruit.‚Äù Stannis was fuming. ‚ÄúMy lords!‚Äù Catelyn said. ‚ÄúWe ought to be hammering out the terms of an alliance, not trading taunts.‚Äù ‚ÄúA man should never refuse to taste a peach,‚Äù Renly said as he tossed the stone away. ‚ÄúHe may never get the chance again. Life is short, Stannis. Remember what the Starks say. Winter is coming.‚Äù He wiped his mouth with the back of his hand. ‚ÄúI did not come here to be threatened, either.‚Äù ‚ÄúNor were you,‚Äù Renly snapped back. ‚ÄúWhen I make threats, you‚Äôll know it. If truth be told, I‚Äôve never liked you, Stannis, but you are my own blood, and I have no wish to slay you. So if it is Storm‚Äôs End you want, take it¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýas a brother‚Äôs gift. As Robert once gave it to me, I give it to you.‚Äù ‚ÄúIt is not yours to give. It is mine by rights.‚Äù Sighing, Renly half turned in the saddle. ‚ÄúWhat am I to do with this brother of mine, Brienne? He refuses my peach, he refuses my castle, he even shunned my wedding¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù

> ‚Äú‚Äîyour brother is the lawful heir.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhile he lives,‚Äù Renly admitted. ‚ÄúThough it‚Äôs a fool‚Äôs law, wouldn‚Äôt you agree? Why the oldest son, and not the best-fitted? The crown will suit me, as it never suited Robert and would not suit Stannis. I have it in me to be a great king, strong yet generous, clever, just, diligent, loyal to my friends and terrible to my enemies, yet capable of forgiveness, patient‚Äî‚Äù ‚Äú‚Äîhumble?‚Äù Catelyn supplied. Renly laughed. ‚ÄúYou must allow a king some flaws, my lady.‚Äù

> Their camp was well sited atop a low stony ridge that ran from north to south. It was far more orderly than the sprawling encampment on the Mander, though only a quarter as large. When he‚Äôd learned of his brother‚Äôs assault on Storm‚Äôs End, Renly had split his forces, much as Robb had done at the Twins. His great mass of foot he had left behind at Bitterbridge with his young queen, his wagons, carts, draft animals, and all his cumbersome siege machinery, while Renly himself led his knights and freeriders in a swift dash east. How like his brother Robert he was, even in that¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýonly Robert had always had Eddard Stark to temper his boldness with caution. Ned would surely have prevailed upon Robert to bring up his whole force, to encircle Stannis and besiege the besiegers. That choice Renly had denied himself in his headlong rush to come to grips with his brother. He had outdistanced his supply lines, left food and forage days behind with all his wagons and mules and oxen. He must come to battle soon, or starve.

> ‚ÄúI am not without mercy,‚Äù thundered he who was notoriously without mercy. ‚ÄúNor do I wish to sully Lightbringer with a brother‚Äôs blood. For the sake of the mother who bore us both, I will give you this night to rethink your folly, Renly. Strike your banners and come to me before dawn, and I will grant you Storm‚Äôs End and your old seat on the council and even name you my heir until a son is born to me. Otherwise, I shall destroy you.‚Äù Renly laughed. ‚ÄúStannis, that‚Äôs a very pretty sword, I‚Äôll grant you, but I think the glow off it has ruined your eyes. Look across the fields, brother. Can you see all those banners?‚Äù ‚ÄúDo you think a few bolts of cloth will make you king?‚Äù ‚ÄúTyrell swords will make me king. Rowan and Tarly and Caron will make me king, with axe and mace and warhammer. Tarth arrows and Penrose lances, Fossoway, Cuy, Mullendore, Estermont, Selmy, Hightower, Oakheart, Crane, Caswell, Blackbar, Morrigen, Beesbury, Shermer, Dunn, Footly¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýeven House Florent, your own wife‚Äôs brothers and uncles, they will make me king. All the chivalry of the south rides with me, and that is the least part of my power. My foot is coming behind, a hundred thousand swords and spears and pikes. And you will destroy me? With what, pray? That paltry rabble I see there huddled under the castle walls? I‚Äôll call them five thousand and be generous, codfish lords and onion knights and sellswords. Half of them are like to come over to me before the battle starts. You have fewer than four hundred horse, my scouts tell me‚Äîfreeriders in boiled leather who will not stand an instant against armored lances. I do not care how seasoned a warrior you think you are, Stannis, that host of yours won‚Äôt survive the first charge of my vanguard.‚Äù ‚ÄúWe shall see, brother.‚Äù Some of the light seemed to go out of the world when Stannis slid his sword back into its scabbard. ‚ÄúCome the dawn, we shall see.‚Äù

{:#Brienne-of-Tarth}
**Brienne of Tarth**

> She heard King Renly declare the Lady Brienne of Tarth the victor of the great melee at Bitterbridge, last mounted of one hundred sixteen knights. ‚ÄúAs champion, you may ask of me any boon that you desire. If it lies in my power, it is yours.‚Äù ‚ÄúYour Grace,‚Äù Brienne answered, ‚ÄúI ask the honor of a place among your Rainbow Guard. I would be one of your seven, and pledge my life to yours, to go where you go, ride at your side, and keep you safe from all hurt and harm.‚Äù ‚ÄúDone,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúRise, and remove your helm.‚Äù She did as he bid her. And when the greathelm was lifted, Catelyn understood Ser Colen‚Äôs words. Beauty, they called her¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýmocking. The hair beneath the visor was a squirrel‚Äôs nest of dirty straw, and her face¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝBrienne‚Äôs eyes were large and very blue, a young girl‚Äôs eyes, trusting and guileless, but the rest¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýher features were broad and coarse, her teeth prominent and crooked, her mouth too wide, her lips so plump they seemed swollen. A thousand freckles speckled her cheeks and brow, and her nose had been broken more than once. Pity filled Catelyn‚Äôs heart. Is there any creature on earth as unfortunate as an ugly woman? And yet, when Renly cut away her torn cloak and fastened a rainbow in its place, Brienne of Tarth did not look unfortunate. Her smile lit up her face, and her voice was strong and proud as she said, ‚ÄúMy life for yours, Your Grace. From this day on, I am your shield, I swear it by the old gods and the new.‚Äù The way she looked at the king‚Äîlooked down at him, she was a good hand higher, though Renly was near as tall as his brother had been‚Äîwas painful to see.

> Lord Rowan beside her did not join the merriment. ‚ÄúThey are all so young,‚Äù he said. It was true. The Knight of Flowers could not have reached his second name day when Robert slew Prince Rhaegar on the Trident. Few of the others were very much older. They had been babes during the Sack of King‚Äôs Landing, and no more than boys when Balon Greyjoy raised the Iron Islands in rebellion. They are still unblooded, Catelyn thought as she watched Lord Bryce goad Ser Robar into juggling a brace of daggers. It is all a game to them still, a tourney writ large, and all they see is the chance for glory and honor and spoils. They are boys drunk on song and story, and like all boys, they think themselves immortal. ‚ÄúWar will make them old,‚Äù Catelyn said, ‚Äúas it did us.‚Äù She had been a girl when Robert and Ned and Jon Arryn raised their banners against Aerys Targaryen, a woman by the time the fighting was done. ‚ÄúI pity them.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhy?‚Äù Lord Rowan asked her. ‚ÄúLook at them. They‚Äôre young and strong, full of life and laughter. And lust, aye, more lust than they know what to do with. There will be many a bastard bred this night, I promise you. Why pity?‚Äù ‚ÄúBecause it will not last,‚Äù Catelyn answered, sadly. ‚ÄúBecause they are the knights of summer, and winter is coming.‚Äù ‚ÄúLady Catelyn, you are wrong.‚Äù Brienne regarded her with eyes as blue as her armor. ‚ÄúWinter will never come for the likes of us. Should we die in battle, they will surely sing of us, and it‚Äôs always summer in the songs. In the songs all knights are gallant, all maids are beautiful, and the sun is always shining.‚Äù Winter comes for all of us, Catelyn thought. For me, it came when Ned died. It will come for you too, child, and sooner than you like. She did not have the heart to say it.

> Rymund the Rhymer sat by the brewhouse amidst a circle of listeners, his deep voice ringing as he sang of Lord Deremond at the Bloody Meadow. And there he stood with sword in hand, the last of Darry‚Äôs ten¬Ý‚Ä¶ Brienne paused to listen for a moment, broad shoulders hunched and thick arms crossed against her chest. A mob of ragged boys raced by, screeching and flailing at each other with sticks. Why do boys so love to play at war? Catelyn wondered if Rymund was the answer. The singer‚Äôs voice swelled as he neared the end of his song. And red the grass beneath his feet, and red his banners bright, and red the glow of setting sun that bathed him in its light. ‚ÄúCome on, come on,‚Äù the great lord called, ‚Äúmy sword is hungry still.‚Äù And with a cry of savage rage, They swarmed across the rill¬Ý‚Ä¶ ‚ÄúFighting is better than this waiting,‚Äù Brienne said. ‚ÄúYou don‚Äôt feel so helpless when you fight. You have a sword and a horse, sometimes an axe. When you‚Äôre armored it‚Äôs hard for anyone to hurt you.‚Äù ‚ÄúKnights die in battle,‚Äù Catelyn reminded her. Brienne looked at her with those blue and beautiful eyes. ‚ÄúAs ladies die in childbed. No one sings songs about them.‚Äù

> Wrapped in a bedrobe, Catelyn climbed to the roof of the keep. From there she could see over the walls and the moonlit river to where the battle raged. The defenders had built watchfires along the bank, and perhaps the Lannisters thought to find them night-blind or unwary. If so, it was folly. Darkness was a chancy ally at best. As they waded in to breast their way across, men stepped in hidden pools and went down splashing, while others stumbled over stones or gashed their feet on the hidden caltrops. The Mallister bowmen sent a storm of fire arrows hissing across the river, strangely beautiful from afar. One man, pierced through a dozen times, his clothes afire, danced and whirled in the knee-deep water until at last he fell and was swept downstream. By the time his body came bobbing past Riverrun, the fires and his life had both been extinguished. A small victory, Catelyn thought when the fighting had ended and the surviving foemen had melted back into the night, yet a victory nonetheless. As they descended the winding turret steps, Catelyn asked Brienne for her thoughts. ‚ÄúThat was the brush of Lord Tywin‚Äôs fingertip, my lady,‚Äù the girl said. ‚ÄúHe is probing, feeling for a weak point, an undefended crossing. If he does not find one, he will curl all his fingers into a fist and try and make one.‚Äù Brienne hunched her shoulders. ‚ÄúThat‚Äôs what I‚Äôd do. Were I him.‚Äù

> She is stronger than I am. The realization chilled him. Robert had been stronger than him, to be sure. The White Bull Gerold Hightower as well, in his heyday, and Ser Arthur Dayne. Amongst the living, Greatjon Umber was stronger, Strongboar of Crakehall most likely, both Cleganes for a certainty. The Mountain‚Äôs strength was like nothing human. It did not matter. With speed and skill, Jaime could beat them all. But this was a woman. A huge cow of a woman, to be sure, but even so¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýby rights, she should be the one wearing down. Instead she forced him back into the brook again, shouting, ‚ÄúYield! Throw down the sword!‚Äù A slick stone turned under Jaime‚Äôs foot. As he felt himself falling, he twisted the mischance into a diving lunge. His point scraped past her parry and bit into her upper thigh. A red flower blossomed, and Jaime had an instant to savor the sight of her blood before his knee slammed into a rock. The pain was blinding. Brienne splashed into him and kicked away his sword. ‚ÄúYIELD!‚Äù Jaime drove his shoulder into her legs, bringing her down on top of him. They rolled, kicking and punching until finally she was sitting astride him. He managed to jerk her dagger from its sheath, but before he could plunge it into her belly she caught his wrist and slammed his hands back on a rock so hard he thought she‚Äôd wrenched an arm from its socket. Her other hand spread across his face. ‚ÄúYield!‚Äù She shoved his head down, held it under, pulled it up. ‚ÄúYield!‚Äù Jaime spit water into her face. A shove, a splash, and he was under again, kicking uselessly, fighting to breathe. Up again. ‚ÄúYield, or I‚Äôll drown you!‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd break your oath?‚Äù he snarled. ‚ÄúLike me?‚Äù She let him go, and he went down with a splash. And the woods rang with coarse laughter. Brienne lurched to her feet. She was all mud and blood below the waist, her clothing askew, her face red. She looks as if they caught us fucking instead of fighting. Jaime crawled over the rocks to shallow water, wiping the blood from his eye with his chained hands. Armed men lined both sides of the brook. Small wonder, we were making enough noise to wake a dragon. ‚ÄúWell met, friends,‚Äù he called to them amiably. ‚ÄúMy pardons if I disturbed you. You caught me chastising my wife.‚Äù ‚ÄúSeemed to me she was doing the chastising.‚Äù The man who spoke was thick and powerful, and the nasal bar of his iron halfhelm did not wholly conceal his lack of a nose. These were not the outlaws who had killed Ser Cleos, Jaime realized suddenly. The scum of the earth surrounded them: swarthy Dornishmen and blond Lyseni, Dothraki with bells in their braids, hairy Ibbenese, coal-black Summer Islanders in feathered cloaks. He knew them. The Brave Companions.

{:#Petyr-Littlefinger-Baelish}
**Petyr "Littlefinger" Baelish**

> ‚ÄúI know the secret Jon Arryn was murdered to protect. Robert will leave no trueborn son behind him. Joffrey and Tommen are Jaime Lannister‚Äôs bastards, born of his incestuous union with the queen.‚Äù Littlefinger lifted an eyebrow. ‚ÄúShocking,‚Äù he said in a tone that suggested he was not shocked at all. ‚ÄúThe girl as well? No doubt. So when the king dies¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúThe throne by rights passes to Lord Stannis, the elder of Robert‚Äôs two brothers.‚Äù Lord Petyr stroked his pointed beard as he considered the matter. ‚ÄúSo it would seem. Unless¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúUnless, my lord? There is no seeming to this. Stannis is the heir. Nothing can change that.‚Äù ‚ÄúStannis cannot take the throne without your help. If you‚Äôre wise, you‚Äôll make certain Joffrey succeeds.‚Äù Ned gave him a stony stare. ‚ÄúHave you no shred of honor?‚Äù ‚ÄúOh, a shred, surely,‚Äù Littlefinger replied negligently. ‚ÄúHear me out. Stannis is no friend of yours, nor of mine. Even his brothers can scarcely stomach him. The man is iron, hard and unyielding. He‚Äôll give us a new Hand and a new council, for a certainty. No doubt he‚Äôll thank you for handing him the crown, but he won‚Äôt love you for it. And his ascent will mean war. Stannis cannot rest easy on the throne until Cersei and her bastards are dead. Do you think Lord Tywin will sit idly while his daughter‚Äôs head is measured for a spike? Casterly Rock will rise, and not alone. Robert found it in him to pardon men who served King Aerys, so long as they did him fealty. Stannis is less forgiving. He will not have forgotten the siege of Storm‚Äôs End, and the Lords Tyrell and Redwyne dare not. Every man who fought beneath the dragon banner or rose with Balon Greyjoy will have good cause to fear. Seat Stannis on the Iron Throne and I promise you, the realm will bleed. ‚ÄúNow look at the other side of the coin. Joffrey is but twelve, and Robert gave you the regency, my lord. You are the Hand of the King and Protector of the Realm. The power is yours, Lord Stark. All you need do is reach out and take it. Make your peace with the Lannisters. Release the Imp. Wed Joffrey to your Sansa. Wed your younger girl to Prince Tommen, and your heir to Myrcella. It will be four years before Joffrey comes of age. By then he will look to you as a second father, and if not, well¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýfour years is a good long while, my lord. Long enough to dispose of Lord Stannis. Then, should Joffrey prove troublesome, we can reveal his little secret and put Lord Renly on the throne.‚Äù ‚ÄúWe?‚Äù Ned repeated. Littlefinger gave a shrug. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôll need someone to share your burdens. I assure you, my price would be modest.‚Äù ‚ÄúYour price.‚Äù Ned‚Äôs voice was ice. ‚ÄúLord Baelish, what you suggest is treason.‚Äù ‚ÄúOnly if we lose.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúYour Grace, my lord Hand,‚Äù said Littlefinger, ‚Äúthe king needs both of you here. Let me go in your stead.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou?‚Äù What gain does he see in this? Tyrion wondered. ‚ÄúI am of the king‚Äôs council, yet not the king‚Äôs blood, so I would make a poor hostage. I knew Ser Loras passing well when he was here at court, and gave him no cause to mislike me. Mace Tyrell bears me no enmity that I know of, and I flatter myself that I am not unskilled in negotiation.‚Äù He has us. Tyrion did not trust Petyr Baelish, nor did he want the man out of his sight, yet what other choice was left him? It must be Littlefinger or Tyrion himself, and he knew full well that if he left King‚Äôs Landing for any length of time, all that he had managed to accomplish would be undone. ‚ÄúThere is fighting between here and Bitterbridge,‚Äù he said cautiously. ‚ÄúAnd you can be past certain that Lord Stannis will be dispatching his own shepherds to gather in his brother‚Äôs wayward lambs.‚Äù ‚ÄúI‚Äôve never been frightened of shepherds. It‚Äôs the sheep who trouble me. Still, I suppose an escort might be in order.‚Äù ‚ÄúI can spare a hundred gold cloaks,‚Äù Tyrion said. ‚ÄúFive hundred.‚Äù ‚ÄúThree hundred.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd forty more‚Äîtwenty knights with as many squires. If I arrive without a knightly tail, the Tyrells will think me of small account.‚Äù That was true enough. ‚ÄúAgreed.‚Äù

> The suggestion was carried without protest, but Littlefinger was not done. ‚ÄúWe‚Äôll want horses. Swift and strong. The fighting will make remounts hard to come by. A goodly supply of gold will also be needed, for those gifts we spoke of earlier.‚Äù ‚ÄúTake as much as you require. If the city falls, Stannis will steal it all anyway.‚Äù ‚ÄúI‚Äôll want my commission in writing. A document that will leave Mace Tyrell in no doubt as to my authority, granting me full power to treat with him concerning this match and any other arrangements that might be required, and to make binding pledges in the king‚Äôs name. It should be signed by Joffrey and every member of this council, and bear all our seals.‚Äù Tyrion shifted uncomfortably. ‚ÄúDone. Will that be all? I remind you, there‚Äôs a long road between here and Bitterbridge.‚Äù ‚ÄúI‚Äôll be riding it before dawn breaks.‚Äù Littlefinger rose. ‚ÄúI trust that on my return, the king will see that I am suitably rewarded for my valiant efforts in his cause?‚Äù Varys giggled. ‚ÄúJoffrey is such a grateful sovereign, I‚Äôm certain you will have no cause to complain, my good brave lord.‚Äù The queen was more direct. ‚ÄúWhat do you want, Petyr?‚Äù Littlefinger glanced at Tyrion with a sly smile. ‚ÄúI shall need to give that some consideration. No doubt I‚Äôll think of something.‚Äù He sketched an airy bow and took his leave, as casual as if he were off to one of his brothels.

> If ever truly a man had armored himself in gold, it was Petyr Baelish, not Jaime Lannister. Jaime‚Äôs famous armor was but gilded steel, but Littlefinger, ah¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝTyrion had learned a few things about sweet Petyr, to his growing disquiet. Ten years ago, Jon Arryn had given him a minor sinecure in customs, where Lord Petyr had soon distinguished himself by bringing in three times as much as any of the king‚Äôs other collectors. King Robert had been a prodigious spender. A man like Petyr Baelish, who had a gift for rubbing two golden dragons together to breed a third, was invaluable to his Hand. Littlefinger‚Äôs rise had been arrow-swift. Within three years of his coming to court, he was master of coin and a member of the small council, and today the crown‚Äôs revenues were ten times what they had been under his beleaguered predecessor¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthough the crown‚Äôs debts had grown vast as well. A master juggler was Petyr Baelish. Oh, he was clever. He did not simply collect the gold and lock it in a treasure vault, no. He paid the king‚Äôs debts in promises, and put the king‚Äôs gold to work. He bought wagons, shops, ships, houses. He bought grain when it was plentiful and sold bread when it was scarce. He bought wool from the north and linen from the south and lace from Lys, stored it, moved it, dyed it, sold it. The golden dragons bred and multiplied, and Littlefinger lent them out and brought them home with hatchlings. And in the process, he moved his own men into place. The Keepers of the Keys were his, all four. The King‚Äôs Counter and the King‚Äôs Scales were men he‚Äôd named. The officers in charge of all three mints. Harbormasters, tax farmers, customs sergeants, wool factors, toll collectors, pursers, wine factors; nine of every ten belonged to Littlefinger. They were men of middling birth, by and large; merchants‚Äô sons, lesser lordlings, sometimes even foreigners, but judging from their results, far more able than their highborn predecessors. No one had ever thought to question the appointments, and why should they? Littlefinger was no threat to anyone. A clever, smiling, genial man, everyone‚Äôs friend, always able to find whatever gold the king or his Hand required, and yet of such undistinguished birth, one step up from a hedge knight, he was not a man to fear. He had no banners to call, no army of retainers, no great stronghold, no holdings to speak of, no prospects of a great marriage.

> ‚ÄúMy lord, you were fostered at Riverrun. I‚Äôve heard it said that you grew close to the Tullys.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou might say so. The girls especially.‚Äù ‚ÄúHow close?‚Äù ‚ÄúI had their maidenhoods. Is that close enough?‚Äù The lie‚ÄîTyrion was fairly certain it was a lie‚Äîwas delivered with such an air of nonchalance that one could almost believe it. Could it have been Catelyn Stark who lied? About her defloration, and the dagger as well? The longer he lived, the more Tyrion realized that nothing was simple and little was true.

> ‚ÄúSo,‚Äù Lord Petyr continued after a pause, utterly unabashed, ‚Äúwhat‚Äôs in your pot for me?‚Äù ‚ÄúHarrenhal.‚Äù It was interesting to watch his face. Lord Petyr‚Äôs father had been the smallest of small lords, his grandfather a landless hedge knight; by birth, he held no more than a few stony acres on the windswept shore of the Fingers. Harrenhal was one of the richest plums in the Seven Kingdoms, its lands broad and rich and fertile, its great castle as formidable as any in the realm¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand so large as to dwarf Riverrun, where Petyr Baelish had been fostered by House Tully, only to be brusquely expelled when he dared raise his sights to Lord Hoster‚Äôs daughter. Littlefinger took a moment to adjust the drape of his cape, but Tyrion had seen the flash of hunger in those sly cat‚Äôs eyes. I have him, he knew. ‚ÄúHarrenhal is cursed,‚Äù Lord Petyr said after a moment, trying to sound bored. ‚ÄúThen raze it to the ground and build anew to suit yourself. You‚Äôll have no lack of coin. I mean to make you liege lord of the Trident. These river lords have proven they cannot be trusted. Let them do you fealty for their lands.‚Äù ‚ÄúEven the Tullys?‚Äù ‚ÄúIf there are any Tullys left when we are done.‚Äù Littlefinger looked like a boy who had just taken a furtive bite from a honeycomb. He was trying to watch for bees, but the honey was so sweet. ‚ÄúHarrenhal and all its lands and incomes,‚Äù he mused. ‚ÄúWith a stroke, you‚Äôd make me one of the greatest lords in the realm. Not that I‚Äôm ungrateful, my lord, but‚Äîwhy?‚Äù ‚ÄúYou served my sister well in the matter of the succession.‚Äù ‚ÄúAs did Janos Slynt. On whom this same castle of Harrenhal was quite recently bestowed‚Äîonly to be snatched away when he was no longer of use.‚Äù Tyrion laughed. ‚ÄúYou have me, my lord. What can I say? I need you to deliver the Lady Lysa. I did not need Janos Slynt.‚Äù He gave a crooked shrug. ‚ÄúI‚Äôd sooner have you seated in Harrenhal than Renly seated on the Iron Throne. What could be plainer?‚Äù ‚ÄúWhat indeed. You realize that I may need to bed Lysa Arryn again to get her consent to this marriage?‚Äù ‚ÄúI have little doubt you‚Äôll be equal to the task.‚Äù ‚ÄúI once told Ned Stark that when you find yourself naked with an ugly woman, the only thing to do is close your eyes and get on with it.‚Äù Littlefinger steepled his fingers and gazed into Tyrion‚Äôs mismatched eyes. ‚ÄúGive me a fortnight to conclude my affairs and arrange for a ship to carry me to Gulltown.‚Äù ‚ÄúThat will do nicely.‚Äù His guest rose. ‚ÄúThis has been quite the pleasant morning, Lannister. And profitable¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýfor both of us, I trust.‚Äù He bowed, his cape a swirl of yellow as he strode out the door.

> ‚ÄúA war in the Vale would be most tragic,‚Äù said Pycelle. ‚ÄúWar?‚Äù Orton Merryweather laughed. ‚ÄúLord Baelish is a most amusing man, but one does not fight a war with witticisms. I doubt there will be bloodshed. And does it matter who is regent for little Lord Robert, so long as the Vale remits its taxes?‚Äù No, Cersei decided. If truth be told, Littlefinger had been more use at court. He had a gift for finding gold, and never coughed. ‚ÄúLord Orton has convinced me. Maester Pycelle, instruct these Lords Declarant that no harm must come to Petyr. Elsewise, the crown is content with whatever dispositions they might make for the governance of the Vale during Robert Arryn‚Äôs minority.‚Äù

> I thought it was Ser Dontos, my poor old drunken Florian, but it was Petyr all the while. Littlefinger was only a mask he had to wear. Only sometimes Sansa found it hard to tell where the man ended and the mask began. Littlefinger and Lord Petyr looked so very much alike. She would have fled them both, perhaps, but there was nowhere for her to go. Winterfell was burned and desolate, Bran and Rickon dead and cold. Robb had been betrayed and murdered at the Twins, along with their lady mother. Tyrion had been put to death for killing Joffrey, and if she ever returned to King‚Äôs Landing the queen would have her head as well. The aunt she‚Äôd hoped would keep her safe had tried to murder her instead. Her uncle Edmure was a captive of the Freys, while her great-uncle the Blackfish was under siege at Riverrun. I have no place but here, Sansa thought miserably, and no true friend but Petyr.

> ‚ÄúI did not expect you back so soon,‚Äù she said. ‚ÄúI am glad you‚Äôve come.‚Äù ‚ÄúI would never have known it from the kiss you gave me.‚Äù He pulled her closer, caught her face between his hands, and kissed her on the lips for a long time. ‚ÄúNow that‚Äôs the sort of kiss that says welcome home. See that you do better next time.‚Äù ‚ÄúYes, Father.‚Äù She could feel herself blushing. He did not hold her kiss against her. ‚ÄúYou would not believe half of what is happening in King‚Äôs Landing, sweetling. Cersei stumbles from one idiocy to the next, helped along by her council of the deaf, the dim, and the blind. I always anticipated that she would beggar the realm and destroy herself, but I never expected she would do it quite so fast. It is quite vexing. I had hoped to have four or five quiet years to plant some seeds and allow some fruits to ripen, but now¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýit is a good thing that I thrive on chaos. What little peace and order the five kings left us will not long survive the three queens, I fear.‚Äù

> Grisel reappeared before he could say more, balancing a large platter. She set it down between them. There were apples and pears and pomegranates, some sad-looking grapes, a huge blood orange. The old woman had brought a round of bread as well, and a crock of butter. Petyr cut a pomegranate in two with his dagger, offering half to Sansa. ‚ÄúYou should try and eat, my lady.‚Äù ‚ÄúThank you, my lord.‚Äù Pomegranate seeds were so messy; Sansa chose a pear instead, and took a small delicate bite. It was very ripe. The juice ran down her chin. Lord Petyr loosened a seed with the point of his dagger. ‚ÄúYou must miss your father terribly, I know. Lord Eddard was a brave man, honest and loyal¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut quite a hopeless player.‚Äù He brought the seed to his mouth with the knife. ‚ÄúIn King‚Äôs Landing, there are two sorts of people. The players and the pieces.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd I was a piece?‚Äù She dreaded the answer. ‚ÄúYes, but don‚Äôt let that trouble you. You‚Äôre still half a child. Every man‚Äôs a piece to start with, and every maid as well. Even some who think they are players.‚Äù He ate another seed. ‚ÄúCersei, for one. She thinks herself sly, but in truth she is utterly predictable. Her strength rests on her beauty, birth, and riches. Only the first of those is truly her own, and it will soon desert her. I pity her then. She wants power, but has no notion what to do with it when she gets it. Everyone wants something, Alayne. And when you know what a man wants you know who he is, and how to move him.‚Äù ‚ÄúAs you moved Ser Dontos to poison Joffrey?‚Äù It had to have been Dontos, she had concluded. Littlefinger laughed. ‚ÄúSer Dontos the Red was a skin of wine with legs. He could never have been trusted with a task of such enormity. He would have bungled it or betrayed me. No, all Dontos had to do was lead you from the castle¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand make certain you wore your silver hair net.‚Äù The black amethysts. ‚ÄúBut¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýif not Dontos, who? Do you have other¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýpieces?‚Äù ‚ÄúYou could turn King‚Äôs Landing upside down and not find a single man with a mockingbird sewn over his heart, but that does not mean I am friendless.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúTell me, Alayne‚Äîwhich is more dangerous, the dagger brandished by an enemy, or the hidden one pressed to your back by someone you never even see?‚Äù ‚ÄúThe hidden dagger.‚Äù ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs a clever girl.‚Äù He smiled, his thin lips bright red from the pomegranate seeds. ‚ÄúWhen the Imp sent off her guards, the queen had Ser Lancel hire sellswords for her. Lancel found her the Kettleblacks, which delighted your little lord husband, since the lads were in his pay through his man Bronn.‚Äù He chuckled. ‚ÄúBut it was me who told Oswell to get his sons to King‚Äôs Landing when I learned that Bronn was looking for swords. Three hidden daggers, Alayne, now perfectly placed.‚Äù

> Petyr smiled. ‚ÄúI will wager you that at some point during the evening someone told you that your hair net was crooked and straightened it for you.‚Äù Sansa raised a hand to her mouth. ‚ÄúYou cannot mean¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýshe wanted to take me to Highgarden, to marry me to her grandson¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúGentle, pious, good-hearted Willas Tyrell. Be grateful you were spared, he would have bored you spitless. The old woman is not boring, though, I‚Äôll grant her that. A fearsome old harridan, and not near as frail as she pretends. When I came to Highgarden to dicker for Margaery‚Äôs hand, she let her lord son bluster while she asked pointed questions about Joffrey‚Äôs nature. I praised him to the skies, to be sure¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýwhilst my men spread disturbing tales amongst Lord Tyrell‚Äôs servants. That is how the game is played. ‚ÄúI also planted the notion of Ser Loras taking the white. Not that I suggested it, that would have been too crude. But men in my party supplied grisly tales about how the mob had killed Ser Preston Greenfield and raped the Lady Lollys, and slipped a few silvers to Lord Tyrell‚Äôs army of singers to sing of Ryam Redwyne, Serwyn of the Mirror Shield, and Prince Aemon the Dragonknight. A harp can be as dangerous as a sword, in the right hands. ‚ÄúMace Tyrell actually thought it was his own idea to make Ser Loras‚Äôs inclusion in the Kingsguard part of the marriage contract. Who better to protect his daughter than her splendid knightly brother? And it relieved him of the difficult task of trying to find lands and a bride for a third son, never easy, and doubly difficult in Ser Loras‚Äôs case. ‚ÄúBe that as it may. Lady Olenna was not about to let Joff harm her precious darling granddaughter, but unlike her son she also realized that under all his flowers and finery, Ser Loras is as hot-tempered as Jaime Lannister. Toss Joffrey, Margaery, and Loras in a pot, and you‚Äôve got the makings for kingslayer stew. The old woman understood something else as well. Her son was determined to make Margaery a queen, and for that he needed a king¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut he did not need Joffrey. We shall have another wedding soon, wait and see. Margaery will marry Tommen. She‚Äôll keep her queenly crown and her maidenhead, neither of which she especially wants, but what does that matter? The great western alliance will be preserved¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýfor a time, at least.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúMy cousin means to remove you as Lord Protector.‚Äù ‚ÄúIf so, I cannot stop him. I keep a garrison of twenty men. Lord Royce and his friends can raise twenty thousand.‚Äù Petyr went to the oaken chest that sat beneath the window. ‚ÄúBronze Yohn will do what he will do,‚Äù he said, kneeling. He opened the chest, drew out a roll of parchment, and brought it to Lord Nestor. ‚ÄúMy lord. This is a token of the love my lady bore you.‚Äù Sansa watched Royce unroll the parchment. ‚ÄúThis¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthis is unexpected, my lord.‚Äù She was startled to see tears in his eyes. ‚ÄúUnexpected, but not undeserved. My lady valued you above all her other bannermen. You were her rock, she told me.‚Äù ‚ÄúHer rock.‚Äù Lord Nestor reddened. ‚ÄúShe said that?‚Äù ‚ÄúOften. And this‚Äù‚ÄîPetyr gestured at the parchment‚Äî‚Äúis the proof of it.‚Äù ‚ÄúThat¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthat is good to know. Jon Arryn valued my service, I know, but Lady Lysa¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýshe scorned me when I came to court her, and I feared¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù Lord Nestor furrowed his brow. ‚ÄúIt bears the Arryn seal, I see, but the signature¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúLysa was murdered before the document could be presented for her signature, so I signed as Lord Protector. I knew that would have been her wish.‚Äù ‚ÄúI see.‚Äù Lord Nestor rolled the parchment. ‚ÄúYou are¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýdutiful, my lord. Aye, and not without courage. Some will call this grant unseemly, and fault you for making it. The Keeper‚Äôs post has never been hereditary. The Arryns raised the Gates, in the days when they still wore the Falcon Crown and ruled the Vale as kings. The Eyrie was their summer seat, but when the snows began to fall the court would make its descent. Some would say the Gates were as royal as the Eyrie.‚Äù ‚ÄúThere has been no king in the Vale for three hundred years,‚Äù Petyr Baelish pointed out. ‚ÄúThe dragons came,‚Äù Lord Nestor agreed. ‚ÄúBut even after, the Gates remained an Arryn castle. Jon Arryn himself was Keeper of the Gates whilst his father lived. After his ascent, he named his brother Ronnel to the honor, and later his cousin Denys.‚Äù ‚ÄúLord Robert has no brothers, and only distant cousins.‚Äù ‚ÄúTrue.‚Äù Lord Nestor clutched the parchment tightly. ‚ÄúI will not say I had not hoped for this. Whilst Lord Jon ruled the realm as Hand, it fell to me to rule the Vale for him. I did all that he required of me and asked nothing for myself. But by the gods, I earned this!‚Äù ‚ÄúYou did,‚Äù said Petyr, ‚Äúand Lord Robert sleeps more easily knowing that you are always there, a staunch friend at the foot of his mountain.‚Äù He raised a cup. ‚ÄúSo¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýa toast, my lord. To House Royce, Keepers of the Gates of the Moon¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýnow and forever.‚Äù ‚ÄúNow and forever, aye!‚Äù The silver cups crashed together. Later, much later, after the flagon of Arbor gold was dry, Lord Nestor took his leave to rejoin his company of knights. Sansa was asleep on her feet by then, wanting only to crawl off to her bed, but Petyr caught her by the wrist. ‚ÄúYou see the wonders that can be worked with lies and Arbor gold?‚Äù Why did she feel like weeping? It was good that Nestor Royce was with them. ‚ÄúWere they all lies?‚Äù ‚ÄúNot all. Lysa often called Lord Nestor a rock, though I do not think she meant it as a compliment. She called his son a clod. She knew Lord Nestor dreamed of holding the Gates in his own right, a lord in truth as well as name, but Lysa dreamed of other sons and meant the castle to go to Robert‚Äôs little brother.‚Äù He stood. ‚ÄúDo you understand what happened here, Alayne?‚Äù Sansa hesitated a moment. ‚ÄúYou gave Lord Nestor the Gates of the Moon to be certain of his support.‚Äù ‚ÄúI did,‚Äù Petyr admitted, ‚Äúbut our rock is a Royce, which is to say he is overproud and prickly. Had I asked him his price, he would have swelled up like an angry toad at the slight upon his honor. But this way¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthe man is not utterly stupid, but the lies I served him were sweeter than the truth. He wants to believe that Lysa valued him above her other bannermen. One of those others is Bronze Yohn, after all, and Nestor is very much aware that he was born of the lesser branch of House Royce. He wants more for his son. Men of honor will do things for their children that they would never consider doing for themselves.‚Äù She nodded. ‚ÄúThe signature¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýyou might have had Lord Robert put his hand and seal to it, but instead¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚Äú‚Ä¶¬ÝI signed myself, as Lord Protector. Why?‚Äù ‚ÄúSo ‚Ä¶ if you are removed, or ‚Ä¶ or killed ‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚Äú‚Ä¶¬ÝLord Nestor‚Äôs claim to the Gates will suddenly be called into question. I promise you, that is not lost on him. It was clever of you to see it. Though no more than I‚Äôd expect of mine own daughter.‚Äù

> Petyr was seated at the trestle table with a cup of wine to hand, looking over a crisp white parchment. He glanced up as the Lords Declarant filed in. ‚ÄúMy lords, be welcome. And you as well, my lady. The ascent is wearisome, I know. Please be seated. Alayne, my sweet, more wine for our noble guests.‚Äù ‚ÄúAs you say, Father.‚Äù The candles had been lighted, she was pleased to see; the solar smelled of nutmeg and other costly spices. She went to fetch the flagon whilst the visitors arranged themselves side by side¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýall save Nestor Royce, who hesitated before walking around the table to take the empty chair beside Lord Petyr, and Lyn Corbray, who went to stand beside the hearth instead. The heart-shaped ruby in the pommel of his sword shone redly as he warmed his hands. Alayne saw him smile at Ser Lothor Brune. Ser Lyn is very handsome, for an older man, she thought, but I do not like the way he smiles. ‚ÄúI have been reading this remarkable declaration of yours,‚Äù Petyr began. ‚ÄúSplendid. Whatever maester wrote this has a gift for words. I only wish you had invited me to sign as well.‚Äù That took them unawares. ‚ÄúYou?‚Äù said Belmore. ‚ÄúSign?‚Äù ‚ÄúI wield a quill as well as any man, and no one loves Lord Robert more than I do. As for these false friends and evil counselors, by all means let us root them out. My lords, I am with you, heart and hand. Show me where to sign, I beg you.‚Äù Alayne, pouring, heard Lyn Corbray chuckle. The others seemed at a loss till Bronze Yohn Royce cracked his knuckles, and said, ‚ÄúWe did not come for your signature. Nor do we mean to bandy words with you, Littlefinger.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhat a pity. I do so love a nicely bandied word.‚Äù Petyr set the parchment to one side. ‚ÄúAs you wish. Let us be blunt. What would you have of me, my lords and lady?‚Äù ‚ÄúWe will have naught of you.‚Äù Symond Templeton fixed the Lord Protector with his cold blue stare. ‚ÄúWe will have you gone.‚Äù ‚ÄúGone?‚Äù Petyr feigned surprise. ‚ÄúWhere would I go?‚Äù ‚ÄúThe crown has made you Lord of Harrenhal,‚Äù Young Lord Hunter pointed out. ‚ÄúThat should be enough for any man.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe riverlands have need of a lord,‚Äù old Horton Redfort said. ‚ÄúRiverrun stands besieged, Bracken and Blackwood are at open war, and outlaws roam freely on both sides of the Trident, stealing and killing as they will. Unburied corpses litter the landscape everywhere you go.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou make it sound so wonderfully attractive, Lord Redfort,‚Äù Petyr answered, ‚Äúbut as it happens I have pressing duties here. And there is Lord Robert to consider. Would you have me drag a sickly child into the midst of such carnage?‚Äù ‚ÄúHis lordship will remain in the Vale,‚Äù declared Yohn Royce. ‚ÄúI mean to take the boy with me to Runestone, and raise him up to be a knight that Jon Arryn would be proud of.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhy Runestone?‚Äù Petyr mused. ‚ÄúWhy not Ironoaks or the Redfort? Why not Longbow Hall?‚Äù ‚ÄúAny of these would serve as well,‚Äù declared Lord Belmore, ‚Äúand his lordship will visit each in turn, in due time.‚Äù ‚ÄúWill he?‚Äù Petyr‚Äôs tone seemed to hint at doubts.

> Lady Waynwood sighed. ‚ÄúLord Petyr, if you think to set us one against the other, you may spare yourself the effort. We speak with one voice here. Runestone suits us all. Lord Yohn raised three fine sons of his own, there is no man more fit to foster his young lordship. Maester Helliweg is a good deal older and more experienced than your own Maester Colemon, and better suited to treat Lord Robert‚Äôs frailties. In Runestone the boy will learn the arts of war from Strong Sam Stone. No man could hope for a finer master-at-arms. Septon Lucos will instruct him in matters of the spirit. At Runestone he will also find other boys his own age, more suitable companions than the old women and sellswords that presently surround him.‚Äù Petyr Baelish fingered his beard. ‚ÄúHis lordship needs companions, I do not disagree. Alayne is hardly an old woman, though. Lord Robert loves my daughter dearly, he will be glad to tell you so himself. And as it happens, I have asked Lord Grafton and Lord Lynderly to send me each a son to ward. Each of them has a boy of an age with Robert.‚Äù Lyn Corbray laughed. ‚ÄúTwo pups from a pair of lapdogs.‚Äù ‚ÄúRobert should have an older boy about him too. A promising young squire, say. Someone he could admire and try to emulate.‚Äù Petyr turned to Lady Waynwood. ‚ÄúYou have such a boy at Ironoaks, my lady. Perhaps you might agree to send me Harrold Hardyng.‚Äù Anya Waynwood seemed amused. ‚ÄúLord Petyr, you are as bold a thief as I‚Äôd ever care to meet.‚Äù ‚ÄúI do not wish to steal the boy,‚Äù said Petyr, ‚Äúbut he and Lord Robert should be friends.‚Äù Bronze Yohn Royce leaned forward. ‚ÄúIt is meet and proper that Lord Robert should befriend young Harry, and he shall ‚Ä¶ at Runestone, under my care, as my ward and squire.‚Äù ‚ÄúGive us the boy,‚Äù said Lord Belmore, ‚Äúand you may depart the Vale unmolested for your proper seat at Harrenhal.‚Äù Petyr gave him a look of mild reproach. ‚ÄúAre you suggesting that elsewise I might come to harm, my lord? I cannot think why. My late wife seemed to think this was my proper seat.‚Äù ‚ÄúLord Baelish,‚Äù Lady Waynwood said, ‚ÄúLysa Tully was Jon Arryn‚Äôs widow and the mother of his child, and ruled here as his regent. You ‚Ä¶ let us be frank, you are no Arryn, and Lord Robert is no blood of yours. By what right do you presume to rule us?‚Äù ‚ÄúLysa named me Lord Protector, I do seem to recall.‚Äù Young Lord Hunter said, ‚ÄúLysa Tully was never truly of the Vale, nor had she the right to dispose of us.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd Lord Robert?‚Äù Petyr asked. ‚ÄúWill your lordship also claim that Lady Lysa had no right to dispose of her own son?‚Äù Nestor Royce had been silent all this while, but now he spoke up loudly. ‚ÄúI once hoped to wed Lady Lysa myself. As did Lord Hunter‚Äôs father and Lady Anya‚Äôs son. Corbray scarce left her side for half a year. Had she chosen any one of us, no man here would dispute his right to be the Lord Protector. It happens that she chose Lord Littlefinger, and entrusted her son to his care.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe was Jon Arryn‚Äôs son as well, cousin,‚Äù Bronze Yohn said, frowning at the Keeper. ‚ÄúHe belongs to the Vale.‚Äù Petyr feigned puzzlement. ‚ÄúThe Eyrie is as much a part of the Vale as Runestone. Unless someone has moved it?‚Äù ‚ÄúJape all you like, Littlefinger,‚Äù Lord Belmore blustered. ‚ÄúThe boy shall come with us.‚Äù ‚ÄúI am loath to disappoint you, Lord Belmore, but my stepson will be remaining here with me. He is not a robust child, as all of you know well. The journey would tax him sorely. As his stepfather and Lord Protector, I cannot permit it.‚Äù Symond Templeton cleared his throat, and said, ‚ÄúEach of us has a thousand men at the foot of this mountain, Littlefinger.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhat a splendid place for them.‚Äù ‚ÄúIf need be, we can summon many more.‚Äù ‚ÄúAre you threatening me with war, ser?‚Äù Petyr did not sound the least afraid. Bronze Yohn said, ‚ÄúWe shall have Lord Robert.‚Äù

> For a moment it seemed as though they had come to an impasse, until Lyn Corbray turned from the fire. ‚ÄúAll this talk makes me ill. Littlefinger will talk you out of your smallclothes if you listen long enough. The only way to settle his sort is with steel.‚Äù He drew his longsword. Petyr spread his hands. ‚ÄúI wear no sword, ser.‚Äù ‚ÄúEasily remedied.‚Äù Candlelight rippled along the smoke-grey steel of Corbray‚Äôs blade, so dark that it put Sansa in mind of Ice, her father‚Äôs greatsword. ‚ÄúYour apple-eater holds a blade. Tell him to give it to you, or draw that dagger.‚Äù She saw Lothor Brune reach for his own sword, but before the blades could meet Bronze Yohn rose in wrath. ‚ÄúPut up your steel, ser! Are you a Corbray or a Frey? We are guests here.‚Äù Lady Waynwood pursed her lips, and said, ‚ÄúThis is unseemly.‚Äù ‚ÄúSheathe your sword, Corbray,‚Äù Young Lord Hunter echoed. ‚ÄúYou shame us all with this.‚Äù ‚ÄúCome, Lyn,‚Äù chided Redfort in a softer tone. ‚ÄúThis will serve for nought. Put Lady Forlorn to bed.‚Äù ‚ÄúMy lady has a thirst,‚Äù Ser Lyn insisted. ‚ÄúWhenever she comes out to dance, she likes a drop of red.‚Äù ‚ÄúYour lady must go thirsty.‚Äù Bronze Yohn put himself squarely in Corbray‚Äôs path. ‚ÄúThe Lords Declarant.‚Äù Lyn Corbray snorted. ‚ÄúYou should have named yourselves the Six Old Women.‚Äù He slid the dark sword back into its scabbard and left them, shouldering Brune aside as if he were not there. Alayne listened to his footsteps recede. Anya Waynwood and Horton Redfort exchanged a look. Hunter drained his wine cup and held it out to be refilled. ‚ÄúLord Baelish,‚Äù Ser Symond said, ‚Äúyou must forgive us that display.‚Äù ‚ÄúMust I?‚Äù Littlefinger‚Äôs voice had grown cold. ‚ÄúYou brought him here, my lords.‚Äù Bronze Yohn said, ‚ÄúIt was never our intent‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúYou brought him here. I would be well within my rights to call my guards and have all of you arrested.‚Äù Hunter lurched to his feet so wildly that he almost knocked the flagon out of Alayne‚Äôs hands. ‚ÄúYou gave us safe conduct!‚Äù ‚ÄúYes. Be grateful that I have more honor than some.‚Äù Petyr sounded as angry as she had ever heard him. ‚ÄúI have read your declaration and heard your demands. Now hear mine. Remove your armies from this mountain. Go home and leave my son in peace. Misrule there has been, I will not deny it, but that was Lysa‚Äôs work, not mine. Grant me but a year, and with Lord Nestor‚Äôs help I promise that none of you shall have any cause for grievance.‚Äù ‚ÄúSo you say,‚Äù said Belmore. ‚ÄúYet how shall we trust you?‚Äù ‚ÄúYou dare call me untrustworthy? It was not me who bared steel at a parley. You write of defending Lord Robert even as you deny him food. That must end. I am no warrior, but I will fight you if you do not lift this siege. There are other lords besides you in the Vale, and King‚Äôs Landing will send men as well. If it is war you want, say so now and the Vale will bleed.‚Äù Alayne could see the doubt blooming in the eyes of the Lords Declarant. ‚ÄúA year is not so long a time,‚Äù Lord Redfort said uncertainly. ‚ÄúMayhaps¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýif you gave assurances¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúNone of us wants war,‚Äù acknowledged Lady Waynwood. ‚ÄúAutumn wanes, and we must gird ourselves for winter.‚Äù Belmore cleared his throat. ‚ÄúAt the end of this year¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚Äú‚Ä¶¬Ýif I have not set the Vale to rights, I shall willingly step down as Lord Protector,‚Äù Petyr promised them. ‚ÄúI call that more than fair,‚Äù Lord Nestor Royce put in. ‚ÄúThere must be no reprisals,‚Äù insisted Templeton. ‚ÄúNo talk of treason or rebellion. You must swear to that as well.‚Äù ‚ÄúGladly,‚Äù said Petyr. ‚ÄúIt is friends I want, not foes. I shall pardon all of you, in writing if you wish. Even Lyn Corbray. His brother is a good man, there is no need to bring down shame upon a noble House.‚Äù Lady Waynwood turned to her fellow Lords Declarant. ‚ÄúMy lords, perhaps we might confer?‚Äù ‚ÄúThere is no need. It is plain that he has won.‚Äù Bronze Yohn‚Äôs grey eyes considered Petyr Baelish. ‚ÄúI like it not, but it would seem you have your year. Best use it well, my lord. Not all of us are fooled.‚Äù He opened the door so forcefully that he all but wrenched it off its hinges. Later there was a feast of sorts, though Petyr was forced to make apologies for the humble fare. Robert was trotted out in a doublet of cream and blue, and played the little lord quite graciously. Bronze Yohn was not there to see; he had already departed from the Eyrie to begin the long descent, as had Ser Lyn Corbray before him. The other lords remained with them till morn. He bewitched them, Alayne thought as she lay abed that night listening to the wind howl outside her windows. She could not have said where the suspicion came from, but once it crossed her mind it would not let her sleep. She tossed and turned, worrying at it like a dog at some old bone. Finally, she rose and dressed herself, leaving Gretchel to her dreams. Petyr was still awake, scratching out a letter. ‚ÄúAlayne,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúMy sweet. What brings you here so late?‚Äù ‚ÄúI had to know. What will happen in a year?‚Äù He put down his quill. ‚ÄúRedfort and Waynwood are old. One or both of them may die. Gilwood Hunter will be murdered by his brothers. Most likely by young Harlan, who arranged Lord Eon‚Äôs death. In for a penny, in for a stag, I always say. Belmore is corrupt and can be bought. Templeton I shall befriend. Bronze Yohn Royce will continue to be hostile, I fear, but so long as he stands alone he is not so much a threat.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd Ser Lyn Corbray?‚Äù The candlelight was dancing in his eyes. ‚ÄúSer Lyn will remain my implacable enemy. He will speak of me with scorn and loathing to every man he meets, and lend his sword to every secret plot to bring me down.‚Äù That was when her suspicion turned to certainty. ‚ÄúAnd how shall you reward him for this service?‚Äù Littlefinger laughed aloud. ‚ÄúWith gold and boys and promises, of course. Ser Lyn is a man of simple tastes, my sweetling. All he likes is gold and boys and killing.‚Äù

{:#Dagmer-Cleftjaw}
**Dagmer Cleftjaw**

> Dagmer Cleftjaw stood by the high carved prow of his longship, Foamdrinker. Theon had assigned him the task of guarding the ships; otherwise men would have called it Dagmer‚Äôs victory, not his. A more prickly man might have taken that for a slight, but the Cleftjaw had only laughed. ‚ÄúThe day is won,‚Äù Dagmer called down. ‚ÄúAnd yet you do not smile, boy. The living should smile, for the dead cannot.‚Äù He smiled himself to show how it was done. It made for a hideous sight. Under a snowy white mane of hair, Dagmer Cleftjaw had the most gut-churning scar Theon had ever seen, the legacy of the longaxe that had near killed him as a boy. The blow had splintered his jaw, shattered his front teeth, and left him four lips where other men had but two. A shaggy beard covered his cheeks and neck, but the hair would not grow over the scar, so a shiny seam of puckered, twisted flesh divided his face like a crevasse through a snowfield.

> ‚ÄúYou are my father‚Äôs man.‚Äù ‚ÄúHis best man, and always have been.‚Äù Pride, Theon thought. He is proud, I must use that, his pride will be the key. ‚ÄúThere is no man in the Iron Islands half so skilled with spear or sword.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou have been too long away, boy. When you left, it was as you say, but I am grown old in Lord Greyjoy‚Äôs service. The singers call Andrik best now. Andrik the Unsmiling, they name him. A giant of a man. He serves Lord Drumm of Old Wyk. And Black Lorren and Qarl the Maid are near as dread.‚Äù ‚ÄúThis Andrik may be a great fighter, but men do not fear him as they fear you.‚Äù ‚ÄúAye, that‚Äôs so,‚Äù Dagmer said. The fingers curled around the drinking horn were heavy with rings, gold and silver and bronze, set with chunks of sapphire and garnet and dragonglass. He had paid the iron price for every one, Theon knew. ‚ÄúIf I had a man like you in my service, I should not waste him on this child‚Äôs business of harrying and burning. This is no work for Lord Balon‚Äôs best man¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù Dagmer‚Äôs grin twisted his lips apart and showed the brown splinters of his teeth. ‚ÄúNor for his trueborn son?‚Äù He hooted. ‚ÄúI know you too well, Theon. I saw you take your first step, helped you bend your first bow. ‚ÄôTis not me who feels wasted.‚Äù ‚ÄúBy rights I should have my sister‚Äôs command,‚Äù he admitted, uncomfortably aware of how peevish that sounded. ‚ÄúYou take this business too hard, boy. It is only that your lord father does not know you. With your brothers dead and you taken by the wolves, your sister was his solace. He learned to rely on her, and she has never failed him.‚Äù ‚ÄúNor have I. The Starks knew my worth. I was one of Brynden Blackfish‚Äôs picked scouts, and I charged with the first wave in the Whispering Wood. I was that close to crossing swords with the Kingslayer himself.‚Äù Theon held his hands two feet apart. ‚ÄúDaryn Hornwood came between us, and died for it.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhy do you tell me this?‚Äù Dagmer asked. ‚ÄúIt was me who put your first sword in your hand. I know you are no craven.‚Äù ‚ÄúDoes my father?‚Äù The hoary old warrior looked as if he had bitten into something he did not like the taste of. ‚ÄúIt is only¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝTheon, the Boy Wolf is your friend, and these Starks had you for ten years.‚Äù ‚ÄúI am no Stark.‚Äù Lord Eddard saw to that. ‚ÄúI am a Greyjoy, and I mean to be my father‚Äôs heir. How can I do that unless I prove myself with some great deed?‚Äù ‚ÄúYou are young: Other wars will come, and you shall do your great deeds. For now, we are commanded to harry the Stony Shore.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúYou will make camp outside their walls and set to building catapults and siege engines.‚Äù ‚ÄúThat is not the Old Way. Have you forgotten? Ironmen fight with swords and axes, not by flinging rocks. There is no glory in starving out a foeman.‚Äù ‚ÄúLeobald will not know that. When he sees you raising siege towers, his old woman‚Äôs blood will run cold, and he will bleat for help. Stay your archers, Uncle, and let the raven fly. The castellan at Winterfell is a brave man, but age has stiffened his wits as well as his limbs. When he learns that one of his king‚Äôs bannermen is under attack by the fearsome Dagmer Cleftjaw, he will summon his strength and ride to Tallhart‚Äôs aid. It is his duty. Ser Rodrik is nothing if not dutiful.‚Äù ‚ÄúAny force he summons will be larger than mine,‚Äù Dagmer said, ‚Äúand these old knights are more cunning than you think, or they would never have lived to see their first grey hair. You set us a battle we cannot hope to win, Theon. This Torrhen‚Äôs Square will never fall.‚Äù Theon smiled. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs not Torrhen‚Äôs Square I mean to take.‚Äù

{:#Tommen-Baratheon}
**Tommen Baratheon**

> Though he had Joffrey‚Äôs golden curls and green eyes, the new king shared little else with his late brother. He inclined to plumpness, his face was pink and round, and he even liked to read. He is still shy of nine, this son of mine. The boy is not the man. It would be seven years before Tommen was ruling in his own right. Until then the realm would remain firmly in the hands of his lord grandfather. ‚ÄúSire,‚Äù he asked, ‚Äúdo I have your leave to go?‚Äù ‚ÄúAs you like, Ser Uncle.‚Äù Tommen looked back to Ser Kevan. ‚ÄúCan I seal them now, Great-Uncle?‚Äù Pressing his royal seal into the hot wax was his favorite part of being king, so far.

> At that hour, her son was fast asleep, but Cersei looked in upon him before seeking her own bed. She was surprised to find three black kittens cuddled up beside him, ‚ÄúWhere did those come from?‚Äù she asked Ser Meryn Trant, outside the royal bedchamber. ‚ÄúThe little queen gave them to him. She only meant to give him one, but he couldn‚Äôt decide which one he liked the best.‚Äù Better than cutting them out of their mother with a dagger, I suppose. Margaery‚Äôs clumsy attempts at seduction were so obvious as to be laughable. Tommen is too young for kisses, so she gives him kittens. Cersei rather wished they were not black, though. Black cats brought ill luck, as Rhaegar‚Äôs little girl had discovered in this very castle. She would have been my daughter, if the Mad King had not played his cruel jape on Father. It had to have been the madness that led Aerys to refuse Lord Tywin‚Äôs daughter and take his son instead, whilst marrying his own son to a feeble Dornish princess with black eyes and a flat chest.

> Outside, the sun was setting. Dorcas had prepared a bath for her. The queen was soaking pleasantly in the warm water and contemplating what she would say to her supper guests when Jaime came bursting through the door and ordered Jocelyn and Dorcas from the room. Her brother looked rather less than immaculate and had a smell of horse about him. He had Tommen with him too. ‚ÄúSweet sister,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúthe king requires a word.‚Äù Cersei‚Äôs golden tresses floated in the bathwater. The room was steamy. A drop of sweat trickled down her cheek. ‚ÄúTommen?‚Äù she said, in a dangerously soft voice. ‚ÄúWhat is it now?‚Äù The boy knew that tone. He shrank back. ‚ÄúHis Grace wants his white courser on the morrow,‚Äù Jaime said. ‚ÄúFor his jousting lesson.‚Äù She sat up in the tub. ‚ÄúThere will be no jousting.‚Äù ‚ÄúYes, there will.‚Äù Tommen puffed out his lower lip. ‚ÄúI have to ride every day.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd you shall,‚Äù the queen declared, ‚Äúonce we have a proper master-at-arms to supervise your training.‚Äù ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt want a proper master-at-arms. I want Ser Loras.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou make too much of that boy. Your little wife has filled your head with foolish notions of his prowess, I know, but Osmund Kettleblack is thrice the knight that Loras is.‚Äù Jaime laughed. ‚ÄúNot the Osmund Kettleblack I know.‚Äù She could have throttled him. Perhaps I need to command Ser Loras to allow Ser Osmund to unhorse him. That might chase the stars from Tommen‚Äôs eyes. Salt a slug and shame a hero, and they shrink right up. ‚ÄúI am sending for a Dornishman to train you,‚Äù she said. ‚ÄúThe Dornish are the finest jousters in the realm.‚Äù ‚ÄúThey are not,‚Äù said Tommen. ‚ÄúAnyway, I don‚Äôt want any stupid Dornishman, I want Ser Loras. I command it.‚Äù Jaime laughed. He is no help at all. Does he think this is amusing? The queen slapped the water angrily. ‚ÄúMust I send for Pate? You do not command me. I am your mother.‚Äù ‚ÄúYes, but I‚Äôm the king. Margaery says that everyone has to do what the king says. I want my white courser saddled on the morrow so Ser Loras can teach me how to joust. I want a kitten too, and I don‚Äôt want to eat beets.‚Äù He crossed his arms. Jaime was still laughing. The queen ignored him. ‚ÄúTommen, come here.‚Äù When he hung back, she sighed. ‚ÄúAre you afraid? A king should not show fear.‚Äù The boy approached the tub, his eyes downcast. She reached out and stroked his golden curls. ‚ÄúKing or no, you are a little boy. Until you come of age, the rule is mine. You will learn to joust, I promise you. But not from Loras. The knights of the Kingsguard have more important duties than playing with a child. Ask the Lord Commander. Isn‚Äôt that so, ser?‚Äù ‚ÄúVery important duties.‚Äù Jaime smiled thinly. ‚ÄúRiding round the city walls, for an instance.‚Äù Tommen looked close to tears. ‚ÄúCan I still have a kitten?‚Äù ‚ÄúPerhaps,‚Äù the queen allowed. ‚ÄúSo long as I hear no more nonsense about jousting. Can you promise me that?‚Äù He shuffled his feet. ‚ÄúYes.‚Äù ‚ÄúGood. Now run along. My guests will be here shortly.‚Äù Tommen ran along, but before he left he turned back to say, ‚ÄúWhen I‚Äôm king in my own right, I‚Äôm going to outlaw beets.‚Äù

> The king seemed happier than Kevan Lannister had seen him in a long time. From soup to sweet Tommen burbled about the exploits of his kittens, whilst feeding them morsels of pike off his own royal plate. ‚ÄúThe bad cat was outside my window last night,‚Äù he informed Kevan at one point, ‚Äúbut Ser Pounce hissed at him and he ran off across the roofs.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe bad cat?‚Äù Ser Kevan said, amused. He is a sweet boy. ‚ÄúAn old black tomcat with a torn ear,‚Äù Cersei told him. ‚ÄúA filthy thing, and foul-tempered. He clawed Joff‚Äôs hand once.‚Äù She made a face. ‚ÄúThe cats keep the rats down, I know, but that one¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýhe‚Äôs been known to attack ravens in the rookery.‚Äù ‚ÄúI will ask the ratters to set a trap for him.‚Äù

{:#Jaime-Lannister}
**Jaime Lannister**

> ‚ÄúHow did my son Bran come to fall?‚Äù ‚ÄúI flung him from a window.‚Äù The easy way he said it took her voice away for an instant. If I had a knife, I would kill him now, she thought, until she remembered the girls. Her throat constricted as she said, ‚ÄúYou were a knight, sworn to defend the weak and innocent.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe was weak enough, but perhaps not so innocent. He was spying on us.‚Äù ‚ÄúBran would not spy.‚Äù ‚ÄúThen blame those precious gods of yours, who brought the boy to our window and gave him a glimpse of something he was never meant to see.‚Äù ‚ÄúBlame the gods?‚Äù she said, incredulous. ‚ÄúYours was the hand that threw him. You meant for him to die.‚Äù His chains chinked softly. ‚ÄúI seldom fling children from towers to improve their health. Yes, I meant for him to die.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúHow can you still count yourself a knight, when you have forsaken every vow you ever swore?‚Äù Jaime reached for the flagon to refill his cup. ‚ÄúSo many vows¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthey make you swear and swear. Defend the king. Obey the king. Keep his secrets. Do his bidding. Your life for his. But obey your father. Love your sister. Protect the innocent. Defend the weak. Respect the gods. Obey the laws. It‚Äôs too much. No matter what you do, you‚Äôre forsaking one vow or the other.‚Äù He took a healthy swallow of wine and closed his eyes for an instant, leaning his head back against the patch of nitre on the wall. ‚ÄúI was the youngest man ever to wear the white cloak.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd the youngest to betray all it stood for, Kingslayer.‚Äù ‚ÄúKingslayer,‚Äù he pronounced carefully. ‚ÄúAnd such a king he was!‚Äù He lifted his cup. ‚ÄúTo Aerys Targaryen, the Second of His Name, Lord of the Seven Kingdoms and Protector of the Realm. And to the sword that opened his throat. A golden sword, don‚Äôt you know. Until his blood ran red down the blade. Those are the Lannister colors, red and gold.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúThith ith a thweet day,‚Äù Vargo Hoat said. Around his neck hung a chain of linked coins, coins of every shape and size, cast and hammered, bearing the likenesses of kings, wizards, gods and demons, and all manner of fanciful beasts. Coins from every land where he has fought, Jaime remembered. Greed was the key to this man. If he was turned once, he can be turned again. ‚ÄúLord Vargo, you were foolish to leave my father‚Äôs service, but it is not too late to make amends. He will pay well for me, you know it.‚Äù ‚ÄúOh yeth,‚Äù said Vargo Hoat. ‚ÄúHalf the gold in Cathterly Rock, I thall have. But firth I mutht thend him a methage.‚Äù He said something in his slithery goatish tongue. Urswyck shoved him in the back, and a jester in green and pink motley kicked his legs out from under him. When he hit the ground one of the archers grabbed the chain between Jaime‚Äôs wrists and used it to yank his arms out in front of him. The fat Dothraki put aside his knife to unsheathe a huge curved arakh, the wickedly sharp scythe-sword the horselords loved. They mean to scare me.
