The fool hopped on Jaime‚Äôs back, giggling, as the Dothraki swaggered toward him. The goat wants me to piss my breeches and beg his mercy, but he‚Äôll never have that pleasure. He was a Lannister of Casterly Rock, Lord Commander of the Kingsguard; no sellsword would make him scream. Sunlight ran silver along the edge of the arakh as it came shivering down, almost too fast to see. And Jaime screamed.

> ‚ÄúYou cannot serve in the Kingsguard without a sword hand‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúI can,‚Äù he interrupted. ‚ÄúAnd I will. There‚Äôs precedent. I‚Äôll look in the White Book and find it, if you like. Crippled or whole, a knight of the Kingsguard serves for life.‚Äù ‚ÄúCersei ended that when she replaced Ser Barristan on grounds of age. A suitable gift to the Faith will persuade the High Septon to release you from your vows. Your sister was foolish to dismiss Selmy, admittedly, but now that she has opened the gates‚Äî‚Äù ‚Äú‚Äîsomeone needs to close them again.‚Äù Jaime stood. ‚ÄúI am tired of having highborn women kicking pails of shit at me, Father. No one ever asked me if I wanted to be Lord Commander of the Kingsguard, but it seems I am. I have a duty‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúYou do.‚Äù Lord Tywin rose as well. ‚ÄúA duty to House Lannister. You are the heir to Casterly Rock. That is where you should be. Tommen should accompany you, as your ward and squire. The Rock is where he‚Äôll learn to be a Lannister, and I want him away from his mother. I mean to find a new husband for Cersei. Oberyn Martell perhaps, once I convince Lord Tyrell that the match does not threaten Highgarden. And it is past time you were wed. The Tyrells are now insisting that Margaery be wed to Tommen, but if I were to offer you instead‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúNO!‚Äù Jaime had heard all that he could stand. No, more than he could stand. He was sick of it, sick of lords and lies, sick of his father, his sister, sick of the whole bloody business. ‚ÄúNo. No. No. No. No. How many times must I say no before you‚Äôll hear it? Oberyn Martell? The man‚Äôs infamous, and not just for poisoning his sword. He has more bastards than Robert, and beds with boys as well. And if you think for one misbegotten moment that I would wed Joffrey‚Äôs widow¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúLord Tyrell swears the girl‚Äôs still maiden.‚Äù ‚ÄúShe can die a maiden as far as I‚Äôm concerned. I don‚Äôt want her, and I don‚Äôt want your Rock!‚Äù ‚ÄúYou are my son‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúI am a knight of the Kingsguard. The Lord Commander of the Kingsguard! And that‚Äôs all I mean to be!‚Äù Firelight gleamed golden in the stiff whiskers that framed Lord Tywin‚Äôs face. A vein pulsed in his neck, but he did not speak. And did not speak. And did not speak. The strained silence went on until it was more than Jaime could endure. ‚ÄúFather¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù he began. ‚ÄúYou are not my son.‚Äù Lord Tywin turned his face away. ‚ÄúYou say you are the Lord Commander of the Kingsguard, and only that. Very well, ser. Go do your duty.‚Äù

> Torchlight fell across his face. He shielded his eyes with a hand. ‚ÄúCome on, are you frightened of a dwarf? Do it, you son of a poxy whore.‚Äù His voice had grown hoarse from disuse. ‚ÄúIs that any way to speak about our lady mother?‚Äù The man moved forward, a torch in his left hand. ‚ÄúThis is even more ghastly than my cell at Riverrun, though not quite so dank.‚Äù For a moment Tyrion could not breathe. ‚ÄúYou?‚Äù ‚ÄúWell, most of me.‚Äù Jaime was gaunt, his hair hacked short. ‚ÄúI left a hand at Harrenhal. Bringing the Brave Companions across the narrow sea was not one of Father‚Äôs better notions.‚Äù He lifted his arm, and Tyrion saw the stump. A bark of hysterical laughter burst from his lips. ‚ÄúOh, gods,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúJaime, I am so sorry, but¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýgods be good, look at the two of us. Handless and Noseless, the Lannister boys.‚Äù ‚ÄúThere were days when my hand smelled so bad I wished I was noseless.‚Äù Jaime lowered the torch, so the light bathed his brother‚Äôs face. ‚ÄúAn impressive scar.‚Äù Tyrion turned away from the glare. ‚ÄúThey made me fight a battle without my big brother to protect me.‚Äù ‚ÄúI heard tell you almost burned the city down.‚Äù ‚ÄúA filthy lie. I only burned the river.‚Äù Abruptly, Tyrion remembered where he was, and why. ‚ÄúAre you here to kill me?‚Äù ‚ÄúNow that‚Äôs ungrateful. Perhaps I should leave you here to rot if you‚Äôre going to be so discourteous.‚Äù ‚ÄúRotting is not the fate Cersei has in mind for me.‚Äù ‚ÄúWell no, if truth be told. You‚Äôre to be beheaded on the morrow, out on the old tourney grounds.‚Äù Tyrion laughed again. ‚ÄúWill there be food? You‚Äôll have to help me with my last words, my wits have been running about like a rat in a root cellar.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou won‚Äôt need last words. I‚Äôm rescuing you.‚Äù Jaime‚Äôs voice was strangely solemn. ‚ÄúWho said I required rescue?‚Äù ‚ÄúYou know, I‚Äôd almost forgotten what an annoying little man you are. Now that you‚Äôve reminded me, I do believe I‚Äôll let Cersei cut your head off after all.‚Äù ‚ÄúOh no you won‚Äôt.‚Äù He waddled out of the cell. ‚ÄúIs it day or night up above? I‚Äôve lost all sense of time.‚Äù ‚ÄúThree hours past midnight. The city sleeps.‚Äù Jaime slid the torch back into its sconce, on the wall between the cells. The corridor was so poorly lit that Tyrion almost stumbled on the turnkey, sprawled across the cold stone floor. He prodded him with a toe. ‚ÄúIs he dead?‚Äù ‚ÄúAsleep. The other three as well. The eunuch dosed their wine with sweetsleep, but not enough to kill them. Or so he swears. He is waiting back at the stair, dressed up in a septon‚Äôs robe. You‚Äôre going down into the sewers, and from there to the river. A galley is waiting in the bay. Varys has agents in the Free Cities who will see that you do not lack for funds¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut try not to be conspicuous. Cersei will send men after you, I have no doubt. You might do well to take another name.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnother name? Oh, certainly. And when the Faceless Men come to kill me, I‚Äôll say, ‚ÄòNo, you have the wrong man, I‚Äôm a different dwarf with a hideous facial scar.‚Äô¬Ý‚Äù Both Lannisters laughed at the absurdity of it all. Then Jaime went to one knee and kissed him quickly once on each cheek, his lips brushing against the puckered ribbon of scar tissue. ‚ÄúThank you, Brother,‚Äù Tyrion said. ‚ÄúFor my life.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúWhoever did it,‚Äù he told them, ‚ÄúJoffrey is dead, and the Iron Throne belongs to Tommen now. I mean for him to sit on it until his hair turns white and his teeth fall out. And not from poison.‚Äù Jaime turned to Ser Boros Blount. The man had grown stout in recent years, though he was big-boned enough to carry it. ‚ÄúSer Boros, you look like a man who enjoys his food. Henceforth you‚Äôll taste everything Tommen eats or drinks.‚Äù Ser Osmund Kettleblack laughed aloud and the Knight of Flowers smiled, but Ser Boros turned a deep beet red. ‚ÄúI am no food taster! I am a knight of the Kingsguard!‚Äù ‚ÄúSad to say, you are.‚Äù Cersei should never have stripped the man of his white cloak. But their father had only compounded the shame by restoring it. ‚ÄúMy sister has told me how readily you yielded my nephew to Tyrion‚Äôs sellswords. You will find carrots and pease less threatening, I hope. When your Sworn Brothers are training in the yard with sword and shield, you may train with spoon and trencher. Tommen loves applecakes. Try not to let any sellswords make off with them.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou speak to me thus? You?‚Äù ‚ÄúYou should have died before you let Tommen be taken.‚Äù ‚ÄúAs you died protecting Aerys, ser?‚Äù Ser Boros lurched to his feet, and clasped the hilt of his sword. ‚ÄúI won‚Äôt¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝI won‚Äôt suffer this. You should be the food taster, it seems to me. What else is a cripple good for?‚Äù Jaime smiled. ‚ÄúI agree. I am as unfit to guard the king as you are. So draw that sword you‚Äôre fondling, and we shall see how your two hands fare against my one. At the end one of us will be dead, and the Kingsguard will be improved.‚Äù He rose. ‚ÄúOr, if you prefer, you may return to your duties.‚Äù ‚ÄúBah!‚Äù Ser Boros hawked up a glob of green phlegm, spat it at Jaime‚Äôs feet, and walked out, his sword still in its sheath. The man is craven, and a good thing. Though fat, aging, and never more than ordinary, Ser Boros could still have hacked him into bloody pieces. But Boros does not know that, and neither must the rest. They feared the man I was; the man I am they‚Äôd pity.

> Jaime settled on a stool while Tully had his bath. The filth came off in grey clouds. ‚ÄúOnce you‚Äôve eaten, my men will escort you to Riverrun. What happens after that is up to you.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhat do you mean?‚Äù ‚ÄúYour uncle is an old man. Valiant, yes, but the best part of his life is done. He has no bride to grieve for him, no children to defend. A good death is all the Blackfish can hope for¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut you have years remaining, Edmure. And you are the rightful lord of House Tully, not him. Your uncle serves at your pleasure. The fate of Riverrun is in your hands.‚Äù Edmure stared. ‚ÄúThe fate of Riverrun¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúYield the castle and no one dies. Your smallfolk may go in peace or stay to serve Lord Emmon. Ser Brynden will be allowed to take the black, along with as many of the garrison as choose to join him. You as well, if the Wall appeals to you. Or you may go to Casterly Rock as my captive and enjoy all the comforts and courtesy that befits a hostage of your rank. I‚Äôll send your wife to join you, if you like. If her child is a boy, he will serve House Lannister as a page and a squire, and when he earns his knighthood we‚Äôll bestow some lands upon him. Should Roslin give you a daughter, I‚Äôll see her well dowered when she‚Äôs old enough to wed. You yourself may even be granted parole, once the war is done. All you need do is yield the castle.‚Äù Edmure raised his hands from the tub and watched the water run between his fingers. ‚ÄúAnd if I will not yield?‚Äù Must you make me say the words? Pia was standing by the flap of the tent with her arms full of clothes. His squires were listening as well, and the singer. Let them hear, Jaime thought. Let the world hear. It makes no matter. He forced himself to smile, ‚ÄúYou‚Äôve seen our numbers, Edmure. You‚Äôve seen the ladders, the towers, the trebuchets, the rams. If I speak the command, my coz will bridge your moat and break your gate. Hundreds will die, most of them your own. Your former bannermen will make up the first wave of attackers, so you‚Äôll start your day by killing the fathers and brothers of men who died for you at the Twins. The second wave will be Freys, I have no lack of those. My westermen will follow when your archers are short of arrows and your knights so weary they can hardly lift their blades. When the castle falls, all those inside will be put to the sword. Your herds will be butchered, your godswood will be felled, your keeps and towers will burn. I‚Äôll pull your walls down, and divert the Tumblestone over the ruins. By the time I‚Äôm done no man will ever know that a castle once stood here.‚Äù Jaime got to his feet. ‚ÄúYour wife may whelp before that. You‚Äôll want your child, I expect. I‚Äôll send him to you when he‚Äôs born. With a trebuchet.‚Äù Silence followed his speech. Edmure sat in his bath. Pia clutched the clothing to her breasts. The singer tightened a string on his harp. Little Lew hollowed out a loaf of stale bread to make a trencher, pretending that he had not heard. With a trebuchet, Jaime thought. If his aunt had been there, would she still say Tyrion was Tywin‚Äôs son?

{:#Quentyn-Martell-Quotes}
**Quentyn Martell**

> Adventure stank. She boasted sixty oars, a single sail, and a long lean hull that promised speed. Small, but she might serve, Quentyn thought when he saw her, but that was before he went aboard and got a good whiff of her. Pigs, was his first thought, but after a second sniff he changed his mind. Pigs had a cleaner smell. This stink was piss and rotting meat and nightsoil, this was the reek of corpse flesh and weeping sores and wounds gone bad, so strong that it overwhelmed the salt air and fish smell of the harbor.

> Tall and fair, with blue-green eyes, sandy hair streaked by the sun, and a lean and comely body, Gerris Drinkwater had a swagger to him, a confidence bordering on arrogance. He never seemed ill at ease, and even when he did not speak the language, he had ways of making himself understood. Quentyn cut a poor figure by comparison‚Äîshort-legged and stocky, thickly built, with hair the brown of new-turned earth. His forehead was too high, his jaw too square, his nose too broad. A good honest face, a girl had called it once, but you should smile more. Smiles had never come easily for Quentyn Martell, any more than they did for his lord father.

> ‚ÄúNew Ghis is an island, and a much smaller port than this. We would be closer, yes, but we could find ourselves stranded. And New Ghis has allied with the Yunkai‚Äôi.‚Äù That news had not come as a surprise to Quentyn. New Ghis and Yunkai were both Ghiscari cities. ‚ÄúIf Volantis should ally with them as well‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúWe need to find a ship from Westeros,‚Äù suggested Gerris, ‚Äúsome trader out of Lannisport or Oldtown.‚Äù ‚ÄúFew come this far, and those who do fill their holds with silk and spice from the Jade Sea, then bend their oars for home.‚Äù ‚ÄúPerhaps a Braavosi ship? One hears of purple sails as far away as Asshai and the islands of the Jade Sea.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe Braavosi are descended from escaped slaves. They do not trade in Slaver‚Äôs Bay.‚Äù ‚ÄúDo we have enough gold to buy a ship?‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd who will sail her? You? Me?‚Äù Dornishmen had never been seafarers, not since Nymeria burned her ten thousand ships. ‚ÄúThe seas around Valyria are perilous, and thick with corsairs.‚Äù ‚ÄúI have had enough of corsairs. Let‚Äôs not buy a ship.‚Äù This is still just a game to him, Quentyn realized, no different than the time he led six of us up into the mountains to find the old lair of the Vulture King. It was not in Gerris Drinkwater‚Äôs nature to imagine they might fail, let alone that they might die. Even the deaths of three friends had not served to chasten him, it would seem. He leaves that to me. He knows my nature is as cautious as his is bold. ‚ÄúPerhaps the big man is right,‚Äù Ser Gerris said. ‚ÄúPiss on the sea, we can finish the journey overland.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou know why he says that,‚Äù Quentyn said. ‚ÄúHe‚Äôd rather die than set foot on another ship.‚Äù The big man had been greensick every day of their voyage. In Lys, it had taken him four days to recover his strength. They‚Äôd had to take rooms in an inn so Maester Kedry could tuck him into a feather bed and feed him broths and potions until some pink returned to his cheeks. It was possible to go overland to Meereen, that much was true. The old Valyrian roads would take them there. Dragon roads, men called the great stone roadways of the Freehold, but the one that ran eastward from Volantis to Meereen had earned a more sinister name: the demon road. ‚ÄúThe demon road is dangerous, and too slow,‚Äù Quentyn said. ‚ÄúTywin Lannister will send his own men after the queen once word of her reaches King‚Äôs Landing.‚Äù His father had been certain of that. ‚ÄúHis will come with knives. If they reach her first‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúLet‚Äôs hope her dragons will sniff them out and eat them,‚Äù said Gerris. ‚ÄúWell, if we cannot find a ship, and you will not let us ride, we had as well book passage back to Dorne.‚Äù Crawl back to Sunspear defeated, with my tail between my legs? His father‚Äôs disappointment would be more than Quentyn could bear, and the scorn of the Sand Snakes would be withering. Doran Martell had put the fate of Dorne into his hands, he could not fail him, not whilst life remained.

> When first he‚Äôd come to Yronwood, he had been smitten with Ynys, the eldest of Lord Yronwood‚Äôs daughters. Though he never said a word about his feelings, he nursed his dreams for years¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýuntil the day she was dispatched to wed Ser Ryon Allyrion, the heir to Godsgrace. The last time he had seen her, she‚Äôd had one boy at her breast and another clinging to her skirts. After Ynys had come the Drinkwater twins, a pair of tawny young maidens who loved hawking, hunting, climbing rocks, and making Quentyn blush. One of them had given him his first kiss, though he never knew which one. As daughters of a landed knight, the twins were too lowborn to marry, but Cletus did not think that was any reason to stop kissing them. ‚ÄúAfter you‚Äôre wed you can take one of them for a paramour. Or both, why not?‚Äù But Quentyn thought of several reasons why not, so he had done his best to avoid the twins thereafter, and there had been no second kiss. More recently, the youngest of Lord Yronwood‚Äôs daughters had taken to following him about the castle. Gwyneth was but twelve, a small, scrawny girl whose dark eyes and brown hair set her apart in that house of blue-eyed blondes. She was clever, though, as quick with words as with her hands, and fond of telling Quentyn that he had to wait for her to flower, so she could marry him. That was before Prince Doran had summoned him to the Water Gardens. And now the most beautiful woman in the world was waiting in Meereen, and he meant to do his duty and claim her for his bride. She will not refuse me. She will honor the agreement. Daenerys Targaryen would need Dorne to win the Seven Kingdoms, and that meant that she would need him. It does not mean that she will love me, though. She may not even like me.

> Wed her or fight her; either way, I will face her soon. The more Quentyn heard of Daenerys Targaryen, the more he feared that meeting. The Yunkai‚Äôi claimed that she fed her dragons on human flesh and bathed in the blood of virgins to keep her skin smooth and supple. Beans laughed at that but relished the tales of the silver queen‚Äôs promiscuity. ‚ÄúOne of her captains comes of a line where the men have foot-long members,‚Äù he told them, ‚Äúbut even he‚Äôs not big enough for her. She rode with the Dothraki and grew accustomed to being fucked by stallions, so now no man can fill her.‚Äù And Books, the clever Volantene swordsman who always seemed to have his nose poked in some crumbly scroll, thought the dragon queen both murderous and mad. ‚ÄúHer khal killed her brother to make her queen. Then she killed her khal to make herself khaleesi. She practices blood sacrifice, lies as easily as she breathes, turns against her own on a whim. She‚Äôs broken truces, tortured envoys¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýher father was mad too. It runs in the blood.‚Äù It runs in the blood. King Aerys II had been mad, all of Westeros knew that. He had exiled two of his Hands and burned a third. If Daenerys is as murderous as her father, must I still marry her? Prince Doran had never spoken of that possibility.

> The parchment was written in the Common Tongue. The queen unrolled it slowly, studying the seals and signatures. When she saw the name Ser Willem Darry, her heart beat a little faster. She read it over once, and then again. ‚ÄúMay we know what it says, Your Grace?‚Äù asked Ser Barristan. ‚ÄúIt is a secret pact,‚Äù Dany said, ‚Äúmade in Braavos when I was just a little girl. Ser Willem Darry signed for us, the man who spirited my brother and myself away from Dragonstone before the Usurper‚Äôs men could take us. Prince Oberyn Martell signed for Dorne, with the Sealord of Braavos as witness.‚Äù She handed the parchment to Ser Barristan, so he might read it for himself. ‚ÄúThe alliance is to be sealed by a marriage, it says. In return for Dorne‚Äôs help overthrowing the Usurper, my brother Viserys is to take Prince Doran‚Äôs daughter Arianne for his queen.‚Äù The old knight read the pact slowly. ‚ÄúIf Robert had known of this, he would have smashed Sunspear as he once smashed Pyke, and claimed the heads of Prince Doran and the Red Viper¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand like as not, the head of this Dornish princess too.‚Äù ‚ÄúNo doubt that was why Prince Doran chose to keep the pact a secret,‚Äù suggested Daenerys. ‚ÄúIf my brother Viserys had known that he had a Dornish princess waiting for him, he would have crossed to Sunspear as soon as he was old enough to wed.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd thereby brought Robert‚Äôs warhammer down upon himself, and Dorne as well,‚Äù said Frog. ‚ÄúMy father was content to wait for the day that Prince Viserys found his army.‚Äù ‚ÄúYour father?‚Äù ‚ÄúPrince Doran.‚Äù He sank back onto one knee. ‚ÄúYour Grace, I have the honor to be Quentyn Martell, a prince of Dorne and your most leal subject.‚Äù Dany laughed. The Dornish prince flushed red, whilst her own court and counselors gave her puzzled looks. ‚ÄúRadiance?‚Äù said Skahaz Shavepate, in the Ghiscari tongue. ‚ÄúWhy do you laugh?‚Äù ‚ÄúThey call him frog,‚Äù she said, ‚Äúand we have just learned why. In the Seven Kingdoms there are children‚Äôs tales of frogs who turn into enchanted princes when kissed by their true love.‚Äù Smiling at the Dornish knights, she switched back to the Common Tongue. ‚ÄúTell me, Prince Quentyn, are you enchanted?‚Äù ‚ÄúNo, Your Grace.‚Äù ‚ÄúI feared as much.‚Äù Neither enchanted nor enchanting, alas. A pity he‚Äôs the prince, and not the one with the wide shoulders and the sandy hair.

{:#Jaqen-Hghar}
**Jaqen H'ghar**

> He looked down at her pitilessly. ‚ÄúThree lives were snatched from a god. Three lives must be repaid. The gods are not mocked.‚Äù His voice was silk and steel. ‚ÄúI never mocked.‚Äù She thought for a moment. ‚ÄúThe name¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýcan I name anyone? And you‚Äôll kill him?‚Äù Jaqen H‚Äôghar inclined his head. ‚ÄúA man has said.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnyone?‚Äù she repeated. ‚ÄúA man, a woman, a little baby, or Lord Tywin, or the High Septon, or your father?‚Äù ‚ÄúA man‚Äôs sire is long dead, but did he live, and did you know his name, he would die at your command.‚Äù ‚ÄúSwear it,‚Äù Arya said. ‚ÄúSwear it by the gods.‚Äù ‚ÄúBy all the gods of sea and air, and even him of fire, I swear it.‚Äù He placed a hand in the mouth of the weirwood. ‚ÄúBy the seven new gods and the old gods beyond count, I swear it.‚Äù He has sworn. ‚ÄúEven if I named the king¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúSpeak the name, and death will come. On the morrow, at the turn of the moon, a year from this day, it will come. A man does not fly like a bird, but one foot moves and then another and one day a man is there, and a king dies.‚Äù He knelt beside her, so they were face-to-face. ‚ÄúA girl whispers if she fears to speak aloud. Whisper it now. Is it Joffrey?‚Äù Arya put her lips to his ear. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs Jaqen H‚Äôghar.‚Äù Even in the burning barn, with walls of flame towering all around and him in chains, he had not seemed so distraught as he did now. ‚ÄúA girl¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýshe makes a jest.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou swore. The gods heard you swear.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe gods did hear.‚Äù There was a knife in his hand suddenly, its blade thin as her little finger. Whether it was meant for her or him, Arya could not say. ‚ÄúA girl will weep. A girl will lose her only friend.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou‚Äôre not my friend. A friend would help me.‚Äù She stepped away from him, balanced on the balls of her feet in case he threw his knife. ‚ÄúI‚Äôd never kill a friend.‚Äù Jaqen‚Äôs smile came and went. ‚ÄúA girl might¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýname another name then, if a friend did help?‚Äù ‚ÄúA girl might,‚Äù she said. ‚ÄúIf a friend did help.‚Äù The knife vanished. ‚ÄúCome.‚Äù ‚ÄúNow?‚Äù She had never thought he would act so quickly. ‚ÄúA man hears the whisper of sand in a glass. A man will not sleep until a girl unsays a certain name. Now, evil child.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúDie?‚Äù she said, confused. What did he mean? ‚ÄúBut I unsaid the name. You don‚Äôt need to die now.‚Äù ‚ÄúI do. My time is done.‚Äù Jaqen passed a hand down his face from forehead to chin, and where it went he changed. His cheeks grew fuller, his eyes closer; his nose hooked, a scar appeared on his right cheek where no scar had been before. And when he shook his head, his long straight hair, half red and half white, dissolved away to reveal a cap of tight black curls. Arya‚Äôs mouth hung open. ‚ÄúWho are you?‚Äù she whispered, too astonished to be afraid. ‚ÄúHow did you do that? Was it hard?‚Äù He grinned, revealing a shiny gold tooth. ‚ÄúNo harder than taking a new name, if you know the way.‚Äù ‚ÄúShow me,‚Äù she blurted. ‚ÄúI want to do it too.‚Äù ‚ÄúIf you would learn, you must come with me.‚Äù Arya grew hesitant. ‚ÄúWhere?‚Äù ‚ÄúFar and away, across the narrow sea.‚Äù ‚ÄúI can‚Äôt. I have to go home. To Winterfell.‚Äù ‚ÄúThen we must part,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúfor I have duties too.‚Äù He lifted her hand and pressed a small coin into her palm. ‚ÄúHere.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhat is it?‚Äù ‚ÄúA coin of great value.‚Äù Arya bit it. It was so hard it could only be iron. ‚ÄúIs it worth enough to buy a horse?‚Äù ‚ÄúIt is not meant for the buying of horses.‚Äù ‚ÄúThen what good is it?‚Äù ‚ÄúAs well ask what good is life, what good is death? If the day comes when you would find me again, give that coin to any man from Braavos, and say these words to him‚Äîvalar morghulis.‚Äù ‚ÄúValar morghulis,‚Äù Arya repeated. It wasn‚Äôt hard. Her fingers closed tight over the coin. Across the yard, she could hear men dying. ‚ÄúPlease don‚Äôt go, Jaqen.‚Äù ‚ÄúJaqen is as dead as Arry,‚Äù he said sadly, ‚Äúand I have promises to keep. Valar morghulis, Arya Stark. Say it again.‚Äù ‚ÄúValar morghulis,‚Äù she said again, and the stranger in Jaqen‚Äôs clothes bowed to her and stalked off through the darkness, cloak swirling.

> The purple galley was still there. If the ship had sailed while she was being robbed, that would have been too much to bear. A cask of mead was being rolled up the plank when she arrived. When she tried to follow, a sailor up on deck shouted down at her in a tongue she did not know. ‚ÄúI want to see the captain,‚Äù Arya told him. He only shouted louder. But the commotion drew the attention of a stout grey-haired man in a coat of purple wool, and he spoke the Common Tongue. ‚ÄúI am captain here,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúWhat is your wish? Be quick, child, we have a tide to catch.‚Äù ‚ÄúI want to go north, to the Wall. Here, I can pay.‚Äù She gave him the purse. ‚ÄúThe Night‚Äôs Watch has a castle on the sea.‚Äù ‚ÄúEastwatch.‚Äù The captain spilled out the silver onto his palm and frowned. ‚ÄúIs this all you have?‚Äù It is not enough, Arya knew without being told. She could see it on his face. ‚ÄúI wouldn‚Äôt need a cabin or anything,‚Äù she said. ‚ÄúI could sleep down in the hold, or¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúTake her on as cabin girl,‚Äù said a passing oarsman, a bolt of wool over one shoulder. ‚ÄúShe can sleep with me.‚Äù ‚ÄúMind your tongue,‚Äù the captain snapped. ‚ÄúI could work,‚Äù said Arya. ‚ÄúI could scrub the decks. I scrubbed a castle steps once. Or I could row¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúNo,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúyou couldn‚Äôt.‚Äù He gave her back her coins. ‚ÄúIt would make no difference if you could, child. The north has nothing for us. Ice and war and pirates. We saw a dozen pirate ships making north as we rounded Crackclaw Point, and I have no wish to meet them again. From here we bend our oars for home, and I suggest you do the same.‚Äù I have no home, Arya thought. I have no pack. And now I don‚Äôt even have a horse. The captain was turning away when she said, ‚ÄúWhat ship is this, my lord?‚Äù He paused long enough to give her a weary smile. ‚ÄúThis is the galleas Titan‚Äôs Daughter, of the Free City of Braavos.‚Äù ‚ÄúWait,‚Äù Arya said suddenly. ‚ÄúI have something else.‚Äù She had stuffed it down inside her smallclothes to keep it safe, so she had to dig deep to find it, while the oarsmen laughed and the captain lingered with obvious impatience. ‚ÄúOne more silver will make no difference, child,‚Äù he finally said. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs not silver.‚Äù Her fingers closed on it. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs iron. Here.‚Äù She pressed it into his hand, the small black iron coin that Jaqen H‚Äôghar had given her, so worn the man whose head it bore had no features. It‚Äôs probably worthless, but¬Ý‚Ä¶ The captain turned it over and blinked at it, then looked at her again. ‚ÄúThis¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýhow ‚Ä¶?‚Äù Jaqen said to say the words too. Arya crossed her arms against her chest. ‚ÄúValar morghulis,‚Äù she said, as loud as if she‚Äôd known what it meant. ‚ÄúValar dohaeris,‚Äù he replied, touching his brow with two fingers. ‚ÄúOf course you shall have a cabin.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúShow me your face.‚Äù ‚ÄúAs you wish.‚Äù The alchemist pulled his hood down. He was just a man, and his face was just a face. A young man‚Äôs face, ordinary, with full cheeks and the shadow of a beard. A scar showed faintly on his right cheek. He had a hooked nose, and a mat of dense black hair that curled tightly around his ears. It was not a face Pate recognized. ‚ÄúI do not know you.‚Äù ‚ÄúNor I you.‚Äù ‚ÄúWho are you?‚Äù ‚ÄúA stranger. No one. Truly.‚Äù

{:#Lancel-Lannister}
**Lancel Lannister**

> It seemed to Tyrion that the lad had grown three inches since being knighted. Lancel had thick sandy hair, green Lannister eyes, and a line of soft blond fuzz on his upper lip. At sixteen, he was cursed with all the certainty of youth, unleavened by any trace of humor or self-doubt, and wed to the arrogance that came so naturally to those born blond and strong and handsome.

> Lancel was sixteen, and not known for his patience. Let him wait, and grow more anxious in the waiting. When his bowels were empty, Tyrion slipped on a bedrobe and roughed his thin flaxen hair with his fingers, all the more to look as if he had wakened from sleep. Lancel was pacing before the ashes of the hearth, garbed in slashed red velvet with black silk under-sleeves, a jeweled dagger and a gilded scabbard hanging from his swordbelt. ‚ÄúCousin,‚Äù Tyrion greeted him. ‚ÄúYour visits are too few. To what do I owe this undeserved pleasure?‚Äù ‚ÄúHer Grace the Queen Regent has sent me to command you to release Grand Maester Pycelle.‚Äù Ser Lancel showed Tyrion a crimson ribbon, bearing Cersei‚Äôs lion seal impressed in golden wax. ‚ÄúHere is her warrant.‚Äù ‚ÄúSo it is.‚Äù Tyrion waved it away. ‚ÄúI hope my sister is not overtaxing her strength, so soon after her illness. It would be a great pity if she were to suffer a relapse.‚Äù ‚ÄúHer Grace is quite recovered,‚Äù Ser Lancel said curtly. ‚ÄúMusic to my ears.‚Äù Though not a tune I‚Äôm fond of. I should have given her a larger dose. Tyrion had hoped for a few more days without Cersei‚Äôs interference, but he was not too terribly surprised by her return to health. She was Jaime‚Äôs twin, after all. He made himself smile pleasantly. ‚ÄúPod, build us a fire, the air is too chilly for my taste. Will you take a cup with me, Lancel? I find that mulled wine helps me sleep.‚Äù ‚ÄúI need no help sleeping,‚Äù Ser Lancel said. ‚ÄúI am come at Her Grace‚Äôs behest, not to drink with you, Imp.‚Äù Knighthood had made the boy bolder, Tyrion reflected‚Äîthat, and the sorry part he had played in murdering King Robert. ‚ÄúWine does have its dangers.‚Äù He smiled as he poured. ‚ÄúAs to Grand Maester Pycelle¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýif my sweet sister is so concerned for him, I would have thought she‚Äôd come herself. Instead she sends you. What am I to make of that?‚Äù ‚ÄúMake of it what you will, so long as you release your prisoner. The Grand Maester is a staunch friend to the Queen Regent, and under her personal protection.‚Äù A hint of a sneer played about the lad‚Äôs lips; he was enjoying this. He takes his lessons from Cersei. ‚ÄúHer Grace will never consent to this outrage. She reminds you that she is Joffrey‚Äôs regent.‚Äù ‚ÄúAs I am Joffrey‚Äôs Hand.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe Hand serves,‚Äù the young knight informed him airily. ‚ÄúThe regent rules until the king is of age.‚Äù ‚ÄúPerhaps you ought write that down so I‚Äôll remember it better.‚Äù The fire was crackling merrily. ‚ÄúYou may leave us, Pod,‚Äù Tyrion told his squire. Only when the boy was gone did he turn back to Lancel. ‚ÄúThere is more?‚Äù ‚ÄúYes. Her Grace bids me inform you that Ser Jacelyn Bywater defied a command issued in the king‚Äôs own name.‚Äù Which means that Cersei has already ordered Bywater to release Pycelle, and been rebuffed. ‚ÄúI see.‚Äù ‚ÄúShe insists that the man be removed from his office and placed under arrest for treason. I warn you‚Äî‚Äù He set aside his wine cup. ‚ÄúI‚Äôll hear no warnings from you, boy.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúSer,‚Äù Lancel said stiffly. He touched his sword, perhaps to remind Tyrion that he wore one. ‚ÄúHave a care how you speak to me, Imp.‚Äù Doubtless he meant to sound threatening, but that absurd wisp of a mustache ruined the effect. ‚ÄúOh, unhand your sword. One cry from me and Shagga will burst in and kill you. With an axe, not a wineskin.‚Äù Lancel reddened; was he such a fool as to believe his part in Robert‚Äôs death had gone unnoted? ‚ÄúI am a knight‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúSo I‚Äôve noted. Tell me‚Äîdid Cersei have you knighted before or after she took you into her bed?‚Äù The flicker in Lancel‚Äôs green eyes was all the admission Tyrion needed. So Varys told it true. Well, no one can ever claim that my sister does not love her family. ‚ÄúWhat, nothing to say? No more warnings for me, ser?‚Äù ‚ÄúYou will withdraw these filthy accusations or‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúPlease. Have you given any thought to what Joffrey will do when I tell him you murdered his father to bed his mother?‚Äù ‚ÄúIt was not like that!‚Äù Lancel protested, horrified. ‚ÄúNo? What was it like, pray?‚Äù ‚ÄúThe queen gave me the strongwine! Your own father Lord Tywin, when I was named the king‚Äôs squire, he told me to obey her in everything.‚Äù ‚ÄúDid he tell you to fuck her too?‚Äù Look at him. Not quite so tall, his features not so fine, and his hair is sand instead of spun gold, yet still¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýeven a poor copy of Jaime is sweeter than an empty bed, I suppose. ‚ÄúNo, I thought not.‚Äù ‚ÄúI never meant¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝI only did as I was bid, I¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚Äú‚Ä¶¬Ýhated every instant of it, is that what you would have me believe? A high place at court, knighthood, my sister‚Äôs legs opening for you at night, oh, yes, it must have been terrible for you.‚Äù Tyrion pushed himself to his feet. ‚ÄúWait here. His Grace will want to hear this.‚Äù The defiance went from Lancel all at once. The young knight fell to his knees a frightened boy. ‚ÄúMercy, my lord, I beg you.‚Äù ‚ÄúSave it for Joffrey. He likes a good beg.‚Äù ‚ÄúMy lord, it was your sister‚Äôs bidding, the queen, as you said, but His Grace¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýhe‚Äôd never understand¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúWould you have me keep the truth from the king?‚Äù ‚ÄúFor my father‚Äôs sake! I‚Äôll leave the city, it will be as if it never happened! I swear, I will end it¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù It was hard not to laugh. ‚ÄúI think not.‚Äù Now the lad looked lost. ‚ÄúMy lord?‚Äù ‚ÄúYou heard me. My father told you to obey my sister? Very well, obey her. Stay close to her side, keep her trust, pleasure her as often as she requires it. No one need ever know¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýso long as you keep faith with me. I want to know what Cersei is doing. Where she goes, who she sees, what they talk of, what plans she is hatching. All. And you will be the one to tell me, won‚Äôt you?‚Äù ‚ÄúYes, my lord.‚Äù Lancel spoke without a moment‚Äôs hesitation. Tyrion liked that. ‚ÄúI will. I swear it. As you command.‚Äù ‚ÄúRise.‚Äù Tyrion filled the second cup and pressed it on him. ‚ÄúDrink to our understanding. I promise, there are no boars in the castle that I know of.‚Äù Lancel lifted the cup and drank, albeit stiffly. ‚ÄúSmile, cousin. My sister is a beautiful woman, and it‚Äôs all for the good of the realm. You could do well out of this. Knighthood is nothing. If you‚Äôre clever, you‚Äôll have a lordship from me before you‚Äôre done.‚Äù Tyrion swirled the wine in his cup. ‚ÄúWe want Cersei to have every faith in you. Go back and tell her I beg her forgiveness. Tell her that you frightened me, that I want no conflict between us, that henceforth I shall do nothing without her consent.‚Äù ‚ÄúBut ‚Ä¶ her demands ‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúOh, I‚Äôll give her Pycelle.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou will?‚Äù Lancel seemed astonished. Tyrion smiled. ‚ÄúI‚Äôll release him on the morrow. I could swear that I hadn‚Äôt harmed a hair on his head, but it wouldn‚Äôt be strictly true. In any case, he‚Äôs well enough, though I won‚Äôt vouch for his vigor. The black cells are not a healthy place for a man his age. Cersei can keep him as a pet or send him to the Wall, I don‚Äôt care which, but I won‚Äôt have him on the council.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd Ser Jacelyn?‚Äù ‚ÄúTell my sister you believe you can win him away from me, given time. That ought to content her for a while.‚Äù ‚ÄúAs you say.‚Äù Lancel finished his wine. ‚ÄúOne last thing. With King Robert dead, it would be most embarrassing should his grieving widow suddenly grow great with child.‚Äù ‚ÄúMy lord, I¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýwe¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthe queen has commanded me not to¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù His ears had turned Lannister crimson. ‚ÄúI spill my seed on her belly, my lord.‚Äù ‚ÄúA lovely belly, I have no doubt. Moisten it as often as you wish¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut see that your dew falls nowhere else. I want no more nephews, is that clear?‚Äù Ser Lancel made a stiff bow and took his leave.

> Tyrion allowed himself a moment to feel sorry for the boy. Another fool, and a weakling as well; but he does not deserve what Cersei and I are doing to him. It was a kindness that his uncle Kevan had two other sons; this one was unlikely to live out the year. Cersei would have him killed out of hand if she learned he was betraying her, and if by some grace of the gods she did not, Lancel would never survive the day Jaime Lannister returned to King‚Äôs Landing. The only question would be whether Jaime cut him down in a jealous rage, or Cersei murdered him first to keep Jaime from finding out. Tyrion‚Äôs silver was on Cersei.

> Lancel looked even thinner than he had at King‚Äôs Landing. He was barefoot, and dressed in a plain, roughspun tunic of undyed wool that made him look more like a beggar than a lord. The crown of his head had been shaved smooth, but his beard had grown out a little. To call it peach fuzz would have given insult to the peach. It went queerly with the white hair around his ears. ‚ÄúCousin,‚Äù said Jaime when they were alone within the sept, ‚Äúhave you lost your bloody wits?‚Äù ‚ÄúI prefer to say I‚Äôve found my faith.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhere is your father?‚Äù ‚ÄúGone. We quarreled.‚Äù Lancel knelt before the altar of his other Father. ‚ÄúWill you pray with me, Jaime?‚Äù ‚ÄúIf I pray nicely, will the Father give me a new hand?‚Äù ‚ÄúNo. But the Warrior will give you courage, the Smith will lend you strength, and the Crone will give you wisdom.‚Äù ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs a hand I need.‚Äù The seven gods loomed above carved altars, the dark wood gleaming in the candlelight. A faint smell of incense hung in the air. ‚ÄúYou sleep down here?‚Äù ‚ÄúEach night I make my bed beneath a different altar, and the Seven send me visions.‚Äù Baelor the Blessed once had visions too. Especially when he was fasting. ‚ÄúHow long has it been since you‚Äôve eaten?‚Äù ‚ÄúMy faith is all the nourishment I need.‚Äù ‚ÄúFaith is like porridge. Better with milk and honey.‚Äù ‚ÄúI dreamed that you would come. In the dream you knew what I had done. How I‚Äôd sinned. You killed me for it.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou‚Äôre more like to kill yourself with all this fasting. Didn‚Äôt Baelor the Blessed fast himself onto a bier?‚Äù ‚ÄúOur lives are candle flames, says The Seven-Pointed Star. Any errant puff of wind can snuff us out. Death is never far in this world, and seven hells await sinners who do not repent their sins. Pray with me, Jaime.‚Äù ‚ÄúIf I do, will you eat a bowl of porridge?‚Äù When his coz did not answer, Jaime sighed. ‚ÄúYou should be sleeping with your wife, not with the Maid. You need a son with Darry blood if you want to keep this castle.‚Äù ‚ÄúA pile of cold stones. I never asked for it. I never wanted it. I only wanted¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù Lancel shuddered. ‚ÄúSeven save me, but I wanted to be you.‚Äù Jaime had to laugh. ‚ÄúBetter me than Blessed Baelor. Darry needs a lion, coz. So does your little Frey. She gets moist between the legs every time someone mentions Hardstone. If she hasn‚Äôt bedded him yet, she will soon.‚Äù ‚ÄúIf she loves him, I wish them joy of one another.‚Äù ‚ÄúA lion shouldn‚Äôt have horns. You took the girl to wife.‚Äù ‚ÄúI said some words and gave her a red cloak, but only to please Father. Marriage requires consummation. King Baelor was made to wed his sister Daena, but they never lived as man and wife, and he put her aside as soon as he was crowned.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe realm would have been better served if he had closed his eyes and fucked her. I know enough history to know that. In any case, you‚Äôre not like to be taken for Baelor the Blessed.‚Äù ‚ÄúNo,‚Äù Lancel allowed. ‚ÄúHe was a rare spirit, pure and brave and innocent, untouched by all the evils of the world. I am a sinner, with much and more to atone for.‚Äù Jaime put his hand on his cousin‚Äôs shoulder. ‚ÄúWhat do you know of sin, coz? I killed my king.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe brave man slays with a sword, the craven with a wineskin. We are both kingslayers, ser‚Äù ‚ÄúRobert was no true king. Some might even say that a stag is a lion‚Äôs natural prey.‚Äù Jaime could feel the bones beneath his cousin‚Äôs skin¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand something else as well. Lancel was wearing a hair shirt underneath his tunic. ‚ÄúWhat else did you do, to require so much atonement? Tell me.‚Äù

> His cousin bowed his head, tears running down his cheeks. Those tears were all the answer Jaime needed. ‚ÄúYou killed the king,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúthen you fucked the queen.‚Äù ‚ÄúI never ‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚Äú‚Ä¶¬Ýlay with my sweet sister?‚Äù Say it. Say it! ‚ÄúNever spilled my seed in ‚Ä¶ in her ‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚Äú‚Ä¶ cunt?‚Äù suggested Jaime. ‚Äú‚Ä¶¬Ýwomb,‚Äù Lancel finished. ‚ÄúIt is not treason unless you finish inside. I gave her comfort, after the king died. You were a captive, your father was in the field, and your brother¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýshe was afraid of him, and with good reason. He made me betray her.‚Äù ‚ÄúDid he?‚Äù Lancel and Ser Osmund and how many more? Was the part about Moon Boy just a gibe? ‚ÄúDid you force her?‚Äù ‚ÄúNo! I loved her. I wanted to protect her.‚Äù You wanted to be me. His phantom fingers itched. The day his sister had come to White Sword Tower to beg him to renounce his vows, she had laughed after he refused her and boasted of having lied to him a thousand times. Jaime had taken that for a clumsy attempt to hurt him as he‚Äôd hurt her. It may have been the only true thing that she ever said to me. ‚ÄúDo not think ill of the queen,‚Äù Lancel pleaded. ‚ÄúAll flesh is weak, Jaime. No harm came of our sin. No¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýno bastard.‚Äù ‚ÄúNo. Bastards are seldom made upon the belly.‚Äù He wondered what his cousin would say if he were to confess his own sins, the three treasons Cersei had named Joffrey, Tommen, and Myrcella. ‚ÄúI was angry with Her Grace after the battle, but the High Septon said I must forgive her.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou confessed your sins to His High Holiness, did you?‚Äù ‚ÄúHe prayed for me when I was wounded. He was a good man.‚Äù He‚Äôs a dead man. They rang the bells for him. He wondered if his cousin had any notion what fruit his words had borne. ‚ÄúLancel, you‚Äôre a bloody fool.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou are not wrong,‚Äù said Lancel, ‚Äúbut my folly is behind me, ser. I have asked the Father Above to show me the way, and he has. I am renouncing this lordship and this wife. Hardstone is welcome to the both of them, if he likes. On the morrow I will return to King‚Äôs Landing and swear my sword to the new High Septon and the Seven. I mean to take vows and join the Warrior‚Äôs Sons.‚Äù The boy was not making sense. ‚ÄúThe Warrior‚Äôs Sons were proscribed three hundred years ago.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe new High Septon has revived them. He‚Äôs sent out a call for worthy knights to pledge their lives and swords to the service of the Seven. The Poor Fellows are to be restored as well.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhy would the Iron Throne allow that?‚Äù One of the early Targaryen kings had fought for years to suppress the two military orders, Jaime recalled, though he did not remember which. Maegor, perhaps, or the first Jaehaerys. Tyrion would have known. ‚ÄúHis High Holiness writes that King Tommen has given his consent. I will show you the letter, if you like.‚Äù ‚ÄúEven if this is true¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýyou are a lion of the Rock, a lord. You have a wife, a castle, lands to defend, people to protect. If the gods are good, you will have sons of your blood to follow you. Why would you throw all that away for¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýfor some vow?‚Äù ‚ÄúWhy did you?‚Äù asked Lancel softly. For honor, Jaime might have said. For glory. That would have been a lie, though. Honor and glory had played their parts, but most of it had been for Cersei. A laugh escaped his lips. ‚ÄúIs it the High Septon you‚Äôre running to, or my sweet sister? Pray on that one, coz. Pray hard.‚Äù ‚ÄúWill you pray with me, Jaime?‚Äù He glanced about the sept, at the gods. The Mother, full of mercy. The Father, stern in judgment. The Warrior, one hand upon his sword. The Stranger in the shadows, his half-human face concealed beneath a hooded mantle. I thought that I was the Warrior and Cersei was the Maid, but all the time she was the Stranger, hiding her true face from my gaze. ‚ÄúPray for me, if you like,‚Äù he told his cousin. ‚ÄúI‚Äôve forgotten all the words.‚Äù

{:#Quhuru-Mo}
**Quhuru Mo**

> ‚ÄúTorn by a monstrous boar whilst hunting in his kingswood, or so I heard in Oldtown. Others say his queen betrayed him, or his brother, or Lord Stark who was his Hand. Yet all the tales agree in this: King Robert is dead and in his grave.‚Äù Dany had never looked upon the Usurper‚Äôs face, yet seldom a day had passed when she had not thought of him. His great shadow had lain across her since the hour of her birth, when she came forth amidst blood and storm into a world where she no longer had a place. And now this ebony stranger had lifted that shadow. ‚ÄúThe boy sits the Iron Throne now,‚Äù Ser Jorah said. ‚ÄúKing Joffrey reigns,‚Äù Quhuru Mo agreed, ‚Äúbut the Lannisters rule. Robert‚Äôs brothers have fled King‚Äôs Landing. The talk is, they mean to claim the crown. And the Hand has fallen, Lord Stark who was King Robert‚Äôs friend. He has been seized for treason.‚Äù ‚ÄúNed Stark a traitor?‚Äù Ser Jorah snorted. ‚ÄúNot bloody likely. The Long Summer will come again before that one would besmirch his precious honor.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhat honor could he have?‚Äù Dany said. ‚ÄúHe was a traitor to his true king, as were these Lannisters.‚Äù It pleased her to hear that the Usurper‚Äôs dogs were fighting amongst themselves, though she was unsurprised. The same thing happened when her Drogo died, and his great khalasar tore itself to pieces. ‚ÄúMy brother is dead as well, Viserys who was the true king,‚Äù she told the Summer Islander. ‚ÄúKhal Drogo my lord husband killed him with a crown of molten gold.‚Äù Would her brother have been any wiser, had he known that the vengeance he had prayed for was so close at hand? ‚ÄúThen I grieve for you, Dragonmother, and for bleeding Westeros, bereft of its rightful king.‚Äù Beneath Dany‚Äôs gentle fingers, green Rhaegal stared at the stranger with eyes of molten gold. When his mouth opened, his teeth gleamed like black needles. ‚ÄúWhen does your ship return to Westeros, Captain?‚Äù ‚ÄúNot for a year or more, I fear. From here the Cinnamon Wind sails east, to make the trader‚Äôs circle round the Jade Sea.‚Äù ‚ÄúI see,‚Äù said Dany, disappointed. ‚ÄúI wish you fair winds and good trading, then. You have brought me a precious gift.‚Äù ‚ÄúI have been amply repaid, great queen.‚Äù She puzzled at that. ‚ÄúHow so?‚Äù His eyes gleamed. ‚ÄúI have seen dragons.‚Äù Dany laughed. ‚ÄúAnd will see more of them one day, I hope. Come to me in King‚Äôs Landing when I am on my father‚Äôs throne, and you shall have a great reward.‚Äù

{:#Xaro-Xhoan-Daxos}
**Xaro Xhoan Daxos**

> Xaro Xhoan Daxos had offered Dany the hospitality of his home while she was in the city. She had expected something grand. She had not expected a palace larger than many a market town. It makes Magister Illyrio‚Äôs manse in Pentos look like a swineherd‚Äôs hovel, she thought. Xaro swore that his home could comfortably house all of her people and their horses besides; indeed, it swallowed them. An entire wing was given over to her. She would have her own gardens, a marble bathing pool, a scrying tower and warlock‚Äôs maze. Slaves would tend her every need. In her private chambers, the floors were green marble, the walls draped with colorful silk hangings that shimmered with every breath of air. ‚ÄúYou are too generous,‚Äù she told Xaro Xhoan Daxos. ‚ÄúFor the Mother of Dragons, no gift is too great.‚Äù Xaro was a languid, elegant man with a bald head and a great beak of a nose crusted with rubies, opals, and flakes of jade.

> ‚ÄúMarry me, bright light, and sail the ship of my heart. I cannot sleep at night for thinking of your beauty.‚Äù Dany smiled. Xaro‚Äôs flowery protestations of passion amused her, but his manner was at odds with his words. While Ser Jorah had scarcely been able to keep his eyes from her bare breast when he‚Äôd helped her into the palanquin, Xaro hardly deigned to notice it, even in these close confines. And she had seen the beautiful boys who surrounded the merchant prince, flitting through his palace halls in wisps of silk. ‚ÄúYou speak sweetly, Xaro, but under your words I hear another no.‚Äù ‚ÄúThis Iron Throne you speak of sounds monstrous cold and hard. I cannot bear the thought of jagged barbs cutting your sweet skin.‚Äù The jewels in Xaro‚Äôs nose gave him the aspect of some strange glittery bird. His long, elegant fingers waved dismissal. ‚ÄúLet this be your kingdom, most exquisite of queens, and let me be your king. I will give you a throne of gold, if you like. When Qarth begins to pall, we can journey round Yi Ti and search for the dreaming city of the poets, to sip the wine of wisdom from a dead man‚Äôs skull.‚Äù ‚ÄúI mean to sail to Westeros, and drink the wine of vengeance from the skull of the Usurper.‚Äù She scratched Rhaegal under one eye, and his jade-green wings unfolded for a moment, stirring the still air in the palanquin. A single perfect tear ran down the cheek of Xaro Xhoan Daxos. ‚ÄúWill nothing turn you from this madness?‚Äù ‚ÄúNothing,‚Äù she said, wishing she was as certain as she sounded. ‚ÄúIf each of the Thirteen would lend me ten ships‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúYou would have one hundred thirty ships, and no crew to sail them. The justice of your cause means naught to the common men of Qarth. Why should my sailors care who sits upon the throne of some kingdom at the edge of the world?‚Äù

> The dancers shimmered, their sleek shaved bodies covered with a fine sheen of oil. Blazing torches whirled from hand to hand to the beat of drums and the trilling of a flute. Whenever two torches crossed in the air, a naked girl leapt between them, spinning. The torchlight shone off oiled limbs and breasts and buttocks. The three men were erect. The sight of their arousal was arousing, though Daenerys Targaryen found it comical as well. The men were all of a height, with long legs and flat bellies, every muscle as sharply etched as if it had been chiseled out of stone. Even their faces looked the same, somehow ‚Ä¶ which was passing strange, since one had skin as dark as ebony, while the second was as pale as milk, and the third gleamed like burnished copper. Are they meant to inflame me? Dany stirred amongst her silken cushions. Against the pillars her Unsullied stood like statues in their spiked caps, their smooth faces expressionless. Not so the whole men. Reznak mo Reznak‚Äôs mouth was open, and his lips glistened wetly as he watched. Hizdahr zo Loraq was saying something to the man beside him, yet all the time his eyes were on the dancing girls. The Shavepate‚Äôs ugly, oily face was as stern as ever, but he missed nothing. It was harder to know what her honored guest was dreaming. The pale, lean, hawk-faced man who shared her high table was resplendent in robes of maroon silk and cloth-of-gold, his bald head shining in the torchlight as he devoured a fig with small, precise, elegant bites. Opals winked along the nose of Xaro Xhoan Daxos as his head turned to follow the dancers. In his honor Daenerys had donned a Qartheen gown, a sheer confection of violet samite cut so as to leave her left breast bare. Her silver-gold hair brushed lightly over her shoulder, falling almost to her nipple. Half the men in the hall had stolen glances at her, but not Xaro. It was the same in Qarth. She could not sway the merchant prince that way. Sway him I must, however. He had come from Qarth upon the galleas Silken Cloud with thirteen galleys sailing attendance, his fleet an answered prayer. Meereen‚Äôs trade had dwindled away to nothing since she had ended slavery, but Xaro had the power to restore it. As the drums reached a crescendo, three of the girls leapt above the flames, spinning in the air. The male dancers caught them about the waists and slid them down onto their members. Dany watched as the women arched their backs and coiled their legs around their partners while the flutes wept and the men thrust in time to the music. She had seen the act of love before; the Dothraki mated as openly as their mares and stallions. This was the first time she had seen lust put to music, though.

> ‚ÄúI had to come,‚Äù said Xaro in a languid tone. ‚ÄúEven far away in Qarth, fearful tales had reached my ears. I wept to hear them. It is said that your enemies have promised wealth and glory and a hundred virgin slave girls to any man who slays you.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe Sons of the Harpy.‚Äù How does he know that? ‚ÄúThey scrawl on walls by night and cut the throats of honest freedmen as they sleep. When the sun comes up they hide like roaches. They fear my Brazen Beasts.‚Äù Skahaz mo Kandaq had given her the new watch she had asked for, made up in equal numbers of freedmen and shavepate Meereenese. They walked the streets both day and night, in dark hoods and brazen masks. The Sons of the Harpy had promised grisly death to any traitor who dared serve the dragon queen, and to their kith and kin as well, so the Shavepate‚Äôs men went about as jackals, owls, and other beasts, keeping their true faces hidden. ‚ÄúI might have cause to fear the Sons if they saw me wandering alone through the streets, but only if it was night and I was naked and unarmed. They are craven creatures.‚Äù ‚ÄúA craven‚Äôs knife can slay a queen as easily as a hero‚Äôs. I would sleep more soundly if I knew my heart‚Äôs delight had kept her fierce horselords close around her. In Qarth, you had three bloodriders who never left your side. Wherever have they gone?‚Äù ‚ÄúAggo, Jhoqo, and Rakharo still serve me.‚Äù He is playing games with me. Dany could play as well. ‚ÄúI am only a young girl and know little of such things, but older, wiser men tell me that to hold Meereen I must control its hinterlands, all the land west of Lhazar as far south as the Yunkish hills.‚Äù ‚ÄúYour hinterlands are not precious to me. Your person is. Should any ill befall you, this world would lose its savor.‚Äù ‚ÄúMy lord is good to care so much, but I am well protected.‚Äù Dany gestured toward where Barristan Selmy stood with one hand resting on his sword hilt. ‚ÄúBarristan the Bold, they call him. Twice he has saved me from assassins.‚Äù Xaro gave Selmy a cursory inspection. ‚ÄúBarristan the Old, did you say? Your bear knight was younger, and devoted to you.‚Äù ‚ÄúI do not wish to speak of Jorah Mormont.‚Äù ‚ÄúTo be sure. The man was coarse and hairy.‚Äù The merchant prince leaned across the table. ‚ÄúLet us speak instead of love, of dreams and desire and Daenerys, the fairest woman in this world. I am drunk with the sight of you.‚Äù She was no stranger to the overblown courtesies of Qarth. ‚ÄúIf you are drunk, blame the wine.‚Äù ‚ÄúNo wine is half so intoxicating as your beauty. My manse has seemed as empty as a tomb since Daenerys departed, and all the pleasures of the Queen of Cities have been as ashes in my mouth. Why did you abandon me?‚Äù I was hounded from your city in fear for my life. ‚ÄúIt was time. Qarth wished me gone.‚Äù ‚ÄúWho? The Pureborn? They have water in their veins. The Spicers? There are curds between their ears. And the Undying are all dead. You should have taken me to husband. I am almost certain that I asked you for your hand. Begged you, even.‚Äù ‚ÄúOnly half a hundred times,‚Äù Dany teased. ‚ÄúYou gave up too easily, my lord. For I must marry, all agree.‚Äù ‚ÄúA khaleesi must have a khal,‚Äù said Irri, as she filled the queen‚Äôs cup once again. ‚ÄúThis is known.‚Äù ‚ÄúShall I ask again?‚Äù wondered Xaro. ‚ÄúNo, I know that smile. It is a cruel queen who dices with men‚Äôs hearts. Humble merchants like myself are no more than stones beneath your jeweled sandals.‚Äù A single tear ran slowly down his pale white cheek. Dany knew him too well to be moved. Qartheen men could weep at will. ‚ÄúOh, stop that.‚Äù She took a cherry from the bowl on the table and threw it at his nose. ‚ÄúI may be a young girl, but I am not so foolish as to wed a man who finds a fruit platter more enticing than my breast. I saw which dancers you were watching.‚Äù Xaro wiped away his tear. ‚ÄúThe same ones Your Grace was following, I believe. You see, we are alike. If you will not take me for your husband, I am content to be your slave.‚Äù ‚ÄúI want no slave. I free you.‚Äù His jeweled nose made a tempting target. This time Dany threw an apricot at him.

> Xaro caught it in the air and took a bite. ‚ÄúWhence came this madness? Should I count myself fortunate that you did not free my own slaves when you were my guest in Qarth?‚Äù I was a beggar queen and you were Xaro of the Thirteen, Dany thought, and all you wanted were my dragons. ‚ÄúYour slaves seemed well treated and content. It was not till Astapor that my eyes were opened. Do you know how Unsullied are made and trained?‚Äù ‚ÄúCruelly, I have no doubt. When a smith makes a sword, he thrusts the blade into the fire, beats on it with a hammer, then plunges it into iced water to temper the steel. If you would savor the sweet taste of the fruit, you must water the tree.‚Äù ‚ÄúThis tree has been watered with blood.‚Äù ‚ÄúHow else, to grow a soldier? Your Radiance enjoyed my dancers. Would it surprise you to know that they are slaves, bred and trained in Yunkai? They have been dancing since they were old enough to walk. How else to achieve such perfection?‚Äù He took a swallow of his wine. ‚ÄúThey are expert in all the erotic arts as well. I had thought to make Your Grace a gift of them.‚Äù ‚ÄúBy all means.‚Äù Dany was unsurprised. ‚ÄúI shall free them.‚Äù That made him wince. ‚ÄúAnd what would they do with freedom? As well give a fish a suit of mail. They were made to dance.‚Äù ‚ÄúMade by who? Their masters? Perhaps your dancers would sooner build or bake or farm. Have you asked them?‚Äù ‚ÄúPerhaps your elephants would sooner be nightingales. Instead of sweet song, Meereen‚Äôs nights would be filled with thunderous trumpetings, and your trees would shatter beneath the weight of great grey birds.‚Äù Xaro sighed. ‚ÄúDaenerys, my delight, beneath that sweet young breast beats a tender heart¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut take counsel from an older, wiser head. Things are not always as they seem. Much that may seem evil can be good. Consider rain.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúRain?‚Äù Does he take me for a fool, or just a child? ‚ÄúWe curse the rain when it falls upon our heads, yet without it we should starve. The world needs rain ‚Ä¶ and slaves. You make a face, but it is true. Consider Qarth. In art, music, magic, trade, all that makes us more than beasts, Qarth sits above the rest of mankind as you sit at the summit of this pyramid ‚Ä¶ but below, in place of bricks, the magnificence that is the Queen of Cities rests upon the backs of slaves. Ask yourself, if all men must grub in the dirt for food, how shall any man lift his eyes to contemplate the stars? If each of us must break his back to build a hovel, who shall raise the temples to glorify the gods? For some men to be great, others must be enslaved.‚Äù He was too eloquent for her. Dany had no answer for him, only the raw feeling in her belly. ‚ÄúSlavery is not the same as rain,‚Äù she insisted. ‚ÄúI have been rained on and I have been sold. It is not the same. No man wants to be owned.‚Äù Xaro gave a languid shrug. ‚ÄúAs it happens, when I came ashore in your sweet city, I chanced to see upon the riverbank a man who had once been a guest in my manse, a merchant who dealt in rare spices and choice wines. He was naked from the waist up, red and peeling, and seemed to be digging a hole.‚Äù ‚ÄúNot a hole. A ditch, to bring water from the river to the fields. We mean to plant beans. The beanfields must have water.‚Äù ‚ÄúHow kind of my old friend to help with the digging. And how very unlike him. Is it possible he was given no choice in the matter? No, surely not. You have no slaves in Meereen.‚Äù Dany flushed. ‚ÄúYour friend is being paid with food and shelter. I cannot give him back his wealth. Meereen needs beans more than it needs rare spices, and beans require water.‚Äù ‚ÄúWould you set my dancers to digging ditches as well? Sweet queen, when he saw me, my old friend fell to his knees and begged me to buy him as a slave and take him back to Qarth.‚Äù She felt as if he‚Äôd slapped her. ‚ÄúBuy him, then.‚Äù ‚ÄúIf it please you. I know it will please him.‚Äù

> She took him out onto the terrace that overlooked the city. A full moon swam in the black sky above Meereen. ‚ÄúShall we walk?‚Äù Dany slipped her arm through his. The air was heavy with the scent of night-blooming flowers. ‚ÄúYou spoke of help. Trade with me, then. Meereen has salt to sell, and wine¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúGhiscari wine?‚Äù Xaro made a sour face. ‚ÄúThe sea provides all the salt that Qarth requires, but I would gladly take as many olives as you cared to sell me. Olive oil as well.‚Äù ‚ÄúI have none to offer. The slavers burned the trees.‚Äù Olives had been grown along the shores of Slaver‚Äôs Bay for centuries; but the Meereenese had put their ancient groves to the torch as Dany‚Äôs host advanced on them, leaving her to cross a blackened wasteland. ‚ÄúWe are replanting, but it takes seven years before an olive tree begins to bear, and thirty years before it can truly be called productive. What of copper?‚Äù ‚ÄúA pretty metal, but fickle as a woman. Gold, now¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýgold is sincere. Qarth will gladly give you gold¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýfor slaves.‚Äù ‚ÄúMeereen is a free city of free men.‚Äù ‚ÄúA poor city that once was rich. A hungry city that once was fat. A bloody city that once was peaceful.‚Äù His accusations stung. There was too much truth in them. ‚ÄúMeereen will be rich and fat and peaceful once again, and free as well. Go to the Dothraki if you must have slaves.‚Äù ‚ÄúDothraki make slaves, Ghiscari train them. And to reach Qarth, the horselords must needs drive their captives across the red waste. Hundreds would die, if not thousands¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand many horses too, which is why no khal will risk it. And there is this: Qarth wants no khalasars seething round our walls. The stench of all those horses¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýmeaning no offense, Khaleesi.‚Äù ‚ÄúA horse has an honest smell. That is more than can be said of some great lords and merchant princes.‚Äù

> Xaro took no notice of the sally. ‚ÄúDaenerys, let me be honest with you, as befits a friend. You will not make Meereen rich and fat and peaceful. You will only bring it to destruction, as you did Astapor. You are aware that there was battle joined at the Horns of Hazzat? The Butcher King has fled back to his palace, his new Unsullied running at his heels.‚Äù ‚ÄúThis is known.‚Äù Brown Ben Plumm had sent back word of the battle from the field. ‚ÄúThe Yunkai‚Äôi have bought themselves new sellswords, and two legions from New Ghis fought beside them.‚Äù ‚ÄúTwo will soon become four, then ten. And Yunkish envoys have been sent to Myr and Volantis to hire more blades. The Company of the Cat, the Long Lances, the Windblown. Some say that the Wise Masters have bought the Golden Company as well.‚Äù Her brother Viserys had once feasted the captains of the Golden Company, in hopes they might take up his cause. They ate his food and heard his pleas and laughed at him. Dany had only been a little girl, but she remembered. ‚ÄúI have sellswords too.‚Äù ‚ÄúTwo companies. The Yunkai‚Äôi will send twenty against you if they must. And when they march, they will not march alone. Tolos and Mantarys have agreed to an alliance.‚Äù That was ill news, if true. Daenerys had sent missions to Tolos and Mantarys, hoping to find new friends to the west to balance the enmity of Yunkai to the south. Her envoys had not returned. ‚ÄúMeereen has made alliance with Lhazar.‚Äù That only made him chuckle. ‚ÄúThe Dothraki horselords call the Lhazarene the Lamb Men. When you shear them, all they do is bleat. They are not a martial people.‚Äù Even a sheepish friend is better than none. ‚ÄúThe Wise Masters should follow their example. I spared Yunkai before, but I will not make that mistake again. If they should dare attack me, this time I shall raze their Yellow City to the ground.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd whilst you are razing Yunkai, my sweet, Meereen shall rise behind you. Do not close your eyes to your peril, Daenerys. Your eunuchs are fine soldiers, but they are too few to match the hosts that Yunkai will send against you, once Astapor has fallen.‚Äù ‚ÄúMy freedman‚Äî‚Äù Dany started. ‚ÄúBedslaves, barbers, and brickmakers win no battles.‚Äù He was wrong in that, she hoped. The freedmen had been a rabble once, but she had organized the men of fighting age into companies and commanded Grey Worm to make them into soldiers. Let him think what he will. ‚ÄúHave you forgotten? I have dragons.‚Äù ‚ÄúDo you? In Qarth, you were seldom seen without a dragon on your shoulder ‚Ä¶ yet now that shapely shoulder is as fair and bare as your sweet breast, I observe.‚Äù ‚ÄúMy dragons have grown, my shoulders have not. They range far afield, hunting.‚Äù Hazzea, forgive me. She wondered how much Xaro knew, what whispers he had heard. ‚ÄúAsk the Good Masters of Astapor about my dragons if you doubt them.‚Äù I saw a slaver‚Äôs eyes melt and go running down his cheeks. ‚ÄúTell me true, old friend, why did you seek me out if not to trade?‚Äù ‚ÄúTo bring a gift, for the queen of my heart.‚Äù ‚ÄúSay on.‚Äù What trap is this, now? ‚ÄúThe gift you begged of me in Qarth. Ships. There are thirteen galleys in the bay. Yours, if you will have them. I have brought you a fleet, to carry you home to Westeros.‚Äù A fleet. It was more than she could hope for, so of course it made her wary. In Qarth, Xaro had offered her thirty ships¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýfor a dragon. ‚ÄúAnd what price do you ask for these ships?‚Äù ‚ÄúNone. I no longer lust for dragons. I saw their work at Astapor on my way here, when my Silken Cloud put in for water. The ships are yours, sweet queen. Thirteen galleys, and men to pull the oars.‚Äù Thirteen. To be sure. Xaro was one of the Thirteen. No doubt he had convinced each of his fellow members to give up one ship. She knew the merchant prince too well to think that he would sacrifice thirteen of his own ships. ‚ÄúI must consider this. May I inspect these ships?‚Äù ‚ÄúYou have grown suspicious, Daenerys.‚Äù Always. ‚ÄúI have grown wise, Xaro.‚Äù ‚ÄúInspect all you wish. When you are satisfied, swear to me that you shall return to Westeros forthwith, and the ships are yours. Swear by your dragons and your seven-faced god and the ashes of your fathers, and go.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd if I should decide to wait a year, or three?‚Äù A mournful look crossed Xaro‚Äôs face. ‚ÄúThat would make me very sad, my sweet delight¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýfor young and strong as you now seem, you shall not live so long. Not here.‚Äù He offers the honeycomb with one hand and shows the whip with the other. ‚ÄúThe Yunkai‚Äôi are not so fearsome as all that.‚Äù ‚ÄúNot all your enemies are in the Yellow City. Beware men with cold hearts and blue lips. You had not been gone from Qarth a fortnight when Pyat Pree set out with three of his fellow warlocks, to seek for you in Pentos.‚Äù Dany was more amused than afraid. ‚ÄúIt is good I turned aside, then. Pentos is half a world from Meereen.‚Äù ‚ÄúThis is so,‚Äù he allowed, ‚Äúyet soon or late word must reach them of the dragon queen of Slaver‚Äôs Bay.‚Äù ‚ÄúIs that meant to frighten me? I lived in fear for fourteen years, my lord. I woke afraid each morning and went to sleep afraid each night¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut my fears were burned away the day I came forth from the fire. Only one thing frightens me now.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd what is it that you fear, sweet queen?‚Äù ‚ÄúI am only a foolish young girl.‚Äù Dany rose on her toes and kissed his cheek. ‚ÄúBut not so foolish as to tell you that. My men shall look at these ships. Then you shall have my answer.‚Äù ‚ÄúAs you say.‚Äù He touched her bare breast lightly, and whispered, ‚ÄúLet me stay and help persuade you.‚Äù For a moment she was tempted. Perhaps the dancers had stirred her after all. I could close my eyes and pretend that he was Daario. A dream Daario would be safer than the real one. But she pushed the thought aside. ‚ÄúNo, my lord. I thank you, but no.‚Äù Dany slipped from his arms. ‚ÄúSome other night, perhaps.‚Äù ‚ÄúSome other night.‚Äù His mouth was sad, but his eyes seemed more relieved than disappointed.

> She received the merchant prince alone, seated on her bench of polished ebony, on the cushions Ser Barristan had brought her. Four Qartheen sailors accompanied him, bearing a rolled tapestry upon their shoulders. ‚ÄúI have brought another gift for the queen of my heart,‚Äù Xaro announced. ‚ÄúIt has been in my family vaults since before the Doom that took Valyria.‚Äù The sailors unrolled the tapestry across the floor. It was old, dusty, faded ‚Ä¶ and huge. Dany had to move to Xaro‚Äôs side before the patterns became plain. ‚ÄúA map? It is beautiful.‚Äù It covered half the floor. The seas were blue, the lands were green, the mountains black and brown. Cities were shown as stars in gold or silver thread. There is no Smoking Sea, she realized. Valyria is not yet an island. ‚ÄúThere you see Astapor, and Yunkai, and Meereen.‚Äù Xaro pointed at three silver stars beside the blue of Slaver‚Äôs Bay. ‚ÄúWesteros is ‚Ä¶ somewhere down there.‚Äù His hand waved vaguely toward the far end of the hall. ‚ÄúYou turned north when you should have continued south and west, across the Summer Sea, but with my gift you shall soon be back where you belong. Accept my galleys with a joyful heart, and bend your oars westward.‚Äù Would that I could. ‚ÄúMy lord, I will gladly have those ships, but I cannot give you the promise that you ask.‚Äù She took his hand. ‚ÄúGive me the galleys, and I swear that Qarth will have the friendship of Meereen until the stars go out. Let me trade with them, and you will have a good part of the profits.‚Äù Xaro‚Äôs glad smile died upon his lips. ‚ÄúWhat are you saying? Are you telling me you will not go?‚Äù ‚ÄúI cannot go.‚Äù Tears welled from his eyes, creeping down his nose, past emeralds, amethysts, and black diamonds. ‚ÄúI told the Thirteen that you would heed my wisdom. It grieves me to learn that I was wrong. Take these ships and sail away, or you will surely die screaming. You cannot know how many enemies you have made.‚Äù I know one stands before me now, weeping mummer‚Äôs tears. The realization made her sad. ‚ÄúWhen I went to the Hall of a Thousand Thrones to beg the Pureborn for your life, I said that you were no more than a child,‚Äù Xaro went on, ‚Äúbut Egon Emeros the Exquisite rose and said, ‚ÄòShe is a foolish child, mad and heedless and too dangerous to live.‚Äô When your dragons were small they were a wonder. Grown, they are death and devastation, a flaming sword above the world.‚Äù He wiped away the tears. ‚ÄúI should have slain you in Qarth.‚Äù ‚ÄúI was a guest beneath your roof and ate of your meat and mead,‚Äù she said. ‚ÄúIn memory of all you did for me, I will forgive those words ‚Ä¶ once ‚Ä¶ but never presume to threaten me again.‚Äù ‚ÄúXaro Xhoan Daxos does not threaten. He promises.‚Äù Her sadness turned to fury. ‚ÄúAnd I promise you that if you are not gone before the sun comes up, we will learn how well a liar‚Äôs tears can quench dragonfire. Leave me, Xaro. Quickly.‚Äù He went but left his world behind. Dany seated herself upon her bench again to gaze across the blue silk sea, toward distant Westeros. One day, she promised herself. The next morning Xaro‚Äôs galleas was gone, but the ‚Äúgift‚Äù that he had brought her remained behind in Slaver‚Äôs Bay. Long red streamers flew from the masts of the thirteen Qartheen galleys, writhing in the wind. And when Daenerys descended to hold court, a messenger from the ships awaited her. He spoke no word but laid at her feet a black satin pillow, upon which rested a single bloodstained glove. ‚ÄúWhat is this?‚Äù Skahaz demanded. ‚ÄúA bloody glove¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚Äú‚Ä¶¬Ýmeans war,‚Äù said the queen.

{:#Tywin-Lannister-Quotes}
**Tywin Lannister**

> ‚ÄúPod tells me that Littlefinger‚Äôs been made Lord of Harrenhal.‚Äù ‚ÄúAn empty title, so long as Roose Bolton holds the castle for Robb Stark, yet Lord Baelish was desirous of the honor. He did us good service in the matter of the Tyrell marriage. A Lannister pays his debts.‚Äù The Tyrell marriage had been Tyrion‚Äôs notion, in point of fact, but it would seem churlish to try to claim that now. ‚ÄúThat title may not be as empty as you think,‚Äù he warned. ‚ÄúLittlefinger does nothing without good reason. But be that as it may. You said something about paying debts, I believe?‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd you want your own reward, is that it? Very well. What is it you would have of me? Lands, castle, some office?‚Äù ‚ÄúA little bloody gratitude would make a nice start.‚Äù Lord Tywin stared at him, unblinking. ‚ÄúMummers and monkeys require applause. So did Aerys, for that matter. You did as you were commanded, and I am sure it was to the best of your ability. No one denies the part you played.‚Äù ‚ÄúThat part I played?‚Äù What nostrils Tyrion had left must surely have flared. ‚ÄúI saved your bloody city, it seems to me.‚Äù ‚ÄúMost people seem to feel that it was my attack on Lord Stannis‚Äôs flank that turned the tide of battle. Lords Tyrell, Rowan, Redwyne, and Tarly fought nobly as well, and I‚Äôm told it was your sister Cersei who set the pyromancers to making the wildfire that destroyed the Baratheon fleet.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhile all I did was get my nosehairs trimmed, is that it?‚Äù Tyrion could not keep the bitterness out of his voice. ‚ÄúYour chain was a clever stroke, and crucial to our victory. Is that what you wanted to hear? I am told we have you to thank for our Dornish alliance as well. You may be pleased to learn that Myrcella has arrived safely at Sunspear. Ser Arys Oakheart writes that she has taken a great liking to Princess Arianne, and that Prince Trystane is enchanted with her. I mislike giving House Martell a hostage, but I suppose that could not be helped.‚Äù ‚ÄúWe‚Äôll have our own hostage,‚Äù Tyrion said. ‚ÄúA council seat was also part of the bargain. Unless Prince Doran brings an army when he comes to claim it, he‚Äôll be putting himself in our power.‚Äù ‚ÄúWould that a council seat were all Martell came to claim,‚Äù Lord Tywin said. ‚ÄúYou promised him vengeance as well.‚Äù ‚ÄúI promised him justice.‚Äù ‚ÄúCall it what you will. It still comes down to blood.‚Äù

> Tyrion cocked his head. ‚ÄúPerhaps you should speak more softly to me, then. Monsters are dangerous beasts, and just now kings seem to be dying like flies.‚Äù ‚ÄúI could have your tongue out for saying that,‚Äù the boy king said, reddening. ‚ÄúI‚Äôm the king.‚Äù Cersei put a protective hand on her son‚Äôs shoulder. ‚ÄúLet the dwarf make all the threats he likes, Joff. I want my lord father and my uncle to see what he is.‚Äù Lord Tywin ignored that; it was Joffrey he addressed. ‚ÄúAerys also felt the need to remind men that he was king. And he was passing fond of ripping tongues out as well. You could ask Ser Ilyn Payne about that, though you‚Äôll get no reply.‚Äù ‚ÄúSer Ilyn never dared provoke Aerys the way your Imp provokes Joff,‚Äù said Cersei. ‚ÄúYou heard him. ‚ÄòMonster,‚Äô he said. To the King‚Äôs Grace. And he threatened him¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúBe quiet, Cersei. Joffrey, when your enemies defy you, you must serve them steel and fire. When they go to their knees, however, you must help them back to their feet. Elsewise no man will ever bend the knee to you. And any man who must say ‚ÄòI am the king‚Äô is no true king at all. Aerys never understood that, but you will. When I‚Äôve won your war for you, we will restore the king‚Äôs peace and the king‚Äôs justice. The only head that need concern you is Margaery Tyrell‚Äôs maidenhead.‚Äù Joffrey had that sullen, sulky look he got. Cersei had him firmly by the shoulder, but perhaps she should have had him by the throat. The boy surprised them all. Instead of scuttling safely back under his rock, Joff drew himself up defiantly and said, ‚ÄúYou talk about Aerys, Grandfather, but you were scared of him.‚Äù Oh, my, hasn‚Äôt this gotten interesting? Tyrion thought. Lord Tywin studied his grandchild in silence, gold flecks shining in his pale green eyes. ‚ÄúJoffrey, apologize to your grandfather,‚Äù said Cersei. He wrenched free of her, ‚ÄúWhy should I? Everyone knows it‚Äôs true. My father won all the battles. He killed Prince Rhaegar and took the crown, while your father was hiding under Casterly Rock.‚Äù The boy gave his grandfather a defiant look. ‚ÄúA strong king acts boldly, he doesn‚Äôt just talk.‚Äù ‚ÄúThank you for that wisdom, Your Grace,‚Äù Lord Tywin said, with a courtesy so cold it was like to freeze their ears off. ‚ÄúSer Kevan, I can see the king is tired. Please see him safely back to his bedchamber. Pycelle, perhaps some gentle potion to help His Grace sleep restfully?‚Äù ‚ÄúDreamwine, my lord?‚Äù ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt want any dreamwine,‚Äù Joffrey insisted. Lord Tywin would have paid more heed to a mouse squeaking in the corner. ‚ÄúDreamwine will serve. Cersei, Tyrion, remain.‚Äù Ser Kevan took Joffrey firmly by the arm and marched him out the door, where two of the Kingsguard were waiting.

> ‚ÄúGive me the boy for R‚Äôhllor,‚Äù the red woman said, ‚Äúand the ancient prophecy shall be fulfilled. Your dragon shall awaken and spread his stony wings. The kingdom shall be yours.‚Äù Ser Axell went to one knee. ‚ÄúOn bended knee I beg you, sire. Wake the stone dragon and let the traitors tremble. Like Aegon you begin as Lord of Dragonstone. Like Aegon you shall conquer. Let the false and the fickle feel your flames.‚Äù ‚ÄúYour own wife begs as well, lord husband.‚Äù Queen Selyse went down on both knees before the king, hands clasped as if in prayer. ‚ÄúRobert and Delena defiled our bed and laid a curse upon our union. This boy is the foul fruit of their fornications. Lift his shadow from my womb and I will bear you many trueborn sons, I know it.‚Äù She threw her arms around his legs. ‚ÄúHe is only one boy, born of your brother‚Äôs lust and my cousin‚Äôs shame.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe is mine own blood. Stop clutching me, woman.‚Äù King Stannis put a hand on her shoulder, awkwardly untangling himself from her grasp. ‚ÄúPerhaps Robert did curse our marriage bed. He swore to me that he never meant to shame me, that he was drunk and never knew which bedchamber he entered that night. But does it matter? The boy was not at fault, whatever the truth.‚Äù Melisandre put her hand on the king‚Äôs arm. ‚ÄúThe Lord of Light cherishes the innocent. There is no sacrifice more precious. From his king‚Äôs blood and his untainted fire, a dragon shall be born.‚Äù Stannis did not pull away from Melisandre‚Äôs touch as he had from his queen‚Äôs.
