The red woman was all Selyse was not; young, full-bodied, and strangely beautiful, with her heart-shaped face, coppery hair, and unearthly red eyes. ‚ÄúIt would be a wondrous thing to see stone come to life,‚Äù he admitted, grudging. ‚ÄúAnd to mount a dragon¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝI remember the first time my father took me to court, Robert had to hold my hand. I could not have been older than four, which would have made him five or six. We agreed afterward that the king had been as noble as the dragons were fearsome.‚Äù Stannis snorted. ‚ÄúYears later, our father told us that Aerys had cut himself on the throne that morning, so his Hand had taken his place. It was Tywin Lannister who‚Äôd so impressed us.‚Äù

> The silent sisters had armored Lord Tywin as if to fight some final battle. He wore his finest plate, heavy steel enameled a deep, dark crimson, with gold inlay on his gauntlets, greaves, and breastplate. His rondels were golden sunbursts; a golden lioness crouched upon each shoulder; a maned lion crested the greathelm beside his head. Upon his chest lay a longsword in a gilded scabbard studded with rubies, his hands folded about its hilt in gloves of gilded mail. Even in death his face is noble, she thought, although the mouth¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝThe corners of her father‚Äôs lips curved upward ever so slightly, giving him a look of vague bemusement. That should not be. She blamed Pycelle; he should have told the silent sisters that Lord Tywin Lannister never smiled. The man is as useless as nipples on a breastplate. That half smile made Lord Tywin seem less fearful, somehow. That, and the fact that his eyes were closed. Her father‚Äôs eyes had always been unsettling; pale green, almost luminous, flecked with gold. His eyes could see inside you, could see how weak and worthless and ugly you were down deep. When he looked at you, you knew.

> ‚ÄúYour brother has been covering himself with glory,‚Äù his father said. ‚ÄúHe smashed the Lords Vance and Piper at the Golden Tooth, and met the massed power of the Tullys under the walls of Riverrun. The lords of the Trident have been put to rout. Ser Edmure Tully was taken captive, with many of his knights and bannermen. Lord Blackwood led a few survivors back to Riverrun, where Jaime has them under siege. The rest fled to their own strongholds.‚Äù ‚ÄúYour father and I have been marching on each in turn,‚Äù Ser Kevan said. ‚ÄúWith Lord Blackwood gone, Raventree fell at once, and Lady Whent yielded Harrenhal for want of men to defend it. Ser Gregor burnt out the Pipers and the Brackens¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúLeaving you unopposed?‚Äù Tyrion said. ‚ÄúNot wholly,‚Äù Ser Kevan said. ‚ÄúThe Mallisters still hold Seagard and Walder Frey is marshaling his levies at the Twins.‚Äù ‚ÄúNo matter,‚Äù Lord Tywin said. ‚ÄúFrey only takes the field when the scent of victory is in the air, and all he smells now is ruin. And Jason Mallister lacks the strength to fight alone. Once Jaime takes Riverrun, they will both be quick enough to bend the knee. Unless the Starks and the Arryns come forth to oppose us, this war is good as won.‚Äù ‚ÄúI would not fret overmuch about the Arryns if I were you,‚Äù Tyrion said. ‚ÄúThe Starks are another matter. Lord Eddard‚Äî‚Äù ‚Äú‚Äîis our hostage,‚Äù his father said. ‚ÄúHe will lead no armies while he rots in a dungeon under the Red Keep.‚Äù ‚ÄúNo,‚Äù Ser Kevan agreed, ‚Äúbut his son has called the banners and sits at Moat Cailin with a strong host around him.‚Äù ‚ÄúNo sword is strong until it‚Äôs been tempered,‚Äù Lord Tywin declared. ‚ÄúThe Stark boy is a child. No doubt he likes the sound of warhorns well enough, and the sight of his banners fluttering in the wind, but in the end it comes down to butcher‚Äôs work. I doubt he has the stomach for it.‚Äù

> Above him, all the windows had gone black, and he could see the faint light of distant stars. The sun had set for good and all. The stench of death was growing stronger, despite the scented candles. The smell reminded Jaime Lannister of the pass below the Golden Tooth, where he had won a glorious victory in the first days of the war. On the morning after the battle, the crows had feasted on victors and vanquished alike, as once they had feasted on Rhaegar Targaryen after the Trident. How much can a crown be worth, when a crow can dine upon a king? There were crows circling the seven towers and great dome of Baelor‚Äôs Sept even now, Jaime suspected, their black wings beating against the night air as they searched for a way inside. Every crow in the Seven Kingdoms should pay homage to you, Father. From Castamere to the Blackwater, you fed them well. That notion pleased Lord Tywin; his smile widened further. Bloody hell, he‚Äôs grinning like a bridegroom at his bedding. That was so grotesque it made Jaime laugh aloud.

> ‚ÄúI was seven when Walder Frey persuaded my lord father to give my hand to Emm. His second son, not even his heir. Father was himself a thirdborn son, and younger children crave the approval of their elders. Frey sensed that weakness in him, and Father agreed for no better reason than to please him. My betrothal was announced at a feast with half the west in attendance. Ellyn Tarbeck laughed and the Red Lion went angry from the hall. The rest sat on their tongues. Only Tywin dared speak against the match. A boy of ten. Father turned as white as mare‚Äôs milk, and Walder Frey was quivering.‚Äù She smiled. ‚ÄúHow could I not love him, after that? That is not to say that I approved of all he did, or much enjoyed the company of the man that he became¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut every little girl needs a big brother to protect her. Tywin was big even when he was little.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúJaime,‚Äù she said, tugging on his ear, ‚Äúsweetling, I have known you since you were a babe at Joanna‚Äôs breast. You smile like Gerion and fight like Tyg, and there‚Äôs some of Kevan in you, else you would not wear that cloak¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut Tyrion is Tywin‚Äôs son, not you. I said so once to your father‚Äôs face, and he would not speak to me for half a year. Men are such thundering great fools. Even the sort who come along once in a thousand years.‚Äù

> Snow in the riverlands. If it was snowing here, it could well be snowing on Lannisport as well, and on King‚Äôs Landing. Winter is marching south, and half our granaries are empty. Any crops still in the fields were doomed. There would be no more plantings, no more hopes of one last harvest. He found himself wondering what his father would do to feed the realm, before he remembered that Tywin Lannister was dead.

{:#Salladhor-Saan}
**Salladhor Saan**

> Across the noisy common room, Salladhor Saan sat eating grapes from a wooden bowl. When he spied Davos, he beckoned him closer. ‚ÄúSer knight, come sit with me. Eat a grape. Eat two. They are marvelously sweet.‚Äù The Lyseni was a sleek, smiling man whose flamboyance was a byword on both sides of the narrow sea. Today he wore flashing cloth-of-silver, with dagged sleeves so long the ends of them pooled on the floor. His buttons were carved jade monkeys, and atop his wispy white curls perched a jaunty green cap decorated with a fan of peacock feathers. Davos threaded his way through the tables to a chair. In the days before his knighthood, he had often bought cargoes from Salladhor Saan. The Lyseni was a smuggler himself, as well as a trader, a banker, a notorious pirate, and the self-styled Prince of the Narrow Sea. When a pirate grows rich enough, they make him a prince.

> Davos ordered a tankard of ale, turned back to Saan, and said, ‚ÄúHow well is the city defended?‚Äù The other shrugged. ‚ÄúThe walls are high and strong, but who will man them? They are building scorpions and spitfires, oh, yes, but the men in the golden cloaks are too few and too green, and there are no others. A swift strike, like a hawk plummeting at a hare, and the great city will be ours. Grant us wind to fill our sails, and your king could sit upon his Iron Throne by evenfall on the morrow. We could dress the dwarf in motley and prick his little cheeks with the points of our spears to make him dance for us, and mayhaps your goodly king would make me a gift of the beautiful Queen Cersei to warm my bed for a night. I have been too long away from my wives, and all in his service.‚Äù ‚ÄúPirate,‚Äù said Davos. ‚ÄúYou have no wives, only concubines, and you have been well paid for every day and every ship.‚Äù ‚ÄúOnly in promises,‚Äù said Salladhor Saan mournfully. ‚ÄúGood ser, it is gold I crave, not words on papers.‚Äù He popped a grape into his mouth. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôll have your gold when we take the treasury in King‚Äôs Landing. No man in the Seven Kingdoms is more honorable than Stannis Baratheon. He will keep his word.‚Äù Even as Davos spoke, he thought, This world is twisted beyond hope, when lowborn smugglers must vouch for the honor of kings.

> Salladhor Saan appeared not long after. ‚ÄúYou must be forgiving me for the wine, my friend. These Pentoshi would drink their own water if it were purple.‚Äù ‚ÄúIt will help my chest,‚Äù said Davos. ‚ÄúHot wine is better than a compress, my mother used to say.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou shall be needing compresses as well, I am thinking. Sitting on a spear all this long time, oh my. How are you finding that excellent chair? He has fat cheeks, does he not?‚Äù ‚ÄúWho?‚Äù asked Davos, between sips of hot wine. ‚ÄúIllyrio Mopatis. A whale with whiskers, I am telling you truly. These chairs were built to his measure, though he is seldom bestirring himself from Pentos to sit in them. A fat man always sits comfortably, I am thinking, for he takes his pillow with him wherever he goes.‚Äù ‚ÄúHow is it you come by a Pentoshi ship?‚Äù asked Davos. ‚ÄúHave you gone pirate again, my lord?‚Äù He set his empty cup aside. ‚ÄúVile calumny. Who has suffered more from pirates than Salladhor Saan? I ask only what is due me. Much gold is owed, oh yes, but I am not without reason, so in place of coin I have taken a handsome parchment, very crisp. It bears the name and seal of Lord Alester Florent, the Hand of the King. I am made Lord of Blackwater Bay, and no vessel may be crossing my lordly waters without my lordly leave, no. And when these outlaws are trying to steal past me in the night to avoid my lawful duties and customs, why, they are no better than smugglers, so I am well within my rights to seize them.‚Äù The old pirate laughed. ‚ÄúI cut off no man‚Äôs fingers, though. What good are bits of fingers? The ships I am taking, the cargoes, a few ransoms, nothing unreasonable.‚Äù

> "The men of Westeros are ever rushing," complained Salladhor Saan. "What good is this, I ask you? He who hurries through life hurries to his grave."

{:#Davos-Seaworth}
**Davos Seaworth**

> Davos was a slight man, his low birth written plain upon a common face. A well-worn green cloak, stained by salt and spray and faded from the sun, draped his thin shoulders, over brown doublet and breeches that matched brown eyes and hair. About his neck a pouch of worn leather hung from a thong. His small beard was well peppered with grey, and he wore a leather glove on his maimed left hand.

> Maester Cressen remembered the day Davos had been knighted, after the siege of Storm‚Äôs End. Lord Stannis and a small garrison had held the castle for close to a year, against the great host of the Lords Tyrell and Redwyne. Even the sea was closed against them, watched day and night by Redwyne galleys flying the burgundy banners of the Arbor. Within Storm‚Äôs End, the horses had long since been eaten, the dogs and cats were gone, and the garrison was down to roots and rats. Then came a night when the moon was new and black clouds hid the stars. Cloaked in that darkness, Davos the smuggler had dared the Redwyne cordon and the rocks of Shipbreaker Bay alike. His little ship had a black hull, black sails, black oars, and a hold crammed with onions and salt fish. Little enough, yet it had kept the garrison alive long enough for Eddard Stark to reach Storm‚Äôs End and break the siege. Lord Stannis had rewarded Davos with choice lands on Cape Wrath, a small keep, and a knight‚Äôs honors¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut he had also decreed that he lose a joint of each finger on his left hand, to pay for all his years of smuggling. Davos had submitted, on the condition that Stannis wield the knife himself; he would accept no punishment from lesser hands. The lord had used a butcher‚Äôs cleaver, the better to cut clean and true. Afterward, Davos had chosen the name Seaworth for his new-made house, and he took for his banner a black ship on a pale grey field‚Äîwith an onion on its sails. The onetime smuggler was fond of saying that Lord Stannis had done him a boon, by giving him four less fingernails to clean and trim.

> The steps seemed longer and steeper than before, or perhaps it was just that he was tired. The Mother never made me for tasks like this. He had risen too high and too fast, and up here on the mountain the air was too thin for him to breathe. As a boy he‚Äôd dreamed of riches, but that was long ago. Later, grown, all he had wanted was a few acres of good land, a hall to grow old in, a better life for his sons. The Blind Bastard used to tell him that a clever smuggler did not overreach, nor draw too much attention to himself. A few acres, a timbered roof, a ‚Äúser‚Äù before my name, I should have been content. If he survived this night, he would take Devan and sail home to Cape Wrath and his gentle Marya. We will grieve together for our dead sons, raise the living ones to be good men, and speak no more of kings.

> ‚ÄúHis name is Edric Storm, sire.‚Äù ‚ÄúI know his name. Was there ever a name so apt? It proclaims his bastardy, his high birth, and the turmoil he brings with him. Edric Storm. There, I have said it. Are you satisfied, my lord Hand?‚Äù ‚ÄúEdric‚Äî‚Äù he started. ‚Äú‚Äîis one boy! He may be the best boy who ever drew breath and it would not matter. My duty is to the realm.‚Äù His hand swept across the Painted Table. ‚ÄúHow many boys dwell in Westeros? How many girls? How many men, how many women? The darkness will devour them all, she says. The night that never ends. She talks of prophecies¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýa hero reborn in the sea, living dragons hatched from dead stone¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýshe speaks of signs and swears they point to me. I never asked for this, no more than I asked to be king. Yet dare I disregard her?‚Äù He ground his teeth. ‚ÄúWe do not choose our destinies. Yet we must¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýwe must do our duty, no? Great or small, we must do our duty. Melisandre swears that she has seen me in her flames, facing the dark with Lightbringer raised on high. Lightbringer!‚Äù Stannis gave a derisive snort. ‚ÄúIt glimmers prettily, I‚Äôll grant you, but on the Blackwater this magic sword served me no better than any common steel. A dragon would have turned that battle. Aegon once stood here as I do, looking down on this table. Do you think we would name him Aegon the Conqueror today if he had not had dragons?‚Äù ‚ÄúYour Grace,‚Äù said Davos, ‚Äúthe cost¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúI know the cost! Last night, gazing into that hearth, I saw things in the flames as well. I saw a king, a crown of fire on his brows, burning¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýburning, Davos. His own crown consumed his flesh and turned him into ash. Do you think I need Melisandre to tell me what that means? Or you?‚Äù The king moved, so his shadow fell upon King‚Äôs Landing. ‚ÄúIf Joffrey should die¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýwhat is the life of one bastard boy against a kingdom?‚Äù ‚ÄúEverything,‚Äù said Davos, softly. Stannis looked at him, jaw clenched. ‚ÄúGo,‚Äù the king said at last, ‚Äúbefore you talk yourself back into the dungeon.‚Äù

> Salla shrugged. ‚ÄúWe shall be seeing. Or you shall. For myself, I am returning to sea. Even now, rascally smugglers may be sailing across the Blackwater Bay, hoping to avoid paying their lord‚Äôs lawful duties.‚Äù He slapped Davos on the back. ‚ÄúTake care. You with your mute friends. You are grown so very great now, yet the higher a man climbs the farther he has to fall.‚Äù Davos reflected on those words as he climbed the steps of Sea Dragon Tower to the maester‚Äôs chambers below the rookery. He did not need Salla to tell him that he had risen too high. I cannot read, I cannot write, the lords despise me, I know nothing of ruling, how can I be the King‚Äôs Hand? I belong on the deck of a ship, not in a castle tower. He had said as much to Maester Pylos. ‚ÄúYou are a notable captain,‚Äù the maester replied. ‚ÄúA captain rules his ship, does he not? He must navigate treacherous waters, set his sails to catch the rising wind, know when a storm is coming and how best to weather it. This is much the same.‚Äù Pylos meant it kindly, but his assurances rang hollow. ‚ÄúIt is not at all the same!‚Äù Davos had protested. ‚ÄúA kingdom‚Äôs not a ship¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand a good thing, or this kingdom would be sinking. I know wood and rope and water, yes, but how will that serve me now? Where do I find the wind to blow King Stannis to his throne?‚Äù The maester laughed at that. ‚ÄúAnd there you have it, my lord. Words are wind, you know, and you‚Äôve blown mine away with your good sense. His Grace knows what he has in you, I think.‚Äù ‚ÄúOnions,‚Äù said Davos glumly. ‚ÄúThat is what he has in me. The King‚Äôs Hand should be a highborn lord, someone wise and learned, a battle commander or a great knight¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúSer Ryam Redwyne was the greatest knight of his day, and one of the worst Hands ever to serve a king. Septon Murmison‚Äôs prayers worked miracles, but as Hand he soon had the whole realm praying for his death. Lord Butterwell was renowned for wit, Myles Smallwood for courage, Ser Otto Hightower for learning, yet they failed as Hands, every one. As for birth, the dragonkings oft chose Hands from amongst their own blood, with results as various as Baelor Breakspear and Maegor the Cruel. Against this, you have Septon Barth, the blacksmith‚Äôs son the Old King plucked from the Red Keep‚Äôs library, who gave the realm forty years of peace and plenty.‚Äù Pylos smiled. ‚ÄúRead your history, Lord Davos, and you will see that your doubts are groundless.‚Äù ‚ÄúHow can I read history, when I cannot read?‚Äù ‚ÄúAny man can read, my lord,‚Äù said Maester Pylos. ‚ÄúThere is no magic needed, nor high birth. I am teaching the art to your son, at the king‚Äôs command. Let me teach you as well.‚Äù It was a kindly offer, and not one that Davos could refuse. And so every day he repaired to the maester‚Äôs chambers high atop Sea Dragon Tower, to frown over scrolls and parchments and great leather tomes and try to puzzle out a few more words. His efforts often gave him headaches, and made him feel as big a fool as Patchface besides. His son Devan was not yet twelve, yet he was well ahead of his father, and for Princess Shireen and Edric Storm reading seemed as natural as breathing. When it came to books, Davos was more a child than any of them. Yet he persisted. He was the King‚Äôs Hand now, and a King‚Äôs Hand should read.

> Ser Axell turned to Davos, with a look on his face much like the look that proud Lord Belgrave must have worn, the day King Baelor the Blessed had commanded him to wash the beggar‚Äôs ulcerous feet. Nonetheless, he obeyed. The plan Ser Axell had devised with Salladhor Saan was simple. A few hours‚Äô sail from Dragonstone lay Claw Isle, ancient sea-girt seat of House Celtigar. Lord Ardrian Celtigar had fought beneath the fiery heart on the Blackwater, but once taken, he had wasted no time in going over to Joffrey. He remained in King‚Äôs Landing even now. ‚ÄúToo frightened of His Grace‚Äôs wrath to come near Dragonstone, no doubt,‚Äù Ser Axell declared. ‚ÄúAnd wisely so. The man has betrayed his rightful king.‚Äù Ser Axell proposed to use Salladhor Saan‚Äôs fleet and the men who had escaped the Blackwater‚ÄîStannis still had some fifteen hundred on Dragonstone, more than half of them Florents‚Äîto exact retribution for Lord Celtigar‚Äôs defection. Claw Isle was but lightly garrisoned, its castle reputedly stuffed with Myrish carpets, Volantene glass, gold and silver plate, jeweled cups, magnificent hawks, an axe of Valyrian steel, a horn that could summon monsters from the deep, chests of rubies, and more wines than a man could drink in a hundred years. Though Celtigar had shown the world a niggardly face, he had never stinted on his own comforts. ‚ÄúPut his castle to the torch and his people to the sword, I say,‚Äù Ser Axell concluded. ‚ÄúLeave Claw Isle a desolation of ash and bone, fit only for carrion crows, so the realm might see the fate of those who bed with Lannisters.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt doubt Lord Celtigar bent the knee to the boy Joffrey. He is an old done man, who wants no more than to end his days in his castle, drinking his fine wine out of his jeweled cups.‚Äù He turned back to Stannis. ‚ÄúYet he came when you called, sire. Came, with his ships and swords. He stood by you at Storm‚Äôs End when Lord Renly came down on us, and his ships sailed up the Blackwater. His men fought for you, killed for you, burned for you. Claw Isle is weakly held, yes. Held by women and children and old men. And why is that? Because their husbands and sons and fathers died on the Blackwater, that‚Äôs why. Died at their oars, or with swords in their hands, fighting beneath our banners. Yet Ser Axell proposes we swoop down on the homes they left behind, to rape their widows and put their children to the sword. These smallfolk are no traitors¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúThey are,‚Äù insisted Ser Axell. ‚ÄúNot all of Celtigar‚Äôs men were slain on the Blackwater. Hundreds were taken with their lord, and bent the knee when he did.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhen he did,‚Äù Davos repeated. ‚ÄúThey were his men. His sworn men. What choice were they given?‚Äù ‚ÄúEvery man has choices. They might have refused to kneel. Some did, and died for it. Yet they died true men, and loyal.‚Äù ‚ÄúSome men are stronger than others.‚Äù It was a feeble answer, and Davos knew it. Stannis Baratheon was a man of iron will who neither understood nor forgave weakness in others. I am losing, he thought, despairing. ‚ÄúIt is every man‚Äôs duty to remain loyal to his rightful king, even if the lord he serves proves false,‚Äù Stannis declared in a tone that brooked no argument. A desperate folly took hold of Davos, a recklessness akin to madness. ‚ÄúAs you remained loyal to King Aerys when your brother raised his banners?‚Äù he blurted. Shocked silence followed, until Ser Axell cried, ‚ÄúTreason!‚Äù and snatched his dagger from its sheath. ‚ÄúYour Grace, he speaks his infamy to your face!‚Äù Davos could hear Stannis grinding his teeth. A vein bulged, blue and swollen, in the king‚Äôs brow. Their eyes met. ‚ÄúPut up your knife, Ser Axell. And leave us.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúI am your man, Your Grace. So it is your tongue, to do with as you please.‚Äù ‚ÄúIt is,‚Äù he said, calmer. ‚ÄúAnd I would have it speak the truth. Though the truth is a bitter draught at times. Aerys? If you only knew¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthat was a hard choosing. My blood or my liege. My brother or my king.‚Äù He grimaced. ‚ÄúHave you ever seen the Iron Throne? The barbs along the back, the ribbons of twisted steel, the jagged ends of swords and knives all tangled up and melted? It is not a comfortable seat, ser. Aerys cut himself so often men took to calling him King Scab, and Maegor the Cruel was murdered in that chair. By that chair, to hear some tell it. It is not a seat where a man can rest at ease. Ofttimes I wonder why my brothers wanted it so desperately.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhy would you want it, then?‚Äù Davos asked him. ‚ÄúIt is not a question of wanting. The throne is mine, as Robert‚Äôs heir. That is law. After me, it must pass to my daughter, unless Selyse should finally give me a son.‚Äù He ran three fingers lightly down the table, over the layers of smooth hard varnish, dark with age. ‚ÄúI am king. Wants do not enter into it. I have a duty to my daughter. To the realm. Even to Robert. He loved me but little, I know, yet he was my brother. The Lannister woman gave him horns and made a motley fool of him. She may have murdered him as well, as she murdered Jon Arryn and Ned Stark. For such crimes there must be justice. Starting with Cersei and her abominations. But only starting. I mean to scour that court clean. As Robert should have done, after the Trident. Ser Barristan once told me that the rot in King Aerys‚Äôs reign began with Varys. The eunuch should never have been pardoned. No more than the Kingslayer. At the least, Robert should have stripped the white cloak from Jaime and sent him to the Wall, as Lord Stark urged. He listened to Jon Arryn instead. I was still at Storm‚Äôs End, under siege and unconsulted.‚Äù He turned abruptly, to give Davos a hard shrewd look. ‚ÄúThe truth, now. Why did you wish to murder Lady Melisandre?‚Äù So he does know. Davos could not lie to him. ‚ÄúFour of my sons burned on the Blackwater. She gave them to the flames.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou wrong her. Those fires were no work of hers. Curse the Imp, curse the pyromancers, curse that fool of Florent who sailed my fleet into the jaws of a trap. Or curse me for my stubborn pride, for sending her away when I needed her most. But not Melisandre. She remains my faithful servant.‚Äù ‚ÄúMaester Cressen was your faithful servant. She slew him, as she killed Ser Cortnay Penrose and your brother Renly.‚Äù ‚ÄúNow you sound a fool,‚Äù the king complained. ‚ÄúShe saw Renly‚Äôs end in the flames, yes, but she had no more part in it than I did. The priestess was with me. Your Devan would tell you so. Ask him, if you doubt me. She would have spared Renly if she could. It was Melisandre who urged me to meet with him, and give him one last chance to amend his treason. And it was Melisandre who told me to send for you when Ser Axell wished to give you to R‚Äôhllor.‚Äù

> Stannis touched the Painted Table. ‚ÄúLook at it, onion knight. My realm, by rights. My Westeros.‚Äù He swept a hand across it. ‚ÄúThis talk of Seven Kingdoms is a folly. Aegon saw that three hundred years ago when he stood where we are standing. They painted this table at his command. Rivers and bays they painted, hills and mountains, castles and cities and market towns, lakes and swamps and forests¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut no borders. It is all one. One realm, for one king to rule alone.‚Äù ‚ÄúOne king,‚Äù agreed Davos. ‚ÄúOne king means peace.‚Äù ‚ÄúI shall bring justice to Westeros. A thing Ser Axell understands as little as he does war. Claw Isle would gain me naught¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand it was evil, just as you said. Celtigar must pay the traitor‚Äôs price himself, in his own person. And when I come into my kingdom, he shall. Every man shall reap what he has sown, from the highest lord to the lowest gutter rat. And some will lose more than the tips off their fingers, I promise you. They have made my kingdom bleed, and I do not forget that.‚Äù King Stannis turned from the table. ‚ÄúOn your knees, Onion Knight.‚Äù ‚ÄúYour Grace?‚Äù ‚ÄúFor your onions and fish, I made you a knight once. For this, I am of a mind to raise you to lord.‚Äù This? Davos was lost. ‚ÄúI am content to be your knight, Your Grace. I would not know how to begin being lordly.‚Äù ‚ÄúGood. To be lordly is to be false. I have learned that lesson hard. Now, kneel. Your king commands.‚Äù Davos knelt, and Stannis drew his longsword. Lightbringer, Melisandre had named it; the red sword of heroes, drawn from the fires where the seven gods were consumed. The room seemed to grow brighter as the blade slid from its scabbard. The steel had a glow to it; now orange, now yellow, now red. The air shimmered around it, and no jewel had ever sparkled so brilliantly. But when Stannis touched it to Davos‚Äôs shoulder, it felt no different than any other longsword. ‚ÄúSer Davos of House Seaworth,‚Äù the king said, ‚Äúare you my true and honest liege man, now and forever?‚Äù ‚ÄúI am, Your Grace.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd do you swear to serve me loyally all your days, to give me honest counsel and swift obedience, to defend my rights and my realm against all foes in battles great and small, to protect my people and punish my enemies?‚Äù ‚ÄúI do, Your Grace.‚Äù ‚ÄúThen rise again, Davos Seaworth, and rise as Lord of the Rainwood, Admiral of the Narrow Sea, and Hand of the King.‚Äù

> Davos sat beside his candle and looked at the letters he had scratched out word by word during the days of his confinement. I was a better smuggler than a knight, he had written to his wife, a better knight than a King‚Äôs Hand, a better King‚Äôs Hand than a husband. I am so sorry. Marya, I have loved you. Please forgive the wrongs I did you. Should Stannis lose his war, our lands will be lost as well. Take the boys across the narrow sea to Braavos and teach them to think kindly of me, if you would. Should Stannis gain the Iron Throne, House Seaworth will survive and Devan will remain at court. He will help you place the other boys with noble lords, where they can serve as pages and squires and win their knighthoods. It was the best counsel he had for her, though he wished it sounded wiser. He had written to each of his three surviving sons as well, to help them remember the father who had bought them names with his fingertips. His notes to Steffon and young Stannis were short and stiff and awkward; if truth be told, he did not know them half as well as he had his older boys, the ones who‚Äôd burned or drowned upon the Blackwater. To Devan he wrote more, telling him how proud he was to see his own son as a king‚Äôs squire and reminding him that as the eldest it was his duty to protect his lady mother and his younger brothers. Tell His Grace I did my best, he ended. I am sorry that I failed him. I lost my luck when I lost my fingerbones, the day the river burned below King‚Äôs Landing. Davos shuffled through the letters slowly, reading each one over several times, wondering whether he should change a word here or add one there. A man should have more to say when staring at the end of his life, he thought, but the words came hard. I did not do so ill, he tried to tell himself. I rose up from Flea Bottom to be a King‚Äôs Hand, and I learned to read and write.

> His fellow drinkers were talking about dragons now. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôre bloody mad,‚Äù said an oarsman off Storm Dancer. ‚ÄúThe Beggar King‚Äôs been dead for years. Some Dothraki horselord cut his head off.‚Äù ‚ÄúSo they tell us,‚Äù said the old fellow. ‚ÄúMight be they‚Äôre lying, though. He died half a world away, if he died at all. Who‚Äôs to say? If a king wanted me dead, might be I‚Äôd oblige him and pretend to be a corpse. None of us has ever seen his body.‚Äù ‚ÄúI never saw Joffrey‚Äôs corpse, nor Robert‚Äôs,‚Äù growled the Eel‚Äôs proprietor. ‚ÄúMaybe they‚Äôre all alive as well. Maybe Baelor the Blessed‚Äôs just been having him a little nap all these years.‚Äù The old fellow made a face. ‚ÄúPrince Viserys weren‚Äôt the only dragon, were he? Are we sure they killed Prince Rhaegar‚Äôs son? A babe, he was.‚Äù ‚ÄúWasn‚Äôt there some princess too?‚Äù asked a whore. She was the same one who‚Äôd said the meat was grey. ‚ÄúTwo,‚Äù said the old fellow. ‚ÄúOne was Rhaegar‚Äôs daughter, t‚Äôother was his sister.‚Äù ‚ÄúDaena,‚Äù said the riverman. ‚ÄúThat was the sister. Daena of Dragonstone. Or was it Daera?‚Äù ‚ÄúDaena was old King Baelor‚Äôs wife,‚Äù said the oarsman. ‚ÄúI rowed on a ship named for her once. The Princess Daena.‚Äù ‚ÄúIf she was a king‚Äôs wife, she‚Äôd be a queen.‚Äù ‚ÄúBaelor never had a queen. He was holy.‚Äù ‚ÄúDon‚Äôt mean he never wed his sister,‚Äù said the whore. ‚ÄúHe just never bedded her, is all. When they made him king, he locked her up in a tower. His other sisters too. There was three.‚Äù ‚ÄúDaenela,‚Äù the proprietor said loudly. ‚ÄúThat was her name. The Mad King‚Äôs daughter, I mean, not Baelor‚Äôs bloody wife.‚Äù ‚ÄúDaenerys,‚Äù Davos said. ‚ÄúShe was named for the Daenerys who wed the Prince of Dorne during the reign of Daeron the Second. I don‚Äôt know what became of her.‚Äù ‚ÄúI do,‚Äù said the man who‚Äôd started all the talk of dragons, a Braavosi oarsman in a somber woolen jack. ‚ÄúWhen we were down to Pentos we moored beside a trader called the Sloe-Eyed Maid, and I got to drinking with her captain‚Äôs steward. He told me a pretty tale about some slip of a girl who come aboard in Qarth, to try and book passage back to Westeros for her and three dragons. Silver hair she had, and purple eyes. ‚ÄòI took her to the captain my own self,‚Äô this steward swore to me, ‚Äòbut he wasn‚Äôt having none of that. There‚Äôs more profit in cloves and saffron, he tells me, and spices won‚Äôt set fire to your sails.‚Äô ‚Äù Laughter swept the cellar. Davos did not join in. He knew what had befallen the Sloe-Eyed Maid. The gods were cruel to let a man sail across half the world, then send him chasing a false light when he was almost home. That captain was a bolder man than me, he thought, as he made his way to the door. One voyage to the east, and a man could live as rich as a lord until the end of his days. When he‚Äôd been younger, Davos had dreamed of making such voyages himself, but the years went dancing by like moths around a flame, and somehow the time had never been quite right. One day, he told himself. One day when the war is done and King Stannis sits the Iron Throne and has no more need of onion knights. I‚Äôll take Devan with me. Steff and Stanny too if they‚Äôre old enough. We‚Äôll see these dragons and all the wonders of the world.

{:#Tycho-Nestoris}
**Tycho Nestoris**

> ‚ÄúAnd here we have the honorable Tycho Nestoris, an emissary of the Iron Bank of Braavos, come to treat with His Grace King Stannis.‚Äù The banker doffed his hat and made a sweeping bow. ‚ÄúLord Commander. I thank you and your brothers for your hospitality.‚Äù He spoke the Common Tongue flawlessly, with only the slightest hint of accent. Half a foot taller than Jon, the Braavosi sported a beard as thin as a rope sprouting from his chin and reaching almost to his waist. His robes were a somber purple, trimmed with ermine. A high stiff collar framed his narrow face. ‚ÄúI hope we shall not inconvenience you too greatly.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúLord Tycho,‚Äù Jon called. ‚ÄúA moment, please.‚Äù The Braavosi halted. ‚ÄúNo lord I. Only a simple servant of the Iron Bank of Braavos.‚Äù ‚ÄúCotter Pyke informs me that you came to Eastwatch with three ships. A galleas, a galley, and a cog.‚Äù ‚ÄúJust so, my lord. The crossing can be perilous in this season. One ship alone may founder, where three together may aid one another. The Iron Bank is always prudent in such matters.‚Äù ‚ÄúPerhaps before you leave we might have a quiet word?‚Äù ‚ÄúI am at your service, Lord Commander. And in Braavos we say there is no time like the present. Will that suit?‚Äù ‚ÄúAs good as any. Shall we repair to my solar, or would you like to see the top of the Wall?‚Äù The banker glanced up, to where the ice loomed vast and pale against the sky. ‚ÄúI fear it will be bitter cold up top.‚Äù ‚ÄúThat, and windy. You learn to walk well away from the edge. Men have been blown off. Still. The Wall is like nothing else on earth. You may never have another chance to see it.‚Äù ‚ÄúNo doubt I shall rue my caution upon my deathbed, but after a long day in the saddle, a warm room sounds preferable to me.‚Äù ‚ÄúMy solar, then. Satin, some mulled wine, if you would.‚Äù Jon‚Äôs rooms behind the armory were quiet enough, if not especially warm. His fire had gone out some time ago; Satin was not as diligent in feeding it as Dolorous Edd had been. Mormont‚Äôs raven greeted them with a shriek of ‚ÄúCorn!‚Äù Jon hung up his cloak. ‚ÄúYou come seeking Stannis, is that correct?‚Äù ‚ÄúIt is, my lord. Queen Selyse has suggested that we might send word to Deepwood Motte by raven, to inform His Grace that I await his pleasure at the Nightfort. The matter that I mean to put to him is too delicate to entrust to letters.‚Äù ‚ÄúA debt.‚Äù What else could it be? ‚ÄúHis own debt? Or his brother‚Äôs?‚Äù The banker pressed his fingers together. ‚ÄúIt would not be proper for me to discuss Lord Stannis‚Äôs indebtedness or lack of same. As to King Robert¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýit was indeed our pleasure to assist His Grace in his need. For so long as Robert lived, all was well. Now, however, the Iron Throne has ceased all repayment.‚Äù Could the Lannisters truly be so foolish? ‚ÄúYou cannot mean to hold Stannis responsible for his brother‚Äôs debts.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe debts belong to the Iron Throne,‚Äù Tycho declared, ‚Äúand whosoever sits on that chair must pay them. Since young King Tommen and his councillors have become so obdurate, we mean to broach the subject with King Stannis. Should he prove himself more worthy of our trust, it would of course be our great pleasure to lend him whatever help he needs.‚Äù ‚ÄúHelp,‚Äù the raven screamed. ‚ÄúHelp, help, help.‚Äù Much of this Jon had surmised the moment he learned that the Iron Bank had sent an envoy to the Wall. ‚ÄúWhen last we heard, His Grace was marching on Winterfell to confront Lord Bolton and his allies. You may seek him there if you wish, though that carries a risk. You could find yourself caught up in his war.‚Äù Tycho bowed his head. ‚ÄúWe who serve the Iron Bank face death full as often as you who serve the Iron Throne.‚Äù

> It took the better part of an hour before the impossible became possible, and another hour before they could agree on terms. The flagon of mulled wine that Satin delivered helped them settle the more nettlesome points. By the time Jon Snow signed the parchment the Braavosi drew up, both of them were half-drunk and quite unhappy. Jon thought that a good sign.

> Tycho Nestoris had left behind a copy of their agreement. Jon read it over thrice. That was simple, he reflected. Simpler than I dared hope. Simpler than it should have been. It gave him an uneasy feeling. Braavosi coin would allow the Night‚Äôs Watch to buy food from the south when their own stores ran short, food enough to see them through the winter, however long it might prove to be. A long hard winter will leave the Watch so deep in debt that we will never climb out, Jon reminded himself, but when the choice is debt or death, best borrow. He did not have to like it, though. And come spring, when the time came to repay all that gold, he would like it even less. Tycho Nestoris had impressed him as cultured and courteous, but the Iron Bank of Braavos had a fearsome reputation when collecting debts. Each of the Nine Free Cities had its bank, and some had more than one, fighting over every coin like dogs over a bone, but the Iron Bank was richer and more powerful than all the rest combined. When princes defaulted on their debts to lesser banks, ruined bankers sold their wives and children into slavery and opened their own veins. When princes failed to repay the Iron Bank, new princes sprang up from nowhere and took their thrones. As poor plump Tommen may be about to learn. No doubt the Lannisters had good reason for refusing to honor King Robert‚Äôs debts, but it was folly all the same. If Stannis was not too stiff-necked to accept their terms, the Braavosi would give him all the gold and silver he required, coin enough to buy a dozen sellsword companies, to bribe a hundred lords, to keep his men paid, fed, clothed, and armed. Unless Stannis is lying dead beneath the walls of Winterfell, he may just have won the Iron Throne. He wondered if Melisandre had seen that in her fires.

{:#Cotter-Pyke}
**Cotter Pyke**

> ‚ÄúWhere is the Old Bear?‚Äù ‚ÄúJon¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýit grieves me to say, but Lord Commander Mormont was murdered at Craster‚Äôs Keep, at the hands of his Sworn Brothers.‚Äù ‚ÄúBro¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýour own men?‚Äù Aemon‚Äôs words hurt a hundred times worse than his fingers. Jon remembered the Old Bear as last he‚Äôd seen him, standing before his tent with his raven on his arm croaking for corn. Mormont gone? He had feared it ever since he‚Äôd seen the aftermath of battle on the Fist, yet it was no less a blow. ‚ÄúWho was it? Who turned on him?‚Äù ‚ÄúGarth of Oldtown, Ollo Lophand, Dirk¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthieves, cowards and killers, the lot of them. We should have seen it coming. The Watch is not what it was. Too few honest men to keep the rogues in line.‚Äù Donal Noye turned the maester‚Äôs blades in the fire. ‚ÄúA dozen true men made it back. Dolorous Edd, Giant, your friend the Aurochs. We had the tale from them.‚Äù Only a dozen? Two hundred men had left Castle Black with Lord Commander Mormont, two hundred of the Watch‚Äôs best. ‚ÄúDoes this mean Marsh is Lord Commander, then?‚Äù The Old Pomegranate was amiable, and a diligent First Steward, but he was woefully ill-suited to face a wildling host. ‚ÄúFor the nonce, until we can hold a choosing,‚Äù said Maester Aemon. ‚ÄúClydas, bring me the flask.‚Äù A choosing. With Qhorin Halfhand and Ser Jaremy Rykker both dead and Ben Stark still missing, who was there? Not Bowen Marsh or Ser Wynton Stout, that was certain. Had Thoren Smallwood survived the Fist, or Ser Ottyn Wythers? No, it will be Cotter Pyke or Ser Denys Mallister. Which, though? The commanders at the Shadow Tower and Eastwatch were good men, but very different; Ser Denys courtly and cautious, as chivalrous as he was elderly, Pyke younger, bastard-born, rough-tongued, and bold to a fault. Worse, the two men despised each other. The Old Bear had always kept them far apart, at opposite ends of the Wall. The Mallisters had a bone-deep mistrust of the ironborn, Jon knew.

> Cotter Pyke was the scarier of the two commanders, so Sam went to him first, while his courage was still hot. He found him in the old Shieldhall, dicing with three of his Eastwatch men and a red-headed sergeant who had come from Dragonstone with Stannis. When Sam begged leave to speak with him, though, Pyke barked an order, and the others took the dice and coins and left them. No man would ever call Cotter Pyke handsome, though the body under his studded brigantine and roughspun breeches was lean and hard and wiry strong. His eyes were small and close-set, his nose broken, his widow‚Äôs peak as sharply pointed as the head of a spear. The pox had ravaged his face badly, and the beard he‚Äôd grown to hide the scars was thin and scraggly. ‚ÄúSam the Slayer!‚Äù he said, by way of greeting. ‚ÄúAre you sure you stabbed an Other, and not some child‚Äôs snow knight?‚Äù This isn‚Äôt starting well. ‚ÄúIt was the dragonglass that killed it, my lord,‚Äù Sam explained feebly. ‚ÄúAye, no doubt. Well, out with it, Slayer. Did the maester send you to me?‚Äù ‚ÄúThe maester?‚Äù Sam swallowed. ‚ÄúI¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝI just left him, my lord.‚Äù That wasn‚Äôt truly a lie, but if Pyke chose to read it wrong, it might make him more inclined to listen. Sam took a deep breath and launched into his plea. Pyke cut him off before he‚Äôd said twenty words. ‚ÄúYou want me to kneel down and kiss the hem of Mallister‚Äôs pretty cloak, is that it? I might have known. You lordlings all flock like sheep. Well, tell Aemon that he‚Äôs wasted your breath and my time. If anyone withdraws it should be Mallister. The man‚Äôs too bloody old for the job, maybe you ought to go tell him that. We choose him, and we‚Äôre like to be back here in a year, choosing someone else.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe‚Äôs old,‚Äù Sam agreed, ‚Äúbut he‚Äôs well ex-experienced.‚Äù ‚ÄúAt sitting in his tower and fussing over maps, maybe. What does he plan to do, write letters to the wights? He‚Äôs a knight, well and good, but he‚Äôs not a fighter, and I don‚Äôt give a kettle of piss who he unhorsed in some fool tourney fifty years ago. The Halfhand fought all his battles, even an old blind man should see that. And we need a fighter more than ever with this bloody king on top of us. Today it‚Äôs ruins and empty fields, well and good, but what will His Grace want come the morrow? You think Mallister has the belly to stand up to Stannis Baratheon and that red bitch?‚Äù He laughed. ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou won‚Äôt support him, then?‚Äù said Sam, dismayed. ‚ÄúAre you Sam the Slayer or Deaf Dick? No, I won‚Äôt support him.‚Äù Pyke jabbed a finger at his face. ‚ÄúUnderstand this, boy. I don‚Äôt want the bloody job, and never did. I fight best with a deck beneath me, not a horse, and Castle Black is too far from the sea. But I‚Äôll be buggered with a red-hot sword before I turn the Night‚Äôs Watch over to that preening eagle from the Shadow Tower. And you can run back to the old man and tell him I said so, if he asks.‚Äù He stood. ‚ÄúGet out of my sight.‚Äù It took all the courage Sam had left in him to say, ‚ÄúW-what if there was someone else? Could you s-support someone else?‚Äù ‚ÄúWho? Bowen Marsh? The man counts spoons. Othell‚Äôs a follower, does what he‚Äôs told and does it well, but no more‚Äôn that. Slynt¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýwell, his men like him, I‚Äôll grant you, and it would almost be worth it to stick him down the royal craw and see if Stannis gagged, but no. There‚Äôs too much of King‚Äôs Landing in that one. A toad grows wings and thinks he‚Äôs a bloody dragon.‚Äù Pyke laughed. ‚ÄúWho does that leave, Hobb? We could pick him, I suppose, only then who‚Äôs going to boil your mutton, Slayer? You look like a man who likes his bloody mutton.‚Äù

{:#Denys-Mallister}
**Denys Mallister**

> The commander of the Shadow Tower had been born beneath the Booming Tower of Seagard, and looked every inch a Mallister. Sable trimmed his collar and accented the sleeves of his black velvet doublet. A silver eagle fastened its claws in the gathered folds of his cloak. His beard was white as snow, his hair was largely gone, and his face was deeply lined, it was true. Yet he still had grace in his movements and teeth in his mouth, and the years had dimmed neither his blue-grey eyes nor his courtesy. ‚ÄúMy lord of Tarly,‚Äù he said, when his steward brought Sam to him in the Lance, where the Shadow Tower men were staying. ‚ÄúI am pleased to see that you‚Äôve recovered from your ordeal. Might I offer you a cup of wine? Your lady mother is a Florent, I recall. One day I must tell you about the time I unhorsed both of your grandfathers in the same tourney. Not today, though, I know we have more pressing concerns. You come from Maester Aemon, to be sure. Does he have counsel to offer me?‚Äù Sam took a sip of wine, and chose his words with care. ‚ÄúA maester chained and sworn¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýit would not be proper for him to be seen as having influenced the choice of Lord Commander¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù The old knight smiled. ‚ÄúWhich is why he has not come to me himself. Yes, I quite understand, Samwell. Aemon and I are both old men, and wise in such matters. Say what you came to say.‚Äù The wine was sweet, and Ser Denys listened to Sam‚Äôs plea with grave courtesy, unlike Cotter Pyke. But when he was done, the old knight shook his head. ‚ÄúI agree that it would be a dark day in our history if a king were to name our Lord Commander. This king especially. He is not like to keep his crown for long. But truly, Samwell, it ought to be Pyke who withdraws. I have more support than he does, and I am better suited to the office.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou are,‚Äù Sam agreed, ‚Äúbut Cotter Pyke might serve. It‚Äôs said he has oft proved himself in battle.‚Äù He did not mean to offend Ser Denys by praising his rival, but how else could he convince him to withdraw? ‚ÄúMany of my brothers have proved themselves in battle. It is not enough. Some matters cannot be settled with a battleaxe. Maester Aemon will understand that, though Cotter Pyke does not. The Lord Commander of the Night‚Äôs Watch is a lord, first and foremost. He must be able to treat with other lords¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand with kings as well. He must be a man worthy of respect.‚Äù Ser Denys leaned forward. ‚ÄúWe are the sons of great lords, you and I. We know the importance of birth, blood, and that early training that can ne‚Äôer be replaced. I was a squire at twelve, a knight at eighteen, a champion at two-and-twenty. I have been the commander at the Shadow Tower for thirty-three years. Blood, birth, and training have fitted me to deal with kings. Pyke¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýwell, did you hear him this morning, asking if His Grace would wipe his bottom? Samwell, it is not my habit to speak unkindly of my brothers, but let us be frank¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthe ironborn are a race of pirates and thieves, and Cotter Pyke was raping and murdering when he was still half a boy. Maester Harmune reads and writes his letters, and has for years. No, loath as I am to disappoint Maester Aemon, I could not in honor stand aside for Pyke of Eastwatch.‚Äù

{:#Jon-Snow}
**Jon Snow**

> Noye drew him closer. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôve heard these tidings of your brother?‚Äù ‚ÄúLast night.‚Äù Conwy and his charges had brought the news north with them, and the talk in the common room had been of little else. Jon was still not certain how he felt about it. Robb a king? The brother he‚Äôd played with, fought with, shared his first cup of wine with? But not mother‚Äôs milk, no. So now Robb will sip summerwine from jeweled goblets, while I‚Äôm kneeling beside some stream sucking snowmelt from cupped hands. ‚ÄúRobb will make a good king,‚Äù he said loyally.

> ‚ÄúI‚Äôve always known that Robb would be Lord of Winterfell.‚Äù Mormont gave a whistle, and the bird flew to him again and settled on his arm. ‚ÄúA lord‚Äôs one thing, a king‚Äôs another.‚Äù He offered the raven a handful of corn from his pocket. ‚ÄúThey will garb your brother Robb in silks, satins, and velvets of a hundred different colors, while you live and die in black ringmail. He will wed some beautiful princess and father sons on her. You‚Äôll have no wife, nor will you ever hold a child of your own blood in your arms. Robb will rule, you will serve. Men will call you a crow. Him they‚Äôll call Your Grace. Singers will praise every little thing he does, while your greatest deeds all go unsung. Tell me that none of this troubles you, Jon¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand I‚Äôll name you a liar, and know I have the truth of it.‚Äù Jon drew himself up, taut as a bowstring. ‚ÄúAnd if it did trouble me, what might I do, bastard as I am?‚Äù ‚ÄúWhat will you do?‚Äù Mormont asked. ‚ÄúBastard as you are?‚Äù ‚ÄúBe troubled,‚Äù said Jon, ‚Äúand keep my vows.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúWe‚Äôll hear no scolds from you, bastard.‚Äù Chett blamed Jon for the loss of his comfortable position with Maester Aemon, and not without justice. If he had not gone to Aemon about Sam Tarly, Chett would still be tending an old blind man instead of a pack of ill-tempered hunting hounds. ‚ÄúYou may be the Lord Commander‚Äôs pet, but you‚Äôre not the Lord Commander¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand you wouldn‚Äôt talk so bloody bold without that monster of yours always about.‚Äù ‚ÄúI‚Äôll not fight a brother while we‚Äôre beyond the Wall,‚Äù Jon answered, his voice cooler than he felt. Lark got to one knee. ‚ÄúHe‚Äôs afraid of you, Chett. On the Sisters, we have a name for them like him.‚Äù ‚ÄúI know all the names. Save your breath.‚Äù He walked away, Ghost at his side.

> He woke to the sight of his own breath misting in the cold morning air. When he moved, his bones ached. Ghost was gone, the fire burnt out. Jon reached to pull aside the cloak he‚Äôd hung over the rock, and found it stiff and frozen. He crept beneath it and stood up in a forest turned to crystal. The pale pink light of dawn sparkled on branch and leaf and stone. Every blade of grass was carved from emerald, every drip of water turned to diamond. Flowers and mushrooms alike wore coats of glass. Even the mud puddles had a bright brown sheen. Through the shimmering greenery, the black tents of his brothers were encased in a fine glaze of ice. So there is magic beyond the Wall after all. He found himself thinking of his sisters, perhaps because he‚Äôd dreamed of them last night. Sansa would call this an enchantment, and tears would fill her eyes at the wonder of it, but Arya would run out laughing and shouting, wanting to touch it all.

> ‚ÄúCraster is his own man. He has sworn us no vows. Nor is he subject to our laws. Your heart is noble, Jon, but learn a lesson here. We cannot set the world to rights. That is not our purpose. The Night‚Äôs Watch has other wars to fight.‚Äù Other wars. Yes. I must remember.

> Sam, you‚Äôre a sweet fool, he could hear Jon saying, all the way back to the maester‚Äôs keep. Open your eyes. It‚Äôs been happening for days. Could he be right? A man needed the votes of two-thirds of the Sworn Brothers to become the Lord Commander of the Night‚Äôs Watch, and after nine days and nine votes no one was even close to that. Lord Janos had been gaining, true, creeping up past first Bowen Marsh and then Othell Yarwyck, but he was still well behind Ser Denys Mallister of the Shadow Tower and Cotter Pyke of Eastwatch-by-the-Sea. One of them will be the new Lord Commander, surely, Sam told himself.

> It was a good hour before he could excuse himself to feed the ravens. On the way up to the rookery, he stopped to check the tally he had made of last night‚Äôs count. At the start of the choosing, more than thirty names had been offered, but most had withdrawn once it became clear they could not win. Seven remained as of last night. Ser Denys Mallister had collected two hundred and thirteen tokens, Cotter Pyke one hundred and eighty-seven, Lord Slynt seventy-four, Othell Yarwyck sixty, Bowen Marsh forty-nine, Three-Finger Hobb five, and Dolorous Edd Tollett one. Pyp and his stupid japes. Sam shuffled through the earlier counts. Ser Denys, Cotter Pyke, and Bowen Marsh had all been falling since the third day, Othell Yarwyck since the sixth. Only Lord Janos Slynt was climbing, day after day after day.

> ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs another man,‚Äù Sam blurted out. ‚ÄúLord Commander Mormont trusted him. So did Donal Noye and Qhorin Halfhand. Though he‚Äôs not as highly born as you, he comes from old blood. He was castle-born and castle-raised, and he learned sword and lance from a knight and letters from a maester of the Citadel. His father was a lord, and his brother a king.‚Äù Ser Denys stroked his long white beard. ‚ÄúMayhaps,‚Äù he said, after a long moment. ‚ÄúHe is very young, but¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýmayhaps. He might serve, I grant you, though I would be more suitable. I have no doubt of that. I would be the wiser choice.‚Äù Jon said there could be honor in a lie, if it were told for the right reason. Sam said, ‚ÄúIf we do not choose a Lord Commander tonight, King Stannis means to name Cotter Pyke. He said as much to Maester Aemon this morning, after all of you had left.‚Äù ‚ÄúI see.‚Äù Ser Denys rose. ‚ÄúI must think on this. Thank you, Samwell. And give my thanks to Maester Aemon as well.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúYou won‚Äôt withdraw for Ser Denys, you said, but you might for someone else.‚Äù ‚ÄúWho is it this time, Slayer? You?‚Äù ‚ÄúNo. A fighter. Donal Noye gave him the Wall when the wildlings came, and he was the Old Bear‚Äôs squire. The only thing is, he‚Äôs bastard-born.‚Äù Cotter Pyke laughed. ‚ÄúBloody hell. That would shove a spear up Mallister‚Äôs arse, wouldn‚Äôt it? Might be worth it just for that. How bad could the boy be?‚Äù He snorted. ‚ÄúI‚Äôd be better, though. I‚Äôm what‚Äôs needed, any fool can see that.‚Äù ‚ÄúAny fool,‚Äù Sam agreed, ‚Äúeven me. But¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýwell, I shouldn‚Äôt be telling you, but¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝKing Stannis means to force Ser Denys on us, if we do not choose a man tonight. I heard him tell Maester Aemon that, after the rest of you were sent away.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúI know you held the gate here,‚Äù King Stannis said. ‚ÄúIf not, I would have come too late.‚Äù ‚ÄúDonal Noye held the gate. He died below in the tunnel, fighting the king of the giants.‚Äù Stannis grimaced. ‚ÄúNoye made my first sword for me, and Robert‚Äôs warhammer as well. Had the god seen fit to spare him, he would have made a better Lord Commander for your order than any of these fools who are squabbling over it now.‚Äù ‚ÄúCotter Pyke and Ser Denys Mallister are no fools, sire,‚Äù Jon said. ‚ÄúThey‚Äôre good men, and capable. Othell Yarwyck as well, in his own way. Lord Mormont trusted each of them.‚Äù ‚ÄúYour Lord Mormont trusted too easily. Else he would not have died as he did. But we were speaking of you. I have not forgotten that it was you who brought us this magic horn, and captured Mance Rayder‚Äôs wife and son.‚Äù ‚ÄúDalla died.‚Äù Jon was saddened by that still. ‚ÄúVal is her sister. She and the babe did not require much capturing, Your Grace. You had put the wildlings to flight, and the skinchanger Mance had left to guard his queen went mad when the eagle burned.‚Äù Jon looked at Melisandre. ‚ÄúSome say that was your doing.‚Äù She smiled, her long copper hair tumbling across her face. ‚ÄúThe Lord of Light has fiery talons, Jon Snow.‚Äù Jon nodded, and turned back to the king. ‚ÄúYour Grace, you spoke of Val. She has asked to see Mance Rayder, to bring his son to him. It would be a¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýa kindness.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe man is a deserter from your order. Your brothers are all insisting on his death. Why should I do him a kindness?‚Äù Jon had no answer for that. ‚ÄúIf not for him, for Val. For her sister‚Äôs sake, the child‚Äôs mother.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou are fond of this Val?‚Äù ‚ÄúI scarcely know her.‚Äù ‚ÄúThey tell me she is comely.‚Äù ‚ÄúVery,‚Äù Jon admitted. ‚ÄúBeauty can be treacherous. My brother learned that lesson from Cersei Lannister. She murdered him, do not doubt it. Your father and Jon Arryn as well.‚Äù He scowled. ‚ÄúYou rode with these wildlings. Is there any honor in them, do you think?‚Äù ‚ÄúYes,‚Äù Jon said, ‚Äúbut their own sort of honor, sire.‚Äù ‚ÄúIn Mance Rayder?‚Äù ‚ÄúYes. I think so.‚Äù ‚ÄúIn the Lord of Bones?‚Äù Jon hesitated. ‚ÄúRattleshirt, we called him. Treacherous and blood-thirsty. If there‚Äôs honor in him, he hides it down beneath his suit of bones.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd this other man, this Tormund of the many names who eluded us after the battle? Answer me truly.‚Äù ‚ÄúTormund Giantsbane seemed to me the sort of man who would make a good friend and a bad enemy, Your Grace.‚Äù Stannis gave a curt nod. ‚ÄúYour father was a man of honor. He was no friend to me, but I saw his worth. Your brother was a rebel and a traitor who meant to steal half my kingdom, but no man can question his courage. What of you?‚Äù Does he want me to say I love him? Jon‚Äôs voice was stiff and formal as he said, ‚ÄúI am a man of the Night‚Äôs Watch.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúWords. Words are wind. Why do you think I abandoned Dragonstone and sailed to the Wall, Lord Snow?‚Äù ‚ÄúI am no lord, sire. You came because we sent for you, I hope. Though I could not say why you took so long about it.‚Äù Surprisingly, Stannis smiled at that. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôre bold enough to be a Stark. Yes, I should have come sooner. If not for my Hand, I might not have come at all. Lord Seaworth is a man of humble birth, but he reminded me of my duty, when all I could think of was my rights. I had the cart before the horse, Davos said. I was trying to win the throne to save the kingdom, when I should have been trying to save the kingdom to win the throne.‚Äù Stannis pointed north. ‚ÄúThere is where I‚Äôll find the foe that I was born to fight.‚Äù ‚ÄúHis name may not be spoken,‚Äù Melisandre added softly. ‚ÄúHe is the God of Night and Terror, Jon Snow, and these shapes in the snow are his creatures.‚Äù ‚ÄúThey tell me that you slew one of these walking corpses to save Lord Mormont‚Äôs life,‚Äù Stannis said. ‚ÄúIt may be that this is your war as well, Lord Snow. If you will give me your help.‚Äù ‚ÄúMy sword is pledged to the Night‚Äôs Watch, Your Grace,‚Äù Jon Snow answered carefully. That did not please the king. Stannis ground his teeth and said, ‚ÄúI need more than a sword from you.‚Äù Jon was lost. ‚ÄúMy lord?‚Äù ‚ÄúI need the north.‚Äù The north. ‚ÄúI¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýmy brother Robb was King in the North¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúYour brother was the rightful Lord of Winterfell. If he had stayed home and done his duty, instead of crowning himself and riding off to conquer the riverlands, he might be alive today. Be that as it may. You are not Robb, no more than I am Robert.‚Äù The harsh words had blown away whatever sympathy Jon might have had for Stannis. ‚ÄúI loved my brother,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúAnd I mine. Yet they were what they were, and so are we. I am the only true king in Westeros, north or south. And you are Ned Stark‚Äôs bastard.‚Äù Stannis studied him with those dark blue eyes. ‚ÄúTywin Lannister has named Roose Bolton his Warden of the North, to reward him for betraying your brother. The ironmen are fighting amongst themselves since Balon Greyjoy‚Äôs death, yet they still hold Moat Cailin, Deepwood Motte, Torrhen‚Äôs Square, and most of the Stony Shore. Your father‚Äôs lands are bleeding, and I have neither the strength nor the time to stanch the wounds. What is needed is a Lord of Winterfell. A loyal Lord of Winterfell.‚Äù He is looking at me, Jon thought, stunned. ‚ÄúWinterfell is no more. Theon Greyjoy put it to the torch.‚Äù ‚ÄúGranite does not burn easily,‚Äù Stannis said. ‚ÄúThe castle can be rebuilt, in time. It‚Äôs not the walls that make a lord, it‚Äôs the man. Your northmen do not know me, have no reason to love me, yet I will need their strength in the battles yet to come. I need a son of Eddard Stark to win them to my banner.‚Äù He would make me Lord of Winterfell. The wind was gusting, and Jon felt so light-headed he was half afraid it would blow him off the Wall.

> When Jon had been very young, too young to understand what it meant to be a bastard, he used to dream that one day Winterfell might be his. Later, when he was older, he had been ashamed of those dreams. Winterfell would go to Robb and then his sons, or to Bran or Rickon should Robb die childless. And after them came Sansa and Arya. Even to dream otherwise seemed disloyal, as if he were betraying them in his heart, wishing for their deaths. I never wanted this, he thought as he stood before the blue-eyed king and the red woman. I loved Robb, loved all of them¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝI never wanted any harm to come to any of them, but it did. And now there‚Äôs only me. All he had to do was say the word, and he would be Jon Stark, and nevermore a Snow. All he had to do was pledge this king his fealty, and Winterfell was his. All he had to do¬Ý‚Ä¶ ‚Ä¶ was forswear his vows again. And this time it would not be a ruse. To claim his father‚Äôs castle, he must turn against his father‚Äôs gods. King Stannis gazed off north again, his gold cloak streaming from his shoulders. ‚ÄúIt may be that I am mistaken in you, Jon Snow. We both know the things that are said of bastards. You may lack your father‚Äôs honor, or your brother‚Äôs skill in arms. But you are the weapon the Lord has given me. I have found you here, as you found the cache of dragonglass beneath the Fist, and I mean to make use of you. Even Azor Ahai did not win his war alone. I killed a thousand wildlings, took another thousand captive, and scattered the rest, but we both know they will return. Melisandre has seen that in her fires. This Tormund Thunderfist is likely re-forming them even now, and planning some new assault. And the more we bleed each other, the weaker we shall all be when the real enemy falls upon us.‚Äù Jon had come to that same realization. ‚ÄúAs you say, Your Grace.‚Äù He wondered where this king was going. ‚ÄúWhilst your brothers have been struggling to decide who shall lead them, I have been speaking with this Mance Rayder.‚Äù He ground his teeth. ‚ÄúA stubborn man, that one, and prideful. He will leave me no choice but to give him to the flames. But we took other captives as well, other leaders. The one who calls himself the Lord of Bones, some of their clan chiefs, the new Magnar of Thenn. Your brothers will not like it, no more than your father‚Äôs lords, but I mean to allow the wildlings through the Wall¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthose who will swear me their fealty, pledge to keep the king‚Äôs peace and the king‚Äôs laws, and take the Lord of Light as their god. Even the giants, if those great knees of theirs can bend. I will settle them on the Gift, once I have wrested it away from your new Lord Commander. When the cold winds rise, we shall live or die together. It is time we made alliance against our common foe.‚Äù He looked at Jon. ‚ÄúWould you agree?‚Äù ‚ÄúMy father dreamed of resettling the Gift,‚Äù Jon admitted. ‚ÄúHe and my uncle Benjen used to talk of it.‚Äù He never thought of settling it with wildlings, though¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut he never rode with wildlings, either. He did not fool himself; the free folk would make for unruly subjects and dangerous neighbors. Yet when he weighed Ygritte‚Äôs red hair against the cold blue eyes of the wights, the choice was easy. ‚ÄúI agree.‚Äù ‚ÄúGood,‚Äù King Stannis said, ‚Äúfor the surest way to seal a new alliance is with a marriage. I mean to wed my Lord of Winterfell to this wildling princess.‚Äù Perhaps Jon had ridden with the free folk too long; he could not help but laugh. ‚ÄúYour Grace,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúcaptive or no, if you think you can just give Val to me, I fear you have a deal to learn about wildling women. Whoever weds her had best be prepared to climb in her tower window and carry her off at swordpoint¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúWhoever?‚Äù Stannis gave him a measuring look. ‚ÄúDoes this mean you will not wed the girl? I warn you, she is part of the price you must pay, if you want your father‚Äôs name and your father‚Äôs castle. This match is necessary, to help assure the loyalty of our new subjects. Are you refusing me, Jon Snow?‚Äù ‚ÄúNo,‚Äù Jon said, too quickly. It was Winterfell the king was speaking of, and Winterfell was not to be lightly refused. ‚ÄúI mean¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthis has all come very suddenly, Your Grace. Might I beg you for some time to consider?‚Äù ‚ÄúAs you wish. But consider quickly. I am not a patient man, as your black brothers are about to discover.‚Äù Stannis put a thin, fleshless hand on Jon‚Äôs shoulder. ‚ÄúSay nothing of what we‚Äôve discussed here today. To anyone. But when you return, you need only bend your knee, lay your sword at my feet, and pledge yourself to my service, and you shall rise again as Jon Stark, the Lord of Winterfell.‚Äù

> He was almost ready to lower his blade and call a halt when Emmett feinted low and came in over his shield with a savage forehand slash that caught Jon on the temple. He staggered, his helm and head both ringing from the force of the blow. For half a heartbeat the world beyond his eyeslit was a blur. And then the years were gone, and he was back at Winterfell once more, wearing a quilted leather coat in place of mail and plate. His sword was made of wood, and it was Robb who stood facing him, not Iron Emmett. Every morning they had trained together, since they were big enough to walk; Snow and Stark, spinning and slashing about the wards of Winterfell, shouting and laughing, sometimes crying when there was no one else to see. They were not little boys when they fought, but knights and mighty heroes. ‚ÄúI‚Äôm Prince Aemon the Dragonknight,‚Äù Jon would call out, and Robb would shout back, ‚ÄúWell, I‚Äôm Florian the Fool.‚Äù Or Robb would say, ‚ÄúI‚Äôm the Young Dragon,‚Äù and Jon would reply, ‚ÄúI‚Äôm Ser Ryam Redwyne.‚Äù That morning he called it first. ‚ÄúI‚Äôm Lord of Winterfell!‚Äù he cried, as he had a hundred times before. Only this time, this time, Robb had answered, ‚ÄúYou can‚Äôt be Lord of Winterfell, you‚Äôre bastard-born. My lady mother says you can‚Äôt ever be the Lord of Winterfell.‚Äù I thought I had forgotten that. Jon could taste blood in his mouth, from the blow he‚Äôd taken. In the end Halder and Horse had to pull him away from Iron Emmett, one man on either arm. The ranger sat on the ground dazed, his shield half in splinters, the visor of his helm knocked askew, and his sword six yards away. ‚ÄúJon, enough,‚Äù Halder was shouting, ‚Äúhe‚Äôs down, you disarmed him. Enough!‚Äù No. Not enough. Never enough. Jon let his sword drop. ‚ÄúI‚Äôm sorry,‚Äù he muttered. ‚ÄúEmmett, are you hurt?‚Äù Iron Emmett pulled his battered helm off. ‚ÄúWas there some part of yield you could not comprehend, Lord Snow?‚Äù It was said amiably, though. Emmett was an amiable man, and he loved the song of swords. ‚ÄúWarrior defend me,‚Äù he groaned, ‚Äúnow I know how Qhorin Halfhand must have felt.‚Äù That was too much. Jon wrenched free of his friends and retreated to the armory, alone. His ears were still ringing from the blow Emmett had dealt him. He sat on the bench and buried his head in his hands. Why am I so angry? he asked himself, but it was a stupid question. Lord of Winterfell. I could be the Lord of Winterfell. My father‚Äôs heir. It was not Lord Eddard‚Äôs face he saw floating before him, though; it was Lady Catelyn‚Äôs. With her deep blue eyes and hard cold mouth, she looked a bit like Stannis. Iron, he thought, but brittle. She was looking at him the way she used to look at him at Winterfell, whenever he had bested Robb at swords or sums or most anything. Who are you? that look had always seemed to say. This is not your place. Why are you here?

> Ygritte wanted me to be a wildling. Stannis wants me to be the Lord of Winterfell. But what do I want? The sun crept down the sky to dip behind the Wall where it curved through the western hills. Jon watched as that towering expanse of ice took on the reds and pinks of sunset. Would I sooner be hanged for a turncloak by Lord Janos, or forswear my vows, marry Val, and become the Lord of Winterfell? It seemed an easy choice when he thought of it in those terms¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthough if Ygritte had still been alive, it might have been even easier. Val was a stranger to him. She was not hard on the eyes, certainly, and she had been sister to Mance Rayder‚Äôs queen, but still¬Ý‚Ä¶ I would need to steal her if I wanted her love, but she might give me children. I might someday hold a son of my own blood in my arms. A son was something Jon Snow had never dared dream of, since he decided to live his life on the Wall. I could name him Robb. Val would want to keep her sister‚Äôs son, but we could foster him at Winterfell, and Gilly‚Äôs boy as well. Sam would never need to tell his lie. We‚Äôd find a place for Gilly too, and Sam could come visit her once a year or so. Mance‚Äôs son and Craster‚Äôs would grow up brothers, as I once did with Robb. He wanted it, Jon knew then. He wanted it as much as he had ever wanted anything. I have always wanted it, he thought, guiltily. May the gods forgive me. It was a hunger inside him, sharp as a dragonglass blade. A hunger¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýhe could feel it. It was food he needed, prey, a red deer that stank of fear or a great elk proud and defiant. He needed to kill and fill his belly with fresh meat and hot dark blood. His mouth began to water with the thought. It was a long moment before he understood what was happening. When he did, he bolted to his feet. ‚ÄúGhost?‚Äù He turned toward the wood, and there he came, padding silently out of the green dusk, the breath coming warm and white from his open jaws. ‚ÄúGhost!‚Äù he shouted, and the direwolf broke into a run. He was leaner than he had been, but bigger as well, and the only sound he made was the soft crunch of dead leaves beneath his paws. When he reached Jon he leapt, and they wrestled amidst brown grass and long shadows as the stars came out above them. ‚ÄúGods, wolf, where have you been?‚Äù Jon said when Ghost stopped worrying at his forearm. ‚ÄúI thought you‚Äôd died on me, like Robb and Ygritte and all the rest. I‚Äôve had no sense of you, not since I climbed the Wall, not even in dreams.‚Äù The direwolf had no answer, but he licked Jon‚Äôs face with a tongue like a wet rasp, and his eyes caught the last light and shone like two great red suns. Red eyes, Jon realized, but not like Melisandre‚Äôs. He had a weirwood‚Äôs eyes. Red eyes, red mouth, white fur. Blood and bone, like a heart tree. He belongs to the old gods, this one. And he alone of all the direwolves was white. Six pups they‚Äôd found in the late summer snows, him and Robb; five that were grey and black and brown, for the five Starks, and one white, as white as Snow. He had his answer then.

> The Eastwatch man was pounding his fist on the table again, but now he was shouting for the kettle. Some of his friends took up the cry. ‚ÄúKettle!‚Äù they roared, as one. ‚ÄúKettle, kettle, KETTLE!‚Äù The kettle was in the corner by the hearth, a big black potbellied thing with two huge handles and a heavy lid. Maester Aemon said a word to Sam and Clydas and they went and grabbed the handles and dragged the kettle over to the table. A few of the brothers were already queueing up by the token barrels as Clydas took the lid off and almost dropped it on his foot. With a raucous scream and a clap of wings, a huge raven burst out of the kettle. It flapped upward, seeking the rafters perhaps, or a window to make its escape, but there were no rafters in the vault, nor windows either. The raven was trapped. Cawing loudly, it circled the hall, once, twice, three times. And Jon heard Samwell Tarly shout, ‚ÄúI know that bird! That‚Äôs Lord Mormont‚Äôs raven!‚Äù The raven landed on the table nearest Jon. ‚ÄúSnow,‚Äù it cawed. It was an old bird, dirty and bedraggled. ‚ÄúSnow,‚Äù it said again, ‚ÄúSnow, snow, snow.‚Äù It walked to the end of the table, spread its wings again, and flew to Jon‚Äôs shoulder.

> When the count was done, Jon found himself surrounded. Some clapped him on the back, whilst others bent the knee to him as if he were a lord in truth. Satin, Owen the Oaf, Halder, Toad, Spare Boot, Giant, Mully, Ulmer of the Kingswood, Sweet Donnel Hill, and half a hundred more pressed around him. Dywen clacked his wooden teeth and said, ‚ÄúGods be good, our Lord Commander‚Äôs still in swaddling clothes.‚Äù Iron Emmett said, ‚ÄúI hope this don‚Äôt mean I can‚Äôt beat the bloody piss out of you next time we train, my lord.‚Äù Three-Finger Hobb wanted to know if he‚Äôd still be eating with the men, or if he‚Äôd want his meals sent up to his solar. Even Bowen Marsh came up to say he would be glad to continue as Lord Steward if that was Lord Snow‚Äôs wish. ‚ÄúLord Snow,‚Äù said Cotter Pyke, ‚Äúif you muck this up, I‚Äôm going to rip your liver out and eat it raw with onions.‚Äù Ser Denys Mallister was more courteous. ‚ÄúIt was a hard thing young Samwell asked of me,‚Äù the old knight confessed. ‚ÄúWhen Lord Qorgyle was chosen, I told myself, ‚ÄòNo matter, he has been longer on the Wall than you have, your time will come.‚Äô When it was Lord Mormont, I thought, ‚ÄòHe is strong and fierce, but he is old, your time may yet come.‚Äô But you are half a boy, Lord Snow, and now I must return to the Shadow Tower knowing that my time will never come.‚Äù He smiled a tired smile. ‚ÄúDo not make me die regretful. Your uncle was a great man. Your lord father and his father as well. I shall expect full as much of you.‚Äù ‚ÄúAye,‚Äù said Cotter Pyke. ‚ÄúAnd you can start by telling those king‚Äôs men that it‚Äôs done, and we want our bloody supper.‚Äù ‚ÄúSupper,‚Äù screamed the raven. ‚ÄúSupper, supper.‚Äù The king‚Äôs men cleared the door when they told them of the choosing, and Three-Finger Hobb and half a dozen helpers went trotting off to the kitchen to fetch the food. Jon did not wait to eat. He walked across the castle, wondering if he were dreaming, with the raven on his shoulder and Ghost at his heels. Pyp, Grenn, and Sam trailed after him, chattering, but he hardly heard a word until Grenn whispered, ‚ÄúSam did it,‚Äù and Pyp said, ‚ÄúSam did it!‚Äù Pyp had brought a wineskin with him, and he took a long drink and chanted, ‚ÄúSam, Sam, Sam the wizard, Sam the wonder, Sam Sam the marvel man, he did it. But when did you hide the raven in the kettle, Sam, and how in seven hells could you be certain it would fly to Jon? It would have mucked up everything if the bird had decided to perch on Janos Slynt‚Äôs fat head.‚Äù ‚ÄúI had nothing to do with the bird,‚Äù Sam insisted. ‚ÄúWhen it flew out of the kettle I almost wet myself.‚Äù Jon laughed, half amazed that he still remembered how. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôre all a bunch of mad fools, do you know that?‚Äù ‚ÄúUs?‚Äù said Pyp. ‚ÄúYou call us fools? We‚Äôre not the ones who got chosen as the nine-hundredth-and-ninety-eighth Lord Commander of the Night‚Äôs Watch. You best have some wine, Lord Jon. I think you‚Äôre going to need a lot of wine.‚Äù So Jon Snow took the wineskin from his hand and had a swallow. But only one. The Wall was his, the night was dark, and he had a king to face.

> The younger men were gathered at another table, where Pyp had stabbed a turnip with his knife. ‚ÄúThe night is dark and full of turnips,‚Äù he announced in a solemn voice. ‚ÄúLet us all pray for venison, my children, with some onions and a bit of tasty gravy.‚Äù His friends laughed‚ÄîGrenn, Toad, Satin, the whole lot of them. Jon Snow did not join the laughter. ‚ÄúMaking mock of another man‚Äôs prayer is fool‚Äôs work, Pyp. And dangerous.‚Äù ‚ÄúIf the red god‚Äôs offended, let him strike me down.‚Äù All the smiles had died. ‚ÄúIt was the priestess we were laughing at,‚Äù said Satin, a lithe and pretty youth who had once been a whore in Oldtown. ‚ÄúWe were only having a jape, my lord.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou have your gods and she has hers. Leave her be.‚Äù ‚ÄúShe won‚Äôt let our gods be,‚Äù argued Toad. ‚ÄúShe calls the Seven false gods, m‚Äôlord. The old gods too. She made the wildlings burn weirwood branches. You saw.‚Äù ‚ÄúLady Melisandre is not part of my command. You are. I won‚Äôt have bad blood between the king‚Äôs men and my own.‚Äù Pyp laid a hand on Toad‚Äôs arm. ‚ÄúCroak no more, brave Toad, for our Great Lord Snow has spoken.‚Äù Pyp hopped to his feet and gave Jon a mocking bow. ‚ÄúI beg pardon. Henceforth, I shall not even waggle my ears save by your lordship‚Äôs lordly leave.‚Äù He thinks this is all some game. Jon wanted to shake some sense into him. ‚ÄúWaggle your ears all you like. It‚Äôs your tongue waggling that makes the trouble.‚Äù ‚ÄúI‚Äôll see that he‚Äôs more careful,‚Äù Grenn promised, ‚Äúand I‚Äôll clout him if he‚Äôs not.‚Äù He hesitated. ‚ÄúMy lord, will you sup with us? Owen, shove over and make room for Jon.‚Äù Jon wanted nothing more. No, he had to tell himself, those days are gone. The realization twisted in his belly like a knife. They had chosen him to rule. The Wall was his, and their lives were his as well. A lord may love the men that he commands, he could hear his lord father saying, but he cannot be a friend to them. One day he may need to sit in judgment on them, or send them forth to die. ‚ÄúAnother day,‚Äù the lord commander lied. ‚ÄúEdd, best see to your own supper. I have work to finish.‚Äù

> When Gilly entered, she went at once to her knees. Jon came around the table and drew her to her feet. ‚ÄúYou don‚Äôt need to take a knee for me. That‚Äôs just for kings.‚Äù Though a wife and mother, Gilly still seemed half a child to him, a slender little thing wrapped up in one of Sam‚Äôs old cloaks. The cloak was so big on her that she could have hidden several other girls beneath its folds. ‚ÄúThe babes are well?‚Äù he asked her. The wildling girl smiled timidly from under her cowl. ‚ÄúYes, m‚Äôlord. I was scared I wouldn‚Äôt have milk enough for both, but the more they suck, the more I have. They‚Äôre strong.‚Äù ‚ÄúI have something hard to tell you.‚Äù He almost said ask, but caught himself at the last instant. ‚ÄúIs it Mance? Val begged the king to spare him. She said she‚Äôd let some kneeler marry her and never slit his throat if only Mance could live. That Lord o‚ÄôBones, he‚Äôs to be spared. Craster always swore he‚Äôd kill him if he ever showed his face about the keep. Mance never did half the things he done.‚Äù All Mance ever did was lead an army down upon the realm he once swore to protect. ‚ÄúMance said our words, Gilly. Then he turned his cloak, wed Dalla, and crowned himself King-Beyond-the-Wall. His life is in the king‚Äôs hands now. It‚Äôs not him we need to talk about. It‚Äôs his son. Dalla‚Äôs boy.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe babe?‚Äù Her voice trembled. ‚ÄúHe never broke no oath, m‚Äôlord. He sleeps and cries and sucks, is all; he‚Äôs never done no harm to no one. Don‚Äôt let her burn him.
