They called Hot Pie down from the wall and found Lommy Greenhands where he lay bleeding from a spear thrust through his calf. They found Gerren too, but he was hurt too bad to move.

> The fire beat at her back with hot red wings as she fled the burning barn. It felt blessedly cool outside, but men were dying all around her. She saw Koss throw down his blade to yield, and she saw them kill him where he stood. Smoke was everywhere.

{:#Yoren}
**Yoren**

> ‚ÄúBeen bringing men to the Wall for close on thirty years.‚Äù Froth shone on Yoren‚Äôs lips, like bubbles of blood. ‚ÄúAll that time, I only lost three. Old man died of a fever, city boy got snakebit taking a shit, and one fool tried to kill me in my sleep and got a red smile for his trouble.‚Äù He drew the dirk across his throat, to show her. ‚ÄúThree in thirty years.‚Äù He spat out the old sourleaf. ‚ÄúA ship now, might have been wiser. No chance o‚Äô finding more men on the way, but still ‚Ä¶ clever man, he‚Äôd go by ship, but me ‚Ä¶ thirty years I been taking this kingsroad.‚Äù

> But Arya would not leave until they found Yoren. They couldn‚Äôt have killed him, she told herself, he was too hard and tough, and a brother of the Night‚Äôs Watch besides. She said as much to Gendry as they searched among the corpses. The axe blow that had killed him had split his skull apart, but the great tangled beard could be no one else‚Äôs, or the garb, patched and unwashed and so faded it was more grey than black. Ser Amory Lorch had given no more thought to burying his own dead than to those he had murdered, and the corpses of four Lannister men-at-arms were heaped near Yoren‚Äôs. Arya wondered how many it had taken to bring him down.

{:#Kurz}
**Kurz**

> Yesterday Gendry had caught a frog and shared it with Lommy, and, a few days before, Hot Pie had found blackberries and stripped the bush bare, but mostly they had been living on water and acorns. Kurz had told them how to use rocks and make a kind of acorn paste. It tasted awful. She wished the poacher hadn‚Äôt died. He‚Äôd known more about the woods than all the rest of them together, but he‚Äôd taken an arrow through the shoulder pulling in the ladder at the towerhouse. Tarber had packed it with mud and moss from the lake, and for a day or two Kurz swore the wound was nothing, even though the flesh of his throat was turning dark while angry red welts crept up his jaw and down his chest. Then one morning he couldn‚Äôt find the strength to get up, and by the next he was dead.

{:#Lommy-Greenhands}
**Lommy Greenhands**

> ‚ÄúWe have to yield,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúThat‚Äôs what Yoren should have done. He should have opened the gates like they said.‚Äù Arya was sick of Lommy going on about how Yoren should have yielded. It was all he talked about when they carried him, that and his leg and his empty belly. Hot Pie agreed. ‚ÄúThey told Yoren to open the gates, they told him in the king‚Äôs name. You have to do what they tell you in the king‚Äôs name. It was that stinky old man‚Äôs fault. If he‚Äôd of yielded, they would have left us be.‚Äù Gendry frowned. ‚ÄúKnights and lordlings, they take each other captive and pay ransoms, but they don‚Äôt care if the likes of you yield or not.‚Äù

> One of the spearmen drifted over to Lommy. ‚ÄúSomething wrong with your leg, boy?‚Äù ‚ÄúIt got hurt.‚Äù ‚ÄúCan you walk?‚Äù He sounded concerned. ‚ÄúNo,‚Äù said Lommy. ‚ÄúYou got to carry me.‚Äù ‚ÄúThink so?‚Äù The man lifted his spear casually and drove the point through the boy‚Äôs soft throat. Lommy never even had time to yield again. He jerked once, and that was all. When the man pulled his spear loose, blood sprayed out in a dark fountain. ‚ÄúCarry him, he says,‚Äù he muttered, chuckling.

{:#Chiswyck}
**Chiswyck**

> ‚ÄúAfter the Hand‚Äôs tourney, it were, before the war come,‚Äù Chiswyck was saying. ‚ÄúWe were on our ways back west, seven of us with Ser Gregor. Raff was with me, and young Joss Stilwood, he‚Äôd squired for Ser in the lists. Well, we come on this pisswater river, running high on account there‚Äôd been rains. No way to ford, but there‚Äôs an alehouse near, so there we repair. Ser rousts the brewer and tells him to keep our horns full till the waters fall, and you should see the man‚Äôs pig eyes shine at the sight o‚Äô silver. So he‚Äôs fetching us ale, him and his daughter, and poor thin stuff it is, no more‚Äôn brown piss, which don‚Äôt make me any happier, nor Ser neither. And all the time this brewer‚Äôs saying how glad he is to have us, custom being slow on account o‚Äô them rains. The fool won‚Äôt shut his yap, not him, though Ser is saying not a word, just brooding on the Knight o‚Äô Pansies and that bugger‚Äôs trick he played. You can see how tight his mouth sits, so me and the other lads we know better‚Äôn to say a squeak to him, but this brewer he‚Äôs got to talk, he even asks how m‚Äôlord fared in the jousting. Ser just gave him this look.‚Äù Chiswyck cackled, quaffed his ale, and wiped the foam away with the back of his hand. ‚ÄúMeanwhile, this daughter of his has been fetching and pouring, a fat little thing, eighteen or so‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúThirteen, more like,‚Äù Raff the Sweetling drawled. ‚ÄúWell, be that as it may, she‚Äôs not much to look at, but Eggon‚Äôs been drinking and gets to touching her, and might be I did a little touching meself, and Raff‚Äôs telling young Stilwood that he ought t‚Äô drag the girl upstairs and make hisself a man, giving the lad courage as it were. Finally Joss reaches up under her skirt, and she shrieks and drops her flagon and goes running off to the kitchen. Well, it would have ended right there, only what does the old fool do but he goes to Ser and asks him to make us leave the girl alone, him being an anointed knight and all such. ‚ÄúSer Gregor, he wasn‚Äôt paying no mind to none of our fun, but now he looks, you know how he does, and he commands that the girl be brought before him. Now the old man has to drag her out of the kitchen, and no one to blame but hisself. Ser looks her over and says, ‚ÄòSo this is the whore you‚Äôre so concerned for,‚Äô and this besotted old fool says, ‚ÄòMy Layna‚Äôs no whore, ser,‚Äô right to Gregor‚Äôs face. Ser, he never blinks, just says, ‚ÄòShe is now,‚Äô tosses the old man another silver, rips the dress off the wench, and takes her right there on the table in front of her da, her flopping and wiggling like a rabbit and making these noises. The look on the old man‚Äôs face, I laughed so hard ale was coming out me nose. Then this boy hears the noise, the son I figure, and comes rushing up from the cellar, so Raff has to stick a dirk in his belly. By then Ser‚Äôs done, so he goes back to his drinking and we all have a turn. Tobbot, you know how he is, he flops her over and goes in the back way. The girl was done fighting by the time I had her, maybe she‚Äôd decided she liked it after all, though to tell the truth I wouldn‚Äôt have minded a little wiggling. And now here‚Äôs the best bit¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýwhen it‚Äôs all done, Ser tells the old man that he wants his change. The girl wasn‚Äôt worth a silver, he says¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand damned if that old man didn‚Äôt fetch a fistful of coppers, beg m‚Äôlord‚Äôs pardon, and thank him for the custom!‚Äù

> The men all roared, none louder than Chiswyck himself, who laughed so hard at his own story that snot dribbled from his nose down into his scraggy grey beard. Arya stood in the shadows of the stairwell and watched him. She crept back down to the cellars without saying a word. When Weese found that she hadn‚Äôt asked about the clothes, he yanked down her breeches and caned her until blood ran down her thighs, but Arya closed her eyes and thought of all the sayings Syrio had taught her, so she scarcely felt it. Two nights later, he sent her to the Barracks Hall to serve at table. She was carrying a flagon of wine and pouring when she glimpsed Jaqen H‚Äôghar at his trencher across the aisle. Chewing her lip, Arya glanced around warily to make certain Weese was not in sight. Fear cuts deeper than swords, she told herself. She took a step, and another, and with each she felt less a mouse. She worked her way down the bench, filling wine cups. Rorge sat to Jaqen‚Äôs right, deep drunk, but he took no note of her. Arya leaned close and whispered, ‚ÄúChiswyck,‚Äù right in Jaqen‚Äôs ear. The Lorathi gave no sign that he had heard. When her flagon was empty, Arya hurried down to the cellars to refill it from the cask, and quickly returned to her pouring. No one had died of thirst while she was gone, nor even noted her brief absence. Nothing happened the next day, nor the day after, but on the third day Arya went to the kitchens with Weese to fetch their dinner. ‚ÄúOne of the Mountain‚Äôs men fell off a wallwalk last night and broke his fool neck,‚Äù she heard Weese tell a cook. ‚ÄúDrunk?‚Äù the woman asked. ‚ÄúNo more‚Äôn usual. Some are saying it was Harren‚Äôs ghost flung him down.‚Äù He snorted to show what he thought of such notions. It wasn‚Äôt Harren, Arya wanted to say, it was me. She had killed Chiswyck with a whisper, and she would kill two more before she was through. I‚Äôm the ghost in Harrenhal, she thought. And that night, there was one less name to hate.

{:#Stafford-Lannister-and-Rubert-Brax}
**Stafford Lannister and Rubert Brax**

> ‚ÄúSer Lancel,‚Äù Joff said, ‚Äútell her of this outrage.‚Äù Sansa had always thought Lancel Lannister comely and well spoken, but there was neither pity nor kindness in the look he gave her. ‚ÄúUsing some vile sorcery, your brother fell upon Ser Stafford Lannister with an army of wargs, not three days ride from Lannisport. Thousands of good men were butchered as they slept, without the chance to lift sword. After the slaughter, the northmen feasted on the flesh of the slain.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúYou have a right to know why Joffrey was so wroth. Six nights gone, your brother fell upon my uncle Stafford, encamped with his host at a village called Oxcross not three days ride from Casterly Rock. Your northerners won a crushing victory. We received word only this morning.‚Äù Robb will kill you all, she thought, exulting. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýterrible, my lord. My brother is a vile traitor.‚Äù The dwarf smiled wanly. ‚ÄúWell, he‚Äôs no fawn, he‚Äôs made that clear enough.‚Äù ‚ÄúSer Lancel said Robb led an army of wargs¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù The Imp gave a disdainful bark of laughter. ‚ÄúSer Lancel‚Äôs a wineskin warrior who wouldn‚Äôt know a warg from a wart. Your brother had his direwolf with him, but I suspect that‚Äôs as far as it went. The northmen crept into my uncle‚Äôs camp and cut his horse lines, and Lord Stark sent his wolf among them. Even war-trained destrier‚Äôs went mad. Knights were trampled to death in their pavilions, and the rabble woke in terror and fled, casting aside their weapons to run the faster. Ser Stafford was slain as he chased after a horse. Lord Rickard Karstark drove a lance through his chest. Ser Rubert Brax is also dead, along with Ser Lymond Vikary, Lord Crakehall, and Lord Jast. Half a hundred more have been taken captive, including Jast‚Äôs sons and my nephew Martyn Lannister. Those who survived are spreading wild tales and swearing that the old gods of the north march with your brother.‚Äù

{:#Gawen-Wylde}
**Gawen Wylde**

> ‚ÄúWhen my brother falls, see that no insult is done to his corpse. He is my own blood, I will not have his head paraded about on a spear.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd if he yields?‚Äù Lord Tarly asked. ‚ÄúYields?‚Äù Lord Rowan laughed. ‚ÄúWhen Mace Tyrell laid siege to Storm‚Äôs End, Stannis ate rats rather than open his gates.‚Äù ‚ÄúWell I remember.‚Äù Renly lifted his chin to allow Brienne to fasten his gorget in place. ‚ÄúNear the end, Ser Gawen Wylde and three of his knights tried to steal out a postern gate to surrender. Stannis caught them and ordered them flung from the walls with catapults. I can still see Gawen‚Äôs face as they strapped him down. He had been our master-at-arms.‚Äù Lord Rowan appeared puzzled. ‚ÄúNo men were hurled from the walls. I would surely remember that.‚Äù ‚ÄúMaester Cressen told Stannis that we might be forced to eat our dead, and there was no gain in flinging away good meat.‚Äù Renly pushed back his hair. Brienne bound it with a velvet tie and pulled a padded cap down over his ears, to cushion the weight of his helm. ‚ÄúThanks to the Onion Knight we were never reduced to dining on corpses, but it was a close thing. Too close for Ser Gawen, who died in his cell.‚Äù

{:#Renly-Baratheon}
**Renly Baratheon**

> ‚ÄúThe Lannisters tried to kill my son Bran. A thousand times I have asked myself why. Your brother gave me my answer. There was a hunt the day he fell. Robert and Ned and most of the other men rode out after boar, but Jaime Lannister remained at Winterfell, as did the queen.‚Äù Renly was not slow to take the implication. ‚ÄúSo you believe the boy caught them at their incest¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúI beg you, my lord, grant me leave to go to your brother Stannis and tell him what I suspect.‚Äù ‚ÄúTo what end?‚Äù ‚ÄúRobb will set aside his crown if you and your brother will do the same,‚Äù she said, hoping it was true. She would make it true if she must; Robb would listen to her, even if his lords would not. ‚ÄúLet the three of you call for a Great Council, such as the realm has not seen for a hundred years. We will send to Winterfell, so Bran may tell his tale and all men may know the Lannisters for the true usurpers. Let the assembled lords of the Seven Kingdoms choose who shall rule them.‚Äù Renly laughed. ‚ÄúTell me, my lady, do direwolves vote on who should lead the pack?‚Äù Brienne brought the king‚Äôs gauntlets and greathelm, crowned with golden antlers that would add a foot and a half to his height. ‚ÄúThe time for talk is done. Now we see who is stronger.‚Äù Renly pulled a lobstered green-and-gold gauntlet over his left hand, while Brienne knelt to buckle on his belt, heavy with the weight of longsword and dagger.

> ‚ÄúI beg you in the name of the Mother,‚Äù Catelyn began when a sudden gust of wind flung open the door of the tent. She thought she glimpsed movement, but when she turned her head, it was only the king‚Äôs shadow shifting against the silken walls. She heard Renly begin a jest, his shadow moving, lifting its sword, black on green, candles guttering, shivering, something was queer, wrong, and then she saw Renly‚Äôs sword still in its scabbard, sheathed still, but the shadowsword¬Ý‚Ä¶ ‚ÄúCold,‚Äù said Renly in a small puzzled voice, a heartbeat before the steel of his gorget parted like cheesecloth beneath the shadow of a blade that was not there. He had time to make a small thick gasp before the blood came gushing out of his throat. ‚ÄúYour Gr‚Äîno!‚Äù cried Brienne the Blue when she saw that evil flow, sounding as scared as any little girl. The king stumbled into her arms, a sheet of blood creeping down the front of his armor, a dark red tide that drowned his green and gold. More candles guttered out. Renly tried to speak, but he was choking on his own blood. His legs collapsed, and only Brienne‚Äôs strength held him up. She threw back her head and screamed, wordless in her anguish. The shadow. Something dark and evil had happened here, she knew, something that she could not begin to understand. Renly never cast that shadow. Death came in that door and blew the life out of him as swift as the wind snuffed out his candles.

> Renly‚Äôs battles were already coming apart as the rumors spread from mouth to mouth. The nightfires had burned low, and as the east began to lighten the immense mass of Storm‚Äôs End emerged like a dream of stone while wisps of pale mist raced across the field, flying from the sun on wings of wind. Morning ghosts, she had heard Old Nan call them once, spirits returning to their graves. And Renly one of them now, gone like his brother Robert, like her own dear Ned. ‚ÄúI never held him but as he died,‚Äù Brienne said quietly as they walked through the spreading chaos. Her voice sounded as if she might break at any instant. ‚ÄúHe was laughing one moment, and suddenly the blood was everywhere¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýmy lady, I do not understand. Did you see, did you¬Ý‚Ä¶?‚Äù ‚ÄúI saw a shadow. I thought it was Renly‚Äôs shadow at the first, but it was his brother‚Äôs.‚Äù ‚ÄúLord Stannis?‚Äù ‚ÄúI felt him. It makes no sense, I know¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù It made sense enough for Brienne. ‚ÄúI will kill him,‚Äù the tall homely girl declared. ‚ÄúWith my lord‚Äôs own sword, I will kill him. I swear it. I swear it. I swear it.‚Äù

> As the long fingers of dawn fanned across the fields, color was returning to the world. Where grey men had sat grey horses armed with shadow spears, the points of ten thousand lances now glinted silverly cold, and on the myriad flapping banners Catelyn saw the blush of red and pink and orange, the richness of blues and browns, the blaze of gold and yellow. All the power of Storm‚Äôs End and Highgarden, the power that had been Renly‚Äôs an hour ago. They belong to Stannis now, she realized, even if they do not know it themselves yet. Where else are they to turn, if not to the last Baratheon? Stannis has won all with a single evil stroke. I am the rightful king, he had declared, his jaw clenched hard as iron, and your son no less a traitor than my brother here. His day will come as well. A chill went through her.

> ‚ÄúI have no doubt that Cersei had a hand in Robert‚Äôs death. I will have justice for him. Aye, and for Ned Stark and Jon Arryn as well.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd for Renly?‚Äù The words were out before Davos could stop to consider them. For a long time the king did not speak. Then, very softly, he said, ‚ÄúI dream of it sometimes. Of Renly‚Äôs dying. A green tent, candles, a woman screaming. And blood.‚Äù Stannis looked down at his hands. ‚ÄúI was still abed when he died. Your Devan will tell you. He tried to wake me. Dawn was nigh and my lords were waiting, fretting. I should have been ahorse, armored. I knew Renly would attack at break of day. Devan says I thrashed and cried out, but what does it matter? It was a dream. I was in my tent when Renly died, and when I woke my hands were clean.‚Äù Ser Davos Seaworth could feel his phantom fingertips start to itch. Something is wrong here, the onetime smuggler thought. Yet he nodded and said, ‚ÄúI see.‚Äù ‚ÄúRenly offered me a peach. At our parley. Mocked me, defied me, threatened me, and offered me a peach. I thought he was drawing a blade and went for mine own. Was that his purpose, to make me show fear? Or was it one of his pointless jests? When he spoke of how sweet the peach was, did his words have some hidden meaning?‚Äù The king gave a shake of his head, like a dog shaking a rabbit to snap its neck. ‚ÄúOnly Renly could vex me so with a piece of fruit. He brought his doom on himself with his treason, but I did love him, Davos. I know that now. I swear, I will go to my grave thinking of my brother‚Äôs peach.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs said you fought magnificently in the battle¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýalmost as well as Lord Renly‚Äôs ghost beside you. A Sworn Brother has no secrets from his Lord Commander. Tell me, ser. Who was wearing Renly‚Äôs armor?‚Äù For a moment Loras Tyrell looked as though he might refuse, but in the end he remembered his vows. ‚ÄúMy brother,‚Äù he said sullenly. ‚ÄúRenly was taller than me, and broader in the chest. His armor was too loose on me, but it suited Garlan well.‚Äù ‚ÄúWas the masquerade your notion, or his?‚Äù ‚ÄúLord Littlefinger suggested it. He said it would frighten Stannis‚Äôs ignorant men-at-arms.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd so it did.‚Äù And some knights and lordlings too. ‚ÄúWell, you gave the singers something to make rhymes about, I suppose that‚Äôs not to be despised. What did you do with Renly?‚Äù ‚ÄúI buried him with mine own hands, in a place he showed me once when I was a squire at Storm‚Äôs End. No one shall ever find him there to disturb his rest.‚Äù He looked at Jaime defiantly. ‚ÄúI will defend King Tommen with all my strength, I swear it. I will give my life for his if need be. But I will never betray Renly, by word or deed. He was the king that should have been. He was the best of them.‚Äù

{:#Stevron-Frey}
**Stevron Frey**

> ‚ÄúSadly, no victory is without cost.‚Äù Maester Luwin turned to the Walders. ‚ÄúMy lords, your uncle Ser Stevron Frey was among those who lost their lives at Oxcross. He took a wound in the battle, Robb writes. It was not thought to be serious, but three days later he died in his tent, asleep.‚Äù Big Walder shrugged. ‚ÄúHe was very old. Five-and-sixty, I think. Too old for battles. He was always saying he was tired.‚Äù Little Walder hooted. ‚ÄúTired of waiting for our grandfather to die, you mean. Does this mean Ser Emmon‚Äôs the heir now?‚Äù ‚ÄúDon‚Äôt be stupid,‚Äù his cousin said. ‚ÄúThe sons of the first son come before the second son. Ser Ryman is next in line, and then Edwyn and Black Walder and Petyr Pimple. And then Aegon and all his sons.‚Äù ‚ÄúRyman is old too,‚Äù said Little Walder. ‚ÄúPast forty, I bet. And he has a bad belly. Do you think he‚Äôll be lord?‚Äù ‚ÄúI‚Äôll be lord. I don‚Äôt care if he is.‚Äù

{:#Donella-Hornwood}
**Donella Hornwood**

> Alebelly was the only one who paid the warning any heed. He went to talk to Jojen himself, and afterward stopped bathing and refused to go near the well. Finally he stank so bad that six of the other guards threw him into a tub of scalding water and scrubbed him raw while he screamed that they were going to drown him like the frogboy had said. Thereafter he scowled whenever he saw Bran or Jojen about the castle, and muttered under his breath. It was a few days after Alebelly‚Äôs bath that Ser Rodrik returned to Winterfell with his prisoner, a fleshy young man with fat moist lips and long hair who smelled like a privy, even worse than Alebelly had. ‚ÄúReek, he‚Äôs called,‚Äù Hayhead said when Bran asked who it was. ‚ÄúI never heard his true name. He served the Bastard of Bolton and helped him murder Lady Hornwood, they say.‚Äù The Bastard himself was dead, Bran learned that evening over supper. Ser Rodrik‚Äôs men had caught him on Hornwood land doing something horrible (Bran wasn‚Äôt quite sure what, but it seemed to be something you did without your clothes) and shot him down with arrows as he tried to ride away. They came too late for poor Lady Hornwood, though. After their wedding, the Bastard had locked her in a tower and neglected to feed her. Bran had heard men saying that when Ser Rodrik had smashed down the door he found her with her mouth all bloody and her fingers chewed off. ‚ÄúThe monster has tied us a thorny knot,‚Äù the old knight told Maester Luwin. ‚ÄúLike it or no, Lady Hornwood was his wife. He made her say the vows before both septon and heart tree, and bedded her that very night before witnesses. She signed a will naming him as heir and fixed her seal to it.‚Äù ‚ÄúVows made at sword point are not valid,‚Äù the maester argued. ‚ÄúRoose Bolton may not agree. Not with land at issue.‚Äù Ser Rodrik looked unhappy. ‚ÄúWould that I could take this serving man‚Äôs head off as well, he‚Äôs as bad as his master. But I fear I must keep him alive until Robb returns from his wars. He is the only witness to the worst of the Bastard‚Äôs crimes. Perhaps when Lord Bolton hears his tale, he will abandon his claim, but meantime we have Manderly knights and Dreadfort men killing one another in Hornwood forests, and I lack the strength to stop them.‚Äù

{:#Benfred-Tallhart}
**Benfred Tallhart**

> Theon wiped the spittle off his cheek with the back of his hand. ‚ÄúRobb will gut you, Greyjoy,‚Äù Benfred Tallheart screamed. ‚ÄúHe‚Äôll feed your turncloak‚Äôs heart to his wolf, you piece of sheep dung.‚Äù Aeron Damphair‚Äôs voice cut through the insults like a sword through cheese. ‚ÄúNow you must kill him.‚Äù ‚ÄúI have questions for him first,‚Äù said Theon. ‚ÄúFuck your questions.‚Äù Benfred hung bleeding and helpless between Stygg and Werlag. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôll choke on them before you get any answers from me, craven. Turncloak.‚Äù Uncle Aeron was relentless. ‚ÄúWhen he spits on you, he spits on all of us. He spits on the Drowned God. He must die.‚Äù ‚ÄúMy father gave me the command here, Uncle.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd sent me to counsel you.‚Äù And to watch me. Theon dare not push matters too far with his uncle. The command was his, yes, but his men had a faith in the Drowned God that they did not have in him, and they were terrified of Aeron Damphair. I cannot fault them for that. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôll lose your head for this, Greyjoy. The crows will eat the jelly of your eyes.‚Äù Benfred tried to spit again, but only managed a little blood. ‚ÄúThe Others bugger your wet god.‚Äù Tallhart, you‚Äôve spit away your life, Theon thought. ‚ÄúStygg, silence him,‚Äù he said. They forced Benfred to his knees. Werlag tore the rabbitskin off his belt and jammed it between his teeth to stop his shouting. Stygg unlimbered his axe. ‚ÄúNo,‚Äù Aeron Damphair declared. ‚ÄúHe must be given to the god. The old way.‚Äù What does it matter? Dead is dead. ‚ÄúTake him, then.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou will come as well. You command here. The offering should come from you.‚Äù That was more than Theon could stomach. ‚ÄúYou are the priest, Uncle, I leave the god to you. Do me the same kindness and leave the battles to me.‚Äù He waved his hand, and Werlag and Stygg began to drag their captive off toward the shore. Aeron Damphair gave his nephew a reproachful look, then followed. Down to the pebbled beach they would go, to drown Benfred Tallhart in salt water. The old way. Perhaps it‚Äôs a kindness, Theon told himself as he stalked off in the other direction. Stygg was hardly the most expert of headsmen, and Benfred had a neck thick as a boar‚Äôs, heavy with muscle and fat. I used to mock him for it, just to see how angry I could make him, he remembered. That had been, what, three years past? When Ned Stark had ridden to Torrhen‚Äôs Square to see Ser Helman, Theon had accompanied him and spent a fortnight in Benfred‚Äôs company.

{:#Todric}
**Todric**

> Old Botley, who was called Fishwhiskers, sat scowling by his pile of plunder while his three sons added to it. One of them was in a shoving match with a fat man named Todric, who was reeling among the slain with a horn of ale in one hand and an axe in the other, clad in a cloak of white foxfur only slightly stained by the blood of its previous owner. Drunk, Theon decided, watching him bellow. It was said that the ironmen of old had oft been blood-drunk in battle, so berserk that they felt no pain and feared no foe, but this was a common aledrunk. ‚ÄúWex, my bow and quiver.‚Äù The boy ran and fetched them. Theon bent the bow and slipped the string into its notches as Todric knocked down the Botley boy and flung ale into his eyes. Fishwhiskers leapt up cursing, but Theon was quicker. He drew on the hand that clutched the drinking horn, figuring to give them a shot to talk about, but Todric spoiled it by lurching to one side just as he loosed. The arrow took him through the belly. The looters stopped to gape. Theon lowered his bow. ‚ÄúNo drunkards, I said, and no squabbles over plunder.‚Äù On his knees, Todric was dying noisily. ‚ÄúBotley, silence him.‚Äù Fishwhiskers and his sons were quick to obey. They slit Todric‚Äôs throat as he kicked feebly, and were stripping him of cloak and rings and weapons before he was even dead.

{:#Weese}
**Weese**

> Pale light filled the yard when Lord Tywin Lannister took his leave of Harrenhal. Arya watched from an arched window halfway up the Wailing Tower. His charger wore a blanket of enameled crimson scales and gilded crinet and chamfron, while Lord Tywin himself sported a thick ermine cloak. His brother Ser Kevan looked near as splendid. No less than four standard-bearers went before them, carrying huge crimson banners emblazoned with the golden lion. Behind the Lannisters came their great lords and captains. Their banners flared and flapped, a pageant of color: red ox and golden mountain, purple unicorn and bantam rooster, brindled boar and badger, a silver ferret and a juggler in motley, stars and sunbursts, peacock and panther, chevron and dagger, black hood and blue beetle and green arrow. Last of all came Ser Gregor Clegane in his grey plate steel, astride a stallion as bad-tempered as his rider. Polliver rode beside him, with the black dog standard in his hand and Gendry‚Äôs horned helm on his head. He was a tall man, but he looked no more than a half-grown boy when he rode in his master‚Äôs shadow. A shiver crept up Arya‚Äôs spine as she watched them pass under the great iron portcullis of Harrenhal. Suddenly she knew that she had made a terrible mistake. I‚Äôm so stupid, she thought. Weese did not matter, no more than Chiswyck had. These were the men who mattered, the ones she ought to have killed. Last night she could have whispered any of them dead, if only she hadn‚Äôt been so mad at Weese for hitting her and lying about the capon. Lord Tywin, why didn‚Äôt I say Lord Tywin?

> Perhaps it was not too late to change her mind. Weese was not killed yet. If she could find Jaqen, tell him¬Ý‚Ä¶ Hurriedly, Arya ran down the twisting steps, her chores forgotten. She heard the rattle of chains as the portcullis was slowly lowered, its spikes sinking deep into the ground¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand then another sound, a shriek of pain and fear. A dozen people got there before her, though none was coming any too close. Arya squirmed between them. Weese was sprawled across the cobbles, his throat a red ruin, eyes gaping sightlessly up at a bank of grey cloud. His ugly spotted dog stood on his chest, lapping at the blood pulsing from his neck, and every so often ripping a mouthful of flesh out of the dead man‚Äôs face. Finally someone brought a crossbow and shot the spotted dog dead while she was worrying at one of Weese‚Äôs ears. ‚ÄúDamnedest thing,‚Äù she heard a man say. ‚ÄúHe had that bitch dog since she was a pup.‚Äù ‚ÄúThis place is cursed,‚Äù the man with the crossbow said. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs Harren‚Äôs ghost, that‚Äôs what it is,‚Äù said Goodwife Amabel. ‚ÄúI‚Äôll not sleep here another night, I swear it.‚Äù Arya lifted her gaze from the dead man and his dead dog. Jaqen H‚Äôghar was leaning up against the side of the Wailing Tower. When he saw her looking, he lifted a hand to his face and laid two fingers casually against his cheek.

{:#Poul-Pemford-and-Myles}
**Poul Pemford and Myles**

> ‚ÄúWho are these men you‚Äôve hanged?‚Äù Edmure glanced up uncomfortably. ‚ÄúThey came with Ser Cleos when he brought the queen‚Äôs answer to our peace offer.‚Äù Catelyn was shocked. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôve killed envoys?‚Äù ‚ÄúFalse envoys,‚Äù Edmure declared. ‚ÄúThey pledged me their peace and surrendered their weapons, so I allowed them freedom of the castle, and for three nights they ate my meat and drank my mead whilst I talked with Ser Cleos. On the fourth night, they tried to free the Kingslayer.‚Äù He pointed up. ‚ÄúThat big brute killed two guards with naught but those ham hands of his, caught them by the throats and smashed their skulls together while that skinny lad beside him was opening Lannister‚Äôs cell with a bit of wire, gods curse him. The one on the end was some sort of damned mummer. He used my own voice to command that the River Gate be opened. The guardsmen swear to it, Enger and Delp and Long Lew, all three. If you ask me, the man sounded nothing like me, and yet the oafs were raising the portcullis all the same.‚Äù This was the Imp‚Äôs work, Catelyn suspected; it stank of the same sort of cunning he had displayed at the Eyrie. Once, she would have named Tyrion the least dangerous of the Lannisters. Now she was not so certain. ‚ÄúHow is it you caught them?‚Äù ‚ÄúAh, as it happened, I was not in the castle. I‚Äôd crossed the Tumblestone to, ah¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúYou were whoring or wenching. Get on with the tale.‚Äù Edmure‚Äôs cheeks flamed as red as his beard. ‚ÄúIt was the hour before dawn, and I was only then returning. When Long Lew saw my boat and recognized me, he finally thought to wonder who was standing below barking commands, and raised a cry.‚Äù ‚ÄúTell me the Kingslayer was retaken.‚Äù ‚ÄúYes, though not easily. Jaime got hold of a sword, slew Poul Pemford and Ser Desmond‚Äôs squire Myles, and wounded Delp so badly that Maester Vyman fears he‚Äôll soon die as well. It was a bloody mess. At the sound of steel, some of the other red cloaks rushed to join him, barehand or no. I hanged those beside the four who freed him, and threw the rest in the dungeons. Jaime too. We‚Äôll have no more escapes from that one. He‚Äôs down in the dark this time, chained hand and foot and bolted to the wall.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd Cleos Frey?‚Äù ‚ÄúHe swears he knew naught of the plot. Who can say? The man is half Lannister, half Frey, and all liar. I put him in Jaime‚Äôs old tower cell.‚Äù

{:#Preston-Greenfield-Aron-Santagar-and-the-High-Septon}
**Preston Greenfield, Aron Santagar, and the High Septon**

> The narrow streets were lined by men of the City Watch, holding back the crowd with the shafts of their spears. Ser Jacelyn Bywater went in front, heading a wedge of mounted lancers in black ringmail and golden cloaks. Behind him came Ser Aron Santagar and Ser Balon Swann, bearing the king‚Äôs banners, the lion of Lannister and crowned stag of Baratheon. King Joffrey followed on a tall grey palfrey, a golden crown set upon his golden curls. Sansa Stark rode a chestnut mare at his side, looking neither right nor left, her thick auburn hair flowing to her shoulders beneath a net of moonstones. Two of the Kingsguard flanked the couple, the Hound on the king‚Äôs right hand and Ser Mandon Moore to the left of the Stark girl. Next came Tommen, snuffling, with Ser Preston Greenfield in his white armor and cloak, and then Cersei, accompanied by Ser Lancel and protected by Meryn Trant and Boros Blount. Tyrion fell in with his sister. After them followed the High Septon in his litter, and a long tail of other courtiers‚ÄîSer Horas Redwyne, Lady Tanda and her daughter, Jalabhar Xho, Lord Gyles Rosby, and the rest. A double column of guardsmen brought up the rear.

> Halfway along the route, a wailing woman forced her way between two watchmen and ran out into the street in front of the king and his companions, holding the corpse of her dead baby above her head. It was blue and swollen, grotesque, but the real horror was the mother‚Äôs eyes. Joffrey looked for a moment as if he meant to ride her down, but Sansa Stark leaned over and said something to him. The king fumbled in his purse, and flung the woman a silver stag. The coin bounced off the child and rolled away, under the legs of the gold cloaks and into the crowd, where a dozen men began to fight for it. The mother never once blinked. Her skinny arms were trembling from the dead weight of her son. ‚ÄúLeave her, Your Grace,‚Äù Cersei called out to the king, ‚Äúshe‚Äôs beyond our help, poor thing.‚Äù The mother heard her. Somehow the queen‚Äôs voice cut through the woman‚Äôs ravaged wits. Her slack face twisted in loathing. ‚ÄúWhore!‚Äù she shrieked. ‚ÄúKingslayer‚Äôs whore! Brotherfucker!‚Äù Her dead child dropped from her arms like a sack of flour as she pointed at Cersei. ‚ÄúBrotherfucker brotherfucker brotherfucker.‚Äù Tyrion never saw who threw the dung. He only heard Sansa‚Äôs gasp and Joffrey‚Äôs bellowed curse, and when he turned his head, the king was wiping brown filth from his cheek. There was more caked in his golden hair and spattered over Sansa‚Äôs legs. ‚ÄúWho threw that?‚Äù Joffrey screamed. He pushed his fingers into his hair, made a furious face, and flung away another handful of dung. ‚ÄúI want the man who threw that!‚Äù he shouted. ‚ÄúA hundred golden dragons to the man who gives him up.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe was up there!‚Äù someone shouted from the crowd. The king wheeled his horse in a circle to survey the rooftops and open balconies above them. In the crowd people were pointing, shoving, cursing one another and the king. ‚ÄúPlease, Your Grace, let him go,‚Äù Sansa pleaded. The king paid her no heed. ‚ÄúBring me the man who flung that filth!‚Äù Joffrey commanded. ‚ÄúHe‚Äôll lick it off me or I‚Äôll have his head. Dog, you bring him here!‚Äù Obedient, Sandor Clegane swung down from his saddle, but there was no way through that wall of flesh, let alone to the roof. Those closest to him began to squirm and shove to get away, while others pushed forward to see. Tyrion smelled disaster. ‚ÄúClegane, leave off, the man is long fled.‚Äù ‚ÄúI want him!‚Äù Joffrey pointed at the roof. ‚ÄúHe was up there! Dog, cut through them and bring‚Äî‚Äù A tumult of sound drowned his last words, a rolling thunder of rage and fear and hatred that engulfed them from all sides. ‚ÄúBastard!‚Äù someone screamed at Joffrey, ‚Äúbastard monster.‚Äù Other voices flung calls of ‚ÄúWhore‚Äù and ‚ÄúBrotherfucker‚Äù at the queen, while Tyrion was pelted with shouts of ‚ÄúFreak‚Äù and ‚ÄúHalfman.‚Äù Mixed in with the abuse, he heard a few cries of ‚ÄúJustice‚Äù and ‚ÄúRobb, King Robb, the Young Wolf,‚Äù of ‚ÄúStannis!‚Äù and even ‚ÄúRenly!‚Äù From both sides of the street, the crowd surged against the spear shafts while the gold cloaks struggled to hold the line. Stones and dung and fouler things whistled overhead. ‚ÄúFeed us!‚Äù a woman shrieked. ‚ÄúBread!‚Äù boomed a man behind her. ‚ÄúWe want bread, bastard!‚Äù In a heartbeat, a thousand voices took up the chant. King Joffrey and King Robb and King Stannis were forgotten, and King Bread ruled alone. ‚ÄúBread,‚Äù they clamored. ‚ÄúBread, bread!‚Äù

> Tyrion spurred to his sister‚Äôs side, yelling, ‚ÄúBack to the castle. Now.‚Äù Cersei gave a curt nod, and Ser Lancel unsheathed his sword. Ahead of the column, Jacelyn Bywater was roaring commands. His riders lowered their lances and drove forward in a wedge. The king was wheeling his palfrey around in anxious circles while hands reached past the line of gold cloaks, grasping for him. One managed to get hold of his leg, but only for an instant. Ser Mandon‚Äôs sword slashed down, parting hand from wrist. ‚ÄúRide!‚Äù Tyrion shouted at his nephew, giving the horse a sharp smack on the rump. The animal reared, trumpeting, and plunged ahead, the press shattering before him. Tyrion drove into the gap hard on the king‚Äôs hooves. Bronn kept pace, sword in hand. A jagged rock flew past his head as he rode, and a rotten cabbage exploded against Ser Mandon‚Äôs shield. To their left, three gold cloaks went down under the surge, and then the crowd was rushing forward, trampling the fallen men. The Hound had vanished behind, though his riderless horse galloped beside them. Tyrion saw Aron Santagar pulled from the saddle, the gold-and-black Baratheon stag torn from his grasp. Ser Balon Swann dropped the Lannister lion to draw his longsword. He slashed right and left as the fallen banner was ripped apart, the thousand ragged pieces swirling away like crimson leaves in a stormwind. In an instant they were gone. Someone staggered in front of Joffrey‚Äôs horse and shrieked as the king rode him down. Whether it had been man, woman, or child Tyrion could not have said. Joffrey was galloping at his side, whey-faced, with Ser Mandon Moore a white shadow on his left.

> And suddenly the madness was behind and they were clattering across the cobbled square that fronted on the castle barbican. A line of spearmen held the gates. Ser Jacelyn was wheeling his lances around for another charge. The spears parted to let the king‚Äôs party pass under the portcullis. Pale red walls loomed up about them, reassuringly high and aswarm with crossbowmen. Tyrion did not recall dismounting. Ser Mandon was helping the shaken king off his horse when Cersei, Tommen, and Lancel rode through the gates with Ser Meryn and Ser Boros close behind. Boros had blood smeared along his blade, while Meryn‚Äôs white cloak had been torn from his back. Ser Balon Swann rode in helmetless, his mount lathered and bleeding at the mouth. Horas Redwyne brought in Lady Tanda, half-crazed with fear for her daughter Lollys, who had been knocked from the saddle and left behind. Lord Gyles, more grey of face than ever, stammered out a tale of seeing the High Septon spilled from his litter, screeching prayers as the crowd swept over him. Jalabhar Xho said he thought he‚Äôd seen Ser Preston Greenfield of the Kingsguard riding back toward the High Septon‚Äôs overturned litter, but he was not certain. Tyrion was dimly aware of a maester asking if he was injured. He pushed his way across the yard to where his nephew stood, his dung-encrusted crown askew. ‚ÄúTraitors,‚Äù Joffrey was babbling excitedly, ‚ÄúI‚Äôll have all their heads, I‚Äôll‚Äî‚Äù The dwarf slapped his flushed face so hard the crown flew from Joffrey‚Äôs head. Then he shoved him with both hands and knocked him sprawling. ‚ÄúYou blind bloody fool.‚Äù ‚ÄúThey were traitors,‚Äù Joffrey squealed from the ground. ‚ÄúThey called me names and attacked me!‚Äù ‚ÄúYou set your dog on them! What did you imagine they would do, bend the knee meekly while the Hound lopped off some limbs? You spoiled witless little boy, you‚Äôve killed Clegane and gods know how many more, and yet you come through unscratched. Damn you!‚Äù And he kicked him. It felt so good he might have done more, but Ser Mandon Moore pulled him off as Joffrey howled, and then Bronn was there to take him in hand. Cersei knelt over her son, while Ser Balon Swann restrained Ser Lancel. Tyrion wrenched free of Bronn‚Äôs grip. ‚ÄúHow many are still out there?‚Äù he shouted to no one and everyone. ‚ÄúMy daughter,‚Äù cried Lady Tanda. ‚ÄúPlease, someone must go back for Lollys¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúSer Preston is not returned,‚Äù Ser Boros Blount reported, ‚Äúnor Aron Santagar.‚Äù ‚ÄúNor Wet Nurse,‚Äù said Ser Horas Redwyne. That was the mocking name the other squires had hung on young Tyrek Lannister. Tyrion glanced round the yard. ‚ÄúWhere‚Äôs the Stark girl?‚Äù For a moment no one answered. Finally Joffrey said, ‚ÄúShe was riding by me. I don‚Äôt know where she went.‚Äù Tyrion pressed blunt fingers into his throbbing temples. If Sansa Stark had come to harm, Jaime was as good as dead.

> The list of the slain was topped by the High Septon, ripped apart as he squealed to his gods for mercy. Starving men take a hard view of priests too fat to walk, Tyrion reflected. Ser Preston‚Äôs corpse had been overlooked at first; the gold cloaks had been searching for a knight in white armor, and he had been stabbed and hacked so cruelly that he was red-brown from head to heel. Ser Aron Santagar had been found in a gutter, his head a red pulp inside a crushed helm. Lady Tanda‚Äôs daughter had surrendered her maidenhood to half a hundred shouting men behind a tanner‚Äôs shop. The gold cloaks found her wandering naked on Sowbelly Row. Tyrek was still missing, as was the High Septon‚Äôs crystal crown. Nine gold cloaks had been slain, two score wounded. No one had troubled to count how many of the mob had died.

> Tyrek Lannister had vanished during the riots in King‚Äôs Landing whilst Jaime himself was still captive at Riverrun. The boy would be fourteen by now, assuming he was still alive. ‚ÄúI led a search myself, at Lord Tywin‚Äôs command,‚Äù offered Addam Marbrand as he boned his fish, ‚Äúbut I found no more than Bywater had before me. The boy was last seen ahorse, when the press of the mob broke the line of gold cloaks. Afterward¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýwell, his palfrey was found, but not the rider. Most like they pulled him down and slew him. But if that‚Äôs so, where is his body? The mob let the other corpses lie, why not his?‚Äù ‚ÄúHe would be of more value alive,‚Äù suggested Strongboar. ‚ÄúAny Lannister would bring a hefty ransom.‚Äù ‚ÄúNo doubt,‚Äù Marbrand agreed, ‚Äúyet no ransom demand was ever made. The boy is simply gone.‚Äù

> Tyrek had served King Robert as a squire, side by side with Lancel. Knowledge could be more valuable than gold, more deadly than a dagger. It was Varys he thought of then, smiling and smelling of lavender. The eunuch had agents and informers all over the city. It would have been a simple matter for him to arrange to have Tyrek snatched during the confusion¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýprovided he knew beforehand that the mob was like to riot. And Varys knew all, or so he would have us believe. Yet he gave Cersei no warning of that riot. Nor did he ride down to the ships to see Myrcella off.

{:#Cortnay-Penrose}
**Cortnay Penrose**

> ‚ÄúAnd what of Edric Storm?‚Äù ‚ÄúMy brother‚Äôs bastard must be surrendered to me.‚Äù ‚ÄúThen my answer is still no, my lord.‚Äù The king clenched his jaw. He said nothing. Melisandre spoke instead. ‚ÄúMay the Lord of Light protect you in your darkness, Ser Cortnay.‚Äù ‚ÄúMay the Others bugger your Lord of Light,‚Äù Penrose spat back, ‚Äúand wipe his arse with that rag you bear.‚Äù Lord Alester Florent cleared his throat. ‚ÄúSer Cortnay, mind your tongue. His Grace means the boy no harm. The child is his own blood, and mine as well. My niece Delena was the mother, as all men know. If you will not trust to the king, trust to me. You know me for a man of honor‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúI know you for a man of ambition,‚Äù Ser Cortnay broke in. ‚ÄúA man who changes kings and gods the way I change my boots. As do these other turncloaks I see before me.‚Äù An angry clamor went up from the king‚Äôs men. He is not far wrong, Davos thought. Only a short time before, the Fossoways, Guyard Morrigen, and the Lords Caron, Varner, Errol, and Estermont had all belonged to Renly. They had sat in his pavilion, helped him make his battle plans, plotted how Stannis might be brought low. And Lord Florent had been with them‚Äîhe might be Queen Selyse‚Äôs own uncle, but that had not kept the Lord of Brightwater from bending his knee to Renly when Renly‚Äôs star was rising.

> ‚ÄúI have heard your proposal, Lord Stannis. Now here is mine.‚Äù He pulled off his glove and flung it full in the king‚Äôs face. ‚ÄúSingle combat. Sword, lance, or any weapon you care to name. Or if you fear to hazard your magic sword and royal skin against an old man, name you a champion, and I shall do the same.‚Äù He gave Guyard Morrigen and Bryce Caron a scathing look. ‚ÄúEither of these pups would do nicely, I should think.‚Äù Ser Guyard Morrigen grew dark with fury. ‚ÄúI will take up the gage, if it please the king.‚Äù ‚ÄúAs would I.‚Äù Bryce Caron looked to Stannis. The king ground his teeth. ‚ÄúNo.‚Äù Ser Cortnay did not seem surprised. ‚ÄúIs it the justice of your cause you doubt, my lord, or the strength of your arm? Are you afraid I‚Äôll piss on your burning sword and put it out?‚Äù ‚ÄúDo you take me for an utter fool, ser?‚Äù asked Stannis. ‚ÄúI have twenty thousand men. You are besieged by land and sea. Why would I choose single combat when my eventual victory is certain?‚Äù The king pointed a finger at him. ‚ÄúI give you fair warning. If you force me to take my castle by storm, you may expect no mercy. I will hang you for traitors, every one of you.‚Äù ‚ÄúAs the gods will it. Bring on your storm, my lord‚Äîand recall, if you do, the name of this castle.‚Äù Ser Cortnay gave a pull on his reins and rode back toward the gate.

> ‚ÄúWhat would you have me do, smuggler?‚Äù Davos considered a moment before he answered. ‚ÄúStrike for King‚Äôs Landing at once.‚Äù The king snorted. ‚ÄúAnd leave Storm‚Äôs End untaken?‚Äù ‚ÄúSer Cortnay does not have the power to harm you. The Lannisters do. A siege would take too long, single combat is too chancy, and an assault would cost thousands of lives with no certainty of success. And there is no need. Once you dethrone Joffrey this castle must come to you with all the rest. It is said about the camp that Lord Tywin Lannister rushes west to rescue Lannisport from the vengeance of the northmen¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúYou have a passing clever father, Devan,‚Äù the king told the boy standing by his elbow. ‚ÄúHe makes me wish I had more smugglers in my service. And fewer lords. Though you are wrong in one respect, Davos. There is a need. If I leave Storm‚Äôs End untaken in my rear, it will be said I was defeated here. And that I cannot permit. Men do not love me as they loved my brothers. They follow me because they fear me¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand defeat is death to fear. The castle must fall.‚Äù His jaw ground side to side. ‚ÄúAye, and quickly. Doran Martell has called his banners and fortified the mountain passes. His Dornishmen are poised to sweep down onto the Marches. And Highgarden is far from spent. My brother left the greater part of his power at Bitterbridge, near sixty thousand foot. I sent my wife‚Äôs brother Ser Errol with Ser Parmen Crane to take them under my command, but they have not returned. I fear that Ser Loras Tyrell reached Bitterbridge before my envoys, and took that host for his own.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúStubborn or craven, what does it matter? Ser Cortnay Penrose seemed hale and hearty to me.‚Äù ‚ÄúSo did my brother, the day before his death. The night is dark and full of terrors, Davos.‚Äù Davos Seaworth felt the small hairs rising on the back of his neck. ‚ÄúMy lord, I do not understand you.‚Äù ‚ÄúI do not require your understanding. Only your service. Ser Cortnay will be dead within the day. Melisandre has seen it in the flames of the future. His death and the manner of it. He will not die in knightly combat, needless to say.‚Äù Stannis held out his cup, and Devan filled it again from the flagon. ‚ÄúHer flames do not lie. She saw Renly‚Äôs doom as well. On Dragonstone she saw it, and told Selyse. Lord Velaryon and your friend Salladhor Saan would have had me sail against Joffrey, but Melisandre told me that if I went to Storm‚Äôs End, I would win the best part of my brother‚Äôs power, and she was right.‚Äù ‚ÄúB-but,‚Äù Davos stammered, ‚ÄúLord Renly only came here because you had laid siege to the castle. He was marching toward King‚Äôs Landing before, against the Lannisters, he would have‚Äî‚Äù Stannis shifted in his seat, frowning. ‚ÄúWas, would have, what is that? He did what he did. He came here with his banners and his peaches, to his doom¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand it was well for me he did. Melisandre saw another day in her flames as well. A morrow where Renly rode out of the south in his green armor to smash my host beneath the walls of King‚Äôs Landing. Had I met my brother there, it might have been me who died in place of him.‚Äù ‚ÄúOr you might have joined your strength to his to bring down the Lannisters,‚Äù Davos protested. ‚ÄúWhy not that? If she saw two futures, well¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýboth cannot be true.‚Äù King Stannis pointed a finger. ‚ÄúThere you err, Onion Knight. Some lights cast more than one shadow. Stand before the nightfire and you‚Äôll see for yourself. The flames shift and dance, never still. The shadows grow tall and short, and every man casts a dozen. Some are fainter than others, that‚Äôs all. Well, men cast their shadows across the future as well. One shadow or many. Melisandre sees them all.

> And so it was that he found himself once more crossing Shipbreaker Bay in the dark of night, steering a tiny boat with a black sail. The sky was the same, and the sea. The same salt smell was in the air, and the water chuckling against the hull was just as he remembered it. A thousand flickering campfires burned around the castle, as the fires of the Tyrells and Redwynes had sixteen years before. But all the rest was different. The last time it was life I brought to Storm‚Äôs End, shaped to look like onions. This time it is death, in the shape of Melisandre of Asshai. Sixteen years ago, the sails had cracked and snapped with every shift of wind, until he‚Äôd pulled them down and gone on with muffled oars. Even so, his heart had been in his gullet. The men on the Redwyne galleys had grown lax after so long, however, and they had slipped through the cordon smooth as black satin. This time, the only ships in sight belonged to Stannis, and the only danger would come from watchers on the castle walls. Even so, Davos was taut as a bowstring.

> The wind was shifting, Davos could feel it, see it in the way the black canvas rippled. He reached for the halyards. ‚ÄúHelp me bring in the sail. I‚Äôll row us the rest of the way.‚Äù Together they tied off the sail as the boat rocked beneath them. As Davos unshipped the oars and slid them into the choppy black water, he said, ‚ÄúWho rowed you to Renly?‚Äù ‚ÄúThere was no need,‚Äù she said. ‚ÄúHe was unprotected. But here¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthis Storm‚Äôs End is an old place. There are spells woven into the stones. Dark walls that no shadow can pass‚Äîancient, forgotten, yet still in place.‚Äù ‚ÄúShadow?‚Äù Davos felt his flesh prickling. ‚ÄúA shadow is a thing of darkness.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou are more ignorant than a child, ser knight. There are no shadows in the dark. Shadows are the servants of light, the children of fire. The brightest flame casts the darkest shadows.‚Äù Frowning, Davos hushed her then. They were coming close to shore once more, and voices carried across the water. He rowed, the faint sound of his oars lost in the rhythm of the waves. The seaward side of Storm‚Äôs End perched upon a pale white cliff, the chalky stone sloping up steeply to half again the height of the massive curtain wall. A mouth yawned in the cliff, and it was that Davos steered for, as he had sixteen years before. The tunnel opened on a cavern under the castle, where the storm lords of old had built their landing. The passage was navigable only during high tide, and was never less than treacherous, but his smuggler‚Äôs skills had not deserted him. Davos threaded their way deftly between the jagged rocks until the cave mouth loomed up before them. He let the waves carry them inside. They crashed around him, slamming the boat this way and that and soaking them to the skin. A half-seen finger of rock came rushing up out of the gloom, snarling foam, and Davos barely kept them off it with an oar. Then they were past, engulfed in darkness, and the waters smoothed. The little boat slowed and swirled. The sound of their breathing echoed until it seemed to surround them. Davos had not expected the blackness. The last time, torches had burned all along the tunnel, and the eyes of starving men had peered down through the murder holes in the ceiling. The portcullis was somewhere ahead, he knew. Davos used the oars to slow them, and they drifted against it almost gently. ‚ÄúThis is as far as we go, unless you have a man inside to lift the gate for us.‚Äù His whispers scurried across the lapping water like a line of mice on soft pink feet.

> ‚ÄúHave we passed within the walls?‚Äù ‚ÄúYes. Beneath. But we can go no farther. The portcullis goes all the way to the bottom. And the bars are too closely spaced for even a child to squeeze through.‚Äù There was no answer but a soft rustling. And then a light bloomed amidst the darkness. Davos raised a hand to shield his eyes, and his breath caught in his throat. Melisandre had thrown back her cowl and shrugged out of the smothering robe. Beneath, she was naked, and huge with child. Swollen breasts hung heavy against her chest, and her belly bulged as if near to bursting. ‚ÄúGods preserve us,‚Äù he whispered, and heard her answering laugh, deep and throaty. Her eyes were hot coals, and the sweat that dappled her skin seemed to glow with a light of its own. Melisandre shone. Panting, she squatted and spread her legs. Blood ran down her thighs, black as ink. Her cry might have been agony or ecstasy or both. And Davos saw the crown of the child‚Äôs head push its way out of her. Two arms wriggled free, grasping, black fingers coiling around Melisandre‚Äôs straining thighs, pushing, until the whole of the shadow slid out into the world and rose taller than Davos, tall as the tunnel, towering above the boat. He had only an instant to look at it before it was gone, twisting between the bars of the portcullis and racing across the surface of the water, but that instant was long enough. He knew that shadow. As he knew the man who‚Äôd cast it.

> "Now tell me how Cortnay Penrose died.‚Äù ‚ÄúIt is said that he threw himself from a tower.‚Äù ‚ÄúThrew himself? No, I will not believe that!‚Äù ‚ÄúHis guards saw no man enter his chambers, nor did they find any within afterward.‚Äù ‚ÄúThen the killer entered earlier and hid under the bed,‚Äù Tyrion suggested, ‚Äúor he climbed down from the roof on a rope. Perhaps the guards are lying. Who‚Äôs to say they did not do the thing themselves?‚Äù ‚ÄúDoubtless you are right, my lord.‚Äù His smug tone said otherwise. ‚ÄúBut you do not think so? How was it done, then?‚Äù For a long moment Varys said nothing. The only sound was the stately clack of horseshoes on cobbles. Finally the eunuch cleared his throat. ‚ÄúMy lord, do you believe in the old powers?‚Äù ‚ÄúMagic, you mean?‚Äù Tyrion said impatiently. ‚ÄúBlood-spells, curses, shapeshifting, those sorts of things?‚Äù He snorted. ‚ÄúDo you mean to suggest that Ser Cortnay was magicked to his death?‚Äù ‚ÄúSer Cortnay had challenged Lord Stannis to single combat on the morning he died. I ask you, is this the act of a man lost to despair? Then there is the matter of Lord Renly‚Äôs mysterious and most fortuitous murder, even as his battle lines were forming up to sweep his brother from the field.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúLord Commander,‚Äù Ser Loras said, ‚Äúyou wished to see the Maid of Tarth?‚Äù ‚ÄúI did.‚Äù Jaime waved them closer with his left hand. ‚ÄúYou have talked with her, I take it?‚Äù ‚ÄúAs you commanded, my lord.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd?‚Äù The lad tensed. ‚ÄúI¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýit may be it happened as she says, ser. That it was Stannis. I cannot be certain.‚Äù ‚ÄúVarys tells me that the castellan of Storm‚Äôs End perished strangely as well,‚Äù said Jaime. ‚ÄúSer Cortnay Penrose,‚Äù said Brienne sadly. ‚ÄúA good man.‚Äù ‚ÄúA stubborn man. One day he stood square in the way of the King of Dragonstone. The next he leapt from a tower.‚Äù Jaime stood. ‚ÄúSer Loras, we will talk more of this later. You may leave Brienne with me.‚Äù

{:#Alfyn-Crowkiller}
**Alfyn Crowkiller**

> Jon knew Qhorin Halfhand the instant he saw him, though they had never met. The big ranger was half a legend in the Watch; a man of slow words and swift action, tall and straight as a spear, long-limbed and solemn. Unlike his men, he was clean-shaven. His hair fell from beneath his helm in a heavy braid touched with hoarfrost, and the blacks he wore were so faded they might have been greys. Only thumb and forefinger remained on the hand that held the reins; the other fingers had been sheared off catching a wildling‚Äôs axe that would otherwise have split his skull. It was told that he had thrust his maimed fist into the face of the axeman so the blood spurted into his eyes, and slew him while he was blind. Since that day, the Wildlings beyond the Wall had known no foe more implacable. Jon hailed him. ‚ÄúLord Commander Mormont would see you at once. I‚Äôll show you to his tent.‚Äù Qhorin swung down from his saddle. ‚ÄúMy men are hungry, and our horses require tending.‚Äù ‚ÄúThey‚Äôll all be seen to.‚Äù The ranger gave his horse into the care of one of his men and followed. ‚ÄúYou are Jon Snow. You have your father‚Äôs look.‚Äù ‚ÄúDid you know him, my lord?‚Äù ‚ÄúI am no lordling. Only a brother of the Night‚Äôs Watch. I knew Lord Eddard, yes. And his father before him.‚Äù Jon had to hurry his steps to keep up with Qhorin‚Äôs long strides. ‚ÄúLord Rickard died before I was born.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe was a friend to the Watch.‚Äù Qhorin glanced behind. ‚ÄúIt is said that a direwolf runs with you.‚Äù ‚ÄúGhost should be back by dawn. He hunts at night.‚Äù They found Dolorous Edd frying a rasher of bacon and boiling a dozen eggs in a kettle over the Old Bear‚Äôs cookfire. Mormont sat in his wood-and-leather camp chair. ‚ÄúI had begun to fear for you. Did you meet with trouble?‚Äù ‚ÄúWe met with Alfyn Crowkiller. Mance had sent him to scout along the Wall, and we chanced on him returning.‚Äù Qhorin removed his helm. ‚ÄúAlfyn will trouble the realm no longer, but some of his company escaped us. We hunted down as many as we could, but it may be that a few will win back to the mountains.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd the cost?‚Äù ‚ÄúFour brothers dead. A dozen wounded. A third as many as the foe. And we took captives. One died quickly from his wounds, but the other lived long enough to be questioned.‚Äù ‚ÄúBest talk of this inside. Jon will fetch you a horn of ale. Or would you prefer hot spiced wine?‚Äù ‚ÄúBoiled water will suffice. An egg and a bite of bacon.‚Äù ‚ÄúAs you wish.‚Äù Mormont lifted the flap of the tent and Qhorin Halfhand stooped and stepped through. Edd stood over the kettle swishing the eggs about with a spoon. ‚ÄúI envy those eggs,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúI could do with a bit of boiling about now. If the kettle were larger, I might jump in. Though I would sooner it were wine than water. There are worse ways to die than warm and drunk. I knew a brother drowned himself in wine once. It was a poor vintage, though, and his corpse did not improve it.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou drank the wine?‚Äù ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs an awful thing to find a brother dead. You‚Äôd have need of a drink as well, Lord Snow.‚Äù Edd stirred the kettle and added a pinch more nutmeg. Restless, Jon squatted by the fire and poked at it with a stick. He could hear the Old Bear‚Äôs voice inside the tent, punctuated by the raven‚Äôs squawks and Qhorin Halfhand‚Äôs quieter tones, but he could not make out the words. Alfyn Crowkiller dead, that‚Äôs good. He was one of the bloodiest of the Wildling raiders, taking his name from the black brothers he‚Äôd slain. So why does Qhorin sound so grave, after such a victory? Jon had hoped that the arrival of men from the Shadow Tower would lift the spirits in the camp. Only last night, he was coming back through the dark from a piss when he heard five or six men talking in low voices around the embers of a fire. When he heard Chett muttering that it was past time they turned back, Jon stopped to listen. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs an old man‚Äôs folly, this ranging,‚Äù he heard. ‚ÄúWe‚Äôll find nothing but our graves in them mountains.‚Äù ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs giants in the Frostfangs, and wargs, and worse things,‚Äù said Lark the Sisterman. ‚ÄúI‚Äôll not be going there, I promise you.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe Old Bear‚Äôs not like to give you a choice.‚Äù ‚ÄúMight be we won‚Äôt give him one,‚Äù said Chett.

{:#Leo-Lefford}
**Leo Lefford**

> It was three days later when the hammer blow that Brienne had foretold fell, and five days before they heard of it. Catelyn was sitting with her father when Edmure‚Äôs messenger arrived. The man‚Äôs armor was dinted, his boots dusty, and he had a ragged hole in his surcoat, but the look on his face as he knelt was enough to tell her that the news was good. ‚ÄúVictory, my lady.‚Äù He handed her Edmure‚Äôs letter. Her hand trembled as she broke the seal. Lord Tywin had tried to force a crossing at a dozen different fords, her brother wrote, but every thrust had been thrown back. Lord Lefford had been drowned, the Crakehall knight called Strongboar taken captive, Ser Addam Marbrand thrice forced to retreat ‚Ä¶ but the fiercest battle had been fought at Stone Mill, where Ser Gregor Clegane had led the assault. So many of his men had fallen that their dead horses threatened to dam the flow. In the end the Mountain and a handful of his best had gained the west bank, but Edmure had thrown his reserve at them, and they had shattered and reeled away bloody and beaten. Ser Gregor himself had lost his horse and staggered back across the Red Fork bleeding from a dozen wounds while a rain of arrows and stones fell all around him. ‚ÄúThey shall not cross, Cat,‚Äù Edmure scrawled, ‚ÄúLord Tywin is marching to the southeast. A feint perhaps, or full retreat, it matters not. They shall not cross.‚Äù

> Edmure was filling his uncle‚Äôs ear with the whole story of the fight at the Stone Mill. It was only after the servants had come and gone that the Blackfish cleared his throat and said, ‚ÄúI think we‚Äôve all heard sufficient of your boasting, Nephew.‚Äù Edmure was taken aback. ‚ÄúBoasting? What do you mean?‚Äù ‚ÄúI mean,‚Äù said the Blackfish, ‚Äúthat you owe His Grace your thanks for his forbearance. He played out that mummer‚Äôs farce in the Great Hall so as not to shame you before your own people. Had it been me I would have flayed you for your stupidity rather than praising this folly of the fords.‚Äù ‚ÄúGood men died to defend those fords, Uncle.‚Äù Edmure sounded outraged. ‚ÄúWhat, is no one to win victories but the Young Wolf? Did I steal some glory meant for you, Robb?‚Äù ‚ÄúYour Grace,‚Äù Robb corrected, icy. ‚ÄúYou took me for your king, Uncle. Or have you forgotten that as well?‚Äù The Blackfish said, ‚ÄúYou were commanded to hold Riverrun, Edmure, no more.‚Äù ‚ÄúI held Riverrun, and I bloodied Lord Tywin‚Äôs nose‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúSo you did,‚Äù said Robb. ‚ÄúBut a bloody nose won‚Äôt win the war, will it? Did you ever think to ask yourself why we remained in the west so long after Oxcross? You knew I did not have enough men to threaten Lannisport or Casterly Rock.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhy¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthere were other castles¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýgold, cattle¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúYou think we stayed for plunder?‚Äù Robb was incredulous. ‚ÄúUncle, I wanted Lord Tywin to come west.‚Äù ‚ÄúWe were all horsed,‚Äù Ser Brynden said. ‚ÄúThe Lannister host was mainly foot. We planned to run Lord Tywin a merry chase up and down the coast, then slip behind him to take up a strong defensive position athwart the gold road, at a place my scouts had found where the ground would have been greatly in our favor. If he had come at us there, he would have paid a grievous price. But if he did not attack, he would have been trapped in the west, a thousand leagues from where he needed to be. All the while we would have lived off his land, instead of him living off ours.‚Äù ‚ÄúLord Stannis was about to fall upon King‚Äôs Landing,‚Äù Robb said. ‚ÄúHe might have rid us of Joffrey, the queen, and the Imp in one red stroke. Then we might have been able to make a peace.‚Äù Edmure looked from uncle to nephew. ‚ÄúYou never told me.‚Äù ‚ÄúI told you to hold Riverrun,‚Äù said Robb. ‚ÄúWhat part of that command did you fail to comprehend?‚Äù ‚ÄúWhen you stopped Lord Tywin on the Red Fork,‚Äù said the Blackfish, ‚Äúyou delayed him just long enough for riders out of Bitterbridge to reach him with word of what was happening to the east. Lord Tywin turned his host at once, joined up with Matthis Rowan and Randyll Tarly near the headwaters of the Blackwater, and made a forced march to Tumbler‚Äôs Falls, where he found Mace Tyrell and two of his sons waiting with a huge host and a fleet of barges. They floated down the river, disembarked half a day‚Äôs ride from the city, and took Stannis in the rear.‚Äù

{:#Alebelly}
**Alebelly**

> ‚ÄúIt is the sea that comes.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe sea?‚Äù ‚ÄúI dreamed that the sea was lapping all around Winterfell. I saw black waves crashing against the gates and towers, and then the salt water came flowing over the walls and filled the castle. Drowned men were floating in the yard. When I first dreamed the dream, back at Greywater, I didn‚Äôt know their faces, but now I do. That Alebelly is one, the guard who called our names at the feast. Your septon‚Äôs another. Your smith as well.‚Äù ‚ÄúMikken?‚Äù Bran was as confused as he was dismayed. ‚ÄúBut the sea is hundreds and hundreds of leagues away, and Winterfell‚Äôs walls are so high the water couldn‚Äôt get in even if it did come.‚Äù ‚ÄúIn the dark of night the salt sea will flow over these walls,‚Äù said Jojen. ‚ÄúI saw the dead, bloated and drowned.‚Äù ‚ÄúWe have to tell them,‚Äù Bran said. ‚ÄúAlebelly and Mikken, and Septon Chayle. Tell them not to drown.‚Äù ‚ÄúIt will not save them,‚Äù replied the boy in green.

> The sea has come. It‚Äôs flowing over the walls, just as Jojen saw. Bran grabbed the bar overhead and pulled himself up, shouting for help. No one came, and after a moment he remembered that no one would. They had taken the guard off his door. Ser Rodrik had needed every man of fighting age he could lay his hands on, so Winterfell had been left with only a token garrison. The rest had left eight days past, six hundred men from Winterfell and the nearest holdfasts. Cley Cerwyn was bringing three hundred more to join them on the march, and Maester Luwin had sent ravens before them, summoning levies from White Harbor and the barrow-lands and even the deep places inside the wolfswood. Torrhen‚Äôs Square was under attack by some monstrous war chief named Dagmer Cleftjaw. Old Nan said he couldn‚Äôt be killed, that once a foe had cut his head in two with an axe, but Dagmer was so fierce he‚Äôd just pushed the two halves back together and held them until they healed up. Could Dagmer have won? Torrhen‚Äôs Square was many days from Winterfell, yet still¬Ý‚Ä¶

> The maester set down the candle and wiped the blood off his cheek. ‚ÄúThey swam the moat. Climbed the walls with hook and rope. Came over wet and dripping, steel in hand.‚Äù He sat on the chair by the door, as fresh blood flowed. ‚ÄúAlebelly was on the gate, they surprised him in the turret and killed him. Hayhead‚Äôs wounded as well. I had time to send off two ravens before they burst in. The bird to White Harbor got away, but they brought down the other with an arrow.‚Äù The maester stared at the rushes. ‚ÄúSer Rodrik took too many of our men, but I am to blame as much as he is. I never saw this danger, I never¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù Jojen saw it, Bran thought.

{:#Mikken}
**Mikken**

> ‚ÄúI‚Äôve yielded Winterfell to Theon.‚Äù ‚ÄúLouder, Bran. And call me prince.‚Äù He raised his voice. ‚ÄúI have yielded Winterfell to Prince Theon. All of you should do as he commands you.‚Äù ‚ÄúDamned if I will!‚Äù bellowed Mikken. Theon ignored the outburst. ‚ÄúMy father has donned the ancient crown of salt and rock, and declared himself King of the Iron Islands. He claims the north as well, by right of conquest. You are all his subjects.‚Äù ‚ÄúBugger that.‚Äù Mikken wiped the blood from his mouth. ‚ÄúI serve the Starks, not some treasonous squid of‚Äîaah.‚Äù The butt of the spear smashed him face first into the stone floor. ‚ÄúSmiths have strong arms and weak heads,‚Äù observed Theon. ‚ÄúBut if the rest of you serve me as loyally as you served Ned Stark, you‚Äôll find me as generous a lord as you could want.‚Äù On his hands and knees, Mikken spat blood. Please don‚Äôt, Bran wished at him, but the blacksmith shouted, ‚ÄúIf you think you can hold the north with this sorry lot o‚Äô‚Äî‚Äù The bald man drove the point of his spear into the back of Mikken‚Äôs neck. Steel slid through flesh and came out his throat in a welter of blood. A woman screamed, and Meera wrapped her arms around Rickon. It‚Äôs blood he drowned on, Bran thought numbly. His own blood. ‚ÄúWho else has something to say?‚Äù asked Theon Greyjoy.

{:#Amory-Lorch}
**Amory Lorch**

> Four Brave Companions climbed to the ramparts and hauled down the lion of Lannister and Ser Amory‚Äôs own black manticore. In their place they raised the flayed man of the Dreadfort and the direwolf of Stark. And that evening, a page named Nan poured wine for Roose Bolton and Vargo Hoat as they stood on the gallery, watching the Brave Companions parade Ser Amory Lorch naked through the middle ward. Ser Amory pleaded and sobbed and clung to the legs of his captors, until Rorge pulled him loose, and Shagwell kicked him down into the bear pit. The bear is all in black, Arya thought. Like Yoren. She filled Roose Bolton‚Äôs cup, and did not spill a drop.

{:#Septon-Chayle}
**Septon Chayle**

> Outside he heard sobbing as the castle folk were pulled from their beds and driven into the yard. I‚Äôll give them reason to sob. I‚Äôve used them gently, and this is how they repay me. He‚Äôd even had two of his own men whipped bloody for raping that kennel girl, to show them he meant to be just. They still blame me for the rape, though. And the rest. He deemed that unfair. Mikken had killed himself with his mouth, just as Benfred had. As for Chayle, he had to give someone to the Drowned God, his men expected it. ‚ÄúI bear you no ill will,‚Äù he‚Äôd told the septon before they threw him down the well, ‚Äúbut you and your gods have no place here now.‚Äù You‚Äôd think the others might be grateful he hadn‚Äôt chosen one of them, but no. He wondered how many of them were part of this plot against him.

{:#Drennan-and-Squint}
**Drennan and Squint**

> Urzen returned with Black Lorren. ‚ÄúThe Hunter‚Äôs Gate,‚Äù Lorren said. ‚ÄúBest come see.‚Äù The Hunter‚Äôs Gate was conveniently sited close to the kennels and kitchens. It opened directly on fields and forests, allowing riders to come and go without first passing through the winter town, and so was favored by hunting parties. ‚ÄúWho had the guard here?‚Äù Theon demanded. ‚ÄúDrennan and Squint.‚Äù Drennan was one of the men who‚Äôd raped Palla. ‚ÄúIf they‚Äôve let the boys escape, I‚Äôll have more than a little skin off their back this time, I swear it.‚Äù ‚ÄúNo need for that,‚Äù Black Lorren said curtly. Nor was there. They found Squint floating facedown in the moat, his entrails drifting behind him like a nest of pale snakes. Drennan lay half-naked in the gatehouse, in the snug room where the drawbridge was worked. His throat had been opened ear to ear. A ragged tunic concealed the half-healed scars on his back, but his boots were scattered amidst the rushes, and his breeches tangled about his feet. There was cheese on a small table near the door, beside an empty flagon. And two cups. Theon picked one up and sniffed at the dregs of wine in the bottom. ‚ÄúSquint was up on the wallwalk, no?‚Äù ‚ÄúAye,‚Äù said Lorren. Theon flung the cup into the hearth. ‚ÄúI‚Äôd say Drennan was pulling down his breeches to stick it in the woman when she stuck it in him. His own cheese knife, by the look of it. Someone find a pike and fish the other fool out of the moat.‚Äù The other fool was in a deal worse shape than Drennan. When Black Lorren drew him out of the water, they saw that one of his arms had been wrenched off at the elbow, half of his neck was missing, and there was a ragged hole where his navel and groin once had been. The pike tore through his bowels as Lorren was pulling him in. The stench was awful. ‚ÄúThe direwolves,‚Äù Theon said. ‚ÄúBoth of them, at a guess.‚Äù Disgusted, he walked back to the drawbridge.

{:#Orell}
**Orell**

> One was asleep, curled up tight and buried beneath a great mound of skins. Jon could see nothing of him but his hair, bright red in the firelight. The second sat close to the flames, feeding them twigs and branches and complaining of the wind in a querulous tone. The third watched the pass, though there was little to see, only a vast bowl of darkness ringed by the snowy shoulders of the mountains. It was the watcher who wore the horn. Three. For a moment Jon was uncertain. There was only supposed to be two. One was asleep, though. And whether there was two or three or twenty, he still must do what he had come to do. Stonesnake touched his arm, pointed at the wildling with the horn. Jon nodded toward the one by the fire. It felt queer, picking a man to kill. Half the days of his life had been spent with sword and shield, training for this moment. Did Robb feel this way before his first battle? he wondered, but there was no time to ponder the question. Stonesnake moved as fast as his namesake, leaping down on the wildlings in a rain of pebbles. Jon slid Longclaw from its sheath and followed. It all seemed to happen in a heartbeat. Afterward Jon could admire the courage of the wildling who reached first for his horn instead of his blade. He got it to his lips, but before he could sound it Stonesnake knocked the horn aside with a swipe of his shortsword. Jon‚Äôs man leapt to his feet, thrusting at his face with a burning brand. He could feel the heat of the flames as he flinched back. Out of the corner of his eye, he saw the sleeper stirring, and knew he must finish his man quick. When the brand swung again, he bulled into it, swinging the bastard sword with both hands. The Valyrian steel sheared through leather, fur, wool, and flesh, but when the wildling fell he twisted, ripping the sword from Jon‚Äôs grasp. On the ground the sleeper sat up beneath his furs. Jon slid his dirk free, grabbing the man by the hair and jamming the point of the knife up under his chin as he reached for his‚Äîno, her‚Äî His hand froze. ‚ÄúA girl.‚Äù ‚ÄúA watcher,‚Äù said Stonesnake. ‚ÄúA wildling. Finish her.‚Äù Jon could see fear and fire in her eyes. Blood ran down her white throat from where the point of his dirk had pricked her. One thrust and it‚Äôs done, he told himself. He was so close he could smell onion on her breath. She is no older than I am. Something about her made him think of Arya, though they looked nothing at all alike. ‚ÄúWill you yield?‚Äù he asked, giving the dirk a half turn. And if she doesn‚Äôt? ‚ÄúI yield.‚Äù Her words steamed in the cold air. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôre our captive, then.‚Äù He pulled the dirk away from the soft skin of her throat.

> The skinchanger was grey-faced, round-shouldered, and bald, a mouse of a man with a wolfling‚Äôs eyes. ‚ÄúOnce a horse is broken to the saddle, any man can mount him,‚Äù he said in a soft voice. ‚ÄúOnce a beast‚Äôs been joined to a man, any skinchanger can slip inside and ride him. Orell was withering inside his feathers, so I took the eagle for my own. But the joining works both ways, warg. Orell lives inside me now, whispering how much he hates you. And I can soar above the Wall, and see with eagle eyes.‚Äù

{:#Bael-the-Bard}
**Bael the Bard**

> ‚ÄúWho was your mother?‚Äù ‚ÄúSome woman. Most of them are.‚Äù Someone had said that to him once. He did not remember who. She smiled again, a flash of white teeth. ‚ÄúAnd she never sung you the song o‚Äô the winter rose?‚Äù ‚ÄúI never knew my mother. Or any such song.‚Äù ‚ÄúBael the Bard made it,‚Äù said Ygritte. ‚ÄúHe was King-beyond-the-Wall a long time back. All the free folk know his songs, but might be you don‚Äôt sing them in the south.‚Äù ‚ÄúWinterfell‚Äôs not in the south,‚Äù Jon objected. ‚ÄúYes it is. Everything below the Wall‚Äôs south to us.‚Äù He had never thought of it that way. ‚ÄúI suppose it‚Äôs all in where you‚Äôre standing.‚Äù ‚ÄúAye,‚Äù Ygritte agreed. ‚ÄúIt always is.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúWell, long before he was king over the free folk, Bael was a great raider.‚Äù Stonesnake gave a snort. ‚ÄúA murderer, robber, and raper, is what you mean.‚Äù ‚ÄúThat‚Äôs all in where you‚Äôre standing too,‚Äù Ygritte said. ‚ÄúThe Stark in Winterfell wanted Bael‚Äôs head, but never could take him, and the taste o‚Äô failure galled him. One day in his bitterness he called Bael a craven who preyed only on the weak. When word o‚Äô that got back, Bael vowed to teach the lord a lesson. So he scaled the Wall, skipped down the kingsroad, and walked into Winterfell one winter‚Äôs night with harp in hand, naming himself Sygerrik of Skagos. Sygerrik means ‚Äòdeceiver‚Äô in the Old Tongue, that the First Men spoke, and the giants still speak. ‚ÄúNorth or south, singers always find a ready welcome, so Bael ate at Lord Stark‚Äôs own table, and played for the lord in his high seat until half the night was gone. The old songs he played, and new ones he‚Äôd made himself, and he played and sang so well that when he was done, the lord offered to let him name his own reward. ‚ÄòAll I ask is a flower,‚Äô Bael answered, ‚Äòthe fairest flower that blooms in the gardens o‚Äô Winterfell.‚Äô ‚ÄúNow as it happened the winter roses had only then come into bloom, and no flower is so rare nor precious. So the Stark sent to his glass gardens and commanded that the most beautiful o‚Äô the winter roses be plucked for the singer‚Äôs payment. And so it was done. But when morning come, the singer had vanished ‚Ä¶ and so had Lord Brandon‚Äôs maiden daughter. Her bed they found empty, but for the pale blue rose that Bael had left on the pillow where her head had lain.‚Äù
