> The captain‚Äôs sister found her in the common room, drinking a cup of milk and honey with three raw eggs mixed in. ‚ÄúYou did beautifully,‚Äù she said, when the woman showed her the freshly painted shield. It was more a picture than a proper coat of arms, and the sight of it took her back through the long years, to the cool dark of her father‚Äôs armory. She remembered how she‚Äôd run her fingertips across the cracked and fading paint, over the green leaves of the tree, and along the path of the falling star. Brienne paid the captain‚Äôs sister half again the sum they had agreed, and slung the shield across one shoulder when she left the inn, after buying some hardbread, cheese, and flour from the cook. She left the town by the north gate, riding slowly through the fields and farms where the worst of the fighting had been, when the wolves came down on Duskendale. Lord Randyll Tarly had commanded Joffrey‚Äôs army, made up of westermen and stormlanders and knights from the Reach. Those men of his who had died here had been carried back inside the walls, to rest in heroes‚Äô tombs beneath the septs of Duskendale. The northern dead, far more numerous, were buried in a common grave beside the sea. Above the cairn that marked their resting place, the victors had raised a rough-hewn wooden marker. HERE LIE THE WOLVES was all it said. Brienne stopped beside it and said a silent prayer for them, and for Catelyn Stark and her son Robb and all the men who‚Äôd died with them as well.

{:#The-Reynes-of-Castamere-and-the-Tarbecks-of-Tarbeck-Hall}
**The Reynes of Castamere and the Tarbecks of Tarbeck Hall**

> This Westerling betrayal did not seem to have enraged his father as much as Tyrion would have expected. Lord Tywin did not suffer disloyalty in his vassals. He had extinguished the proud Reynes of Castamere and the ancient Tarbecks of Tarbeck Hall root and branch when he was still half a boy. The singers had even made a rather gloomy song of it. Some years later, when Lord Farman of Faircastle grew truculent, Lord Tywin sent an envoy bearing a lute instead of a letter. But once he‚Äôd heard ‚ÄúThe Rains of Castamere‚Äù echoing through his hall, Lord Farman gave no further trouble. And if the song were not enough, the shattered castles of the Reynes and Tarbecks still stood as mute testimony to the fate that awaited those who chose to scorn the power of Casterly Rock. ‚ÄúThe Crag is not so far from Tarbeck Hall and Castamere,‚Äù Tyrion pointed out. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôd think the Westerlings might have ridden past and seen the lesson there.‚Äù ‚ÄúMayhaps they have,‚Äù Lord Tywin said. ‚ÄúThey are well aware of Castamere, I promise you.‚Äù

> My father. Jaime wondered whether Lord Tywin had received the goat‚Äôs demand for ransom, with or without his rotted hand. What is a swordsman worth without his sword hand? Half the gold in Casterly Rock? Three hundred dragons? Or nothing? His father had never been unduly swayed by sentiment. Tywin Lannister‚Äôs own father Lord Tytos had once imprisoned an unruly bannerman, Lord Tarbeck. The redoubtable Lady Tarbeck responded by capturing three Lannisters, including young Stafford, whose sister was betrothed to cousin Tywin. ‚ÄúSend back my lord and love, or these three shall answer for any harm that comes him,‚Äù she had written to Casterly Rock. Young Tywin suggested his father oblige by sending back Lord Tarbeck in three pieces. Lord Tytos was a gentler sort of lion, however, so Lady Tarbeck won a few more years for her muttonheaded lord, and Stafford wed and bred and blundered on till Oxcross. But Tywin Lannister endured, eternal as Casterly Rock. And now you have a cripple for a son as well as a dwarf, my lord. How you will hate that¬Ý‚Ä¶

{:#Tion-Frey-and-Willem-Lannister}
**Tion Frey and Willem Lannister**

> She stood at Robb‚Äôs left hand beside the high seat, and for a moment felt almost as if she were looking down at her own dead, at Bran and Rickon. These boys had been much older, but death had shrunken them. Naked and wet, they seemed such little things, so still it was hard to remember them living. The blond boy had been trying to grow a beard. Pale yellow peach fuzz covered his cheeks and jaw above the red ruin the knife had made of his throat. His long golden hair was still wet, as if he had been pulled from a bath. By the look of him, he had died peacefully, perhaps in sleep, but his brown-haired cousin had fought for life. His arms bore slashes where he‚Äôd tried to block the blades, and red still trickled slowly from the stab wounds that covered his chest and belly and back like so many tongueless mouths, though the rain had washed him almost clean. Robb had donned his crown before coming to the hall, and the bronze shone darkly in the torchlight. Shadows hid his eyes as he looked upon the dead. Does he see Bran and Rickon as well? She might have wept, but there were no tears left in her. The dead boys were pale from long imprisonment, and both had been fair; against their smooth white skin, the blood was shockingly red, unbearable to look upon. Will they lay Sansa down naked beneath the Iron Throne after they have killed her? Will her skin seem as white, her blood as red? From outside came the steady wash of rain and the restless howling of a wolf.

{:#Delp-and-Elwood}
**Delp and Elwood**

> She looked down again upon the corpses of the squires Tion Frey and Willem Lannister, and waited for her son to speak. It seemed a very long time before Robb lifted his eyes from the bloody dead. ‚ÄúSmalljon,‚Äù he said, ‚Äútell your father to bring them in.‚Äù Wordless, Smalljon Umber turned to obey, his steps echoing in the great stone hall. As the Greatjon marched his prisoners through the doors, Catelyn made note of how some other men stepped back to give them room, as if treason could somehow be passed by a touch, a glance, a cough. The captors and the captives looked much alike; big men, every one, with thick beards and long hair. Two of the Greatjon‚Äôs men were wounded, and three of their prisoners. Only the fact that some had spears and others empty scabbards served to set them apart. All were clad in mail hauberks or shirts of sewn rings, with heavy boots and thick cloaks, some of wool and some of fur. The north is hard and cold, and has no mercy, Ned had told her when she first came to Winterfell a thousand years ago. ‚ÄúFive,‚Äù said Robb when the prisoners stood before him, wet and silent. ‚ÄúIs that all of them?‚Äù ‚ÄúThere were eight,‚Äù rumbled the Greatjon. ‚ÄúWe killed two taking them, and a third is dying now.‚Äù Robb studied the faces of the captives. ‚ÄúIt required eight of you to kill two unarmed squires.‚Äù Edmure Tully spoke up. ‚ÄúThey murdered two of my men as well, to get into the tower. Delp and Elwood.‚Äù ‚ÄúIt was no murder, ser,‚Äù said Lord Rickard Karstark, no more discomfited by the ropes about his wrists than by the blood that trickled down his face. ‚ÄúAny man who steps between a father and his vengeance asks for death.‚Äù His words rang against Catelyn‚Äôs ears, harsh and cruel as the pounding of a war drum. Her throat was dry as bone. I did this. These two boys died so my daughters might live. ‚ÄúI saw your sons die, that night in the Whispering Wood,‚Äù Robb told Lord Karstark. ‚ÄúTion Frey did not kill Torrhen. Willem Lannister did not slay Eddard. How then can you call this vengeance? This was folly, and bloody murder. Your sons died honorably on a battlefield, with swords in their hands.‚Äù ‚ÄúThey died,‚Äù said Rickard Karstark, yielding no inch of ground. ‚ÄúThe Kingslayer cut them down. These two were of his ilk. Only blood can pay for blood.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe blood of children?‚Äù Robb pointed at the corpses.

{:#Rickard-Karstark}
**Rickard Karstark**

> ‚ÄúHow can it be treason to kill Lannisters, when it is not treason to free them?‚Äù asked Karstark harshly. ‚ÄúHas Your Grace forgotten that we are at war with Casterly Rock? In war you kill your enemies. Didn‚Äôt your father teach you that, boy?‚Äù ‚ÄúBoy?‚Äù The Greatjon dealt Rickard Karstark a buffet with a mailed fist that sent the other lord to his knees. ‚ÄúLeave him!‚Äù Robb‚Äôs voice rang with command. Umber stepped back away from the captive. Lord Karstark spit out a broken tooth. ‚ÄúYes, Lord Umber, leave me to the king. He means to give me a scolding before he forgives me. That‚Äôs how he deals with treason, our King in the North.‚Äù He smiled a wet red smile. ‚ÄúOr should I call you the King Who Lost the North, Your Grace?‚Äù

> ‚ÄúLysa was never brave. When we were girls together, she would run and hide whenever she‚Äôd done something wrong. Perhaps she thought our lord father would forget to be wroth with her if he could not find her. It is no different now. She ran from King‚Äôs Landing for fear, to the safest place she knows, and she sits on her mountain hoping everyone will forget her.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe knights of the Vale could make all the difference in this war,‚Äù said Robb, ‚Äúbut if she will not fight, so be it. I‚Äôve asked only that she open the Bloody Gate for us, and provide ships at Gulltown to take us north. The high road would be hard, but not so hard as fighting our way up the Neck. If I could land at White Harbor I could flank Moat Cailin and drive the ironmen from the north in half a year.‚Äù ‚ÄúIt will not happen, sire,‚Äù said the Blackfish. ‚ÄúCat is right. Lady Lysa is too fearful to admit an army to the Vale. Any army. The Bloody Gate will remain closed.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe Others can take her, then,‚Äù Robb cursed, in a fury of despair. ‚ÄúBloody Rickard Karstark as well. And Theon Greyjoy, Walder Frey, Tywin Lannister, and all the rest of them. Gods be good, why would any man ever want to be king? When everyone was shouting King in the North, King in the North, I told myself¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýswore to myself¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthat I would be a good king, as honorable as Father, strong, just, loyal to my friends and brave when I faced my enemies¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýnow I can‚Äôt even tell one from the other. How did it all get so confused? Lord Rickard‚Äôs fought at my side in half a dozen battles. His sons died for me in the Whispering Wood. Tion Frey and Willem Lannister were my enemies. Yet now I have to kill my dead friends‚Äô father for their sakes.‚Äù He looked at them all. ‚ÄúWill the Lannisters thank me for Lord Rickard‚Äôs head? Will the Freys?‚Äù ‚ÄúNo,‚Äù said Brynden Blackfish, blunt as ever. ‚ÄúAll the more reason to spare Lord Rickard‚Äôs life and keep him hostage,‚Äù Edmure urged. Robb reached down with both hands, lifted the heavy bronze-and-iron crown, and set it back atop his head, and suddenly he was a king again. ‚ÄúLord Rickard dies.‚Äù ‚ÄúBut why?‚Äù said Edmure. ‚ÄúYou said yourself‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúI know what I said, Uncle. It does not change what I must do.‚Äù The swords in his crown stood stark and black against his brow. ‚ÄúIn battle I might have slain Tion and Willem myself, but this was no battle. They were asleep in their beds, naked and unarmed, in a cell where I put them. Rickard Karstark killed more than a Frey and a Lannister. He killed my honor. I shall deal with him at dawn.‚Äù

> Long Lew waited beside the block, but Robb took the poleaxe from his hand and ordered him to step aside. ‚ÄúThis is my work,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúHe dies at my word. He must die by my hand.‚Äù Lord Rickard Karstark dipped his head stiffly. ‚ÄúFor that much, I thank you. But for naught else.‚Äù He had dressed for death in a long black wool surcoat emblazoned with the white sunburst of his House. ‚ÄúThe blood of the First Men flows in my veins as much as yours, boy. You would do well to remember that. I was named for your grandfather. I raised my banners against King Aerys for your father, and against King Joffrey for you. At Oxcross and the Whispering Wood and in the Battle of the Camps, I rode beside you, and I stood with Lord Eddard on the Trident. We are kin, Stark and Karstark.‚Äù ‚ÄúThis kinship did not stop you from betraying me,‚Äù Robb said. ‚ÄúAnd it will not save you now. Kneel, my lord.‚Äù Lord Rickard had spoken truly, Catelyn knew. The Karstarks traced their descent to Karlon Stark, a younger son of Winterfell who had put down a rebel lord a thousand years ago, and been granted lands for his valor. The castle he built had been named Karl‚Äôs Hold, but that soon became Karhold, and over the centuries the Karhold Starks had become Karstarks. ‚ÄúOld gods or new, it makes no matter,‚Äù Lord Rickard told her son, ‚Äúno man is so accursed as the kinslayer.‚Äù ‚ÄúKneel, traitor,‚Äù Robb said again. ‚ÄúOr must I have them force your head onto the block?‚Äù Lord Karstark knelt. ‚ÄúThe gods shall judge you, as you have judged me.‚Äù He laid his head upon the block. ‚ÄúRickard Karstark, Lord of Karhold.‚Äù Robb lifted the heavy axe with both hands. ‚ÄúHere in sight of gods and men, I judge you guilty of murder and high treason. In mine own name I condemn you. With mine own hand I take your life. Would you speak a final word?‚Äù ‚ÄúKill me, and be cursed. You are no king of mine.‚Äù The axe crashed down. Heavy and well-honed, it killed at a single blow, but it took three to sever the man‚Äôs head from his body, and by the time it was done both living and dead were drenched in blood. Robb flung the poleaxe down in disgust, and turned wordless to the heart tree. He stood shaking with his hands half-clenched and the rain running down his cheeks. Gods forgive him, Catelyn prayed in silence. He is only a boy, and he had no other choice.

> Jon took a knee. The king frowned at him, and rattled the parchment angrily. ‚ÄúRise. Tell me, who is Lyanna Mormont?‚Äù ‚ÄúOne of Lady Maege‚Äôs daughters, Sire. The youngest. She was named for my lord father‚Äôs sister.‚Äù ‚ÄúTo curry your lord father‚Äôs favor, I don‚Äôt doubt. I know how that game is played. How old is this wretched girl child?‚Äù Jon had to think a moment. ‚ÄúTen. Or near enough to make no matter. Might I know how she has offended Your Grace?‚Äù Stannis read from the letter. ‚ÄúBear Island knows no king but the King in the North, whose name is STARK. A girl of ten, you say, and she presumes to scold her lawful king.‚Äù His close-cropped beard lay like a shadow over his hollow cheeks. ‚ÄúSee that you keep these tidings to yourself, Lord Snow. Karhold is with me, that is all the men need know. I will not have your brothers trading tales of how this child spat on me.‚Äù ‚ÄúAs you command, Sire.‚Äù Maege Mormont had ridden south with Robb, Jon knew. Her eldest daughter had joined the Young Wolf‚Äôs host as well. Even if both of them had died, however, Lady Maege had other daughters, some with children of their own. Had they gone with Robb as well? Surely Lady Maege would have left at least one of the older girls behind as castellan. He did not understand why Lyanna should be writing Stannis, and could not help but wonder if the girl‚Äôs answer might have been different if the letter had been sealed with a direwolf instead of a crowned stag, and signed by Jon Stark, Lord of Winterfell. It is too late for such misgivings. You made your choice. ‚ÄúTwo score ravens were sent out,‚Äù the king complained, ‚Äúyet we get no response but silence and defiance. Homage is the duty every leal subject owes his king. Yet your father‚Äôs bannermen all turn their back on me, save the Karstarks. Is Arnolf Karstark the only man of honor in the north?‚Äù Arnolf Karstark was the late Lord Rickard‚Äôs uncle. He had been made the castellan of Karhold when his nephew and his sons went south with Robb, and he had been the first to respond to King Stannis‚Äôs call for homage, with a raven declaring his allegiance. The Karstarks have no other choice, Jon might have said. Rickard Karstark had betrayed the direwolf and spilled the blood of lions. The stag was Karhold‚Äôs only hope.

{:#Cleos-Frey}
**Cleos Frey**

> He did not look a lion, Catelyn reflected. This Ser Cleos Frey was a son of the Lady Genna who was sister to Lord Tywin Lannister, but he had none of the fabled Lannister beauty, the fair hair and green eyes. Instead he had inherited the stringy brown locks, weak chin, and thin face of his sire, Ser Emmon Frey, old Lord Walder‚Äôs second son. His eyes were pale and watery and he could not seem to stop blinking, but perhaps that was only the light. The cells below Riverrun were dark and damp¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand these days crowded as well.

> Jaime saw Ser Cleos lurch from the saddle, twisting as his foot caught in the stirrup. His palfrey bolted, and Frey was dragged past shouting, head bouncing against the ground.

> They found Cleos still tangled in his stirrup. He had an arrow through his right arm and a second in his chest, but it was the ground that had done for him. The top of his head was matted with blood and mushy to the touch, pieces of broken bone moving under the skin beneath the pressure of Jaime‚Äôs hand. Brienne knelt and held his hand. ‚ÄúHe‚Äôs still warm.‚Äù

> Genna settled her ample rump upon a camp stool, which creaked alarmingly beneath her weight. ‚ÄúNephew, speak to us of our son Cleos and the manner of his death.‚Äù Jaime undid the last fastening and set his hand aside. ‚ÄúWe were set upon by outlaws. Ser Cleos scattered them, but it cost his life.‚Äù The lie came easy; he could see that it pleased them. ‚ÄúThe boy had courage, I always said so. It was in his blood.‚Äù A pinkish froth glistened on Ser Emmon‚Äôs lips when he spoke, courtesy of the sourleaf he liked to chew. ‚ÄúHis bones should be interred beneath the Rock, in the Hall of Heroes,‚Äù Lady Genna declared. ‚ÄúWhere was he laid to rest?‚Äù Nowhere. The Bloody Mummers stripped his corpse and left his flesh to feast the carrion crows. ‚ÄúBeside a stream,‚Äù he lied. ‚ÄúWhen this war is done, I will find the place and send him home.‚Äù Bones were bones; these days, nothing was easier to come by.

{:#Imry-Florent-Monford-Velaryon-and-Guncer-Sunglass}
**Imry Florent, Monford Velaryon, and Guncer Sunglass**

> Had he been admiral, he might have done it all differently. For a start, he would have sent a few of his swiftest ships to probe upriver and see what awaited them, instead of smashing in headlong. When he had suggested as much to Ser Imry, the Lord High Captain had thanked him courteously, but his eyes were not as polite. Who is this lowborn craven? those eyes asked. Is he the one who bought his knighthood with an onion? With four times as many ships as the boy king, Ser Imry saw no need for caution or deceptive tactics. He had organized the fleet into ten lines of battle, each of twenty ships. The first two lines would sweep up the river to engage and destroy Joffrey‚Äôs little fleet, or ‚Äúthe boy‚Äôs toys‚Äù as Ser Imry dubbed them, to the mirth of his lordly captains. Those that followed would land companies of archers and spearmen beneath the city walls, and only then join the fight on the river. The smaller, slower ships to the rear would ferry over the main part of Stannis‚Äôs host from the south bank, protected by Salladhor Saan and his Lyseni, who would stand out in the bay in case the Lannisters had other ships hidden up along the coast, poised to sweep down on their rear. To be fair, there was reason for Ser Imry‚Äôs haste. The winds had not used them kindly on the voyage up from Storm‚Äôs End. They had lost two cogs to the rocks of Shipbreaker Bay on the very day they set sail, a poor way to begin. One of the Myrish galleys had foundered in the Straits of Tarth, and a storm had overtaken them as they were entering the Gullet, scattering the fleet across half the narrow sea. All but twelve ships had finally regrouped behind the sheltering spine of Massey‚Äôs Hook, in the calmer waters of Blackwater Bay, but not before they had lost considerable time.

> In his smuggling days, Davos had often jested that he knew the waterfront at King‚Äôs Landing a deal better than the back of his hand, since he had not spent a good part of his life sneaking in and out of the back of his hand. The squat towers of raw new stone that stood opposite one another at the mouth of the Blackwater might mean nothing to Ser Imry Florent, but to him it was as if two extra fingers had sprouted from his knuckles. Shading his eyes against the westering sun, he peered at those towers more closely. They were too small to hold much of a garrison. The one on the north bank was built against the bluff with the Red Keep frowning above; its counterpart on the south shore had its footing in the water. They dug a cut through the bank, he knew at once. That would make the tower very difficult to assault; attackers would need to wade through the water or bridge the little channel. Stannis had posted bowmen below, to fire up at the defenders whenever one was rash enough to lift his head above the ramparts, but otherwise had not troubled. Something flashed down low where the dark water swirled around the base of the tower. It was sunlight on steel, and it told Davos Seaworth all he needed to know. A chain boom¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand yet they have not closed the river against us. Why? He could make a guess at that as well, but there was no time to consider the question. A shout went up from the ships ahead, and the warhorns blew again: the enemy was before them.

> ‚ÄúMy own son is safe at Brightwater,‚Äù the lord went on, ‚Äúbut I lost a nephew on the Fury. Ser Imry, my brother Ryam‚Äôs son.‚Äù It had been Ser Imry Florent who led them blindly up the Blackwater Rush with all oars pulling, paying no heed to the small stone towers at the mouth of the river. Davos was not like to forget him. ‚ÄúMy son Maric was your nephew‚Äôs oarmaster.‚Äù He remembered his last sight of Fury, engulfed in wildfire. ‚ÄúHas there been any word of survivors?‚Äù ‚ÄúThe Fury burned and sank with all hands,‚Äù his lordship said. ‚ÄúYour son and my nephew were lost, with countless other good men. The war itself was lost that day, ser.‚Äù This man is defeated. Davos remembered Melisandre‚Äôs talk of embers in the ashes igniting great blazes. Small wonder he ended here. ‚ÄúHis Grace will never yield, my lord.‚Äù ‚ÄúFolly, that‚Äôs folly.‚Äù Lord Alester sat on the floor again, as if the effort of standing for a moment had been too much for him. ‚ÄúStannis Baratheon will never sit the Iron Throne. Is it treason to say the truth? A bitter truth, but no less true for that. His fleet is gone, save for the Lyseni, and Salladhor Saan will flee at the first sight of a Lannister sail. Most of the lords who supported Stannis have gone over to Joffrey or died¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúEven the lords of the narrow sea? The lords sworn to Dragonstone?‚Äù Lord Alester waved his hand feebly. ‚ÄúLord Celtigar was captured and bent the knee. Monford Velaryon died with his ship, the red woman burned Sunglass, and Lord Bar Emmon is fifteen, fat, and feeble. Those are your lords of the narrow sea. Only the strength of House Florent is left to Stannis, against all the might of Highgarden, Sunspear, and Casterly Rock, and now most of the storm lords as well. The best hope that remains is to try and salvage something with a peace. That is all I meant to do. Gods be good, how can they call it treason?‚Äù Davos stood frowning. ‚ÄúMy lord, what did you do?‚Äù ‚ÄúNot treason. Never treason. I love His Grace as much as any man. My own niece is his queen, and I remained loyal to him when wiser men fled. I am his Hand, the Hand of the King, how can I be a traitor? I only meant to save our lives, and¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýhonor¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýyes.‚Äù He licked his lips. ‚ÄúI penned a letter. Salladhor Saan swore that he had a man who could get it to King‚Äôs Landing, to Lord Tywin. His lordship is a¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýa man of reason, and my terms¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthe terms were fair¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýmore than fair.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhat terms were these, my lord?‚Äù ‚ÄúIt is filthy here,‚Äù Lord Alester said suddenly. ‚ÄúAnd that odor¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýwhat is that odor?‚Äù ‚ÄúThe pail,‚Äù said Davos, gesturing. ‚ÄúWe have no privy here. What terms?‚Äù His lordship stared at the pail in horror. ‚ÄúThat Lord Stannis give up his claim to the Iron Throne and retract all he said of Joffrey‚Äôs bastardy, on the condition that he be accepted back into the king‚Äôs peace and confirmed as Lord of Dragonstone and Storm‚Äôs End. I vowed to do the same, for the return of Brightwater Keep and all our lands. I thought¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝLord Tywin would see the sense in my proposal. He still has the Starks to deal with, and the ironmen as well. I offered to seal the bargain by wedding Shireen to Joffrey‚Äôs brother Tommen.‚Äù He shook his head. ‚ÄúThe terms¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthey are as good as we are ever like to get. Even you can see that, surely?‚Äù ‚ÄúYes,‚Äù said Davos, ‚Äúeven me.‚Äù Unless Stannis should father a son, such a marriage would mean that Dragonstone and Storm‚Äôs End would one day pass to Tommen, which would doubtless please Lord Tywin. Meanwhile, the Lannisters would have Shireen as hostage to make certain Stannis raised no new rebellions. ‚ÄúAnd what did His Grace say when you proposed these terms to him?‚Äù ‚ÄúHe is always with the red woman, and¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýhe is not in his right mind, I fear. This talk of a stone dragon¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýmadness, I tell you, sheer madness. Did we learn nothing from Aerion Brightfire, from the nine mages, from the alchemists? Did we learn nothing from Summerhall? No good has ever come from these dreams of dragons, I told Axell as much. My way was better. Surer. And Stannis gave me his seal, he gave me leave to rule. The Hand speaks with the king‚Äôs voice.‚Äù ‚ÄúNot in this.‚Äù Davos was no courtier, and he did not even try to blunt his words. ‚ÄúIt is not in Stannis to yield, so long as he knows his claim is just. No more than he can unsay his words against Joffrey, when he believes them true. As for the marriage, Tommen was born of the same incest as Joffrey, and His Grace would sooner see Shireen dead than wed to such.‚Äù A vein throbbed in Florent‚Äôs forehead. ‚ÄúHe has no choice.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou are wrong, my lord. He can choose to die a king.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd us with him? Is that what you desire, Onion Knight?‚Äù ‚ÄúNo. But I am the king‚Äôs man, and I will make no peace without his leave.‚Äù Lord Alester stared at him helplessly for a long moment, and then began to weep.

{:#Gorne}
**Gorne**

> ‚ÄúYgritte?‚Äù ‚ÄúIn here,‚Äù her voice came back, echoing faintly. Jon had to crawl a dozen paces before the cave opened up around him. When he stood again, it took his eyes a moment to adjust. Ygritte had brought a torch, but there was no other light. She stood beside a little waterfall that fell from a cleft in the rock down into a wide dark pool. The orange and yellow flames shone against the pale green water. ‚ÄúWhat are you doing here?‚Äù he asked her. ‚ÄúI heard water. I wanted t‚Äôsee how deep the cave went.‚Äù She pointed with the torch. ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs a passage goes down further. I followed it a hundred paces before I turned back.‚Äù ‚ÄúA dead end?‚Äù ‚ÄúYou know nothing, Jon Snow. It went on and on and on. There are hundreds o‚Äô caves in these hills, and down deep they all connect. There‚Äôs even a way under your Wall. Gorne‚Äôs Way.‚Äù ‚ÄúGorne,‚Äù said Jon. ‚ÄúGorne was King-beyond-the-Wall.‚Äù ‚ÄúAye,‚Äù said Ygritte. ‚ÄúTogether with his brother Gendel, three thousand years ago. They led a host o‚Äô free folk through the caves, and the Watch was none the wiser. But when they come out, the wolves o‚Äô Winterfell fell upon them.‚Äù ‚ÄúThere was a battle,‚Äù Jon recalled. ‚ÄúGorne slew the King in the North, but his son picked up his banner and took the crown from his head, and cut down Gorne in turn.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd the sound o‚Äô swords woke the crows in their castles, and they rode out all in black to take the free folk in the rear.‚Äù ‚ÄúYes. Gendel had the king to the south, the Umbers to the east, and the Watch to the north of him. He died as well.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou know nothing, Jon Snow. Gendel did not die. He cut his way free, through the crows, and led his people back north with the wolves howling at their heels. Only Gendel did not know the caves as Gorne had, and took a wrong turn.‚Äù She swept the torch back and forth, so the shadows jumped and moved. ‚ÄúDeeper he went, and deeper, and when he tried t‚Äô turn back the ways that seemed familiar ended in stone rather than sky. Soon his torches began t‚Äô fail, one by one, till finally there was naught but dark. Gendel‚Äôs folk were never seen again, but on a still night you can hear their children‚Äôs children‚Äôs children sobbing under the hills, still looking for the way back up. Listen? Do you hear them?‚Äù All Jon could hear was the falling water and the faint crackle of flames. ‚ÄúThis way under the Wall was lost as well?‚Äù ‚ÄúSome have searched for it. Them that go too deep find Gendel‚Äôs children, and Gendel‚Äôs children are always hungry.‚Äù Smiling, she set the torch carefully in a notch of rock, and came toward him. ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs naught to eat in the dark but flesh,‚Äù she whispered, biting at his neck.

{:#Kraznys-mo-Nakloz-and-Grazdan}
**Kraznys mo Nakloz and Grazdan**

> Two thousand would never serve for what she meant to do. I must have them all. Dany knew what she must do now, though the taste of it was so bitter that even the persimmon wine could not cleanse it from her mouth. She had considered long and hard and found no other way. It is my only choice. ‚ÄúGive me all,‚Äù she said, ‚Äúand you may have a dragon.‚Äù There was the sound of indrawn breath from Jhiqui beside her. Kraznys smiled at his fellows. ‚ÄúDid I not tell you? Anything, she would give us.‚Äù Whitebeard stared in shocked disbelief. His hand trembled where it grasped the staff. ‚ÄúNo.‚Äù He went to one knee before her. ‚ÄúYour Grace, I beg you, win your throne with dragons, not slaves. You must not do this thing‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúYou must not presume to instruct me. Ser Jorah, remove Whitebeard from my presence.‚Äù Mormont seized the old man roughly by an elbow, yanked him back to his feet, and marched him out onto the terrace. ‚ÄúTell the Good Masters I regret this interruption,‚Äù said Dany to the slave girl. ‚ÄúTell them I await their answer.‚Äù She knew the answer, though; she could see it in the glitter of their eyes and the smiles they tried so hard to hide. Astapor had thousands of eunuchs, and even more slave boys waiting to be cut, but there were only three living dragons in all the great wide world. And the Ghiscari lust for dragons. How could they not? Five times had Old Ghis contended with Valyria when the world was young, and five times gone down to bleak defeat. For the Freehold had dragons, and the Empire had none. The oldest Grazdan stirred in his seat, and his pearls clacked together softly. ‚ÄúA dragon of our choice,‚Äù he said in a thin, hard voice. ‚ÄúThe black one is largest and healthiest.‚Äù ‚ÄúHis name is Drogon.‚Äù She nodded. ‚ÄúAll your goods, save your crown and your queenly raiment, which we will allow you to keep. The three ships. And Drogon.‚Äù ‚ÄúDone,‚Äù she said, in the Common Tongue. ‚ÄúDone,‚Äù the old Grazdan answered in his thick Valyrian. The others echoed that old man of the pearl fringe. ‚ÄúDone,‚Äù the slave girl translated, ‚Äúand done, and done, eight times done.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe Unsullied will learn your savage tongue quick enough,‚Äù added Kraznys mo Nakloz, when all the arrangements had been made, ‚Äúbut until such time you will need a slave to speak to them. Take this one as our gift to you, a token of a bargain well struck.‚Äù ‚ÄúI shall,‚Äù said Dany.

> Dany handed the slaver the end of Drogon‚Äôs chain. In return he presented her with the whip. The handle was black dragonbone, elaborately carved and inlaid with gold. Nine long thin leather lashes trailed from it, each one tipped by a gilded claw. The gold pommel was a woman‚Äôs head, with pointed ivory teeth. ‚ÄúThe harpy‚Äôs fingers,‚Äù Kraznys named the scourge. Dany turned the whip in her hand. Such a light thing, to bear such weight. ‚ÄúIs it done, then? Do they belong to me?‚Äù ‚ÄúIt is done,‚Äù he agreed, giving the chain a sharp pull to bring Drogon down from the litter. Dany mounted her silver. She could feel her heart thumping in her chest. She felt desperately afraid. Was this what my brother would have done? She wondered if Prince Rhaegar had been this anxious when he saw the Usurper‚Äôs host formed up across the Trident with all their banners floating on the wind. She stood in her stirrups and raised the harpy‚Äôs fingers above her head for all the Unsullied to see. ‚ÄúIT IS DONE!‚Äù she cried at the top of her lungs. ‚ÄúYOU ARE MINE!‚Äù She gave the mare her heels and galloped along the first rank, holding the fingers high. ‚ÄúYOU ARE THE DRAGON‚ÄôS NOW! YOU‚ÄôRE BOUGHT AND PAID FOR! IT IS DONE! IT IS DONE!‚Äù She glimpsed old Grazdan turn his grey head sharply. He hears me speak Valyrian. The other slavers were not listening. They crowded around Kraznys and the dragon, shouting advice. Though the Astapori yanked and tugged, Drogon would not budge off the litter. Smoke rose grey from his open jaws, and his long neck curled and straightened as he snapped at the slaver‚Äôs face. It is time to cross the Trident, Dany thought, as she wheeled and rode her silver back. Her bloodriders moved in close around her. ‚ÄúYou are in difficulty,‚Äù she observed. ‚ÄúHe will not come,‚Äù Kraznys said. ‚ÄúThere is a reason. A dragon is no slave.‚Äù And Dany swept the lash down as hard as she could across the slaver‚Äôs face. Kraznys screamed and staggered back, the blood running red down his cheeks into his perfumed beard. The harpy‚Äôs fingers had torn his features half to pieces with one slash, but she did not pause to contemplate the ruin. ‚ÄúDrogon,‚Äù she sang out loudly, sweetly, all her fear forgotten. ‚ÄúDracarys.‚Äù

> The black dragon spread his wings and roared. A lance of swirling dark flame took Kraznys full in the face. His eyes melted and ran down his cheeks, and the oil in his hair and beard burst so fiercely into fire that for an instant the slaver wore a burning crown twice as tall as his head. The sudden stench of charred meat overwhelmed even his perfume, and his wail seemed to drown all other sound. Then the Plaza of Punishment blew apart into blood and chaos. The Good Masters were shrieking, stumbling, shoving one another aside and tripping over the fringes of their tokars in their haste. Drogon flew almost lazily at Kraznys, black wings beating. As he gave the slaver another taste of fire, Irri and Jhiqui unchained Viserion and Rhaegal, and suddenly there were three dragons in the air. When Dany turned to look, a third of Astapor‚Äôs proud demon-horned warriors were fighting to stay atop their terrified mounts, and another third were fleeing in a bright blaze of shiny copper. One man kept his saddle long enough to draw a sword, but Jhogo‚Äôs whip coiled about his neck and cut off his shout. Another lost a hand to Rakharo‚Äôs arakh and rode off reeling and spurting blood. Aggo sat calmly notching arrows to his bowstring and sending them at tokars. Silver, gold, or plain, he cared nothing for the fringe. Strong Belwas had his arakh out as well, and he spun it as he charged.

> ‚ÄúSpears!‚Äù Dany heard one Astapori shout. It was Grazdan, old Grazdan in his tokar heavy with pearls. ‚ÄúUnsullied! Defend us, stop them, defend your masters! Spears! Swords!‚Äù When Rakharo put an arrow through his mouth, the slaves holding his sedan chair broke and ran, dumping him unceremoniously on the ground. The old man crawled to the first rank of eunuchs, his blood pooling on the bricks. The Unsullied did not so much as look down to watch him die. Rank on rank on rank, they stood. And did not move. The gods have heard my prayer. ‚ÄúUnsullied!‚Äù Dany galloped before them, her silver-gold braid flying behind her, her bell chiming with every stride. ‚ÄúSlay the Good Masters, slay the soldiers, slay every man who wears a tokar or holds a whip, but harm no child under twelve, and strike the chains off every slave you see.‚Äù She raised the harpy‚Äôs fingers in the air¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand then she flung the scourge aside. ‚ÄúFreedom!‚Äù she sang out. ‚ÄúDracarys! Dracarys!‚Äù ‚ÄúDracarys!‚Äù they shouted back, the sweetest word she‚Äôd ever heard. ‚ÄúDracarys! Dracarys!‚Äù And all around them slavers ran and sobbed and begged and died, and the dusty air was filled with spears and fire.

{:#Myles-Mooton-and-Denys-Arryn}
**Myles Mooton and Denys Arryn**

> Stoney Sept was the biggest town Arya had seen since King‚Äôs Landing, and Harwin said her father had won a famous battle here. ‚ÄúThe Mad King‚Äôs men had been hunting Robert, trying to catch him before he could rejoin your father,‚Äù he told her as they rode toward the gate. ‚ÄúHe was wounded, being tended by some friends, when Lord Connington the Hand took the town with a mighty force and started searching house by house. Before they could find him, though, Lord Eddard and your grandfather came down on the town and stormed the walls. Lord Connington fought back fierce. They battled in the streets and alleys, even on the rooftops, and all the septons rang their bells so the smallfolk would know to lock their doors. Robert came out of hiding to join the fight when the bells began to ring. He slew six men that day, they say. One was Myles Mooton, a famous knight who‚Äôd been Prince Rhaegar‚Äôs squire. He would have slain the Hand too, but the battle never brought them together. Connington wounded your grandfather Tully sore, though, and killed Ser Denys Arryn, the darling of the Vale. But when he saw the day was lost, he flew off as fast as the griffins on his shield. The Battle of the Bells, they called it after. Robert always said your father won it, not him.‚Äù

{:#Jarl}
**Jarl**

> The Wall was often said to stand seven hundred feet high, but Jarl had found a place where it was both higher and lower. Before them, the ice rose sheer from out of the trees like some immense cliff, crowned by wind-carved battlements that loomed at least eight hundred feet high, perhaps nine hundred in spots. But that was deceptive, Jon realized as they drew closer. Brandon the Builder had laid his huge foundation blocks along the heights wherever feasible, and hereabouts the hills rose wild and rugged. He had once heard his uncle Benjen say that the Wall was a sword east of Castle Black, but a snake to the west. It was true. Sweeping in over one huge humped hill, the ice dipped down into a valley, climbed the knife edge of a long granite ridgeline for a league or more, ran along a jagged crest, dipped again into a valley deeper still, and then rose higher and higher, leaping from hill to hill as far as the eye could see, into the mountainous west. Jarl had chosen to assault the stretch of ice along the ridge. Here, though the top of the Wall loomed eight hundred feet above the forest floor, a good third of that height was earth and stone rather than ice; the slope was too steep for their horses, almost as difficult a scramble as the Fist of the First Men, but still vastly easier to ascend than the sheer vertical face of the Wall itself. And the ridge was densely wooded as well, offering easy concealment. Once brothers in black had gone out every day with axes to cut back the encroaching trees, but those days were long past, and here the forest grew right up to the ice.

> In the very shadow of the Wall the wildlings made ready, winding thick coils of hempen rope around one shoulder and down across their chests, and lacing on queer boots of supple doeskin. The boots had spikes jutting from the toes; iron, for Jarl and two others, bronze for some, but most often jagged bone. Small stone-headed hammers hung from one hip, a leathern bag of stakes from the other. Their ice axes were antlers with sharpened tines, bound to wooden hafts with strips of hide. The eleven climbers sorted themselves into three teams of four; Jarl himself made the twelfth man. ‚ÄúMance promises swords for every man of the first team to reach the top,‚Äù he told them, his breath misting in the cold air. ‚ÄúSouthron swords of castleforged steel. And your name in the song he‚Äôll make of this, that too. What more could a free man ask? Up, and the Others take the hindmost!‚Äù The Others take them all, thought Jon, as he watched them scramble up the steep slope of the ridge and vanish beneath the trees. It would not be the first time wildlings had scaled the Wall, not even the hundred and first. The patrols stumbled on climbers two or three times a year, and rangers sometimes came on the broken corpses of those who had fallen. Along the east coast the raiders most often built boats to slip across the Bay of Seals. In the west they would descend into the black depths of the Gorge to make their way around the Shadow Tower. But in between the only way to defeat the Wall was to go over it, and many a raider had. Fewer come back, though, he thought with a certain grim pride. Climbers must of necessity leave their mounts behind, and many younger, greener raiders began by taking the first horses they found. Then a hue and cry would go up, ravens would fly, and as often as not the Night‚Äôs Watch would hunt them down and hang them before they could get back with their plunder and stolen women. Jarl would not make that mistake, Jon knew, but he wondered about Styr. The Magnar is a ruler, not a raider. He may not know how the game is played.

> ‚ÄúSo slow,‚Äù the Magnar complained loudly, as he watched them edge their way upward. ‚ÄúHas he forgotten the crows? He should climb faster, afore we are discovered.‚Äù Jon had to hold his tongue. He remembered the Skirling Pass all too well, and the moonlight climb he‚Äôd made with Stonesnake. He had swallowed his heart a half-dozen times that night, and by the end his arms and legs had been aching and his fingers were half frozen. And that was stone, not ice. Stone was solid. Ice was treacherous stuff at the best of times, and on a day like this, when the Wall was weeping, the warmth of a climber‚Äôs hand might be enough to melt it. The huge blocks could be frozen rock-hard inside, but their outer surface would be slick, with runnels of water trickling down, and patches of rotten ice where the air had gotten in. Whatever else the wildlings are, they‚Äôre brave. All the same, Jon found himself hoping that Styr‚Äôs fears proved well founded. If the gods are good, a patrol will chance by and put an end to this. ‚ÄúNo wall can keep you safe,‚Äù his father had told him once, as they walked the walls of Winterfall. ‚ÄúA wall is only as strong as the men who defend it.‚Äù The wildlings might have a hundred and twenty men, but four defenders would be enough to see them off, with a few well-placed arrows and perhaps a pail of stones.

> By the sixth hour, Jarl had moved ahead of Grigg the Goat again, and his men were widening the gap. ‚ÄúThe Mance‚Äôs pet must want a sword,‚Äù the Magnar said, shading his eyes. The sun was high in the sky, and the upper third of the Wall was a crystalline blue from below, reflecting so brilliantly that it hurt the eyes to look on it. Jarl‚Äôs four and Grigg‚Äôs were all but lost in the glare, though Errok‚Äôs team was still in shadow. Instead of moving upward they were edging their way sideways at about five hundred feet, making for a chimney. Jon was watching them inch along when he heard the sound‚Äîa sudden crack that seemed to roll along the ice, followed by a shout of alarm. And then the air was full of shards and shrieks and falling men, as a sheet of ice a foot thick and fifty feet square broke off from the Wall and came tumbling, crumbling, rumbling, sweeping all before it. Even down at the foot of the ridge, some chunks came spinning through the trees and rolling down the slope. Jon grabbed Ygritte and pulled her down to shield her, and one of the Thenns was struck in the face by a chunk that broke his nose. And when they looked up Jarl and his team were gone. Men, ropes, stakes, all gone; nothing remained above six hundred feet. There was a wound in the Wall where the climbers had clung half a heartbeat before, the ice within as smooth and white as polished marble and shining in the sun. Far far below there was a faint red smear where someone had smashed against a frozen pinnace. The Wall defends itself, Jon thought as he pulled Ygritte back to her feet. They found Jarl in a tree, impaled upon a splintered branch and still roped to the three men who lay broken beneath him. One was still alive, but his legs and spine were shattered, and most of his ribs as well. ‚ÄúMercy,‚Äù he said when they came upon him. One of the Thenns smashed his head in with a big stone mace. The Magnar gave orders, and his men began to gather fuel for a pyre. The dead were burning when Grigg the Goat reached the top of the Wall. By the time Errok‚Äôs four had joined them, nothing remained of Jarl and his team but bone and ash.

{:#Symon-Silver-Tongue}
**Symon Silver Tongue**

> Tyrion rode on toward Chataya‚Äôs, but suddenly his patience deserted him. He twisted in the saddle, scanning the street behind. There were no signs of followers. Every window was dark or tightly shuttered. He heard nothing but the wind swirling down the alleys. If Cersei has someone stalking me tonight, he must be disguised as a rat. ‚ÄúBugger it all,‚Äù he muttered. He was sick of caution. Wheeling his horse around, he dug in his spurs. If anyone‚Äôs after me, we‚Äôll see how well they ride. He flew through the moonlight streets, clattering over cobbles, darting down narrow alleys and up twisty wynds, racing to his love.

> As he hammered on the gate he heard music wafting faintly over the spiked stone walls. One of the Ibbenese ushered him inside. Tyrion gave the man his horse and said, ‚ÄúWho is that?‚Äù The diamond-shaped panes of the longhall windows shone with yellow light, and he could hear a man singing. The Ibbenese shrugged. ‚ÄúFatbelly singer.‚Äù The sound swelled as he walked from the stable to the house. Tyrion had never been fond of singers, and he liked this one even less than the run of the breed, sight unseen. When he pushed open the door, the man broke off. ‚ÄúMy lord Hand.‚Äù He knelt, balding and kettle-bellied, murmuring, ‚ÄúAn honor, an honor.‚Äù ‚ÄúM‚Äôlord.‚Äù Shae smiled at the sight of him. He liked that smile, the quick unthinking way it came to her pretty face. The girl wore her purple silk, belted with a cloth-of-silver sash. The colors favored her dark hair and the smooth cream of her skin. ‚ÄúSweetling,‚Äù he called her. ‚ÄúAnd who is this?‚Äù The singer raised his eyes. ‚ÄúI am called Symon Silver Tongue, my lord. A player, a singer, a taleteller‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd a great fool,‚Äù Tyrion finished. ‚ÄúWhat did you call me, when I entered?‚Äù ‚ÄúCall? I only¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù The silver in Symon‚Äôs tongue seemed to have turned to lead. ‚ÄúMy lord Hand, I said, an honor¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúA wiser man would have pretended not to recognize me. Not that I would have been fooled, but you ought to have tried. What am I to do with you now? You know of my sweet Shae, you know where she dwells, you know that I visit by night alone.‚Äù ‚ÄúI swear, I‚Äôll tell no one¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúOn that much we agree. Good night to you.‚Äù Tyrion led Shae up the stairs. ‚ÄúMy singer may never sing again now,‚Äù she teased. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôve scared the voice from him.‚Äù ‚ÄúA little fear will help him reach those high notes.‚Äù

> The last time Tyrion had seen the man, a sharp word had been enough to set him sweating, but it seemed the singer had found some courage somewhere. Most like in that flagon. Or perhaps Tyrion himself was to blame for this new boldness. I threatened him, but nothing ever came of the threat, so now he believes me toothless. He sighed. ‚ÄúI am told you are a very gifted singer.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou are most kind to say so, my lord.‚Äù Tyrion gave him a smile. ‚ÄúI think it is time you brought your music to the Free Cities. They are great lovers of song in Braavos and Pentos and Lys, and generous with those who please them.‚Äù He took a sip of wine. It was foul stuff, but strong. ‚ÄúA tour of all nine cities would be best. You wouldn‚Äôt want to deny anyone the joy of hearing you sing. A year in each should suffice.‚Äù He reached inside his cloak, to where the gold was hidden. ‚ÄúWith the port closed, you will need to go to Duskendale to take ship, but my man Bronn will find a horse for you, and I would be honored if you would let me pay your passage¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúBut my lord,‚Äù the man objected, ‚Äúyou have never heard me sing. Pray listen a moment.‚Äù His fingers moved deftly over the strings of the woodharp, and soft music filled the cellar. Symon began to sing. He rode through the streets of the city, down from his hill on high, O‚Äôer the wynds and the steps and the cobbles, he rode to a woman‚Äôs sigh. For she was his secret treasure, she was his shame and his bliss. And a chain and a keep are nothing, compared to a woman‚Äôs kiss. ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs more,‚Äù the man said as he broke off. ‚ÄúOh, a good deal more. The refrain is especially nice, I think. For hands of gold are always cold, but a woman‚Äôs hands are warm¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúEnough.‚Äù Tyrion slid his fingers from his cloak, empty. ‚ÄúThat‚Äôs not a song I would care to hear again. Ever.‚Äù ‚ÄúNo?‚Äù Symon Silver Tongue put his harp aside and took a sip of wine. ‚ÄúA pity. Still, each man has his song, as my old master used to say when he was teaching me to play. Others might like my tune better. The queen, perhaps. Or your lord father.‚Äù

> Symon took a sip of wine. ‚ÄúStill¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýa singer‚Äôs life is not without peril. We ply our trade in alehouses and wine sinks, before unruly drunkards. If one of your sister‚Äôs seven should suffer some mishap, I hope you might consider me to fill his place.‚Äù He smiled slyly, inordinately pleased with himself. ‚ÄúSix singers would be as unfortunate as eight, to be sure. I will inquire after the health of Cersei‚Äôs seven. If any of them should be indisposed, my man Bronn will find you.‚Äù ‚ÄúVery good, my lord.‚Äù Symon might have left it at that, but flushed with triumph, he added, ‚ÄúI shall sing the night of King Joffrey‚Äôs wedding. Should it happen that I am called to court, why, I will want to offer the king my very best compositions, songs I have sung a thousand times that are certain to please. If I should find myself singing in some dreary winesink, though¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýwell, that would be an apt occasion to try my new song. For hands of gold are always cold, but a woman‚Äôs hands are warm.‚Äù ‚ÄúThat will not be necessary,‚Äù said Tyrion. ‚ÄúYou have my word as a Lannister, Bronn will call upon you soon.‚Äù ‚ÄúVery good, my lord.‚Äù The balding kettle-bellied singer took up his woodharp again. Bronn was waiting with the horses at the mouth of the alley. He helped Tyrion into his saddle. ‚ÄúWhen do I take the man to Duskendale?‚Äù ‚ÄúYou don‚Äôt.‚Äù Tyrion turned his horse. ‚ÄúGive him three days, then inform him that Hamish the Harper has broken his arm. Tell him that his clothes will never serve for court, so he must be fitted for new garb at once. He‚Äôll come with you quick enough.‚Äù He grimaced. ‚ÄúYou may want his tongue, I understand it‚Äôs made of silver. The rest of him should never be found.‚Äù Bronn grinned. ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs a pot shop I know in Flea Bottom makes a savory bowl of brown. All kinds of meat in it, I hear.‚Äù ‚ÄúMake certain I never eat there.‚Äù Tyrion spurred to a trot. He wanted a bath, and the hotter the better.

{:#Brown-Bernarr-and-Bannen}
**Brown Bernarr and Bannen**

> Up in the loft a woman was giving birth noisily, while below a man lay dying by the fire. Samwell Tarly could not say which frightened him more. They‚Äôd covered poor Bannen with a pile of furs and stoked the fire high, yet all he could say was, ‚ÄúI‚Äôm cold. Please. I‚Äôm so cold.‚Äù Sam was trying to feed him onion broth, but he could not swallow. The broth dribbled over his lips and down his chin as fast as Sam could spoon it in. ‚ÄúThat one‚Äôs dead.‚Äù Craster eyed the man with indifference as he worried at a sausage. ‚ÄúBe kinder to stick a knife in his chest than that spoon down his throat, you ask me.‚Äù ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt recall as we did.‚Äù Giant was no more than five feet tall‚Äîhis true name was Bedwyck‚Äîbut a fierce little man for all that. ‚ÄúSlayer, did you ask Craster for his counsel?‚Äù Sam cringed at the name, but shook his head. He filled another spoon, brought it to Bannen‚Äôs mouth, and tried to ease it between his lips.

> About the hall, a ragged score of black brothers squatted on the floor or sat on rough-hewn benches, drinking cups of the same thin onion broth and gnawing on chunks of hardbread. A couple were wounded worse than Bannen, to look at them. Fornio had been delirious for days, and Ser Byam‚Äôs shoulder was oozing a foul yellow pus. When they‚Äôd left Castle Black, Brown Bernarr had been carrying bags of Myrish fire, mustard salve, ground garlic, tansy, poppy, kingscopper, and other healing herbs. Even sweetsleep, which gave the gift of painless death. But Brown Bernarr had died on the Fist and no one had thought to search for Maester Aemon‚Äôs medicines. Hake had known some herblore as well, being a cook, but Hake was also lost. So it was left to the surviving stewards to do what they could for the wounded, which was little enough. At least they are dry here, with a fire to warm them. They need more food, though.

> ‚ÄúGo see to Bannen. Now. Before you make me wroth.‚Äù ‚ÄúYes, my lord.‚Äù Sam hurried off quivering. But when he reached the fire, it was only to find Giant pulling a fur cloak up over Bannen‚Äôs head. ‚ÄúHe said he was cold,‚Äù the small man said. ‚ÄúI hope he‚Äôs gone someplace warm, I do.‚Äù ‚ÄúHis wound¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù said Sam. ‚ÄúBugger his wound.‚Äù Dirk prodded the corpse with his foot. ‚ÄúHis foot was hurt. I knew a man back in my village lost a foot. He lived to nine-and-forty.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe cold,‚Äù said Sam. ‚ÄúHe was never warm.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe was never fed,‚Äù said Dirk. ‚ÄúNot proper. That bastard Craster starved him dead.‚Äù Sam looked around anxiously, but Craster had not returned to the hall. If he had, things might have grown ugly. The wildling hated bastards, though the rangers said he was baseborn himself, fathered on a wildling woman by some long-dead crow. ‚ÄúCraster‚Äôs got his own to feed,‚Äù said Giant. ‚ÄúAll these women. He‚Äôs given us what he can.‚Äù ‚ÄúDon‚Äôt you bloody believe it. The day we leave, he‚Äôll tap a keg o‚Äô mead and sit down to feast on ham and honey. And laugh at us, out starving in the snow. He‚Äôs a bloody wildling, is all he is. There‚Äôs none o‚Äô them friends of the Watch.‚Äù He kicked at Bannen‚Äôs corpse. ‚ÄúAsk him if you don‚Äôt believe me.‚Äù They burned the ranger‚Äôs corpse at sunset, in the fire that Grenn had been feeding earlier that day. Tim Stone and Garth of Oldtown carried out the naked corpse and swung him twice between them before heaving him into the flames. The surviving brothers divided up his clothes, his weapons, his armor, and everything else he owned. At Castle Black, the Night‚Äôs Watch buried its dead with all due ceremony. They were not at Castle Black, though. And bones do not come back as wights. ‚ÄúHis name was Bannen,‚Äù Lord Commander Mormont said, as the flames took him. ‚ÄúHe was a brave man, a good ranger. He came to us from¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýwhere did he come from?‚Äù ‚ÄúDown White Harbor way,‚Äù someone called out. Mormont nodded. ‚ÄúHe came to us from White Harbor, and never failed in his duty. He kept his vows as best he could, rode far, fought fiercely. We shall never see his like again.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd now his watch is ended,‚Äù the black brothers said, in solemn chant. ‚ÄúAnd now his watch is ended,‚Äù Mormont echoed. ‚ÄúEnded,‚Äù cried his raven. ‚ÄúEnded.‚Äù Sam was red-eyed and sick from the smoke. When he looked at the fire, he thought he saw Bannen sitting up, his hands coiling into fists as if to fight off the flames that were consuming him, but it was only for an instant, before the swirling smoke hid all. The worst thing was the smell, though. If it had been a foul unpleasant smell he might have stood it, but his burning brother smelled so much like roast pork that Sam‚Äôs mouth began to water, and that was so horrible that as soon as the bird squawked ‚ÄúEnded‚Äù he ran behind the hall to throw up in the ditch. He was there on his knees in the mud when Dolorous Edd came up. ‚ÄúDigging for worms, Sam? Or are you just sick?‚Äù ‚ÄúSick,‚Äù said Sam weakly, wiping his mouth with the back of his hand. ‚ÄúThe smell¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúNever knew Bannen could smell so good.‚Äù Edd‚Äôs tone was as morose as ever. ‚ÄúI had half a mind to carve a slice off him. If we had some applesauce, I might have done it. Pork‚Äôs always best with applesauce, I find.‚Äù Edd undid his laces and pulled out his cock. ‚ÄúYou best not die, Sam, or I fear I might succumb. There‚Äôs bound to be more crackling on you than Bannen ever had, and I never could resist a bit of crackling.‚Äù He sighed as his piss arced out, yellow and steaming.

{:#Craster-Garth-of-Oldtown-Rolley-of-Sisterton-Byam-Flint-and-Jeor-Mormont}
**Craster, Garth of Oldtown, Rolley of Sisterton, Byam Flint, and Jeor Mormont**

> Craster looked fit to spit them all by then. Lord Commander Mormont rose. ‚ÄúSilence. I‚Äôll hear no more such talk.‚Äù ‚ÄúThen stuff bread in your ears, old man.‚Äù Clubfoot Karl pushed back from the table. ‚ÄúOr did you swallow your bloody crumb already?‚Äù Sam saw the Old Bear‚Äôs face go red. ‚ÄúHave you forgotten who I am? Sit, eat, and be silent. That is a command.‚Äù No one spoke. No one moved. All eyes were on the Lord Commander and the big clubfooted ranger, as the two of them stared at each other across the table. It seemed to Sam that Karl broke first, and was about to sit, though sullenly¬Ý‚Ä¶ ¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut Craster stood, and his axe was in his hand. The big black steel axe that Mormont had given him as a guest gift. ‚ÄúNo,‚Äù he growled. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôll not sit. No one who calls me niggard will sleep beneath my roof nor eat at my board. Out with you, cripple. And you and you and you.‚Äù He jabbed the head of the axe toward Dirk and Garth and Garth in turn. ‚ÄúGo sleep in the cold with empty bellies, the lot o‚Äô you, or¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúBloody bastard!‚Äù Sam heard one of the Garths curse. He never saw which one. ‚ÄúWho calls me bastard?‚Äù Craster roared, sweeping platter and meat and wine cups from the table with his left hand while lifting the axe with his right. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs no more than all men know,‚Äù Karl answered. Craster moved quicker than Sam would have believed possible, vaulting across the table with axe in hand. A woman screamed, Garth Greenaway and Orphan Oss drew knives, Karl stumbled back and tripped over Ser Byam lying wounded on the floor. One instant Craster was coming after him spitting curses. The next he was spitting blood. Dirk had grabbed him by the hair, yanked his head back, and opened his throat ear to ear with one long slash. Then he gave him a rough shove, and the wildling fell forward, crashing face first across Ser Byam. Byam screamed in agony as Craster drowned in his own blood, the axe slipping from his fingers. Two of Craster‚Äôs wives were wailing, a third cursed, a fourth flew at Sweet Donnel and tried to scratch his eyes out. He knocked her to the floor. The Lord Commander stood over Craster‚Äôs corpse, dark with anger. ‚ÄúThe gods will curse us,‚Äù he cried. ‚ÄúThere is no crime so foul as for a guest to bring murder into a man‚Äôs hall. By all the laws of the hearth, we‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúThere are no laws beyond the Wall, old man. Remember?‚Äù Dirk grabbed one of Craster‚Äôs wives by the arm, and shoved the point of his bloody dirk up under her chin. ‚ÄúShow us where he keeps the food, or you‚Äôll get the same as he did, woman.‚Äù ‚ÄúUnhand her.‚Äù Mormont took a step. ‚ÄúI‚Äôll have your head for this, you‚Äî‚Äù Garth of Greenaway blocked his path, and Ollo Lophand yanked him back. They both had blades in hand. ‚ÄúHold your tongue,‚Äù Ollo warned. Instead the Lord Commander grabbed for his dagger. Ollo had only one hand, but that was quick. He twisted free of the old man‚Äôs grasp, shoved the knife into Mormont‚Äôs belly, and yanked it out again, all red. And then the world went mad.

> Later, much later, Sam found himself sitting crosslegged on the floor, with Mormont‚Äôs head in his lap. He did not remember how they‚Äôd gotten there, or much of anything else that had happened after the Old Bear was stabbed. Garth of Greenaway had killed Garth of Oldtown, he recalled, but not why. Rolley of Sisterton had fallen from the loft and broken his neck after climbing the ladder to have a taste of Craster‚Äôs wives. Grenn¬Ý‚Ä¶ Grenn had shouted and slapped him, and then he‚Äôd run away with Giant and Dolorous Edd and some others. Craster still sprawled across Ser Byam, but the wounded knight no longer moaned. Four men in black sat on the bench eating chunks of burned horsemeat while Ollo coupled with a weeping woman on the table.

> ‚ÄúTarly.‚Äù When he tried to speak, the blood dribbled from the Old Bear‚Äôs mouth down into his beard. ‚ÄúTarly, go. Go.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhere, my lord?‚Äù His voice was flat and lifeless. I am not afraid. It was a queer feeling. ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs no place to go.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe Wall. Make for the Wall. Now.‚Äù ‚ÄúNow,‚Äù squawked the raven. ‚ÄúNow. Now.‚Äù The bird walked up the old man‚Äôs arm to his chest, and plucked a hair from his beard. ‚ÄúYou must. Must tell them.‚Äù ‚ÄúTell them what, my lord?‚Äù Sam asked politely. ‚ÄúAll. The Fist. The wildlings. Dragonglass. This. All.‚Äù His breathing was very shallow now, his voice a whisper. ‚ÄúTell my son. Jorah. Tell him, take the black. My wish. Dying wish.‚Äù ‚ÄúWish?‚Äù The raven cocked its head, beady black eyes shining. ‚ÄúCorn?‚Äù the bird asked. ‚ÄúNo corn,‚Äù said Mormont feebly. ‚ÄúTell Jorah. Forgive him. My son. Please. Go.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúThe blackest crows are down in the cellar, gorging,‚Äù said the old woman on the left, ‚Äúor up in the loft with the young ones. They‚Äôll be back soon, though. Best you be gone when they do. The horses run off, but Dyah‚Äôs caught two.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou said you‚Äôd help me,‚Äù Gilly reminded him. ‚ÄúI said Jon would help you. Jon‚Äôs brave, and he‚Äôs a good fighter, but I think he‚Äôs dead now. I‚Äôm a craven. And fat. Look how fat I am. Besides, Lord Mormont‚Äôs hurt. Can‚Äôt you see? I couldn‚Äôt leave the Lord Commander.‚Äù ‚ÄúChild,‚Äù said the other old woman, ‚Äúthat old crow‚Äôs gone before you. Look.‚Äù Mormont‚Äôs head was still in his lap, but his eyes were open and staring and his lips no longer moved. The raven cocked its head and squawked, then looked up at Sam. ‚ÄúCorn?‚Äù ‚ÄúNo corn. He has no corn.‚Äù Sam closed the Old Bear‚Äôs eyes and tried to think of a prayer, but all that came to mind was, ‚ÄúMother have mercy. Mother have mercy. Mother have mercy.‚Äù ‚ÄúYour mother can‚Äôt help you none,‚Äù said the old woman on the left. ‚ÄúThat dead old man can‚Äôt neither. You take his sword and you take that big warm fur cloak o‚Äô his and you take his horse if you can find him. And you go.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe girl don‚Äôt lie,‚Äù the old woman on the right said. ‚ÄúShe‚Äôs my girl, and I beat the lying out of her early on. You said you‚Äôd help her. Do what Ferny says, boy. Take the girl and be quick about it.‚Äù ‚ÄúQuick,‚Äù the raven said. ‚ÄúQuick quick quick.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhere?‚Äù asked Sam, puzzled. ‚ÄúWhere should I take her?‚Äù ‚ÄúSomeplace warm,‚Äù the two old women said as one. Gilly was crying. ‚ÄúMe and the babe. Please. I‚Äôll be your wife, like I was Craster‚Äôs. Please, ser crow. He‚Äôs a boy, just like Nella said he‚Äôd be. If you don‚Äôt take him, they will.‚Äù ‚ÄúThey?‚Äù said Sam, and the raven cocked its black head and echoed, ‚ÄúThey. They They.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe boy‚Äôs brothers,‚Äù said the old woman on the left. ‚ÄúCraster‚Äôs sons. The white cold‚Äôs rising out there, crow. I can feel it in my bones. These poor old bones don‚Äôt lie. They‚Äôll be here soon, the sons.‚Äù

{:#Lothar-Mallery-Gladden-Wylde-Lister-Lennocks-Beck-Mudge-Widow-Merriman-Audrey-Charlton-Lucas-Roote-Lord-and-Lady-Deddings-Alyn-of-Winterfell-Joth-Quick-bow-Matt-Randa-Anvil-Ryn-Ormond-Dudley-Pate-of-Mory-Pate-of-Lancewood-Old-Pate-Pate-of-Shermers-Grove-Blind-Wyl-the-Whittler-Goodwife-Maerie-Maerie-the-Whore-Becca-Fletcher Will-Harsley-and-Goodwife-Nolla}
**Lothar Mallery, Gladden Wylde, Lister, Lennocks, Beck, Mudge, Widow Merriman, Audrey Charlton, Lucas Roote, Lord and Lady Deddings, Alyn of Winterfell, Joth Quick-bow, Matt, Randa, Anvil Ryn, Ormond, Dudley, Pate of Mory, Pate of Lancewood, Old Pate, Pate of Shermer's Grove, Blind Wyl the Whittler, Goodwife Maerie, Maerie the Whore, Becca, Fletcher Will, Harsley, and Goodwife Nolla**

> ‚ÄúDo you deny that House Clegane was built upon dead children? I saw them lay Prince Aegon and Princess Rhaenys before the Iron Throne. By rights your arms should bear two bloody infants in place of those ugly dogs.‚Äù The Hound‚Äôs mouth twitched. ‚ÄúDo you take me for my brother? Is being born Clegane a crime?‚Äù ‚ÄúMurder is a crime.‚Äù ‚ÄúWho did I murder?‚Äù ‚ÄúLord Lothar Mallery and Ser Gladden Wylde,‚Äù said Harwin. ‚ÄúMy brothers Lister and Lennocks,‚Äù declared Jack-Be-Lucky. ‚ÄúGoodman Beck and Mudge the miller‚Äôs son, from Donnelwood,‚Äù an old woman called from the shadows. ‚ÄúMerriman‚Äôs widow, who loved so sweet,‚Äù added Greenbeard. ‚ÄúThem septons at Sludgy Pond.‚Äù ‚ÄúSer Audrey Charlton. His squire Lucas Roote. Every man, woman, and child in Fieldstone and Mousedown Mill.‚Äù ‚ÄúLord and Lady Deddings, that was so rich.‚Äù Tom Sevenstrings took up the count. ‚ÄúAlyn of Winterfell, Joth Quick-bow, Little Matt and his sister Randa, Anvil Ryn. Ser Ormond. Ser Dudley. Pate of Mory, Pate of Lancewood, Old Pate, and Pate of Shermer‚Äôs Grove. Blind Wyl the Whittler. Goodwife Maerie. Maerie the Whore. Becca the Baker. Ser Raymun Darry, Lord Darry, young Lord Darry. The Bastard of Bracken. Fletcher Will. Harsley. Goodwife Nolla‚Äî‚Äù

> ‚ÄúEnough.‚Äù The Hound‚Äôs face was tight with anger. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôre making noise. These names mean nothing. Who were they?‚Äù ‚ÄúPeople,‚Äù said Lord Beric. ‚ÄúPeople great and small, young and old. Good people and bad people, who died on the points of Lannister spears or saw their bellies opened by Lannister swords.‚Äù ‚ÄúIt wasn‚Äôt my sword in their bellies. Any man who says it was is a bloody liar.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou serve the Lannisters of Casterly Rock,‚Äù said Thoros. ‚ÄúOnce. Me and thousands more. Is each of us guilty of the crimes of the others?‚Äù Clegane spat. ‚ÄúMight be you are knights after all. You lie like knights, maybe you murder like knights.‚Äù Lem and Jack-Be-Lucky began to shout at him, but Dondarrion raised a hand for silence. ‚ÄúSay what you mean, Clegane.‚Äù ‚ÄúA knight‚Äôs a sword with a horse. The rest, the vows and the sacred oils and the lady‚Äôs favors, they‚Äôre silk ribbons tied round the sword. Maybe the sword‚Äôs prettier with ribbons hanging off it, but it will kill you just as dead. Well, bugger your ribbons, and shove your swords up your arses. I‚Äôm the same as you. The only difference is, I don‚Äôt lie about what I am. So kill me, but don‚Äôt call me a murderer while you stand there telling each other that your shit don‚Äôt stink. You hear me?‚Äù

{:#Hoster-Tully}
**Hoster Tully**

> Let the kings of winter have their cold crypt under the earth, Catelyn thought. The Tullys drew their strength from the river, and it was to the river they returned when their lives had run their course. They laid Lord Hoster in a slender wooden boat, clad in shining silver armor, plate-and-mail. His cloak was spread beneath him, rippling blue and red. His surcoat was divided blue-and-red as well. A trout, scaled in silver and bronze, crowned the crest of the greathelm they placed beside his head. On his chest they placed a painted wooden sword, his fingers curled about its hilt. Mail gauntlets hid his wasted hands, and made him look almost strong again. His massive oak-and-iron shield was set by his left side, his hunting horn to his right. The rest of the boat was filled with driftwood and kindling and scraps of parchment, and stones to make it heavy in the water. His banner flew from the prow, the leaping trout of Riverrun.

> Catelyn watched from the battlements, waiting and watching as she had waited and watched so many times before. Beneath her, the swift wild Tumblestone plunged like a spear into the side of the broad Red Fork, its blue-white current churning the muddy red-brown flow of the greater river. A morning mist hung over the water, as thin as gossamer and the wisps of memory. Bran and Rickon will be waiting for him, Catelyn thought sadly, as once I used to wait. The slim boat drifted out from under the red stone arch of the Water Gate, picking up speed as it was caught in the headlong rush of the Tumblestone and pushed out into the tumult where the waters met. As the boat emerged from beneath the high sheltering walls of the castle, its square sail filled with wind, and Catelyn saw sunlight flashing on her father‚Äôs helm. Lord Hoster Tully‚Äôs rudder held true, and he sailed serenely down the center of the channel, into the rising sun. ‚ÄúNow,‚Äù her uncle urged. Beside him, her brother Edmure‚ÄîLord Edmure now in truth, and how long would that take to grow used to?‚Äînocked an arrow to his bowstring. His squire held a brand to its point. Edmure waited until the flame caught, then lifted the great bow, drew the string to his ear, and let fly. With a deep thrum, the arrow sped upward. Catelyn followed its flight with her eyes and heart, until it plunged into the water with a soft hiss, well astern of Lord Hoster‚Äôs boat. Edmure cursed softly. ‚ÄúThe wind,‚Äù he said, pulling a second arrow. ‚ÄúAgain.‚Äù The brand kissed the oil-soaked rag behind the arrowhead, the flames went licking up, Edmure lifted, pulled, and released. High and far the arrow flew. Too far. It vanished in the river a dozen yards beyond the boat, its fire winking out in an instant. A flush was creeping up Edmure‚Äôs neck, red as his beard. ‚ÄúOnce more,‚Äù he commanded, taking a third arrow from the quiver. He is as tight as his bowstring, Catelyn thought. Ser Brynden must have seen the same thing. ‚ÄúLet me, my lord,‚Äù he offered. ‚ÄúI can do it,‚Äù Edmure insisted. He let them light the arrow, jerked the bow up, took a deep breath, drew back the arrow. For a long moment he seemed to hesitate while the fire crept up the shaft, crackling. Finally he released. The arrow flashed up and up, and finally curved down again, falling, falling¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand hissing past the billowing sail. A narrow miss, no more than a handspan, and yet a miss. ‚ÄúThe Others take it!‚Äù her brother swore. The boat was almost out of range, drifting in and out among the river mists. Wordless, Edmure thrust the bow at his uncle. ‚ÄúSwiftly,‚Äù Ser Brynden said. He nocked an arrow, held it steady for the brand, drew and released before Catelyn was quite sure that the fire had caught¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut as the shot rose, she saw the flames trailing through the air, a pale orange pennon. The boat had vanished in the mists. Falling, the flaming arrow was swallowed up as well¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut only for a heartbeat. Then, sudden as hope, they saw the red bloom flower. The sails took fire, and the fog glowed pink and orange. For a moment Catelyn saw the outline of the boat clearly, wreathed in leaping flames. Watch for me, little cat, she could hear him whisper. Catelyn reached out blindly, groping for her brother‚Äôs hand, but Edmure had moved away, to stand alone on the highest point of the battlements. Her uncle Brynden took her hand instead, twining his strong fingers through hers. Together they watched the little fire grow smaller as the burning boat receded in the distance. And then it was gone¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýdrifting downriver still, perhaps, or broken up and sinking. The weight of his armor would carry Lord Hoster down to rest in the soft mud of the riverbed, in the watery halls where the Tullys held eternal court, with schools of fish their last attendants.

{:#Daemon-Blackfyre-the-Toyne-Brothers-the-Vulture-King-Grand-Maester-Hareth-and-Rhaenyra-Targaryen}
**Daemon Blackfyre, the Toyne Brothers, the Vulture King, Grand Maester Hareth, and Rhaenyra Targaryen**

> ‚ÄúYour Grace,‚Äù Ser Axell said, ‚Äúas it please you, I have brought the onion knight.‚Äù ‚ÄúSo I see.‚Äù Stannis wore a grey wool tunic, a dark red mantle, and a plain black leather belt from which his sword and dagger hung. A red-gold crown with flameshaped points encircled his brows. The look of him was a shock. He seemed ten years older than the man that Davos had left at Storm‚Äôs End when he set sail for the Blackwater and the battle that would be their undoing. The king‚Äôs close-cropped beard was spiderwebbed with grey hairs, and he had dropped two stone or more of weight. He had never been a fleshy man, but now the bones moved beneath his skin like spears, fighting to cut free. Even his crown seemed too large for his head. His eyes were blue pits lost in deep hollows, and the shape of a skull could be seen beneath his face. Yet when he saw Davos, a faint smile brushed his lips. ‚ÄúSo the sea has returned me my knight of the fish and onions.‚Äù ‚ÄúIt did, Your Grace.‚Äù Does he know that he had me in his dungeon? Davos went to one knee. ‚ÄúRise, Ser Davos,‚Äù Stannis commanded. ‚ÄúI have missed you, ser. I have need of good counsel, and you never gave me less. So tell me true‚Äîwhat is the penalty for treason?‚Äù The word hung in the air. A frightful word, thought Davos. Was he being asked to condemn his cellmate? Or himself, perchance? Kings know the penalty for treason better than any man. ‚ÄúTreason?‚Äù he finally managed, weakly. ‚ÄúWhat else would you call it, to deny your king and seek to steal his rightful throne. I ask you again‚Äîwhat is the penalty for treason under the law?‚Äù Davos had no choice but to answer. ‚ÄúDeath,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúThe penalty is death, Your Grace.‚Äù ‚ÄúIt has always been so. I am not¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝI am not a cruel man, Ser Davos. You know me. Have known me long. This is not my decree. It has always been so, since Aegon‚Äôs day and before. Daemon Blackfyre, the brothers Toyne, the Vulture King, Grand Maester Hareth¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýtraitors have always paid with their lives¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýeven Rhaenyra Targaryen. She was daughter to one king and mother to two more, yet she died a traitor‚Äôs death for trying to usurp her brother‚Äôs crown. It is law. Law, Davos. Not cruelty.‚Äù

{:#Lord-Cafferen-and-Lord-Grandison}
**Lord Cafferen and Lord Grandison**

> ‚ÄúSire, Lord Florent meant no treason.‚Äù ‚ÄúDo smugglers have another name for it? I made him Hand, and he would have sold my rights for a bowl of pease porridge. He would even have given them Shireen. Mine only child, he would have wed to a bastard born of incest.‚Äù The king‚Äôs voice was thick with anger. ‚ÄúMy brother had a gift for inspiring loyalty. Even in his foes. At Summerhall he won three battles in a single day, and brought Lords Grandison and Cafferen back to Storm‚Äôs End as prisoners. He hung their banners in the hall as trophies. Cafferen‚Äôs white fawns were spotted with blood and Grandison‚Äôs sleeping lion was torn near in two. Yet they would sit beneath those banners of a night, drinking and feasting with Robert. He even took them hunting. ‚ÄòThese men meant to deliver you to Aerys to be burned,‚Äô I told him after I saw them throwing axes in the yard. ‚ÄòYou should not be putting axes in their hands.‚Äô Robert only laughed. I would have thrown Grandison and Cafferen into a dungeon, but he turned them into friends. Lord Cafferen died at Ashford Castle, cut down by Randyll Tarly whilst fighting for Robert. Lord Grandison was wounded on the Trident and died of it a year after. My brother made them love him, but it would seem that I inspire only betrayal. Even in mine own blood and kin. Brother, grandfather, cousins, good uncle¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúYour Grace,‚Äù said Ser Axell, ‚ÄúI beg you, give me the chance to prove to you that not all Florents are so feeble.‚Äù ‚ÄúSer Axell would have me resume the war,‚Äù King Stannis told Davos. ‚ÄúThe Lannisters think I am done and beaten, and my sworn lords have forsaken me, near every one. Even Lord Estermont, my own mother‚Äôs father, has bent his knee to Joffrey. The few loyal men who remain to me are losing heart. They waste their days drinking and gambling, and lick their wounds like beaten curs.‚Äù

{:#Chelsted-Rossart-Belis-and-Garigus}
**Chelsted, Rossart, Belis, and Garigus**

> He floated in heat, in memory. ‚ÄúAfter dancing griffins lost the Battle of the Bells, Aerys exiled him.‚Äù Why am I telling this absurd ugly child? ‚ÄúHe had finally realized that Robert was no mere outlaw lord to be crushed at whim, but the greatest threat House Targaryen had faced since Daemon Blackfyre. The king reminded Lewyn Martell gracelessly that he held Elia and sent him to take command of the ten thousand Dornishmen coming up the kingsroad. Jon Darry and Barristan Selmy rode to Stoney Sept to rally what they could of griffins‚Äô men, and Prince Rhaegar returned from the south and persuaded his father to swallow his pride and summon my father. But no raven returned from Casterly Rock, and that made the king even more afraid. He saw traitors everywhere, and Varys was always there to point out any he might have missed. So His Grace commanded his alchemists to place caches of wildfire all over King‚Äôs Landing. Beneath Baelor‚Äôs Sept and the hovels of Flea Bottom, under stables and storehouses, at all seven gates, even in the cellars of the Red Keep itself. ‚ÄúEverything was done in the utmost secrecy by a handful of master pyromancers. They did not even trust their own acolytes to help. The queen‚Äôs eyes had been closed for years, and Rhaegar was busy marshaling an army. But Aerys‚Äôs new mace-and-dagger Hand was not utterly stupid, and with Rossart, Belis, and Garigus coming and going night and day, he became suspicious. Chelsted, that was his name, Lord Chelsted.‚Äù It had come back to him suddenly, with the telling. ‚ÄúI‚Äôd thought the man craven, but the day he confronted Aerys he found some courage somewhere. He did all he could to dissuade him. He reasoned, he jested, he threatened, and finally he begged. When that failed he took off his chain of office and flung it down on the floor. Aerys burnt him alive for that, and hung his chain about the neck of Rossart, his favorite pyromancer. The man who had cooked Lord Rickard Stark in his own armor. And all the time, I stood by the foot of the Iron Throne in my white plate, still as a corpse, guarding my liege and all his sweet secrets. ‚ÄúMy Sworn Brothers were all away, you see, but Aerys liked to keep me close. I was my father‚Äôs son, so he did not trust me. He wanted me where Varys could watch me, day and night. So I heard it all.‚Äù He remembered how Rossart‚Äôs eyes would shine when he unrolled his maps to show where the substance must be placed. Garigus and Belis were the same. ‚ÄúRhaegar met Robert on the Trident, and you know what happened there. When the word reached court, Aerys packed the queen off to Dragonstone with Prince Viserys. Princess Elia would have gone as well, but he forbade it. Somehow he had gotten it in his head that Prince Lewyn must have betrayed Rhaegar on the Trident, but he thought he could keep Dorne loyal so long as he kept Elia and Aegon by his side. The traitors want my city, I heard him tell Rossart, but I‚Äôll give them naught but ashes. Let Robert be king over charred bones and cooked meat. The Targaryens never bury their dead, they burn them. Aerys meant to have the greatest funeral pyre of them all. Though if truth be told, I do not believe he truly expected to die.
