He had been the thirteenth man to lead the Night‚Äôs Watch, she said; a warrior who knew no fear. ‚ÄúAnd that was the fault in him,‚Äù she would add, ‚Äúfor all men must know fear.‚Äù A woman was his downfall; a woman glimpsed from atop the Wall, with skin as white as the moon and eyes like blue stars. Fearing nothing, he chased her and caught her and loved her, though her skin was cold as ice, and when he gave his seed to her he gave his soul as well. He brought her back to the Nightfort and proclaimed her a queen and himself her king, and with strange sorceries he bound his Sworn Brothers to his will. For thirteen years they had ruled, Night‚Äôs King and his corpse queen, till finally the Stark of Winterfell and Joramun of the wildlings had joined to free the Watch from bondage. After his fall, when it was found he had been sacrificing to the Others, all records of Night‚Äôs King had been destroyed, his very name forbidden. ‚ÄúSome say he was a Bolton,‚Äù Old Nan would always end. ‚ÄúSome say a Magnar out of Skagos, some say Umber, Flint, or Norrey. Some would have you think he was a Woodfoot, from them who ruled Bear Island before the ironmen came. He never was. He was a Stark, the brother of the man who brought him down.‚Äù She always pinched Bran on the nose then, he would never forget it. ‚ÄúHe was a Stark of Winterfell, and who can say? Mayhaps his name was Brandon. Mayhaps he slept in this very bed in this very room.‚Äù No, Bran thought, but he walked in this castle, where we‚Äôll sleep tonight. He did not like that notion very much at all. Night‚Äôs King was only a man by light of day, Old Nan would always say, but the night was his to rule. And it‚Äôs getting dark.

{:#Scarb}
**Scarb**

> The pink-and-white hero taunted the besiegers for an hour, mocking their manhood, mothers, wives, and gods. Meereen‚Äôs defenders cheered him on from the city walls. ‚ÄúHis name is Oznak zo Pahl,‚Äù Brown Ben Plumm told her when he arrived for the war council. He was the new commander of the Second Sons, chosen by a vote of his fellow sellswords. ‚ÄúI was bodyguard to his uncle once, before I joined the Second Sons. The Great Masters, what a ripe lot o‚Äô maggots. The women weren‚Äôt so bad, though it was worth your life to look at the wrong one the wrong way. I knew a man, Scarb, this Oznak cut his liver out. Claimed to be defending a lady‚Äôs honor, he did, said Scarb had raped her with his eyes. How do you rape a wench with eyes, I ask you? But his uncle is the richest man in Meereen and his father commands the city guard, so I had to run like a rat before he killed me too.‚Äù

{:#Oznak-zo-Pahl}
**Oznak zo Pahl**

> Oznak zo Pahl charged a third time, and now Dany could see plainly that he was riding past Belwas, the way a Westerosi knight might ride at an opponent in a tilt, rather than at him, like a Dothraki riding down a foe. The flat level ground allowed the charger to get up a good speed, but it also made it easy for the eunuch to dodge the cumbersome fourteen-foot lance. Meereen‚Äôs pink-and-white hero tried to anticipate this time, and swung his lance sideways at the last second to catch Strong Belwas when he dodged. But the eunuch had anticipated too, and this time he dropped down instead of spinning sideways. The lance passed harmlessly over his head. And suddenly Belwas was rolling, and bringing the razor-sharp arakh around in a silver arc. They heard the charger scream as the blade bit into his legs, and then the horse was falling, the hero tumbling from the saddle. A sudden silence swept along the brick parapets of Meereen. Now it was Dany‚Äôs people who were screaming and cheering. Oznak leapt clear of his horse and managed to draw his sword before Strong Belwas was on him. Steel sang against steel, too fast and furious for Dany to follow the blows. It could not have been a dozen heartbeats before Belwas‚Äôs chest was awash in blood from a slice below his breasts, and Oznak zo Pahl had an arakh planted right between his ram‚Äôs horns. The eunuch wrenched the blade loose and parted the hero‚Äôs head from his body with three savage blows to the neck. He held it up high for the Meereenese to see, then flung it toward the city gates and let it bounce and roll across the sand. ‚ÄúSo much for the hero of Meereen,‚Äù said Daario, laughing. ‚ÄúA victory without meaning,‚Äù Ser Jorah cautioned. ‚ÄúWe will not win Meereen by killing its defenders one at a time.‚Äù ‚ÄúNo,‚Äù Dany agreed, ‚Äúbut I‚Äôm pleased we killed this one.‚Äù The defenders on the walls began firing their crossbows at Belwas, but the bolts fell short or skittered harmlessly along the ground. The eunuch turned his back on the steeltipped rain, lowered his trousers, squatted, and shat in the direction of the city. He wiped himself with Oznak‚Äôs striped cloak, and paused long enough to loot the hero‚Äôs corpse and put the dying horse out of his agony before trudging back to the olive grove.

{:#Mero-The-Titans-Bastard}
**Mero "The Titan's Bastard"**

> Dany had stopped to speak to a pregnant woman who wanted the Mother of Dragons to name her baby when someone reached up and grabbed her left wrist. Turning, she glimpsed a tall ragged man with a shaved head and a sunburnt face. ‚ÄúNot so hard,‚Äù she started to say, but before she could finish he‚Äôd yanked her bodily from the saddle. The ground came up and knocked the breath from her, as her silver whinnied and backed away. Stunned, Dany rolled to her side and pushed herself onto one elbow¬Ý‚Ä¶ ¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand then she saw the sword. ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs the treacherous sow,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúI knew you‚Äôd come to get your feet kissed one day.‚Äù His head was bald as a melon, his nose red and peeling, but she knew that voice and those pale green eyes. ‚ÄúI‚Äôm going to start by cutting off your teats.‚Äù Dany was dimly aware of Missandei shouting for help. A freedman edged forward, but only a step. One quick slash, and he was on his knees, blood running down his face. Mero wiped his sword on his breeches. ‚ÄúWho‚Äôs next?‚Äù ‚ÄúI am.‚Äù Arstan Whitebeard leapt from his horse and stood over her, the salt wind riffling through his snowy hair, both hands on his tall hardwood staff. ‚ÄúGrandfather,‚Äù Mero said, ‚Äúrun off before I break your stick in two and bugger you with‚Äî‚Äù The old man feinted with one end of the staff, pulled it back, and whipped the other end about faster than Dany would have believed. The Titan‚Äôs Bastard staggered back into the surf, spitting blood and broken teeth from the ruin of his mouth. Whitebeard put Dany behind him. Mero slashed at his face. The old man jerked back, cat-quick. The staff thumped Mero‚Äôs ribs, sending him reeling. Arstan splashed sideways, parried a looping cut, danced away from a second, checked a third mid-swing. The moves were so fast she could hardly follow. Missandei was pulling Dany to her feet when she heard a crack. She thought Arstan‚Äôs staff had snapped until she saw the jagged bone jutting from Mero‚Äôs calf. As he fell, the Titan‚Äôs Bastard twisted and lunged, sending his point straight at the old man‚Äôs chest. Whitebeard swept the blade aside almost contemptuously and smashed the other end of his staff against the big man‚Äôs temple. Mero went sprawling, blood bubbling from his mouth as the waves washed over him. A moment later the freedmen washed over him too, knives and stones and angry fists rising and falling in a frenzy.

{:#Baelor-Targaryen}
**Baelor Targaryen**

> ‚ÄúI once had the great good fortune to see the Citadel‚Äôs copy of Lives of Four Kings,‚Äù Prince Oberyn was telling her lord husband. ‚ÄúThe illuminations were wondrous to behold, but Kaeth was too kind by half to King Viserys.‚Äù Tyrion gave him a sharp look. ‚ÄúToo kind? He scants Viserys shamefully, in my view. It should have been Lives of Five Kings.‚Äù The prince laughed. ‚ÄúViserys hardly reigned a fortnight.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe reigned more than a year,‚Äù said Tyrion. Oberyn gave a shrug. ‚ÄúA year or a fortnight, what does it matter? He poisoned his own nephew to gain the throne and then did nothing once he had it.‚Äù ‚ÄúBaelor starved himself to death, fasting,‚Äù said Tyrion. ‚ÄúHis uncle served him loyally as Hand, as he had served the Young Dragon before him. Viserys might only have reigned a year, but he ruled for fifteen, while Daeron warred and Baelor prayed.‚Äù He made a sour face. ‚ÄúAnd if he did remove his nephew, can you blame him? Someone had to save the realm from Baelor‚Äôs follies.‚Äù Sansa was shocked. ‚ÄúBut Baelor the Blessed was a great king. He walked the Boneway barefoot to make peace with Dorne, and rescued the Dragonknight from a snakepit. The vipers refused to strike him because he was so pure and holy.‚Äù Prince Oberyn smiled. ‚ÄúIf you were a viper, my lady, would you want to bite a bloodless stick like Baelor the Blessed? I‚Äôd sooner save my fangs for someone juicier¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúMy prince is playing with you, Lady Sansa,‚Äù said the woman Ellaria Sand. ‚ÄúThe septons and singers like to say that the snakes did not bite Baelor, but the truth is very different. He was bitten half a hundred times, and should have died from it.‚Äù ‚ÄúIf he had, Viserys would have reigned a dozen years,‚Äù said Tyrion, ‚Äúand the Seven Kingdoms might have been better served. Some believe Baelor was deranged by all that venom.‚Äù ‚ÄúYes,‚Äù said Prince Oberyn, ‚Äúbut I‚Äôve seen no snakes in this Red Keep of yours. So how do you account for Joffrey?‚Äù ‚ÄúI prefer not to.‚Äù

{:#Joffrey-Baratheon}
**Joffrey Baratheon**

> ‚ÄúBribes might sway some of the lesser lords,‚Äù Tyrion said, ‚Äúbut never Highgarden.‚Äù ‚ÄúTrue,‚Äù Littlefinger admitted. ‚ÄúThe Knight of Flowers is the key there. Mace Tyrell has two older sons, but Loras has always been his favorite. Win him, and Highgarden will be yours.‚Äù Yes, Tyrion thought. ‚ÄúIt seems to me we should take a lesson from the late Lord Renly. We can win the Tyrell alliance as he did. With a marriage.‚Äù Varys understood the quickest. ‚ÄúYou think to wed King Joffrey to Margaery Tyrell.‚Äù ‚ÄúI do.‚Äù Renly‚Äôs young queen was no more than fifteen, sixteen, he seemed to recall¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýolder than Joffrey, but a few years were nothing, it was so neat and sweet he could taste it. ‚ÄúJoffrey is betrothed to Sansa Stark,‚Äù Cersei objected. ‚ÄúMarriage contracts can be broken. What advantage is there in wedding the king to the daughter of a dead traitor?‚Äù Littlefinger spoke up. ‚ÄúYou might point out to His Grace that the Tyrells are much wealthier than the Starks, and that Margaery is said to be lovely¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand beddable besides.‚Äù ‚ÄúYes,‚Äù said Tyrion, ‚ÄúJoff ought to like that well enough.‚Äù ‚ÄúMy son is too young to care about such things.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou think so?‚Äù asked Tyrion. ‚ÄúHe‚Äôs thirteen, Cersei. The same age at which I married.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou shamed us all with that sorry episode. Joffrey is made of finer stuff.‚Äù ‚ÄúSo fine that he had Ser Boros rip off Sansa‚Äôs gown.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe was angry with the girl.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe was angry with that cook‚Äôs boy who spilled the soup last night as well, but he didn‚Äôt strip him naked.‚Äù ‚ÄúThis was not a matter of some spilled soup‚Äî‚Äù No, it was a matter of some pretty teats.

> ‚ÄúSing louder!‚Äù the Queen of Thorns shouted at Butterbumps. ‚ÄúThese old ears are almost deaf, you know. Are you whispering at me, you fat fool? I don‚Äôt pay you for whispers. Sing!‚Äù ‚Äú‚Ä¶¬ÝTHE BEAR!‚Äù thundered Butterbumps, his great deep voice echoing off the rafters. ‚ÄúOH, COME, THEY SAID, OH COME TO THE FAIR! THE FAIR! SAID HE, BUT I‚ÄôM A BEAR! ALL BLACK AND BROWN, AND COVERED WITH HAIR!‚Äù The wrinkled old lady smiled. ‚ÄúAt Highgarden we have many spiders amongst the flowers. So long as they keep to themselves we let them spin their little webs, but if they get underfoot we step on them.‚Äù She patted Sansa on the back of the hand. ‚ÄúNow, child, the truth. What sort of man is this Joffrey, who calls himself Baratheon but looks so very Lannister?‚Äù ‚ÄúAND DOWN THE ROAD FROM HERE TO THERE. FROM HERE! TO THERE! THREE BOYS, A GOAT, AND A DANCING BEAR!‚Äù Sansa felt as though her heart had lodged in her throat. The Queen of Thorns was so close she could smell the old woman‚Äôs sour breath. Her gaunt thin fingers were pinching her wrist. To her other side, Margaery was listening as well. A shiver went through her. ‚ÄúA monster,‚Äù she whispered, so tremulously she could scarcely hear her own voice. ‚ÄúJoffrey is a monster. He lied about the butcher‚Äôs boy and made Father kill my wolf. When I displease him, he has the Kingsguard beat me. He‚Äôs evil and cruel, my lady, it‚Äôs so. And the queen as well.‚Äù Lady Olenna Tyrell and her granddaughter exchanged a look. ‚ÄúAh,‚Äù said the old woman, ‚Äúthat‚Äôs a pity.‚Äù Oh, gods, thought Sansa, horrified. If Margaery won‚Äôt marry him, Joff will know that I‚Äôm to blame. ‚ÄúPlease,‚Äù she blurted, ‚Äúdon‚Äôt stop the wedding¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúHave no fear, Lord Puff Fish is determined that Margaery shall be queen. And the word of a Tyrell is worth more than all the gold in Casterly Rock. At least it was in my day. Even so, we thank you for the truth, child.‚Äù

> The dwarf woman drank deep, the wine running down her chin. When she lowered the skin, she wiped her mouth with the back of a wrinkled hand and said, ‚ÄúSour wine for sour tidings, what could be more fitting? The king is dead, is that sour enough for you?‚Äù Arya‚Äôs heart caught in her throat. ‚ÄúWhich bloody king is dead, crone?‚Äù Lem demanded. ‚ÄúThe wet one. The kraken king, m‚Äôlords. I dreamt him dead and he died, and the iron squids now turn on one another. Oh, and Lord Hoster Tully‚Äôs died too, but you know that, don‚Äôt you? In the hall of kings, the goat sits alone and fevered as the great dog descends on him.‚Äù The old woman took another long gulp of wine, squeezing the skin as she raised it to her lips. The great dog. Did she mean the Hound? Or maybe his brother, the Mountain That Rides? Arya was not certain. They bore the same arms, three black dogs on a yellow field. Half the men whose deaths she prayed for belonged to Ser Gregor Clegane; Polliver, Dunsen, Raff the Sweetling, the Tickler, and Ser Gregor himself. Maybe Lord Beric will hang them all. ‚ÄúI dreamt a wolf howling in the rain, but no one heard his grief,‚Äù the dwarf woman was saying. ‚ÄúI dreamt such a clangor I thought my head might burst, drums and horns and pipes and screams, but the saddest sound was the little bells. I dreamt of a maid at a feast with purple serpents in her hair, venom dripping from their fangs. And later I dreamt that maid again, slaying a savage giant in a castle built of snow.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúYou do look quite exquisite, child,‚Äù Lady Olenna Tyrell told Sansa when she tottered up to them in a cloth-of-gold gown that must have weighed more than she did. ‚ÄúThe wind has been at your hair, though.‚Äù The little old woman reached up and fussed at the loose strands, tucking them back into place and straightening Sansa‚Äôs hair net. ‚ÄúI was very sorry to hear about your losses,‚Äù she said as she tugged and fiddled. ‚ÄúYour brother was a terrible traitor, I know, but if we start killing men at weddings they‚Äôll be even more frightened of marriage than they are presently. There, that‚Äôs better.‚Äù Lady Olenna smiled. ‚ÄúI am pleased to say I shall be leaving for Highgarden the day after next. I have had quite enough of this smelly city, thank you. Perhaps you would like to accompany me for a little visit, whilst the men are off having their war? I shall miss my Margaery so dreadfully, and all her lovely ladies. Your company would be such sweet solace.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou are too kind, my lady,‚Äù said Sansa, ‚Äúbut my place is with my lord husband.‚Äù Lady Olenna gave Tyrion a wrinkled, toothless smile. ‚ÄúOh? Forgive a silly old woman, my lord, I did not mean to steal your lovely wife. I assumed you would be off leading a Lannister host against some wicked foe.‚Äù ‚ÄúA host of dragons and stags. The master of coin must remain at court to see that all the armies are paid for.‚Äù ‚ÄúTo be sure. Dragons and stags, that‚Äôs very clever. And dwarf‚Äôs pennies as well. I have heard of these dwarf‚Äôs pennies. No doubt collecting those is such a dreadful chore.‚Äù ‚ÄúI leave the collecting to others, my lady.‚Äù ‚ÄúOh, do you? I would have thought you might want to tend to it yourself. We can‚Äôt have the crown being cheated of its dwarf‚Äôs pennies, now. Can we?‚Äù ‚ÄúGods forbid.‚Äù Tyrion was beginning to wonder whether Lord Luthor Tyrell had ridden off that cliff intentionally.

> The jousters were a pair of dwarfs. One was mounted on an ugly grey dog, long of leg and heavy of jaw. The other rode an immense spotted sow. Painted wooden armor clattered and clacked as the little knights bounced up and down in their saddles. Their shields were bigger than they were, and they wrestled manfully with their lances as they clomped along, swaying this way and that and eliciting gusts of mirth. One knight was all in gold, with a black stag painted on his shield; the other wore grey and white, and bore a wolf device. Their mounts were barded likewise. Tyrion glanced along the dais at all the laughing faces. Joffrey was red and breathless, Tommen was hooting and hopping up and down in his seat, Cersei was chuckling politely, and even Lord Tywin looked mildly amused. Of all those at the high table, only Sansa Stark was not smiling. He could have loved her for that, but if truth be told the Stark girl‚Äôs eyes were far away, as if she had not even seen the ludicrous riders loping toward her. The dwarfs are not to blame, Tyrion decided. When they are done, I shall compliment them and give them a fat purse of silver. And come the morrow, I will find whoever planned this little diversion and arrange for a different sort of thanks. When the dwarfs reined up beneath the dais to salute the king, the wolf knight dropped his shield. As he leaned over to grab for it, the stag knight lost control of his heavy lance and slammed him across the back. The wolf knight fell off his pig, and his lance tumbled over and boinked his foe on the head. They both wound up on the floor in a great tangle. When they rose, both tried to mount the dog. Much shouting and shoving followed. Finally they regained their saddles, only mounted on each other‚Äôs steed, holding the wrong shield and facing backward. It took some time to sort that out, but in the end they spurred to opposite ends of the hall, and wheeled about for the tilt. As the lords and ladies guffawed and giggled, the little men came together with a crash and a clatter, and the wolf knight‚Äôs lance struck the helm of the stag knight and knocked his head clean off. It spun through the air spattering blood to land in the lap of Lord Gyles. The headless dwarf careened around the tables, flailing his arms. Dogs barked, women shrieked, and Moon Boy made a great show of swaying perilously back and forth on his stilts, until Lord Gyles pulled a dripping red melon out of the shattered helm, at which point the stag knight poked his face up out of his armor, and another storm of laughter rocked the hall. The knights waited for it to die, circled around each other trading colorful insults, and were about to separate for another joust when the dog threw its rider to the floor and mounted the sow. The huge pig squealed in distress, while the wedding guests squealed with laughter, especially when the stag knight leapt onto the wolf knight, let down his wooden breeches, and started to pump away frantically at the other‚Äôs nether portions. ‚ÄúI yield, I yield,‚Äù the dwarf on the bottom screamed. ‚ÄúGood ser, put up your sword!‚Äù ‚ÄúI would, I would, if you‚Äôll stop moving the sheath!‚Äù the dwarf on the top replied, to the merriment of all.

> Joffrey was snorting wine from both nostrils. Gasping, he lurched to his feet, almost knocking over his tall two-handed chalice. ‚ÄúA champion,‚Äù he shouted. ‚ÄúWe have a champion!‚Äù The hall began to quiet when it was seen that the king was speaking. The dwarfs untangled, no doubt anticipating the royal thanks. ‚ÄúNot a true champion, though,‚Äù said Joff. ‚ÄúA true champion defeats all challengers.‚Äù The king climbed up on the table. ‚ÄúWho else will challenge our tiny champion?‚Äù With a gleeful smile, he turned toward Tyrion. ‚ÄúUncle! You‚Äôll defend the honor of my realm, won‚Äôt you? You can ride the pig!‚Äù The laughter crashed over him like a wave. Tyrion Lannister did not remember rising, nor climbing on his chair, but he found himself standing on the table. The hall was a torchlit blur of leering faces. He twisted his face into the most hideous mockery of a smile the Seven Kingdoms had ever seen. ‚ÄúYour Grace,‚Äù he called, ‚ÄúI‚Äôll ride the pig¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut only if you ride the dog!‚Äù Joff scowled, confused. ‚ÄúMe? I‚Äôm no dwarf. Why me?‚Äù Stepped right into the cut, Joff. ‚ÄúWhy, you‚Äôre the only man in the hall that I‚Äôm certain of defeating!‚Äù He could not have said which was sweeter; the instant of shocked silence, the gale of laughter that followed, or the look of blind rage on his nephew‚Äôs face. The dwarf hopped back to the floor well satisfied, and by the time he looked back Ser Osmund and Ser Meryn were helping Joff climb down as well. When he noticed Cersei glaring at him, Tyrion blew her a kiss. It was a relief when the musicians began to play. The tiny jousters led dog and sow from the hall, the guests returned to their trenchers of brawn, and Tyrion called for another cup of wine. But suddenly he felt Ser Garlan‚Äôs hand on his sleeve. ‚ÄúMy lord, beware,‚Äù the knight warned. ‚ÄúThe king.‚Äù Tyrion turned in his seat. Joffrey was almost upon him, red-faced and staggering, wine slopping over the rim of the great golden wedding chalice he carried in both hands. ‚ÄúYour Grace,‚Äù was all he had time to say before the king upended the chalice over his head. The wine washed down over his face in a red torrent. It drenched his hair, stung his eyes, burned in his wound, ran down his cheeks, and soaked the velvet of his new doublet. ‚ÄúHow do you like that, Imp?‚Äù Joffrey mocked. Tyrion‚Äôs eyes were on fire. He dabbed at his face with the back of a sleeve and tried to blink the world back into clarity. ‚ÄúThat was ill done, Your Grace,‚Äù he heard Ser Garlan say quietly. ‚ÄúNot at all, Ser Garlan.‚Äù Tyrion dare not let this grow any uglier than it was, not here, with half the realm looking on. ‚ÄúNot every king would think to honor a humble subject by serving him from his own royal chalice. A pity the wine spilled.‚Äù ‚ÄúIt didn‚Äôt spill,‚Äù said Joffrey, too graceless to take the retreat Tyrion offered him. ‚ÄúAnd I wasn‚Äôt serving you, either.‚Äù Queen Margaery appeared suddenly at Joffrey‚Äôs elbow. ‚ÄúMy sweet king,‚Äù the Tyrell girl entreated, ‚Äúcome, return to your place, there‚Äôs another singer waiting.‚Äù ‚ÄúAlaric of Eysen,‚Äù said Lady Olenna Tyrell, leaning on her cane and taking no more notice of the wine-soaked dwarf than her granddaughter had done. ‚ÄúI do so hope he plays us ‚ÄòThe Rains of Castamere.‚Äô It has been an hour, I‚Äôve forgotten how it goes.‚Äù ‚ÄúSer Addam has a toast he wants to make as well,‚Äù said Margaery. ‚ÄúYour Grace, please.‚Äù ‚ÄúI have no wine,‚Äù Joffrey declared. ‚ÄúHow can I drink a toast if I have no wine? Uncle Imp, you can serve me. Since you won‚Äôt joust you‚Äôll be my cupbearer.‚Äù ‚ÄúI would be most honored.‚Äù ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs not meant to be an honor!‚Äù Joffrey screamed. ‚ÄúBend down and pick up my chalice.‚Äù Tyrion did as he was bid, but as he reached for the handle Joff kicked the chalice through his legs. ‚ÄúPick it up! Are you as clumsy as you are ugly?‚Äù He had to crawl under the table to find the thing. ‚ÄúGood, now fill it with wine.‚Äù He claimed a flagon from a serving girl and filled the goblet three-quarters full. ‚ÄúNo, on your knees, dwarf.‚Äù Kneeling, Tyrion raised up the heavy cup, wondering if he was about to get a second bath. But Joffrey took the wedding chalice one-handed, drank deep, and set it on the table. ‚ÄúYou can get up now, Uncle.‚Äù His legs cramped as he tried to rise, and almost spilled him again. Tyrion had to grab hold of a chair to steady himself. Ser Garlan lent him a hand. Joffrey laughed, and Cersei as well. Then others. He could not see who, but he heard them. ‚ÄúYour Grace.‚Äù Lord Tywin‚Äôs voice was impeccably correct. ‚ÄúThey are bringing in the pie. Your sword is needed.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe pie?‚Äù Joffrey took his queen by the hand. ‚ÄúCome, my lady, it‚Äôs the pie.‚Äù The guests stood, shouting and applauding and smashing their wine cups together as the great pie made its slow way down the length of the hall, wheeled along by a half-dozen beaming cooks. Two yards across it was, crusty and golden brown, and they could hear squeaks and thumpings coming from inside it.

> Joffrey and Margaery joined hands to lift the greatsword and swung it down together in a silvery arc. When the piecrust broke, the doves burst forth in a swirl of white feathers, scattering in every direction, flapping for the windows and the rafters. A roar of delight went up from the benches, and the fiddlers and pipers in the gallery began to play a sprightly tune. Joff took his bride in his arms, and whirled her around merrily. A serving man placed a slice of hot pigeon pie in front of Tyrion and covered it with a spoon of lemon cream. The pigeons were well and truly cooked in this pie, but he found them no more appetizing than the white ones fluttering about the hall. Sansa was not eating either. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôre deathly pale, my lady,‚Äù Tyrion said. ‚ÄúYou need a breath of cool air, and I need a fresh doublet.‚Äù He stood and offered her his hand. ‚ÄúCome.‚Äù But before they could make their retreat, Joffrey was back. ‚ÄúUncle, where are you going? You‚Äôre my cupbearer, remember?‚Äù ‚ÄúI need to change into fresh garb, Your Grace. May I have your leave?‚Äù ‚ÄúNo. I like the look of you this way. Serve me my wine.‚Äù The king‚Äôs chalice was on the table where he‚Äôd left it. Tyrion had to climb back onto his chair to reach it. Joff yanked it from his hands and drank long and deep, his throat working as the wine ran purple down his chin. ‚ÄúMy lord,‚Äù Margaery said, ‚Äúwe should return to our places. Lord Buckler wants to toast us.‚Äù ‚ÄúMy uncle hasn‚Äôt eaten his pigeon pie.‚Äù Holding the chalice one-handed, Joff jammed his other into Tyrion‚Äôs pie. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs ill luck not to eat the pie,‚Äù he scolded as he filled his mouth with hot spiced pigeon. ‚ÄúSee, it‚Äôs good.‚Äù Spitting out flakes of crust, he coughed and helped himself to another fistful. ‚ÄúDry, though. Needs washing down.‚Äù Joff took a swallow of wine and coughed again, more violently. ‚ÄúI want to see, kof, see you ride that, kof kof, pig, Uncle. I want¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù His words broke up in a fit of coughing. Margaery looked at him with concern. ‚ÄúYour Grace?‚Äù ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs, kof, the pie, noth‚Äîkof, pie.‚Äù Joff took another drink, or tried to, but all the wine came spewing back out when another spate of coughing doubled him over. His face was turning red. ‚ÄúI, kof, I can‚Äôt, kof kof kof kof¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù The chalice slipped from his hand and dark red wine went running across the dais. ‚ÄúHe‚Äôs choking,‚Äù Queen Margaery gasped. Her grandmother moved to her side. ‚ÄúHelp the poor boy!‚Äù the Queen of Thorns screeched, in a voice ten times her size. ‚ÄúDolts! Will you all stand about gaping? Help your king!‚Äù Ser Garlan shoved Tyrion aside and began to pound Joffrey on the back. Ser Osmund Kettleblack ripped open the king‚Äôs collar. A fearful high thin sound emerged from the boy‚Äôs throat, the sound of a man trying to suck a river through a reed; then it stopped, and that was more terrible still. ‚ÄúTurn him over!‚Äù Mace Tyrell bellowed at everyone and no one. ‚ÄúTurn him over, shake him by his heels!‚Äù A different voice was calling, ‚ÄúWater, give him some water!‚Äù The High Septon began to pray loudly. Grand Maester Pycelle shouted for someone to help him back to his chambers, to fetch his potions. Joffrey began to claw at his throat, his nails tearing bloody gouges in the flesh. Beneath the skin, the muscles stood out hard as stone. Prince Tommen was screaming and crying. He is going to die, Tyrion realized. He felt curiously calm, though pandemonium raged all about him. They were pounding Joff on the back again, but his face was only growing darker. Dogs were barking, children were wailing, men were shouting useless advice at each other. Half the wedding guests were on their feet, some shoving at each other for a better view, others rushing for the doors in their haste to get away. Ser Meryn pried the king‚Äôs mouth open to jam a spoon down his throat. As he did, the boy‚Äôs eyes met Tyrion‚Äôs. He has Jaime‚Äôs eyes. Only he had never seen Jaime look so scared. The boy‚Äôs only thirteen. Joffrey was making a dry clacking noise, trying to speak. His eyes bulged white with terror, and he lifted a hand¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýreaching for his uncle, or pointing¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝIs he begging my forgiveness, or does he think I can save him? ‚ÄúNoooo,‚Äù Cersei wailed, ‚ÄúFather help him, someone help him, my son, my son¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù Tyrion found himself thinking of Robb Stark. My own wedding is looking much better in hindsight. He looked to see how Sansa was taking this, but there was so much confusion in the hall that he could not find her. But his eyes fell on the wedding chalice, forgotten on the floor. He went and scooped it up. There was still a half-inch of deep purple wine in the bottom of it. Tyrion considered it a moment, then poured it on the floor. Margaery Tyrell was weeping in her grandmother‚Äôs arms as the old lady said, ‚ÄúBe brave, be brave.‚Äù Most of the musicians had fled, but one last flutist in the gallery was blowing a dirge. In the rear of the throne room scuffling had broken out around the doors, and the guests were trampling on each other. Ser Addam‚Äôs gold cloaks moved in to restore order. Guests were rushing headlong out into the night, some weeping, some stumbling and retching, others white with fear. It occurred to Tyrion belatedly that it might be wise to leave himself. When he heard Cersei‚Äôs scream, he knew that it was over. I should leave. Now. Instead he waddled toward her. His sister sat in a puddle of wine, cradling her son‚Äôs body. Her gown was torn and stained, her face white as chalk. A thin black dog crept up beside her, sniffing at Joffrey‚Äôs corpse. ‚ÄúThe boy is gone, Cersei,‚Äù Lord Tywin said. He put his gloved hand on his daughter‚Äôs shoulder as one of his guardsmen shooed away the dog. ‚ÄúUnhand him now. Let him go.‚Äù She did not hear. It took two Kingsguard to pry loose her fingers, so the body of King Joffrey Baratheon could slide limp and lifeless to the floor. The High Septon knelt beside him. ‚ÄúFather Above, judge our good King Joffrey justly,‚Äù he intoned, beginning the prayer for the dead. Margaery Tyrell began to sob, and Tyrion heard her mother Lady Alerie saying, ‚ÄúHe choked, sweetling. He choked on the pie. It was naught to do with you. He choked. We all saw.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe did not choke.‚Äù Cersei‚Äôs voice was sharp as Ser Ilyn‚Äôs sword. ‚ÄúMy son was poisoned.‚Äù She looked to the white knights standing helplessly around her. ‚ÄúKingsguard, do your duty.‚Äù ‚ÄúMy lady?‚Äù said Ser Loras Tyrell, uncertain. ‚ÄúArrest my brother,‚Äù she commanded him. ‚ÄúHe did this, the dwarf. Him and his little wife. They killed my son. Your king. Take them! Take them both!‚Äù

> When she pulled it free, her long auburn hair cascaded down her back and across her shoulders. The web of spun silver hung from her fingers, the fine metal glimmering softly, the stones black in the moonlight. Black amethysts from Asshai. One of them was missing. Sansa lifted the net for a closer look. There was a dark smudge in the silver socket where the stone had fallen out. A sudden terror filled her. Her heart hammered against her ribs, and for an instant she held her breath. Why am I so scared, it‚Äôs only an amethyst, a black amethyst from Asshai, no more than that. It must have been loose in the setting, that‚Äôs all. It was loose and it fell out, and now it‚Äôs lying somewhere in the throne room, or in the yard, unless¬Ý‚Ä¶ Ser Dontos had said the hair net was magic, that it would take her home. He told her she must wear it tonight at Joffrey‚Äôs wedding feast. The silver wire stretched tight across her knuckles. Her thumb rubbed back and forth against the hole where the stone had been. She tried to stop, but her fingers were not her own. Her thumb was drawn to the hole as the tongue is drawn to a missing tooth. What kind of magic? The king was dead, the cruel king who had been her gallant prince a thousand years ago. If Dontos had lied about the hair net, had he lied about the rest as well? What if he never comes? What if there is no ship, no boat on the river, no escape? What would happen to her then? She heard a faint rustle of leaves, and stuffed the silver hair net down deep in the pocket of her cloak. ‚ÄúWho‚Äôs there?‚Äù she cried. ‚ÄúWho is it?‚Äù The godswood was dim and dark, and the bells were ringing Joff into his grave. ‚ÄúMe.‚Äù He staggered out from under the trees, reeling drunk. He caught her arm to steady himself. ‚ÄúSweet Jonquil, I‚Äôve come. Your Florian has come, don‚Äôt be afraid.‚Äù Sansa pulled away from his touch. ‚ÄúYou said I must wear the hair net. The silver net with¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýwhat sort of stones are those?‚Äù ‚ÄúAmethysts. Black amethysts from Asshai, my lady.‚Äù ‚ÄúThey‚Äôre no amethysts. Are they? Are they? You lied.‚Äù ‚ÄúBlack amethysts,‚Äù he swore. ‚ÄúThere was magic in them.‚Äù ‚ÄúThere was murder in them!‚Äù ‚ÄúSoftly, my lady, softly. No murder. He choked on his pigeon pie.‚Äù Dontos chortled. ‚ÄúOh, tasty tasty pie. Silver and stones, that‚Äôs all it was, silver and stone and magic.‚Äù The bells were tolling, and the wind was making a noise like he had made as he tried to suck a breath of air. ‚ÄúYou poisoned him. You did. You took a stone from my hair¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúHush, you‚Äôll be the death of us. I did nothing. Come, we must away, they‚Äôll search for you. Your husband‚Äôs been arrested.‚Äù ‚ÄúTyrion?‚Äù she said, shocked. ‚ÄúDo you have another husband? The Imp, the dwarf uncle, she thinks he did it.‚Äù He grabbed her hand and pulled at her. ‚ÄúThis way, we must away, quickly now, have no fear.‚Äù

{:#Dontos-Hollard}
**Dontos Hollard**

> The eastern sky was vague with the first hint of dawn when Sansa finally saw a ghostly shape in the darkness ahead; a trading galley, her sails furled, moving slowly on a single bank of oars. As they drew closer, she saw the ship‚Äôs figurehead, a merman with a golden crown blowing on a great seashell horn. She heard a voice cry out, and the galley swung slowly about. As they came alongside, the galley dropped a rope ladder over the rail. The rower shipped the oars and helped Sansa to her feet. ‚ÄúUp now. Go on, girl, I got you.‚Äù Sansa thanked him for his kindness, but received no answer but a grunt. It was much easier going up the rope ladder than it had been coming down the cliff. The oarsman Oswell followed close behind her, while Ser Dontos remained in the boat. Two sailors were waiting by the rail to help her onto the deck. Sansa was trembling. ‚ÄúShe‚Äôs cold,‚Äù she heard someone say. He took off his cloak and put it around her shoulders. ‚ÄúThere, is that better, my lady? Rest easy, the worst is past and done.‚Äù She knew the voice. But he‚Äôs in the Vale, she thought. Ser Lothor Brune stood beside him with a torch. ‚ÄúLord Petyr,‚Äù Dontos called from the boat. ‚ÄúI must needs row back, before they think to look for me.‚Äù Petyr Baelish put a hand on the rail. ‚ÄúBut first you‚Äôll want your payment. Ten thousand dragons, was it?‚Äù ‚ÄúTen thousand.‚Äù Dontos rubbed his mouth with the back of his hand. ‚ÄúAs you promised, my lord.‚Äù ‚ÄúSer Lothor, the reward.‚Äù Lothor Brune dipped his torch. Three men stepped to the gunwale, raised crossbows, fired. One bolt took Dontos in the chest as he looked up, punching through the left crown on his surcoat. The others ripped into throat and belly. It happened so quickly neither Dontos nor Sansa had time to cry out. When it was done, Lothor Brune tossed the torch down on top of the corpse. The little boat was blazing fiercely as the galley moved away. ‚ÄúYou killed him.‚Äù Clutching the rail, Sansa turned away and retched. Had she escaped the Lannisters to tumble into worse? ‚ÄúMy lady,‚Äù Littlefinger murmured, ‚Äúyour grief is wasted on such a man as that. He was a sot, and no man‚Äôs friend.‚Äù ‚ÄúBut he saved me.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe sold you for a promise of ten thousand dragons. Your disappearance will make them suspect you in Joffrey‚Äôs death. The gold cloaks will hunt, and the eunuch will jingle his purse. Dontos¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýwell, you heard him. He sold you for gold, and when he‚Äôd drunk it up he would have sold you again. A bag of dragons buys a man‚Äôs silence for a while, but a well-placed quarrel buys it forever.‚Äù He smiled sadly. ‚ÄúAll he did he did at my behest. I dared not befriend you openly. When I heard how you saved his life at Joff‚Äôs tourney, I knew he would be the perfect catspaw.‚Äù Sansa felt sick. ‚ÄúHe said he was my Florian.‚Äù ‚ÄúDo you perchance recall what I said to you that day your father sat the Iron Throne?‚Äù The moment came back to her vividly. ‚ÄúYou told me that life was not a song. That I would learn that one day, to my sorrow.‚Äù She felt tears in her eyes, but whether she wept for Ser Dontos Hollard, for Joff, for Tyrion, or for herself, Sansa could not say. ‚ÄúIs it all lies, forever and ever, everyone and everything?‚Äù ‚ÄúAlmost everyone. Save you and I, of course.‚Äù He smiled.

> ‚ÄúMy lord, I¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝI do not understand¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝJoffrey gave you Harrenhal, made you Lord Paramount of the Trident¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýwhy¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúWhy should I wish him dead?‚Äù Littlefinger shrugged. ‚ÄúI had no motive. Besides, I am a thousand leagues away in the Vale. Always keep your foes confused. If they are never certain who you are or what you want, they cannot know what you are like to do next. Sometimes the best way to baffle them is to make moves that have no purpose, or even seem to work against you. Remember that, Sansa, when you come to play the game.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhat ‚Ä¶ what game?‚Äù ‚ÄúThe only game. The game of thrones.‚Äù He brushed back a strand of her hair. ‚ÄúYou are old enough to know that your mother and I were more than friends. There was a time when Cat was all I wanted in this world. I dared to dream of the life we might make and the children she would give me¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut she was a daughter of Riverrun, and Hoster Tully. Family, Duty, Honor, Sansa. Family, Duty, Honor meant I could never have her hand. But she gave me something finer, a gift a woman can give but once. How could I turn my back upon her daughter? In a better world, you might have been mine, not Eddard Stark‚Äôs. My loyal loving daughter¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝPut Joffrey from your mind, sweetling. Dontos, Tyrion, all of them. They will never trouble you again. You are safe now, that‚Äôs all that matters. You are safe with me, and sailing home.‚Äù

{:#Vargo-Hoat}
**Vargo Hoat**

> They had her in the bear pit. King Harren the Black had wished to do even his bearbaiting in lavish style. The pit was ten yards across and five yards deep, walled in stone, floored with sand, and encircled by six tiers of marble benches. The Brave Companions filled only a quarter of the seats, Jaime saw as he swung down clumsily from his horse. The sellswords were so fixed on the spectacle beneath that only those across the pit noticed their arrival. Brienne wore the same ill-fitting gown she‚Äôd worn to supper with Roose Bolton. No shield, no breastplate, no chainmail, not even boiled leather, only pink satin and Myrish lace. Maybe the goat thought she was more amusing when dressed as a woman. Half her gown was hanging off in tatters, and her left arm dripped blood where the bear had raked her. At least they gave her a sword. The wench held it one-handed, moving sideways, trying to put some distance between her and the bear. That‚Äôs no good, the ring‚Äôs too small. She needed to attack, to make a quick end to it. Good steel was a match for any bear. But the wench seemed afraid to close. The Mummers showered her with insults and obscene suggestions. ‚ÄúThis is none of our concern,‚Äù Steelshanks warned Jaime. ‚ÄúLord Bolton said the wench was theirs, to do with as they liked.‚Äù ‚ÄúHer name‚Äôs Brienne.‚Äù Jaime descended the steps, past a dozen startled sellswords. Vargo Hoat had taken the lord‚Äôs box in the lowest tier. ‚ÄúLord Vargo,‚Äù he called over the shouts. The Qohorik almost spilt his wine. ‚ÄúKingthlayer?‚Äù The left side of his face was bandaged clumsily, the linen over his ear spotted with blood. ‚ÄúPull her out of there.‚Äù ‚ÄúThay out of thith, Kingthlayer, unleth you‚Äôd like another thump.‚Äù He waved a wine cup. ‚ÄúYour thee-mooth bit oth my ear. Thmall wonder her father will not ranthom thuch a freak.‚Äù

> Lord Tywin pushed himself out of his chair, breath hissing between his teeth. ‚ÄúWho did this? If Lady Catelyn thinks‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúLady Catelyn held a sword to my throat and made me swear to return her daughters. This was your goat‚Äôs work. Vargo Hoat, the Lord of Harrenhal!‚Äù Lord Tywin looked away, disgusted. ‚ÄúNo longer. Ser Gregor‚Äôs taken the castle. The sellswords deserted their erstwhile captain almost to a man, and some of Lady Whent‚Äôs old people opened a postern gate. Clegane found Hoat sitting alone in the Hall of a Hundred Hearths, half-mad with pain and fever from a wound that festered. His ear, I‚Äôm told.‚Äù Jaime had to laugh. Too sweet! His ear! He could scarcely wait to tell Brienne, though the wench wouldn‚Äôt find it half so funny as he did. ‚ÄúIs he dead yet?‚Äù ‚ÄúSoon. They have taken off his hands and feet, but Clegane seems amused by the way the Qohorik slobbers.‚Äù Jaime‚Äôs smile curdled. ‚ÄúWhat about his Brave Companions?‚Äù ‚ÄúThe few who stayed at Harrenhal are dead. The others scattered. They‚Äôll make for ports, I‚Äôll warrant, or try and lose themselves in the woods.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúWe all went our own ways, after we left Harrenhal. Urswyck and his lot rode south for Oldtown. Rorge thought he might slip out at Saltpans. Me and my lads made for Maidenpool, but we couldn‚Äôt get near a ship.‚Äù The Dornishman hefted his spear. ‚ÄúYou did for Vargo with that bite, you know. His ear turned black and started leaking pus. Rorge and Urswyck were for leaving, but the Goat says we got to hold his castle. Lord of Harrenhal, he says he is, no one was going to take it off him. He said it slobbery, the way he always talked. We heard the Mountain killed him piece by piece. A hand one day, a foot the next, lopped off neat and clean. They bandaged up the stumps so Hoat didn‚Äôt die. He was saving his cock for last, but some bird called him to King‚Äôs Landing, so he finished it and rode off.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúRaff, gather the garrison together in the Hall of a Hundred Hearths. Your captives as well. I‚Äôll want to see them. Those whores from the crossroads too. Oh, and Hoat. I was distraught to hear that he had died. I‚Äôd like to look upon his head.‚Äù When they brought it to him, he found that the Goat‚Äôs lips had been sliced off, along with his ears and most of his nose. The crows had supped upon his eyes. It was still recognizably Hoat, however. Jaime would have known his beard anywhere; an absurd rope of hair two feet long, dangling from a pointed chin. Elsewise, only a few leathery strips of flesh still clung to the Qohorik‚Äôs skull. ‚ÄúWhere is the rest of him?‚Äù he asked. No one wanted to tell him. Finally, Shitmouth lowered his eyes, and muttered, ‚ÄúRotted, ser. And et.‚Äù ‚ÄúOne of the captives was always begging food,‚Äù Rafford admitted, ‚Äúso Ser said to give him roast goat. The Qohorik didn‚Äôt have much meat on him, though. Ser took his hands and feet first, then his arms and legs.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe fat bugger got most, m‚Äôlord,‚Äù Shitmouth offered, ‚Äúbut Ser, he said to see that all the captives had a taste. And Hoat too, his own self. That whoreson ‚Äòud slobber when we fed him, and the grease‚Äôd run down into that skinny beard o‚Äô his.‚Äù Father, Jaime thought, your dogs have both gone mad. He found himself remembering tales he had first heard as a child at Casterly Rock, of mad Lady Lothston who bathed in tubs of blood and presided over feasts of human flesh within these very walls. Somehow revenge had lost its savor. ‚ÄúTake this and throw it in the lake.‚Äù Jaime tossed Hoat‚Äôs head to Peck, and turned to address the garrison. ‚ÄúUntil such time as Lord Petyr arrives to claim his seat, Ser Bonifer Hasty shall hold Harrenhal in the name of the crown. Those of you who wish may join him, if he‚Äôll have you. The rest will ride with me to Riverrun.‚Äù

{:#Donal-Noye-Spotted-Pate-and-Mag-the-Mighty}
**Donal Noye, Spotted Pate, and Mag the Mighty**

> The Wall was too big to be stormed by any conventional means; too high for ladders or siege towers, too thick for battering rams. No catapult could throw a stone large enough to breach it, and if you tried to set it on fire, the icemelt would quench the flames. You could climb over, as the raiders did near Greyguard, but only if you were strong and fit and sure-handed, and even then you might end up like Jarl, impaled on a tree. They must take the gate, or they cannot pass. But the gate was a crooked tunnel through the ice, smaller than any castle gate in the Seven Kingdoms, so narrow that rangers must lead their garrons through single file. Three iron grates closed the inner passage, each locked and chained and protected by a murder hole. The outer door was old oak, nine inches thick and studded with iron, not easy to break through.

> The last twenty feet of the tunnel was where they‚Äôd fought and died. The outer door of studded oak had been hacked and broken and finally torn off its hinges, and one of the giants had crawled in through the splinters. The lantern bathed the grisly scene in a sullen reddish light. Pyp turned aside to retch, and Jon found himself envying Maester Aemon his blindness. Noye and his men had been waiting within, behind a gate of heavy iron bars like the two Pyp had just unlocked. The two crossbows had gotten off a dozen quarrels as the giant struggled toward them. Then the spearmen must have come to the fore, stabbing through the bars. Still the giant found the strength to reach through, twist the head off Spotted Pate, seize the iron gate, and wrench the bars apart. Links of broken chain lay strewn across the floor. One giant. All this was the work of one giant. ‚ÄúAre they all dead?‚Äù Maester Aemon asked softly. ‚ÄúYes. Donal was the last.‚Äù Noye‚Äôs sword was sunk deep in the giant‚Äôs throat, halfway to the hilt. The armorer had always seemed such a big man to Jon, but locked in the giant‚Äôs massive arms he looked almost like a child. ‚ÄúThe giant crushed his spine. I don‚Äôt know who died first.‚Äù He took the lantern and moved forward for a better look. ‚ÄúMag.‚Äù I am the last of the giants. He could feel the sadness there, but he had no time for sadness. ‚ÄúIt was Mag the Mighty. The king of the giants.‚Äù

{:#Robar-Royce-and-Emmon-Cuy}
**Robar Royce and Emmon Cuy**

> Dusk was falling when Catelyn left the pavilion. Ser Robar Royce fell in beside her. She knew him slightly‚Äîone of Bronze Yohn‚Äôs sons, comely in a rough-hewn way, a tourney warrior of some renown. Renly had gifted him with a rainbow cloak and a suit of blood red armor, and named him one of his seven. ‚ÄúYou are a long way from the Vale, ser,‚Äù she told him. ‚ÄúAnd you far from Winterfell, my lady.‚Äù ‚ÄúI know what brought me here, but why have you come? This is not your battle, no more than it is mine.‚Äù ‚ÄúI made it my battle when I made Renly my king.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe Royces are bannermen to House Arryn.‚Äù ‚ÄúMy lord father owes Lady Lysa fealty, as does his heir. A second son must find glory where he can.‚Äù Ser Robar shrugged. ‚ÄúA man grows weary of tourneys.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúWhat of Renly‚Äôs host?‚Äù ‚ÄúThe greater part of his foot remains at Bitterbridge.‚Äù Varys abandoned the brazier to take his seat at the table. ‚ÄúMost of the lords who rode with Lord Renly to Storm‚Äôs End have gone over banner-and-blade to Stannis, with all their chivalry.‚Äù ‚ÄúLed by the Florents, I‚Äôd wager,‚Äù said Littlefinger. Varys gave him a simpering smile. ‚ÄúYou would win, my lord. Lord Alester was indeed the first to bend the knee. Many others followed.‚Äù ‚ÄúMany,‚Äù Tyrion said pointedly, ‚Äúbut not all?‚Äù ‚ÄúNot all,‚Äù agreed the eunuch. ‚ÄúNot Loras Tyrell, nor Randyll Tarly, nor Mathis Rowan. And Storm‚Äôs End itself has not yielded. Ser Cortnay Penrose holds the castle in Renly‚Äôs name, and will not believe his liege is dead. He demands to see the mortal remains before he opens his gates, but it seems that Renly‚Äôs corpse has unaccountably vanished. Carried away, most likely. A fifth of Renly‚Äôs knights departed with Ser Loras rather than bend the knee to Stannis. It‚Äôs said the Knight of Flowers went mad when he saw his king‚Äôs body, and slew three of Renly‚Äôs guards in his wrath, among them Emmon Cuy and Robar Royce.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúAt the Hand‚Äôs tourney, don‚Äôt you remember? You rode a white courser, and your armor was a hundred different kinds of flowers. You gave me a rose. A red rose. You threw white roses to the other girls that day.‚Äù It made her flush to speak of it. ‚ÄúYou said no victory was half as beautiful as me.‚Äù Ser Loras gave her a modest smile. ‚ÄúI spoke only a simple truth, that any man with eyes could see.‚Äù He doesn‚Äôt remember, Sansa realized, startled. He is only being kind to me, he doesn‚Äôt remember me or the rose or any of it. She had been so certain that it meant something, that it meant everything. A red rose, not a white. ‚ÄúIt was after you unhorsed Ser Robar Royce,‚Äù she said, desperately. He took his hand from her arm. ‚ÄúI slew Robar at Storm‚Äôs End, my lady.‚Äù It was not a boast; he sounded sad. Him, and another of King Renly‚Äôs Rainbow Guard as well, yes. Sansa had heard the women talking of it round the well, but for a moment she‚Äôd forgotten. ‚ÄúThat was when Lord Renly was killed, wasn‚Äôt it? How terrible for your poor sister.‚Äù ‚ÄúFor Margaery?‚Äù His voice was tight. ‚ÄúTo be sure. She was at Bitterbridge, though. She did not see.‚Äù ‚ÄúEven so, when she heard¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù Ser Loras brushed the hilt of his sword lightly with his hand. Its grip was white leather, its pommel a rose in alabaster. ‚ÄúRenly is dead. Robar as well. What use to speak of them?‚Äù The sharpness in his tone took her aback. ‚ÄúI¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýmy lord, I¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝI did not mean to give offense, ser.‚Äù ‚ÄúNor could you, Lady Sansa,‚Äù Ser Loras replied, but all the warmth had gone from his voice. Nor did he take her arm again.

> Ser Loras made no move to rise. ‚ÄúShe fled,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúShe and Catelyn Stark, they left him in his blood and ran. Why would they, if it was not their work?‚Äù He stared at the table. ‚ÄúRenly gave me the van. Otherwise it would have been me helping him don his armor. He often entrusted that task to me. We had¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýwe had prayed together that night. I left him with her. Ser Parmen and Ser Emmon were guarding the tent, and Ser Robar Royce was there as well. Ser Emmon swore Brienne had¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýalthough¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúYes?‚Äù Jaime prompted, sensing a doubt. ‚ÄúThe gorget was cut through. One clean stroke, through a steel gorget. Renly‚Äôs armor was the best, the finest steel. How could she do that? I tried myself, and it was not possible. She‚Äôs freakish strong for a woman, but even the Mountain would have needed a heavy axe. And why armor him and then cut his throat?‚Äù He gave Jaime a confused look. ‚ÄúIf not her, though¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýhow could it be a shadow?‚Äù ‚ÄúAsk her.‚Äù Jaime came to a decision. ‚ÄúGo to her cell. Ask your questions and hear her answers. If you are still convinced that she murdered Lord Renly, I will see that she answers for it. The choice will be yours. Accuse her, or release her. All I ask is that you judge her fairly, on your honor as a knight.‚Äù Ser Loras stood. ‚ÄúI shall. On my honor.‚Äù ‚ÄúWe are done, then.‚Äù The younger man started for the door. But there he turned back. ‚ÄúRenly thought she was absurd. A woman dressed in man‚Äôs mail, pretending to be a knight.‚Äù ‚ÄúIf he‚Äôd ever seen her in pink satin and Myrish lace, he would not have complained.‚Äù ‚ÄúI asked him why he kept her close, if he thought her so grotesque. He said that all his other knights wanted things of him, castles or honors or riches, but all that Brienne wanted was to die for him. When I saw him all bloody, with her fled and the three of them unharmed¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýif she‚Äôs innocent, then Robar and Emmon¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù He could not seem to say the words. Jaime had not stopped to consider that aspect of it. ‚ÄúI would have done the same, ser.‚Äù The lie came easy, but Ser Loras seemed grateful for it.

{:#Red-Alyn-of-the-Rosewood}
**Red Alyn of the Rosewood**

> Already their archers were stealing forward, pushing their rolling mantlets. ‚ÄúHere come our breakfast arrows,‚Äù Pyp announced cheerfully, as he did every morning. It‚Äôs good that he can make a jape of it, Jon thought. Someone has to. Three days ago, one of those breakfast arrows had caught Red Alyn of the Rosewood in the leg. You could still see his body at the foot of the Wall, if you cared to lean out far enough. Jon had to think that it was better for them to smile at Pyp‚Äôs jest than to brood over Alyn‚Äôs corpse.

{:#Endrew-Tarth-and-Aladale-Wynch}
**Endrew Tarth and Aladale Wynch**

> When he heard the rattle of the winch chains and the iron groan of the cage door opening, he knew it would be Hobb bringing their breakfast as he did every morning. The sight of Mance‚Äôs turtle had robbed Jon of his appetite. Their oil was all but gone, and the last barrel of pitch had been rolled off the Wall two nights ago. They would soon run short of arrows as well, and there were no fletchers making more. And the night before last, a raven had come from the west, from Ser Denys Mallister. Bowen Marsh had chased the wildlings all the way to the Shadow Tower, it seemed, and then farther, down into the gloom of the Gorge. At the Bridge of Skulls he had met the Weeper and three hundred wildlings and won a bloody battle. But the victory had been a costly one. More than a hundred brothers slain, among them Ser Endrew Tarth and Ser Aladale Wynch. The Old Pomegranate himself had been carried back to the Shadow Tower sorely wounded. Maester Mullin was tending him, but it would be some time before he was fit to return to Castle Black. When he had read that, Jon had dispatched Zei to Mole‚Äôs Town on their best horse to plead with the villagers to help man the Wall. She never returned. When he sent Mully after her, he came back to report the whole village deserted, even the brothel. Most likely Zei had followed them, straight down the kingsroad. Maybe we should all do the same, Jon reflected glumly.

{:#Lyonel-Tyrell}
**Lyonel Tyrell**

> Tyrion rose to give Prince Oberyn a mocking bow. ‚ÄúAre judges permitted to visit the accused?‚Äù ‚ÄúPrinces are permitted to go where they will. Or so I told your guards.‚Äù The Red Viper took a seat. ‚ÄúMy father will be displeased with you.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe happiness of Tywin Lannister has never been high on my list of concerns. Is it Dornish wine you‚Äôre drinking?‚Äù ‚ÄúFrom the Arbor.‚Äù Oberyn made a face. ‚ÄúRed water. Did you poison him?‚Äù ‚ÄúNo. Did you?‚Äù The prince smiled. ‚ÄúDo all dwarfs have tongues like yours? Someone is going to cut it out one of these days.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou are not the first to tell me that. Perhaps I should cut it out myself, it seems to make no end of trouble.‚Äù ‚ÄúSo I‚Äôve seen. I think I may drink some of Lord Redwyne‚Äôs grape juice after all.‚Äù ‚ÄúAs you like.‚Äù Tyrion served him a cup. The man took a sip, sloshed it about in his mouth, and swallowed. ‚ÄúIt will serve, for the moment. I will send you up some strong Dornish wine on the morrow.‚Äù He took another sip. ‚ÄúI have turned up that golden-haired whore I was hoping for.‚Äù ‚ÄúSo you found Chataya‚Äôs?‚Äù ‚ÄúAt Chataya‚Äôs I bedded the black-skinned girl. Alayaya, I believe she is called. Exquisite, despite the stripes on her back. But the whore I referred to is your sister.‚Äù ‚ÄúHas she seduced you yet?‚Äù Tyrion asked, unsurprised. Oberyn laughed aloud. ‚ÄúNo, but she will if I meet her price. The queen has even hinted at marriage. Her Grace needs another husband, and who better than a prince of Dorne? Ellaria believes I should accept. Just the thought of Cersei in our bed makes her wet, the randy wench. And we should not even need to pay the dwarf‚Äôs penny. All your sister requires from me is one head, somewhat overlarge and missing a nose.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd?‚Äù said Tyrion, waiting. By way of answer Prince Oberyn swirled his wine, and said, ‚ÄúWhen the Young Dragon conquered Dorne so long ago, he left the Lord of Highgarden to rule us after the Submission of Sunspear. This Tyrell moved with his tail from keep to keep, chasing rebels and making certain that our knees stayed bent. He would arrive in force, take a castle for his own, stay a moon‚Äôs turn, and ride on to the next castle. It was his custom to turn the lords out of their own chambers and take their beds for himself. One night he found himself beneath a heavy velvet canopy. A sash hung down near the pillows, should he wish to summon a wench. He had a taste for Dornish women, this Lord Tyrell, and who can blame him? So he pulled upon the sash, and when he did the canopy above him split open, and a hundred red scorpions fell down upon his head. His death lit a fire that soon swept across Dorne, undoing all the Young Dragon‚Äôs victories in a fortnight. The kneeling men stood up, and we were free again.‚Äù ‚ÄúI know the tale,‚Äù said Tyrion. ‚ÄúWhat of it?‚Äù ‚ÄúJust this. If I should ever find a sash beside my own bed, and pull on it, I would sooner have the scorpions fall upon me than the queen in all her naked beauty.‚Äù

{:#Lormelle-Long-Lance-Cedrik-Storm-and-Simon-Toyne}
**Lormelle Long Lance, Cedrik Storm, and Simon Toyne**

> Ser Barristan of House Selmy. Firstborn son of Ser Lyonel Selmy of Harvest Hall. Served as squire to Ser Manfred Swann. Named ‚Äúthe Bold‚Äù in his 10th year, when he donned borrowed armor to appear as a mystery knight in the tourney at Blackhaven, where he was defeated and unmasked by Duncan, Prince of Dragonflies. Knighted in his 16th year by King Aegon V Targaryen, after performing great feats of prowess as a mystery knight in the winter tourney at King‚Äôs Landing, defeating Prince Duncan the Small and Ser Duncan the Tall, Lord Commander of the Kingsguard. Slew Maelys the Monstrous, last of the Blackfyre Pretenders, in single combat during the War of the Ninepenny Kings. Defeated Lormelle Long Lance and Cedrik Storm, the Bastard of Bronzegate. Named to the Kingsguard in his 23rd year, by Lord Commander Ser Gerold Hightower. Defended the passage against all challengers in the tourney of the Silver Bridge. Victor in the m√™l√©e at Maidenpool. Brought King Aerys II to safety during the Defiance of Duskendale, despite an arrow wound in the chest. Avenged the murder of his Sworn Brother, Ser Gwayne Gaunt. Rescued Lady Jeyne Swann and her septa from the Kingswood Brotherhood, defeating Simon Toyne and the Smiling Knight, and slaying the former. In the Oldtown tourney, defeated and unmasked the mystery knight Blackshield, revealing him as the Bastard of Uplands. Sole champion of Lord Steffon‚Äôs tourney at Storm‚Äôs End, whereat he unhorsed Lord Robert Baratheon, Prince Oberyn Martell, Lord Leyton Hightower, Lord Jon Connington, Lord Jason Mallister, and Prince Rhaegar Targaryen. Wounded by arrow, spear, and sword at the Battle of the Trident whilst fighting beside his Sworn Brothers and Rhaegar Prince of Dragonstone. Pardoned, and named Lord Commander of the Kingsguard, by King Robert I Baratheon. Served in the honor guard that brought Lady Cersei of House Lannister to King‚Äôs Landing to wed King Robert. Led the attack on Old Wyk during Balon Greyjoy‚Äôs Rebellion. Champion of the tourney at King‚Äôs Landing, in his 57th year. Dismissed from service by King Joffrey I Baratheon in his 61st year, for reasons of advanced age.

{:#The-Smiling-Knight-Jonothor-Darry-and-Sumner-Crakehall}
**The Smiling Knight, Jonothor Darry, and Sumner Crakehall**

> Ser Jaime of House Lannister. Firstborn son of Lord Tywin and Lady Joanna of Casterly Rock. Served against the Kingswood Brotherhood as squire to Lord Sumner Crakehall. Knighted in his 15th year by Ser Arthur Dayne of the Kingsguard, for valor in the field. Chosen for the Kingsguard in his 15th year by King Aerys II Targaryen. During the Sack of King‚Äôs Landing, slew King Aerys II at the foot of the Iron Throne. Thereafter known as the ‚ÄúKingslayer.‚Äù Pardoned for his crime by King Robert I Baratheon. Served in the honor guard that brought his sister the Lady Cersei Lannister to King‚Äôs Landing to wed King Robert. Champion in the tourney held at King‚Äôs Landing on the occasion of their wedding. Summed up like that, his life seemed a rather scant and mingy thing. Ser Barristan could have recorded a few of his other tourney victories, at least. And Ser Gerold might have written a few more words about the deeds he‚Äôd performed when Ser Arthur Dayne broke the Kingswood Brotherhood. He had saved Lord Sumner‚Äôs life as Big Belly Ben was about to smash his head in, though the outlaw had escaped him. And he‚Äôd held his own against the Smiling Knight, though it was Ser Arthur who slew him. What a fight that was, and what a foe. The Smiling Knight was a madman, cruelty and chivalry all jumbled up together, but he did not know the meaning of fear. And Dayne, with Dawn in hand¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝThe outlaw‚Äôs longsword had so many notches by the end that Ser Arthur had stopped to let him fetch a new one. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs that white sword of yours I want,‚Äù the robber knight told him as they resumed, though he was bleeding from a dozen wounds by then. ‚ÄúThen you shall have it, ser,‚Äù the Sword of the Morning replied, and made an end of it. The world was simpler in those days, Jaime thought, and men as well as swords were made of finer steel. Or was it only that he had been fifteen? They were all in their graves now, the Sword of the Morning and the Smiling Knight, the White Bull and Prince Lewyn, Ser Oswell Whent with his black humor, earnest Jon Darry, Simon Toyne and his Kingswood Brotherhood, bluff old Sumner Crakehall. And me, that boy I was¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýwhen did he die, I wonder? When I donned the white cloak? When I opened Aerys‚Äôs throat? That boy had wanted to be Ser Arthur Dayne, but someplace along the way he had become the Smiling Knight instead.

> He looked about the Round Room once more. White wool hangings covered the walls, and there was a white shield and two crossed longswords mounted above the hearth. The chair behind the table was old black oak, with cushions of blanched cowhide, the leather worn thin. Worn by the bony arse of Barristan the Bold and Ser Gerold Hightower before him, by Prince Aemon the Dragonknight, Ser Ryam Redwyne, and the Demon of Darry, by Ser Duncan the Tall and the Pale Griffin Alyn Connington. How could the Kingslayer belong in such exalted company? Yet here he was. The table itself was old weirwood, pale as bone, carved in the shape of a huge shield supported by three white stallions. By tradition the Lord Commander sat at the top of the shield, and the brothers three to a side, on the rare occasions when all seven were assembled. The book that rested by his elbow was massive; two feet tall and a foot and a half wide, a thousand pages thick, fine white vellum bound between covers of bleached white leather with gold hinges and fastenings. The Book of the Brothers was its formal name, but more often it was simply called the White Book. Within the White Book was the history of the Kingsguard. Every knight who‚Äôd ever served had a page, to record his name and deeds for all time. On the top left-hand corner of each page was drawn the shield the man had carried at the time he was chosen, inked in rich colors. Down in the bottom right corner was the shield of the Kingsguard; snow-white, empty, pure. The upper shields were all different; the lower shields were all the same. In the space between were written the facts of each man‚Äôs life and service. The heraldic drawings and illuminations were done by septons sent from the Great Sept of Baelor three times a year, but it was the duty of the Lord Commander to keep the entries up to date. My duty, now. Once he learned to write with his left hand, that is. The White Book was well behind. The deaths of Ser Mandon Moore and Ser Preston Greenfield needed to be entered, and the brief bloody Kingsguard service of Sandor Clegane as well. New pages must be started for Ser Balon Swann, Ser Osmund Kettleblack, and the Knight of Flowers. I will need to summon a septon to draw their shields.

> Jaime sat alone at the table while the shadows crept across the room. As dusk began to settle, he lit a candle and opened the White Book to his own page. Quill and ink he found in a drawer. Beneath the last line Ser Barristan had entered, he wrote in an awkward hand that might have done credit to a six-year-old being taught his first letters by a maester: Defeated in the Whispering Wood by the Young Wolf Robb Stark during the War of the Five Kings. Held captive at Riverrun and ransomed for a promise unfulfilled. Captured again by the Brave Companions, and maimed at the word of Vargo Hoat their captain, losing his sword hand to the blade of Zollo the Fat. Returned safely to King‚Äôs Landing by Brienne, the Maid of Tarth. When he was done, more than three-quarters of his page still remained to be filled between the gold lion on the crimson shield on top and the blank white shield at the bottom. Ser Gerold Hightower had begun his history, and Ser Barristan Selmy had continued it, but the rest Jaime Lannister would need to write for himself. He could write whatever he chose, henceforth. Whatever he chose ‚Ä¶

> ‚ÄúThe smallfolk too,‚Äù sniffed her daughter. ‚ÄúSer Harwyn says they hide them and feed them, and when he asks where they‚Äôve gone, they lie. They lie to their own lords!‚Äù ‚ÄúHave their tongues out,‚Äù urged Strongboar. ‚ÄúGood luck getting answers then,‚Äù said Jaime. ‚ÄúIf you want their help, you need to make them love you. That was how Arthur Dayne did it, when we rode against the Kingswood Brotherhood. He paid the smallfolk for the food we ate, brought their grievances to King Aerys, expanded the grazing lands around their villages, even won them the right to fell a certain number of trees each year and take a few of the king‚Äôs deer during the autumn. The forest folk had looked to Toyne to defend them, but Ser Arthur did more for them than the Brotherhood could ever hope to do, and won them to our side. After that, the rest was easy.‚Äù

{:#Oberyn-Martell}
**Oberyn "The Red Viper" Martell**

{% include yt.html width='75%' embed='
<iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/dJbmGTbPbSk" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen></iframe>
' %}

> This waiting was intolerable. ‚ÄúBanners forward,‚Äù he snapped. ‚ÄúWe‚Äôll meet them.‚Äù He kicked his horse. Bronn and Pod followed, one to either side. When the Dornishmen saw them coming, they spurred their own mounts, banners rippling as they rode. From their ornate saddles were slung the round metal shields they favored, and many carried bundles of short throwing spears, or the double-curved Dornish bows they used so well from horseback. There were three sorts of Dornishmen, the first King Daeron had observed. There were the salty Dornishmen who lived along the coasts, the sandy Dornishmen of the deserts and long river valleys, and the stony Dornishmen who made their fastnesses in the passes and heights of the Red Mountains. The salty Dornishmen had the most Rhoynish blood, the stony Dornishmen the least. All three sorts seemed well represented in Doran‚Äôs retinue. The salty Dornishmen were lithe and dark, with smooth olive skin and long black hair streaming in the wind. The sandy Dornishmen were even darker, their faces burned brown by the hot Dornish sun. They wound long bright scarfs around their helms to ward off sunstroke. The stony Dornishmen were biggest and fairest, sons of the Andals and the First Men, brown-haired or blond, with faces that freckled or burned in the sun instead of browning. The lords wore silk and satin robes with jeweled belts and flowing sleeves. Their armor was heavily enameled and inlaid with burnished copper, shining silver, and soft red gold. They came astride red horses and golden ones and a few as pale as snow, all slim and swift, with long necks and narrow beautiful heads. The fabled sand steeds of Dorne were smaller than proper warhorses and could not bear such weight of armor, but it was said that they could run for a day and night and another day, and never tire. The Dornish leader forked a stallion black as sin with a mane and tail the color of fire. He sat his saddle as if he‚Äôd been born there, tall, slim, graceful. A cloak of pale red silk fluttered from his shoulders, and his shirt was armored with overlapping rows of copper disks that glittered like a thousand bright new pennies as he rode. His high gilded helm displayed a copper sun on its brow, and the round shield slung behind him bore the sun-and-spear of House Martell on its polished metal surface. A Martell sun, but ten years too young, Tyrion thought as he reined up, too fit as well, and far too fierce. He knew what he must deal with by then. How many Dornishmen does it take to start a war? he asked himself. Only one. Yet he had no choice but to smile.

> Oberyn abruptly changed the subject. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs said there are to be seventy-seven dishes served at the king‚Äôs wedding feast.‚Äù ‚ÄúAre you hungry, my prince?‚Äù ‚ÄúI have hungered for a long time. Though not for food. Pray tell me, when will the justice be served?‚Äù ‚ÄúJustice.‚Äù Yes, that is why he‚Äôs here, I should have seen that at once. ‚ÄúYou were close to your sister?‚Äù ‚ÄúAs children Elia and I were inseparable, much like your own brother and sister.‚Äù Gods, I hope not. ‚ÄúWars and weddings have kept us well occupied, Prince Oberyn. I fear no one has yet had the time to look into murders sixteen years stale, dreadful as they were. We shall, of course, just as soon as we may. Any help that Dorne might be able to provide to restore the king‚Äôs peace would only hasten the beginning of my lord father‚Äôs inquiry‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúDwarf,‚Äù said the Red Viper, in a tone grown markedly less cordial, ‚Äúspare me your Lannister lies. Is it sheep you take us for, or fools? My brother is not a bloodthirsty man, but neither has he been asleep for sixteen years. Jon Arryn came to Sunspear the year after Robert took the throne, and you can be sure that he was questioned closely. Him, and a hundred more. I did not come for some mummer‚Äôs show of an inquiry. I came for justice for Elia and her children, and I will have it. Starting with this lummox Gregor Clegane¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut not, I think, ending there. Before he dies, the Enormity That Rides will tell me whence came his orders, please assure your lord father of that.‚Äù He smiled. ‚ÄúAn old septon once claimed I was living proof of the goodness of the gods. Do you know why that is, Imp?‚Äù ‚ÄúNo,‚Äù Tyrion admitted warily. ‚ÄúWhy, if the gods were cruel, they would have made me my mother‚Äôs firstborn, and Doran her third. I am a bloodthirsty man, you see. And it is me you must contend with now, not my patient, prudent, and gouty brother.‚Äù

> Tyrion stared up at his father‚Äôs hard green eyes with their flecks of cold bright gold. ‚ÄúGuilty,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúso guilty. Is that what you wanted to hear?‚Äù Lord Tywin said nothing. Mace Tyrell nodded. Prince Oberyn looked mildly disappointed. ‚ÄúYou admit you poisoned the king?‚Äù ‚ÄúNothing of the sort,‚Äù said Tyrion. ‚ÄúOf Joffrey‚Äôs death I am innocent. I am guilty of a more monstrous crime.‚Äù He took a step toward his father. ‚ÄúI was born. I lived. I am guilty of being a dwarf, I confess it. And no matter how many times my good father forgave me, I have persisted in my infamy.‚Äù ‚ÄúThis is folly, Tyrion,‚Äù declared Lord Tywin. ‚ÄúSpeak to the matter at hand. You are not on trial for being a dwarf.‚Äù ‚ÄúThat is where you err, my lord. I have been on trial for being a dwarf my entire life.‚Äù ‚ÄúHave you nothing to say in your defense?‚Äù ‚ÄúNothing but this: I did not do it. Yet now I wish I had.‚Äù He turned to face the hall, that sea of pale faces. ‚ÄúI wish I had enough poison for you all. You make me sorry that I am not the monster you would have me be, yet there it is. I am innocent, but I will get no justice here. You leave me no choice but to appeal to the gods. I demand trial by battle.‚Äù ‚ÄúHave you taken leave of your wits?‚Äù his father said. ‚ÄúNo, I‚Äôve found them. I demand trial by battle!‚Äù His sweet sister could not have been more pleased. ‚ÄúHe has that right, my lords,‚Äù she reminded the judges. ‚ÄúLet the gods judge. Ser Gregor Clegane will stand for Joffrey. He returned to the city the night before last, to put his sword at my service.‚Äù Lord Tywin‚Äôs face was so dark that for half a heartbeat Tyrion wondered if he‚Äôd drunk some poisoned wine as well. He slammed his fist down on the table, too angry to speak. It was Mace Tyrell who turned to Tyrion and asked the question. ‚ÄúDo you have a champion to defend your innocence?‚Äù ‚ÄúHe does, my lord.‚Äù Prince Oberyn of Dorne rose to his feet. ‚ÄúThe dwarf has quite convinced me.‚Äù The uproar was deafening.

> No matter what happened, Tyrion had the satisfaction of knowing that he‚Äôd kicked Lord Tywin‚Äôs plans to splinters. If Prince Oberyn won, it would further inflame Highgarden against the Dornish; Mace Tyrell would see the man who crippled his son helping the dwarf who almost poisoned his daughter to escape his rightful punishment. And if the Mountain triumphed, Doran Martell might well demand to know why his brother had been served with death instead of the justice Tyrion had promised him. Dorne might crown Myrcella after all.

> That night, surprisingly, Tyrion Lannister slept long and deep. He rose at first light, well rested and with a hearty appetite, and broke his fast on fried bread, blood sausage, applecakes, and a double helping of eggs cooked with onions and fiery Dornish peppers. Then he begged leave of his guards to attend his champion. Ser Addam gave his consent. Tyrion found Prince Oberyn drinking a cup of red wine as he donned his armor. He was attended by four of his younger Dornish lordlings. ‚ÄúGood morrow to you, my lord,‚Äù the prince said. ‚ÄúWill you take a cup of wine?‚Äù ‚ÄúShould you be drinking before battle?‚Äù ‚ÄúI always drink before battle.‚Äù ‚ÄúThat could get you killed. Worse, it could get me killed.‚Äù Prince Oberyn laughed. ‚ÄúThe gods defend the innocent. You are innocent, I trust?‚Äù ‚ÄúOnly of killing Joffrey,‚Äù Tyrion admitted. ‚ÄúI do hope you know what you are about to face. Gregor Clegane is‚Äî‚Äù ‚Äú‚Äîlarge? So I have heard.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe is almost eight feet tall and must weigh thirty stone, all of it muscle. He fights with a two-handed greatsword, but needs only one hand to wield it. He has been known to cut men in half with a single blow. His armor is so heavy that no lesser man could bear the weight, let alone move in it.‚Äù Prince Oberyn was unimpressed. ‚ÄúI have killed large men before. The trick is to get them off their feet. Once they go down, they‚Äôre dead.‚Äù The Dornishman sounded so blithely confident that Tyrion felt almost reassured, until he turned and said, ‚ÄúDaemon, my spear!‚Äù Ser Daemon tossed it to him, and the Red Viper snatched it from the air. ‚ÄúYou mean to face the Mountain with a spear?‚Äù That made Tyrion uneasy all over again. In battle, ranks of massed spears made for a formidable front, but single combat against a skilled swordsman was a very different matter. ‚ÄúWe are fond of spears in Dorne. Besides, it is the only way to counter his reach. Have a look, Lord Imp, but see you do not touch.‚Äù The spear was turned ash eight feet long, the shaft smooth, thick, and heavy. The last two feet of that was steel: a slender leaf-shaped spearhead narrowing to a wicked spike. The edges looked sharp enough to shave with. When Oberyn spun the haft between the palms of his hand, they glistened black. Oil? Or poison? Tyrion decided that he would sooner not know. ‚ÄúI hope you are good with that,‚Äù he said doubtfully. ‚ÄúYou will have no cause for complaint. Though Ser Gregor may. However thick his plate, there will be gaps at the joints. Inside the elbow and knee, beneath the arms¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝI will find a place to tickle him, I promise you.‚Äù He set the spear aside. ‚ÄúIt is said that a Lannister always pays his debts. Perhaps you will return to Sunspear with me when the day‚Äôs bloodletting is done. My brother Doran would be most pleased to meet the rightful heir to Casterly Rock¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýespecially if he brought his lovely wife, the Lady of Winterfell.‚Äù Does the snake think I have Sansa squirreled away somewhere, like a nut I‚Äôm hoarding for winter? If so, Tyrion was not about to disabuse him. ‚ÄúA trip to Dorne might be very pleasant, now that I reflect on it.‚Äù ‚ÄúPlan on a lengthy visit.‚Äù Prince Oberyn sipped his wine. ‚ÄúYou and Doran have many matters of mutual interest to discuss. Music, trade, history, wine, the dwarf‚Äôs penny¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthe laws of inheritance and succession. No doubt an uncle‚Äôs counsel would be of benefit to Queen Myrcella in the trying times ahead.‚Äù If Varys had his little birds listening, Oberyn was giving them a ripe earful. ‚ÄúI believe I will have that cup of wine,‚Äù said Tyrion.

> ‚ÄúDo you recall the tale I told you of our first meeting, Imp?‚Äù Prince Oberyn asked, as the Bastard of Godsgrace knelt before him to fasten his greaves. ‚ÄúIt was not for your tail alone that my sister and I came to Casterly Rock. We were on a quest of sorts. A quest that took us to Starfall, the Arbor, Oldtown, the Shield Islands, Crakehall, and finally Casterly Rock¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut our true destination was marriage. Doran was betrothed to Lady Mellario of Norvos, so he had been left behind as castellan of Sunspear. My sister and I were yet unpromised. ‚ÄúElia found it all exciting. She was of that age, and her delicate health had never permitted her much travel. I preferred to amuse myself by mocking my sister‚Äôs suitors. There was Little Lord Lazyeye, Squire Squishlips, one I named the Whale That Walks, that sort of thing. The only one who was even halfway presentable was young Baelor Hightower. A pretty lad, and my sister was half in love with him until he had the misfortune to fart once in our presence. I promptly named him Baelor Breakwind, and after that Elia couldn‚Äôt look at him without laughing. I was a monstrous young fellow, someone should have sliced out my vile tongue.‚Äù Yes, Tyrion agreed silently. Baelor Hightower was no longer young, but he remained Lord Leyton‚Äôs heir; wealthy, handsome, and a knight of splendid repute. Baelor Brightsmile, they called him now. Had Elia wed him in place of Rhaegar Targaryen, she might be in Oldtown with her children growing tall around her. He wondered how many lives had been snuffed out by that fart.
