His legs had turned to water. ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt understand.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd never will,‚Äù a voice said sadly. The cobblestones rushed up to kiss him. Pate tried to cry for help, but his voice was failing too. His last thought was of Rosey.

{:#Balon-Greyjoy}
**Balon Greyjoy**

> Beside the embers of their campfire, she saw Tom, Lem, and Greenbeard talking to a tiny little woman, a foot shorter than Arya and older than Old Nan, all stooped and wrinkled and leaning on a gnarled black cane. Her white hair was so long it came almost to the ground. When the wind gusted it blew about her head in a fine cloud. Her flesh was whiter, the color of milk, and it seemed to Arya that her eyes were red, though it was hard to tell from the bushes. ‚ÄúThe old gods stir and will not let me sleep,‚Äù she heard the woman say. ‚ÄúI dreamt I saw a shadow with a burning heart butchering a golden stag, aye. I dreamt of a man without a face, waiting on a bridge that swayed and swung. On his shoulder perched a drowned crow with sea-weed hanging from his wings. I dreamt of a roaring river and a woman that was a fish. Dead she drifted, with red tears on her cheeks, but when her eyes did open, oh, I woke from terror. All this I dreamt, and more. Do you have gifts for me, to pay me for my dreams?‚Äù

> ‚ÄúTell me the manner of my brother‚Äôs death.‚Äù ‚ÄúHis Grace was crossing a bridge at Pyke when he fell and was dashed upon the rocks below.‚Äù The Greyjoy stronghold stood upon a broken headland, its keeps and towers built atop massive stone stacks that thrust up from the sea. Bridges knotted Pyke together; arched bridges of carved stone and swaying spans of hempen rope and wooden planks. ‚ÄúWas the storm raging when he fell?‚Äù Aeron demanded of them. ‚ÄúAye,‚Äù the youth said, ‚Äúit was.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe Storm God cast him down,‚Äù the priest announced. For a thousand thousand years sea and sky had been at war. From the sea had come the ironborn, and the fish that sustained them even in the depths of winter, but storms brought only woe and grief. ‚ÄúMy brother Balon made us great again, which earned the Storm God‚Äôs wrath. He feasts now in the Drowned God‚Äôs watery halls, with mermaids to attend his every want. It shall be for us who remain behind in this dry and dismal vale to finish his great work.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúEuron Crow‚Äôs Eye sits the Seastone Chair.‚Äù The Damphair turned. The hall had suddenly grown colder. The Crow‚Äôs Eye is half a world away. Balon sent him off two years ago, and swore that it would be his life if he returned. ‚ÄúTell me,‚Äù he said hoarsely. ‚ÄúHe sailed into Lordsport the day after the king‚Äôs death, and claimed the castle and the crown as Balon‚Äôs eldest brother,‚Äù said Gorold Goodbrother. ‚ÄúNow he sends forth ravens, summoning the captains and the kings from every isle to Pyke, to bend their knees and do him homage as their king.‚Äù

> Nine sons had been born from the loins of Quellon Greyjoy, but only four had lived to manhood. That was the way of this cold world, where men fished the sea and dug in the ground and died, whilst women brought forth short-lived children from beds of blood and pain. Aeron had been the last and least of the four krakens, Balon the eldest and boldest, a fierce and fearless boy who lived only to restore the ironborn to their ancient glory. At ten he scaled the Flint Cliffs to the Blind Lord‚Äôs haunted tower. At thirteen he could run a longship‚Äôs oars and dance the finger dance as well as any man in the isles. At fifteen he had sailed with Dagmer Cleftjaw to the Stepstones and spent a summer reaving. He slew his first man there and took his first two salt wives. At seventeen Balon captained his own ship. He was all that an elder brother ought to be, though he had never shown Aeron aught but scorn. I was weak and full of sin, and scorn was more than I deserved. Better to be scorned by Balon the Brave than beloved of Euron Crow‚Äôs Eye. And if age and grief had turned Balon bitter with the years, they had also made him more determined than any man alive. He was born a lord‚Äôs son and died a king, murdered by a jealous god, Aeron thought, and now the storm is coming, a storm such as these isles have never known.

{:#Quenton-Greyjoy-Donel-Greyjoy-and-Harlon-Greyjoy}
**Quenton Greyjoy, Donel Greyjoy, and Harlon Greyjoy**

> Nine sons had been born from the loins of Quellon Greyjoy, the Lord of the Iron Islands. Harlon, Quenton, and Donel had been born of Lord Quellon‚Äôs first wife, a woman of the Stonetrees. Balon, Euron, Victarion, Urrigon, and Aeron were the sons of his second, a Sunderly of Saltcliffe. For a third wife Quellon took a girl from the green lands, who gave him a sickly idiot boy named Robin, the brother best forgotten. The priest had no memory of Quenton or Donel, who had died as infants. Harlon he recalled but dimly, sitting grey-faced and still in a windowless tower room and speaking in whispers that grew fainter every day as the greyscale turned his tongue and lips to stone. One day we shall feast on fish together in the Drowned God‚Äôs watery halls, the four of us and Urri too.

{:#Urrigon-Greyjoy-and-Quellon-Greyjoy}
**Urrigon Greyjoy and Quellon Greyjoy**

> A flying axe took off half of Urri‚Äôs hand when he was ten-and-four, playing at the finger dance whilst his father and his elder brothers were away at war. Lord Quellon‚Äôs third wife had been a Piper of Pinkmaiden Castle, a girl with big soft breasts and brown doe‚Äôs eyes. Instead of healing Urri‚Äôs hand the Old Way, with fire and seawater, she gave him to her green land maester, who swore that he could sew back the missing fingers. He did that, and later he used potions and poltices and herbs, but the hand mortified and Urri took a fever. By the time the maester sawed his arm off, it was too late. Lord Quellon never returned from his last voyage; the Drowned God in his goodness granted him a death at sea. It was Lord Balon who came back, with his brothers Euron and Victarion. When Balon heard what had befallen Urri, he removed three of the maester‚Äôs fingers with a cook‚Äôs cleaver and sent his father‚Äôs Piper wife to sew them back on. Poltices and potions worked as well for the maester as they had for Urrigon. He died raving, and Lord Quellon‚Äôs third wife followed soon thereafter, as the midwife drew a stillborn daughter from her womb. Aeron had been glad. It had been his axe that sheared off Urri‚Äôs hand, whilst they danced the finger dance together, as friends and brothers will.

{:#Herbert-Bolling}
**Herbert Bolling**

> ‚ÄúMy steed served me well enough on the Blackwater,‚Äù Ser Creighton insisted. ‚ÄúWhy, I did great carnage there and won a dozen ransoms. Was m‚Äôlady familiar with Ser Herbert Bolling? You shall never meet him now. I slew him where he stood. When swords clash, you shall ne‚Äôer find Ser Creighton Longbough to the rear.‚Äù

{:#Gregor-The-Mountain-Clegane}
**Gregor "The Mountain" Clegane**

> A few of the horses still shied away from the dark splotch on the hard-packed ground where the earth had drunk the life‚Äôs blood of the stableboy Gregor Clegane had killed so clumsily. The sight of it made Jaime angry all over again. He had told his Kingsguard to keep the crowd out of the way, but that oaf Ser Boros had let himself be distracted by the duel. The fool boy himself shared some of the blame, to be sure; the dead Dornishman as well. And Clegane most of all. The blow that took the boy‚Äôs arm off had been mischance, but that second cut¬Ý‚Ä¶ Well, Gregor is paying for it now. Grand Maester Pycelle was tending to the man‚Äôs wounds, but the howls heard ringing from the maester‚Äôs chambers suggested that the healing was not going as well as it might. ‚ÄúThe flesh mortifies and the wounds ooze pus,‚Äù Pycelle told the council. ‚ÄúEven maggots will not touch such foulness. His convulsions are so violent that I have had to gag him to prevent him from biting off his tongue. I have cut away as much tissue as I dare, and treated the rot with boiling wine and bread mold, to no avail. The veins in his arm are turning black. When I leeched him, all the leeches died. My lords, I must know what malignant substance Prince Oberyn used on his spear. Let us detain these other Dornishmen until they are more forthcoming.‚Äù Lord Tywin had refused him. ‚ÄúThere will be trouble enough with Sunspear over Prince Oberyn‚Äôs death. I do not mean to make matters worse by holding his companions captive.‚Äù ‚ÄúThen I fear Ser Gregor may die.‚Äù ‚ÄúUndoubtedly. I swore as much in the letter I sent to Prince Doran with his brother‚Äôs body. But it must be seen to be the sword of the King‚Äôs Justice that slays him, not a poisoned spear. Heal him.‚Äù Grand Maester Pycelle blinked in dismay. ‚ÄúMy lord‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúHeal him,‚Äù Lord Tywin said again, vexed. ‚ÄúYou are aware that Lord Varys has sent fishermen into the waters around Dragonstone. They report that only a token force remains to defend the island. The Lyseni are gone from the bay, and the great part of Lord Stannis‚Äôs strength with them.‚Äù ‚ÄúWell and good,‚Äù announced Pycelle. ‚ÄúLet Stannis rot in Lys, I say. We are well rid of the man and his ambitions.‚Äù ‚ÄúDid you turn into an utter fool when Tyrion shaved your beard? This is Stannis Baratheon. The man will fight to the bitter end and then some. If he is gone, it can only mean he intends to resume the war. Most likely he will land at Storm‚Äôs End and try and rouse the storm lords. If so, he‚Äôs finished. But a bolder man might roll the dice for Dorne. If he should win Sunspear to his cause, he might prolong this war for years. So we will not offend the Martells any further, for any reason. The Dornishmen are free to go, and you will heal Ser Gregor.‚Äù And so the Mountain screamed, day and night. Lord Tywin Lannister could cow even the Stranger, it would seem.

> ‚ÄúHe is dying of the venom, but slowly, and in exquisite agony. My efforts to ease his pain have proved as fruitless as Pycelle‚Äôs. Ser Gregor is overly accustomed to the poppy, I fear. His squire tells me that he is plagued by blinding headaches and oft quaffs the milk of the poppy as lesser men quaff ale. Be that as it may, his veins have turned black from head to heel, his water is clouded with pus, and the venom has eaten a hole in his side as large as my fist. It is a wonder that the man is still alive, if truth be told.‚Äù ‚ÄúHis size,‚Äù the queen suggested, frowning. ‚ÄúGregor is a very large man. Also a very stupid one. Too stupid to know when he should die, it seems.‚Äù She held out her cup, and Senelle filled it once again. ‚ÄúHis screaming frightens Tommen. It has even been known to wake me of a night. I would say it is past time we summoned Ilyn Payne.‚Äù ‚ÄúYour Grace,‚Äù said Qyburn, ‚Äúmayhaps I might move Ser Gregor to the dungeons? His screams will not disturb you there, and I will be able to tend to him more freely.‚Äù ‚ÄúTend to him?‚Äù She laughed. ‚ÄúLet Ser Ilyn tend to him.‚Äù ‚ÄúIf that is Your Grace‚Äôs wish,‚Äù Qyburn said, ‚Äúbut this poison¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýit would be useful to know more about it, would it not? Send a knight to slay a knight and an archer to kill an archer, the smallfolk often say. To combat the black arts¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù He did not finish the thought, but only smiled at her. He is not Pycelle, that much is plain. The queen weighed him, wondering. ‚ÄúWhy did the Citadel take your chain?‚Äù ‚ÄúThe archmaesters are all craven at heart. The grey sheep, Marwyn calls them. I was as skilled a healer as Ebrose, but aspired to surpass him. For hundreds of years the men of the Citadel have opened the bodies of the dead, to study the nature of life. I wished to understand the nature of death, so I opened the bodies of the living. For that crime the grey sheep shamed me and forced me into exile¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut I understand the nature of life and death better than any man in Oldtown.‚Äù ‚ÄúDo you?‚Äù That intrigued her. ‚ÄúVery well. The Mountain is yours. Do what you will with him, but confine your studies to the black cells. When he dies, bring me his head. My father promised it to Dorne. Prince Doran would no doubt prefer to kill Gregor himself, but we all must suffer disappointments in this life.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúI had another sort of champion in mind. What he lacks in gallantry he will give you tenfold in devotion. He will protect your son, kill your enemies, and keep your secrets, and no living man will be able to withstand him.‚Äù ‚ÄúSo you say. Words are wind. When the hour is ripe, you may produce this paragon of yours and we will see if he is all that you have promised.‚Äù ‚ÄúThey will sing of him, I swear it.‚Äù

> He allowed himself a brief glance at the chest. The skull rested on a bed of black felt, grinning. All skulls grinned, but this one seemed happier than most. And bigger. The captain of guards had never seen a larger skull. Its brow shelf was thick and heavy, its jaw massive. The bone shone in the candlelight, white as Ser Balon‚Äôs cloak. ‚ÄúPlace it on the pedestal,‚Äù the prince commanded. He had tears glistening in his eyes. The pedestal was a column of black marble three feet taller than Maester Caleotte. The fat little maester hopped up on his toes but still could not quite reach. Areo Hotah was about to go and help him, but Obara Sand moved first. Even without her whip and shield, she had an angry mannish look to her. In place of a gown, she wore men‚Äôs breeches and a calf-length linen tunic, cinched at the waist with a belt of copper suns. Her brown hair was tied back in a knot. Snatching the skull from the maester‚Äôs soft pink hands, she placed it up atop the marble column. ‚ÄúThe Mountain rides no more,‚Äù the prince said, gravely. ‚ÄúWas his dying long and hard, Ser Balon?‚Äù asked Tyene Sand, in the tone a maiden might use to ask if her gown was pretty. ‚ÄúHe screamed for days, my lady,‚Äù the white knight replied, though it was plain that it pleased him little to say so. ‚ÄúWe could hear him all over the Red Keep.‚Äù ‚ÄúDoes that trouble you, ser?‚Äù asked the Lady Nym. She wore a gown of yellow silk so sheer and fine that the candles shone right through it to reveal the spun gold and jewels beneath. So immodest was her garb that the white knight seemed uncomfortable looking at her, but Hotah approved. Nymeria was least dangerous when nearly naked. Elsewise she was sure to have a dozen blades concealed about her person. ‚ÄúSer Gregor was a bloody brute, all men agree. If ever a man deserved to suffer, it was him.‚Äù ‚ÄúThat is as it may be, my lady,‚Äù said Balon Swann, ‚Äúbut Ser Gregor was a knight, and a knight should die with sword in hand. Poison is a foul and filthy way to kill.‚Äù Lady Tyene smiled at that. Her gown was cream and green, with long lace sleeves, so modest and so innocent that any man who looked at her might think her the most chaste of maids. Areo Hotah knew better. Her soft, pale hands were as deadly as Obara‚Äôs callused ones, if not more so. He watched her carefully, alert to every little flutter of her fingers. Prince Doran frowned. ‚ÄúThat is so, Ser Balon, but the Lady Nym is right. If ever a man deserved to die screaming, it was Gregor Clegane. He butchered my good sister, smashed her babe‚Äôs head against a wall. I only pray that now he is burning in some hell, and that Elia and her children are at peace. This is the justice that Dorne has hungered for. I am glad that I lived long enough to taste it. At long last the Lannisters have proved the truth of their boast and paid this old blood debt.‚Äù

> The feast continued late into the night, presided over by the grinning skull on its pillar of black marble. Seven courses were served, in honor of the seven gods and the seven brothers of the Kingsguard. The soup was made with eggs and lemons, the long green peppers stuffed with cheese and onions. There were lamprey pies, capons glazed with honey, a whiskerfish from the bottom of the Greenblood that was so big it took four serving men to carry it to table. After that came a savory snake stew, chunks of seven different sorts of snake slow-simmered with dragon peppers and blood oranges and a dash of venom to give it a good bite. The stew was fiery hot, Hotah knew, though he tasted none of it. Sherbet followed, to cool the tongue. For the sweet, each guest was served a skull of spun sugar. When the crust was broken, they found sweet custard inside and bits of plum and cherry.

> Obara Sand plucked the skull from him and held it at arm‚Äôs length. ‚ÄúWhat did the Mountain look like? How do we know that this is him? They could have dipped the head in tar. Why strip it to the bone?‚Äù ‚ÄúTar would have ruined the box,‚Äù suggested Lady Nym, as Maester Caleotte scurried off. ‚ÄúNo one saw the Mountain die, and no one saw his head removed. That troubles me, I confess, but what could the bitch queen hope to accomplish by deceiving us? If Gregor Clegane is alive, soon or late the truth will out. The man was eight feet tall, there is not another like him in all of Westeros. If any such appears again, Cersei Lannister will be exposed as a liar before all the Seven Kingdoms. She would be an utter fool to risk that. What could she hope to gain?‚Äù ‚ÄúThe skull is large enough, no doubt,‚Äù said the prince. ‚ÄúAnd we know that Oberyn wounded Gregor grievously. Every report we have had since claims that Clegane died slowly, in great pain.‚Äù ‚ÄúJust as Father intended,‚Äù said Tyene. ‚ÄúSisters, truly, I know the poison Father used. If his spear so much as broke the Mountain‚Äôs skin, Clegane is dead, I do not care how big he was. Doubt your little sister if you like, but never doubt our sire.‚Äù Obara bristled. ‚ÄúI never did and never shall.‚Äù She gave the skull a mocking kiss. ‚ÄúThis is a start, I‚Äôll grant.‚Äù ‚ÄúA start?‚Äù said Ellaria Sand, incredulous. ‚ÄúGods forbid. I would it were a finish. Tywin Lannister is dead. So are Robert Baratheon, Amory Lorch, and now Gregor Clegane, all those who had a hand in murdering Elia and her children. Even Joffrey, who was not yet born when Elia died. I saw the boy perish with mine own eyes, clawing at his throat as he tried to draw a breath. Who else is there to kill? Do Myrcella and Tommen need to die so the shades of Rhaenys and Aegon can be at rest? Where does it end?‚Äù ‚ÄúIt ends in blood, as it began,‚Äù said Lady Nym. ‚ÄúIt ends when Casterly Rock is cracked open, so the sun can shine on the maggots and the worms within. It ends with the utter ruin of Tywin Lannister and all his works.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe man died at the hand of his own son,‚Äù Ellaria snapped back. ‚ÄúWhat more could you wish?‚Äù ‚ÄúI could wish that he died at my hand.‚Äù Lady Nym settled in a chair, her long black braid falling across one shoulder to her lap. She had her father‚Äôs widow‚Äôs peak. Beneath it her eyes were large and lustrous. Her wine-red lips curled in a silken smile. ‚ÄúIf he had, his dying would not have been so easy.‚Äù ‚ÄúSer Gregor does look lonely,‚Äù said Tyene, in her sweet septa‚Äôs voice. ‚ÄúHe would like some company, I‚Äôm certain.‚Äù Ellaria‚Äôs cheeks were wet with tears, her dark eyes shining. Even weeping, she has a strength in her, the captain thought. ‚ÄúOberyn wanted vengeance for Elia. Now the three of you want vengeance for him. I have four daughters, I remind you. Your sisters. My Elia is fourteen, almost a woman. Obella is twelve, on the brink of maidenhood. They worship you, as Dorea and Loreza worship them. If you should die, must El and Obella seek vengeance for you, then Dorea and Loree for them? Is that how it goes, round and round forever? I ask again, where does it end?‚Äù Ellaria Sand laid her hand on the Mountain‚Äôs head. ‚ÄúI saw your father die. Here is his killer. Can I take a skull to bed with me, to give me comfort in the night? Will it make me laugh, write me songs, care for me when I am old and sick?‚Äù ‚ÄúWhat would you have us do, my lady?‚Äù asked the Lady Nym. ‚ÄúShall we lay down our spears and smile, and forget all the wrongs that have been done to us?‚Äù ‚ÄúWar will come, whether we wish it or not,‚Äù said Obara. ‚ÄúA boy king sits the Iron Throne. Lord Stannis holds the Wall and is gathering northmen to his cause. The two queens are squabbling over Tommen like bitches with a juicy bone. The ironmen have taken the Shields and are raiding up the Mander, deep into the heart of the Reach, which means Highgarden will be preoccupied as well. Our enemies are in disarray. The time is ripe.‚Äù ‚ÄúRipe for what? To make more skulls?‚Äù Ellaria Sand turned to the prince. ‚ÄúThey will not see. I can hear no more of this.‚Äù ‚ÄúGo back to your girls, Ellaria,‚Äù the prince told her. ‚ÄúI swear to you, no harm will come to them.‚Äù ‚ÄúMy prince.‚Äù Ellaria kissed him on the brow and took her leave. Areo Hotah was sad to see her go. She is a good woman.

> ‚ÄúTell me. What is it?‚Äù ‚ÄúMyrcella. We have had grave news from Dorne.‚Äù ‚ÄúTyrion,‚Äù she said at once. Tyrion had sent her little girl to Dorne, and Cersei had dispatched Ser Balon Swann to bring her home. All Dornishmen were snakes, and the Martells were the worst of them. The Red Viper had even tried to defend the Imp, had come within a hairbreadth of a victory that would have allowed the dwarf to escape the blame for Joffrey‚Äôs murder. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs him, he‚Äôs been in Dorne all this time, and now he‚Äôs seized my daughter.‚Äù Ser Kevan gave her another scowl. ‚ÄúMyrcella was attacked by a Dornish knight named Gerold Dayne. She‚Äôs alive, but hurt. He slashed her face open, she ‚Ä¶ I‚Äôm sorry ‚Ä¶ she lost an ear.‚Äù ‚ÄúAn ear.‚Äù Cersei stared at him, aghast. She was just a child, my precious princess. She was so pretty, too. ‚ÄúHe cut off her ear. And Prince Doran and his Dornish knights, where were they? They could not defend one little girl? Where was Arys Oakheart?‚Äù ‚ÄúSlain, defending her. Dayne cut him down, it‚Äôs said.‚Äù The Sword of the Morning had been a Dayne, the queen recalled, but he was long dead. Who was this Ser Gerold and why would he wish to harm her daughter? She could not make any sense of this, unless ‚Ä¶ ‚ÄúTyrion lost half his nose in the Battle of the Blackwater. Slashing her face, cutting off an ear ‚Ä¶ the Imp‚Äôs grubby little fingers are all over this.‚Äù ‚ÄúPrince Doran says nothing of your brother. And Balon Swann writes that Myrcella puts it all on this Gerold Dayne. Darkstar, they call him.‚Äù She gave a bitter laugh. ‚ÄúWhatever they call him, he is my brother‚Äôs catspaw. Tyrion has friends amongst the Dornish. The Imp planned this all along. It was Tyrion who betrothed Myrcella to Prince Trystane. Now I see why.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou see Tyrion in every shadow.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe is a creature of the shadows. He killed Joffrey. He killed Father. Did you think he would stop there? I feared that the Imp was still in King‚Äôs Landing plotting harm to Tommen, but he must have gone to Dorne instead to kill Myrcella first.‚Äù Cersei paced the width of the cell. ‚ÄúI need to be with Tommen. These Kingsguard knights are as useless as nipples on a breastplate.‚Äù She rounded on her uncle. ‚ÄúSer Arys was killed, you said.‚Äù ‚ÄúAt the hands of this man Darkstar, yes.‚Äù ‚ÄúDead, he‚Äôs dead, you are certain of that?‚Äù ‚ÄúThat is what we have been told.‚Äù ‚ÄúThen there is an empty place amongst the Kingsguard. It must be filled at once. Tommen must be protected.‚Äù ‚ÄúLord Tarly is drawing up a list of worthy knights for your brother to consider, but until Jaime reappears ‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúThe king can give a man a white cloak. Tommen‚Äôs a good boy. Tell him who to name and he will name him.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd who would you have him name?‚Äù She did not have a ready answer. My champion will need a new name as well as a new face. ‚ÄúQyburn will know. Trust him in this. You and I have had our differences, Uncle, but for the blood we share and the love you bore my father, for Tommen‚Äôs sake and the sake of his poor maimed sister, do as I ask you. Go to Lord Qyburn on my behalf, bring him a white cloak, and tell him that the time has come.‚Äù

> Then Jocelyn was bending over her, wrapping her in a soft clean blanket of green wool to cover her nakedness. A shadow fell across them both, blotting out the sun. The queen felt cold steel slide beneath her, a pair of great armored arms lifting her off the ground, lifting her up into the air as easily as she had lifted Joffrey when he was still a babe. A giant, thought Cersei, dizzy, as he carried her with great strides toward the gatehouse. She had heard that giants could still be found in the godless wild beyond the Wall. That is just a tale. Am I dreaming? No. Her savior was real. Eight feet tall or maybe taller, with legs as thick around as trees, he had a chest worthy of a plow horse and shoulders that would not disgrace an ox. His armor was plate steel, enameled white and bright as a maiden‚Äôs hopes, and worn over gilded mail. A greathelm hid his face. From its crest streamed seven silken plumes in the rainbow colors of the Faith. A pair of golden seven-pointed stars clasped his billowing cloak at the shoulders. A white cloak. Ser Kevan had kept his part of the bargain. Tommen, her precious little boy, had named her champion to the Kingsguard. Cersei never saw where Qyburn came from, but suddenly he was there beside them, scrambling to keep up with her champion‚Äôs long strides. ‚ÄúYour Grace,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúit is so good to have you back. May I have the honor of presenting our newest member of the Kingsguard? This is Ser Robert Strong.‚Äù ‚ÄúSer Robert,‚Äù Cersei whispered, as they entered the gates. ‚ÄúIf it please Your Grace, Ser Robert has taken a holy vow of silence,‚Äù Qyburn said. ‚ÄúHe has sworn that he will not speak until all of His Grace‚Äôs enemies are dead and evil has been driven from the realm.‚Äù Yes, thought Cersei Lannister. Oh, yes.

> ‚ÄúTell me, ser, where did this man come from?‚Äù demanded Mace Tyrell. ‚ÄúWhy have we never heard his name before? He does not speak, he will not show his face, he is never seen without his armor. Do we know for a certainty that he is even a knight?‚Äù We do not even know if he‚Äôs alive. Meryn Trant claimed that Strong took neither food nor drink, and Boros Blount went so far as to say he had never seen the man use the privy. Why should he? Dead men do not shit. Kevan Lannister had a strong suspicion of just who this Ser Robert really was beneath that gleaming white armor. A suspicion that Mace Tyrell and Randyll Tarly no doubt shared.

{:#Quenton-Hightower}
**Quenton Hightower**

> ‚ÄúSer Jaime, I have seen terrible things in my time,‚Äù the old man said. ‚ÄúWars, battles, murders most foul¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝI was a boy in Oldtown when the grey plague took half the city and three-quarters of the Citadel. Lord Hightower burned every ship in port, closed the gates, and commanded his guards to slay all those who tried to flee, be they men, women, or babes in arms. They killed him when the plague had run its course. On the very day he reopened the port, they dragged him from his horse and slit his throat, and his young son‚Äôs as well. To this day the ignorant in Oldtown will spit at the sound of his name, but Quenton Hightower did what was needed. Your father was that sort of man as well. A man who did what was needed.‚Äù

{:#Gwayne-Gaunt-Denys-Darklyn-Serala-Darklyn-Jon-Hollard-Robin-Hollard-Symon-Hollard-and-Steffon-Hollard}
**Gwayne Gaunt, Denys Darklyn, Serala Darklyn, Jon Hollard, Robin Hollard, Symon Hollard, and Steffon Hollard**

> ‚ÄúIn Duskendale they love Lord Denys still, despite the woe he brought them. ‚ÄôTis Lady Serala that they blame, his Myrish wife. The Lace Serpent, she is called. If Lord Darklyn had only wed a Staunton or a Stokeworth ‚Ä¶ well, you know how smallfolk will go on. The Lace Serpent filled her husband‚Äôs ear with Myrish poison, they say, until Lord Denys rose against his king and took him captive. In the taking, his master-at-arms Ser Symon Hollard cut down Ser Gwayne Gaunt of the Kingsguard. For half a year Aerys was held within these very walls, whilst the King‚Äôs Hand sat outside Duskendale with a mighty host. Lord Tywin had sufficient strength to storm the town any time he wished, but Lord Denys sent word that at the first sign of assault he‚Äôd kill the king.‚Äù Brienne remembered what came next. ‚ÄúThe king was rescued,‚Äù she said. ‚ÄúBarristan the Bold brought him out.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe did,‚Äù the maester said. ‚ÄúOnce Lord Denys lost his hostage, he opened his gates and ended his defiance rather than let Lord Tywin take the town. He bent the knee and begged for mercy, but the king was not of a forgiving mind. Lord Denys lost his head, as did his brothers and his sister, uncles, cousins, all the lordly Darklyns. The Lace Serpent was burned alive, poor woman, though her tongue was torn out first, and her female parts, with which it was said that she had enslaved her lord. Half of Duskendale will still tell you that Aerys was too kind to her.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd the Hollards?‚Äù ‚ÄúAttainted and destroyed,‚Äù said the maester. ‚ÄúI was forging my chain at the Citadel when this happened, but I have read the accounts of their trials and punishments. Ser Jon Hollard the Steward was wed to Lord Denys‚Äôs sister and died with his wife, as did their young son, who was half-Darklyn. Robin Hollard was a squire, and when the king was seized he danced around him and pulled his beard. He died upon the rack. Ser Symon Hollard was slain by Ser Barristan during the king‚Äôs escape. The Hollard lands were taken, their castle torn down, their villages put to the torch. As with the Darklyns, House Hollard was extinguished.‚Äù ‚ÄúSave for Dontos.‚Äù ‚ÄúTrue enough. Young Dontos was the son of Ser Steffon Hollard, the twin brother of Ser Symon, who had died of a fever some years before and had no part in the Defiance. Aerys would have taken the boy‚Äôs head off nonetheless, but Ser Barristan asked that his life be spared. The king could not refuse the man who‚Äôd saved him, so Dontos was taken to King‚Äôs Landing as a squire. To my knowledge he never returned to Duskendale, and why should he? He held no lands here, had neither kin nor castle. If Dontos and this northern girl helped murder our sweet king, it seems to me that they would want to put as many leagues as they could betwixt themselves and justice. Look for them in Oldtown, if you must, or across the narrow sea. Look for them in Dorne, or on the Wall. Look elsewhere.‚Äù He rose. ‚ÄúI hear my ravens calling. You will forgive me if I bid you good morrow.‚Äù

{:#Marillion}
**Marillion**

> If the Eyrie had been made like other castles, only rats and gaolers would have heard the dead man singing. Dungeon walls were thick enough to swallow songs and screams alike. But the sky cells had a wall of empty air, so every chord the dead man played flew free to echo off the stony shoulders of the Giant‚Äôs Lance. And the songs he chose¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝHe sang of the Dance of the Dragons, of fair Jonquil and her fool, of Jenny of Oldstones and the Prince of Dragonflies. He sang of betrayals, and murders most foul, of hanged men and bloody vengeance. He sang of grief and sadness. No matter where she went in the castle, Sansa could not escape the music. It floated up the winding tower steps, found her naked in her bath, supped with her at dusk, and stole into her bedchamber even when she latched the shutters tight. It came in on the cold thin air, and like the air, it chilled her. Though it had not snowed upon the Eyrie since the day that Lady Lysa fell, the nights had all been bitter cold. The singer‚Äôs voice was strong and sweet. Sansa thought he sounded better than he ever had before, his voice richer somehow, full of pain and fear and longing. She did not understand why the gods would have given such a voice to such a wicked man. He would have taken me by force on the Fingers if Petyr had not set Ser Lothor to watch over me, she had to remind herself. And he played to drown out my cries when Aunt Lysa tried to kill me. That did not make the songs any easier to hear. ‚ÄúPlease,‚Äù she begged Lord Petyr, ‚Äúcan‚Äôt you make him stop?‚Äù ‚ÄúI gave the man my word, sweetling.‚Äù Petyr Baelish, Lord of Harrenhal, Lord Paramount of the Trident, and Lord Protector of the Eyrie and the Vale of Arryn, looked up from the letter he was writing. He had written a hundred letters since Lady Lysa‚Äôs fall. Sansa had seen the ravens coming and going from the rookery. ‚ÄúI‚Äôd sooner suffer his singing than listen to his sobbing.‚Äù It is better that he sings, yes, but¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ý‚ÄúMust he play all night, my lord? Lord Robert cannot sleep. He cries¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚Äú‚Ä¶¬Ýfor his mother. That cannot be helped, the wench is dead.‚Äù Petyr shrugged. ‚ÄúIt will not be much longer. Lord Nestor is making his ascent on the morrow.‚Äù Sansa had met Lord Nestor Royce once before, after Petyr‚Äôs wedding to her aunt. Royce was the Keeper of the Gates of the Moon, the great castle that stood at the base of the mountain and guarded the steps up to the Eyrie. The wedding party had guested with him overnight before beginning their ascent. Lord Nestor had scarce looked at her twice, but the prospect of him coming here terrified her. He was High Steward of the Vale as well, Jon Arryn‚Äôs trusted liege man, and Lady Lysa‚Äôs. ‚ÄúHe won‚Äôt¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýyou won‚Äôt let Lord Nestor see Marillion, will you?‚Äù Her horror must have shown on her face, since Petyr put down his quill. ‚ÄúOn the contrary. I shall insist on it.‚Äù He beckoned her to take the seat beside him. ‚ÄúWe have come to an agreement, Marillion and I. Mord can be most persuasive. And if our singer disappoints us and sings a song we do not care to hear, why, you and I need only say he lies. Whom do you imagine Lord Nestor will believe?‚Äù

> That night the dead man sang ‚ÄúThe Day They Hanged Black Robin,‚Äù ‚ÄúThe Mother‚Äôs Tears,‚Äù and ‚ÄúThe Rains of Castamere.‚Äù Then he stopped for a while, but just as Sansa began to drift off he started to play again. He sang ‚ÄúSix Sorrows,‚Äù ‚ÄúFallen Leaves,‚Äù and ‚ÄúAlysanne.‚Äù Such sad songs, she thought. When she closed her eyes she could see him in his sky cell, huddled in a corner away from the cold black sky, crouched beneath a fur with his woodharp cradled against his chest. I must not pity him, she told herself. He was vain and cruel, and soon he will be dead. She could not save him. And why should she want to? Marillion tried to rape her, and Petyr had saved her life not once but twice. Some lies you have to tell. Lies had been all that kept her alive in King‚Äôs Landing. If she had not lied to Joffrey, his Kingsguard would have beat her bloody. After ‚ÄúAlysanne‚Äù the singer stopped again, long enough for Sansa to snatch an hour‚Äôs rest. But as the first light of dawn was prying at her shutters, she heard the soft strains of ‚ÄúOn a Misty Morn‚Äù drifting up from below, and woke at once. That was more properly a woman‚Äôs song, a lament sung by a mother on the dawn after some terrible battle, as she searches amongst the dead for the body of her only son. The mother sings her grief for her dead son, Sansa thought, but Marillion grieves for his fingers, for his eyes. The words rose like arrows and pierced her in the darkness.

> Marillion was comely, there was no denying it; boyish and slender, with smooth skin, sandy hair, a charming smile. But he had made himself well hated in the Vale, by everyone but her aunt and little Lord Robert. To hear the servants talk, Sansa was not the first maid to suffer his advances, and the others had not had Lothor Brune to defend them. But Lady Lysa would hear no complaints against him. Since coming to the Eyrie, the singer had become her favorite. He sang Lord Robert to sleep every night, and tweaked the noses of Lady Lysa‚Äôs suitors with verses that made mock of their foibles. Her aunt had showered him with gold and gifts; costly clothes, a gold arm ring, a belt studded with moonstones, a fine horse. She had even given him her late husband‚Äôs favorite falcon. It all served to make Marillion unfailingly courteous in Lady Lysa‚Äôs presence, and unfailingly arrogant outside it.

> Petyr Baelish sighed. ‚ÄúIt was unseemly,‚Äù he agreed, ‚Äúand I put an end to it. Lysa agreed to send him away. That was why she met him here, that day. I should have been with her, but I never dreamt¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýif I had not insisted¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýit was I who killed her.‚Äù No, Sansa thought, you mustn‚Äôt say that, you mustn‚Äôt tell them, you mustn‚Äôt. But Albar Royce was shaking his head. ‚ÄúNo, my lord, you must not blame yourself,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúThis was the singer‚Äôs work,‚Äù his father agreed. ‚ÄúBring him up, Lord Petyr. Let us write an end to this sorry business.‚Äù Petyr Baelish composed himself, and said, ‚ÄúAs you wish, my lord.‚Äù He turned to his guardsmen and spoke a command, and the singer was fetched up from the dungeons. The gaoler Mord came with him, a monstrous man with small black eyes and a lopsided, scarred face. One ear and part of his cheek had been cleaved off in some battle, but twenty stone of pallid white flesh remained. His clothes fit poorly and had a rank, ripe smell. Marillion by contrast looked almost elegant. Someone had bathed him and dressed him in a pair of sky-blue breeches and a loose-fitting white tunic with puffed sleeves, belted with a silvery sash that had been a gift from Lady Lysa. White silk gloves covered his hands, while a white silk bandage spared the lords the sight of his eyes. Mord stood behind him with a lash. When the gaoler prodded him in the ribs, the singer went to one knee. ‚ÄúGood lords, I beg your forgiveness.‚Äù Lord Nestor scowled. ‚ÄúYou confess your crime?‚Äù ‚ÄúIf I had eyes I should weep.‚Äù The singer‚Äôs voice, so strong and sure by night, was cracked and whispery now. ‚ÄúI loved her so, I could not bear to see her in another‚Äôs arms, to know she shared his bed. I meant no harm to my sweet lady, I swear it. I barred the door so no one could disturb us whilst I declared my passion, but Lady Lysa was so cold¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýwhen she told that she was carrying Lord Petyr‚Äôs child, a¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýa madness seized me¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù Sansa stared at his hands while he spoke. Fat Maddy claimed that Mord had taken off three of his fingers, both pinkies and a ring finger. His little fingers did appear somewhat stiffer than the others, but with those gloves it was hard to be certain. It might have been no more than a story. How would Maddy know? ‚ÄúLord Petyr has been kind enough to let me keep my harp,‚Äù the blind singer said. ‚ÄúMy harp and¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýmy tongue¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýso I may sing my songs. Lady Lysa dearly loved my singing¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúTake this creature away, or I‚Äôm like to kill him myself,‚Äù Lord Nestor growled. ‚ÄúIt sickens me to look at him.‚Äù ‚ÄúMord, take him back to his sky cell,‚Äù said Petyr. ‚ÄúYes, m‚Äôlord.‚Äù Mord grabbed Marillion roughly by the collar. ‚ÄúNo more mouth.‚Äù When he spoke, Sansa saw to her astonishment that the gaoler‚Äôs teeth were made of gold. They watched as he half dragged half shoved the singer toward the doors. ‚ÄúThe man must die,‚Äù Ser Marywn Belmore declared when they were gone. ‚ÄúHe should have followed Lady Lysa out the Moon Door.‚Äù ‚ÄúWithout his tongue,‚Äù Ser Albar Royce added. ‚ÄúWithout that lying, mocking tongue.‚Äù ‚ÄúI have been too gentle with him, I know,‚Äù Petyr Baelish said in an apologetic tone. ‚ÄúIf truth be told, I pity him. He killed for love.‚Äù ‚ÄúFor love or hate,‚Äù said Belmore, ‚Äúhe must die.‚Äù ‚ÄúSoon enough,‚Äù Lord Nestor said gruffly. ‚ÄúNo man lingers long in the sky cells. The blue will call to him.‚Äù ‚ÄúIt may,‚Äù said Petyr Baelish, ‚Äúbut whether Marillion will answer, only he can say.‚Äù

> Robert pushed his spoon across the bowl and back, but never brought it to his lips. ‚ÄúI am not hungry,‚Äù he decided. ‚ÄúI want to go back to bed. I never slept last night. I heard singing. Maester Colemon gave me dreamwine but I could still hear it.‚Äù Alayne put down her spoon. ‚ÄúIf there had been singing, I should have heard it too. You had a bad dream, that‚Äôs all.‚Äù ‚ÄúNo, it wasn‚Äôt a dream.‚Äù Tears filled his eyes. ‚ÄúMarillion was singing again. Your father says he‚Äôs dead, but he isn‚Äôt.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe is.‚Äù It frightened her to hear him talk like this. Bad enough that he is small and sickly, what if he is mad as well? ‚ÄúSweetrobin, he is. Marillion loved your lady mother too much and could not live with what he‚Äôd done to her, so he walked into the sky.‚Äù Alayne had not seen the body, no more than Robert had, but she did not doubt the fact of the singer‚Äôs death. ‚ÄúHe‚Äôs gone, truly.‚Äù ‚ÄúBut I hear him every night. Even when I close the shutters and put a pillow on my head. Your father should have cut his tongue out. I told him to, but he wouldn‚Äôt.‚Äù He needed a tongue to confess.

{:#Harren-Botley-and-Sawane-Botley}
**Harren Botley and Sawane Botley**

> ‚ÄúI was sorry to hear about your father,‚Äù she told him. ‚ÄúI grieve for yours.‚Äù Why? Asha almost asked. It was Balon who‚Äôd sent the boy away from Pyke, to be a ward of Baelor Blacktyde‚Äôs. ‚ÄúIs it true you are Lord Botley now?‚Äù ‚ÄúIn name, at least. Harren died at Moat Cailin. One of the bog devils shot him with a poisoned arrow. But I am the lord of nothing. When my father denied his claim to the Seastone Chair, the Crow‚Äôs Eye drowned him and made my uncles swear him fealty. Even after that he gave half my father‚Äôs lands to Iron Holt. Lord Wynch was the first man to bend his knee and call him king.‚Äù

{:#The-Three-Leaves-Alester-and-Olyvar-the-Green-Oak}
**The Three Leaves, Alester, and Olyvar the Green Oak**

> The Dornish garb was comfortable, but his father would have been aghast had he lived to see his son so dressed. He was a man of the Reach, and the Dornish were his ancient foes, as the tapestries at Old Oak bore witness. Arys only had to close his eyes to see them still. Lord Edgerran the Open-Handed, seated in splendor with the heads of a hundred Dornishmen piled round his feet. The Three Leaves in the Prince‚Äôs Pass, pierced by Dornish spears, Alester sounding his warhorn with his last breath. Ser Olyvar the Green Oak all in white, dying at the side of the Young Dragon. Dorne is no fit place for any Oakheart.

{:#Terrence-Toyne-and-Aemon-the-Dragonknight}
**Terrence Toyne and Aemon the Dragonknight**

> ‚ÄúSer Terrence Toyne was found abed with his king‚Äôs mistress,‚Äù he said instead. ‚Äú‚ÄôTwas love, he swore, but it cost his life and hers, and brought about the downfall of his House and the death of the noblest knight who ever lived.‚Äù ‚ÄúYes, and what of Lucamore the Lusty, with his three wives and sixteen children? The song always makes me laugh.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe truth is not so funny. He was never called Lucamore the Lusty whilst he lived. His name was Ser Lucamore Strong, and his whole life was a lie. When his deceit was discovered, his own Sworn Brothers gelded him, and the Old King sent him to the Wall. Those sixteen children were left weeping. He was no true knight, no more than Terrence Toyne¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd the Dragonknight?‚Äù She flung the bedclothes aside and swung her legs to the floor. ‚ÄúThe noblest knight who ever lived, you said, and he took his queen to bed and got her with child.‚Äù ‚ÄúI will not believe that,‚Äù he said, offended. ‚ÄúThe tale of Prince Aemon‚Äôs treason with Queen Naerys was only that, a tale, a lie his brother told when he wished to set his trueborn son aside in favor of his bastard. Aegon was not called the Unworthy without cause.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt think it would be proper for me to slay mine own Sworn Brother. What I need to do is geld him and send him to the Wall. That‚Äôs what they did with Lucamore the Lusty. Ser Osmund may not take kindly to the gelding, to be sure. And there are his brothers to consider. Brothers can be dangerous. After Aegon the Unworthy put Ser Terrence Toyne to death for sleeping with his mistress, Toyne‚Äôs brothers did their best to kill him. Their best was not quite good enough, thanks to the Dragonknight, but it was not for want of trying. It‚Äôs written down in the White Book. All of it, save what to do with Cersei.‚Äù

{:#Viserys-Targaryen-the-First}
**Viserys Targaryen the First**

> ‚ÄúThe first Viserys intended his daughter Rhaenyra to follow him, do you deny it? But as the king lay dying the Lord Commander of his Kingsguard decided that it should be otherwise.‚Äù Ser Criston Cole. Criston the Kingmaker had set brother against sister and divided the Kingsguard against itself, bringing on the terrible war the singers named the Dance of the Dragons. Some claimed he acted from ambition, for Prince Aegon was more tractable than his willful older sister. Others allowed him nobler motives, and argued that he was defending ancient Andal custom. A few whispered that Ser Criston had been Princess Rhaenyra‚Äôs lover before he took the white and wanted vengeance on the woman who had spurned him.

{:#Lorimer-the-Belly-and-Cedric-Payne}
**Lorimer the Belly and Cedric Payne**

> She had gotten his story out of him in fits and starts on the road from Duskendale. His was a lesser branch of House Payne, an impoverished offshoot sprouted from the loins of a younger son. His father had spent his life squiring for richer cousins and had sired Podrick upon a chandler‚Äôs daughter he‚Äôd wed before going off to die in the Greyjoy Rebellion. His mother had abandoned him with one of those cousins when he was four, so she could run after a wandering singer who had put another baby in her belly. Podrick did not remember what she looked like. Ser Cedric Payne had been the nearest thing to a parent the boy had ever known, though from his stammered stories it seemed to Brienne that cousin Cedric had treated Podrick more like a servant than a son. When Casterly Rock called its banners, the knight had taken him along to tend his horse and clean his mail. Then Ser Cedric had been slain in the riverlands whilst fighting in Lord Tywin‚Äôs host. Far from home, alone, and penniless, the boy had attached himself to a fat hedge knight named Ser Lorimer the Belly, who was part of Lord Lefford‚Äôs contingent, charged with protecting the baggage train. ‚ÄúThe boys who guard the foodstuffs always eat the best,‚Äù Ser Lorimer liked to say, until he was discovered with a salted ham he‚Äôd stolen from Lord Tywin‚Äôs personal stores. Tywin Lannister chose to hang him as a lesson to other looters. Podrick had shared the ham and might have shared the rope as well, but his name had saved him. Ser Kevan Lannister took charge of him, and sometime later sent the boy to squire for his nephew Tyrion. Ser Cedric had taught Podrick how to groom a horse and check his shoes for stones, and Ser Lorimer had taught him how to steal, but neither had given him much training with a sword. The Imp at least had dispatched him to the Red Keep‚Äôs master-at-arms when they came to court. But during the bread riots Ser Aron Santagar had been amongst those slain, and that had been the end of Podrick‚Äôs training.

{:#Ben-Bushy-Richard-Farrow-and-Will-the-Stork}
**Ben Bushy, Richard Farrow, and Will the Stork**

> ‚ÄúIt was only a game to pass the time. We meant no harm.‚Äù He hesitated. ‚ÄúBen died, you know. Cut down on the Blackwater. Farrow too, and Will the Stork. And Mark Mullendore took a wound that cost him half his arm.‚Äù Good, Brienne wanted to say. Good, he deserved it. But she remembered Mullendore sitting outside his pavilion with his monkey on his shoulder in a little suit of chainmail, the two of them making faces at each other. What was it Catelyn Stark had called them, that night at Bitterbridge? The knights of summer. And now it was autumn and they were falling like leaves.‚Ä¶

{:#Rolland-Darklyn}
**Rolland Darklyn**

> Loras waved at the book. ‚ÄúLord Renly always said that books were for maesters.‚Äù ‚ÄúThis one is for us. The history of every man who has ever worn a white cloak is written here.‚Äù ‚ÄúI have glanced at it. The shields are pretty. I prefer books with more illuminations. Lord Renly owned a few with drawings that would turn a septon blind.‚Äù Jaime had to smile. ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs none of that here, ser, but the histories will open your eyes. You would do well to know about the lives of those who went before.‚Äù ‚ÄúI do. Prince Aemon the Dragonknight, Ser Ryam Redwyne, the Greatheart, Barristan the Bold¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚Äú‚Ä¶¬ÝGwayne Corbray, Alyn Connington, the Demon of Darry, aye. You will have heard of Lucamore Strong as well.‚Äù ‚ÄúSer Lucamore the Lusty?‚Äù Ser Loras seemed amused. ‚ÄúThree wives and thirty children, was it? They cut his cock off. Shall I sing the song for you, my lord?‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd Ser Terrence Toyne?‚Äù ‚ÄúBedded the king‚Äôs mistress and died screaming. The lesson is, men who wear white breeches need to keep them tightly laced.‚Äù ‚ÄúGyles Greycloak? Orivel the Open-Handed?‚Äù ‚ÄúGyles was a traitor, Orivel a coward. Men who shamed the white cloak. What is my lord suggesting?‚Äù ‚ÄúLittle and less. Don‚Äôt take offense where none was meant, ser. How about Long Tom Costayne?‚Äù Ser Loras shook his head. ‚ÄúHe was a Kingsguard knight for sixty years.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhen was that? I‚Äôve never‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúSer Donnel of Duskendale, then?‚Äù ‚ÄúI may have heard the name, but‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúAddison Hill? The White Owl, Michael Mertyns? Jeffory Norcross? They called him Neveryield. Red Robert Flowers? What can you tell me of them?‚Äù ‚ÄúFlowers is a bastard name. So is Hill.‚Äù ‚ÄúYet both men rose to command the Kingsguard. Their tales are in the book. Rolland Darklyn is in here too. The youngest man ever to serve in the Kingsguard, until me. He was given his cloak on a battlefield and died within an hour of donning it.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe can‚Äôt have been very good.‚Äù ‚ÄúGood enough. He died, but his king lived. A lot of brave men have worn the white cloak. Most have been forgotten.‚Äù ‚ÄúMost deserve to be forgotten. The heroes will always be remembered. The best.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe best and the worst.‚Äù So one of us is like to live in song. ‚ÄúAnd a few who were a bit of both. Like him.‚Äù He tapped the page he had been reading. ‚ÄúWho?‚Äù Ser Loras craned his head around to see. ‚ÄúTen black pellets on a scarlet field. I do not know those arms.‚Äù ‚ÄúThey belonged to Criston Cole, who served the first Viserys and the second Aegon.‚Äù Jaime closed the White Book. ‚ÄúThey called him Kingmaker.‚Äù

{:#The-Sparrow-Dwarf}
**The Sparrow Dwarf**

(Not a named character, but he gets more development than some named characters.)

> Not until he hopped off the bench did Brienne realize that the speaker was a dwarf. The little man was not quite five feet tall. His nose was veined and bulbous, his teeth red from sourleaf, and he was dressed in the brown roughspun robes of a holy brother, with the iron hammer of the Smith dangling down about his thick neck.

> ‚ÄúDo you serve some holy house in Duskendale, brother?‚Äù ‚Äú‚ÄôTwas nearer Maidenpool, m‚Äôlady, but the wolves burned us out,‚Äù the man replied, gnawing on a heel of bread. ‚ÄúWe rebuilt as best we could, until some sellswords come. I could not say whose men they were, but they took our pigs and killed the brothers. I squeezed inside a hollow log and hid, but t‚Äôothers were too big. It took me a long time to bury them all, but the Smith, he gave me strength. When that was done I dug up a few coins the elder brother had hid by and set off by myself.‚Äù

> The queen considered her prize, unflinching. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôve killed the wrong dwarf,‚Äù she said at last, grudging every word. ‚ÄúWe never did,‚Äù one of the fools dared to say. ‚ÄúThis is got to be him, ser. A dwarf, see. He‚Äôs rotted some, is all.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe has also grown a new nose,‚Äù Cersei observed. ‚ÄúA rather bulbous one, I‚Äôd say. Tyrion‚Äôs nose was hacked off in a battle.‚Äù The three fools exchanged a look. ‚ÄúNo one told us,‚Äù said the one with head in hand. ‚ÄúThis one come walking along as bold as you please, some ugly dwarf, so we thought¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúHe said he were a sparrow,‚Äù the one with the boil added, ‚Äúand you said he was lying.‚Äù That was directed at the third man. The queen was angry to think that she had kept her small council waiting for this mummer‚Äôs farce. ‚ÄúYou have wasted my time and slain an innocent man. I should have your own heads off.‚Äù But if she did, the next man might hesitate and let the Imp slip the net. She would pile dead dwarfs ten feet high before she let that happen. ‚ÄúRemove yourselves from my sight.‚Äù ‚ÄúAye, Your Grace,‚Äù said the boil. ‚ÄúWe beg your pardons.‚Äù ‚ÄúDo you want the head?‚Äù asked the man who held it. ‚ÄúGive it to Ser Meryn. No, in the sack, you lackwit. Yes. Ser Osmund, see them out.‚Äù

{:#The-High-Septon}
**The High Septon**

> ‚ÄúJaime, Kevan has refused me. He will not serve as Hand, he¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýhe knows about us. He said as much.‚Äù ‚ÄúRefused?‚Äù That surprised him. ‚ÄúHow could he know? He will have read what Stannis wrote, but there is no¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúTyrion knew,‚Äù she reminded him. ‚ÄúWho can say what tales that vile dwarf may have told, or to whom? Uncle Kevan is the least of it. The High Septon¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝTyrion raised him to the crown, when the fat one died. He may know as well.‚Äù She moved closer. ‚ÄúYou must be Tommen‚Äôs Hand. I do not trust Mace Tyrell. What if he had a hand in Father‚Äôs death? He may have been conspiring with Tyrion. The Imp could be on his way to Highgarden¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù

> The pealing of the bells was louder in the yard. He was only a High Septon. How long must we endure this? The ringing was more melodious than the Mountain‚Äôs screams had been, but¬Ý‚Ä¶ Qyburn seemed to sense what she was thinking. ‚ÄúThe bells will stop at sunset, Your Grace.‚Äù

> Within, Osney Kettleblack hung naked from the ceiling, swinging from a pair of heavy iron chains. He had been whipped. His back and shoulders had been laid almost bare, and cuts and welts crisscrossed his legs and arse as well. The queen could hardly stand to look at him. She turned back to the High Septon. ‚ÄúWhat have you done?‚Äù ‚ÄúWe have sought after the truth, most earnestly.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe told you the truth. He came to you of his own free will and confessed his sins.‚Äù ‚ÄúAye. He did that. I have heard many men confess, Your Grace, but seldom have I heard a man so pleased to be so guilty.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou whipped him!‚Äù ‚ÄúThere can be no penance without pain. No man should spare himself the scourge, as I told Ser Osney. I seldom feel so close to god as when I am being whipped for mine own wickedness, though my darkest sins are no wise near as black as his.‚Äù ‚ÄúB-but,‚Äù she sputtered, ‚Äúyou preach the Mother‚Äôs mercy¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúSer Osney shall taste of that sweet milk in the afterlife. In The Seven-Pointed Star it is written that all sins may be forgiven, but crimes must still be punished. Osney Kettleblack is guilty of treason and murder, and the wages of treason are death.‚Äù He is just a priest, he cannot do this. ‚ÄúIt is not for the Faith to condemn a man to death, whatever his offense.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhatever his offense.‚Äù The High Septon repeated the words slowly, weighing them. ‚ÄúStrange to say, Your Grace, the more diligently we applied the scourge, the more Ser Osney‚Äôs offenses seemed to change. He would now have us believe that he never touched Margaery Tyrell. Is that not so, Ser Osney?‚Äù Osney Kettleblack opened his eyes. When he saw the queen standing there before him he ran his tongue across his swollen lips, and said, ‚ÄúThe Wall. You promised me the Wall.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe is mad,‚Äù said Cersei. ‚ÄúYou have driven him mad.‚Äù ‚ÄúSer Osney,‚Äù said the High Septon, in a firm, clear voice, ‚Äúdid you have carnal knowledge of the queen?‚Äù ‚ÄúAye.‚Äù The chains rattled softly as Osney twisted in his shackles. ‚ÄúThat one there. She‚Äôs the queen I fucked, the one sent me to kill the old High Septon. He never had no guards. I just come in when he was sleeping and pushed a pillow down across his face.‚Äù Cersei whirled, and ran.

> ‚ÄúPlease, Uncle, take me out of here.‚Äù ‚ÄúHow? By force of arms?‚Äù Ser Kevan walked to the window and gazed out, frowning. ‚ÄúI would need to make an abbatoir of this holy place. And I do not have the men. The best part of our forces were at Riverrun with your brother. I had no time to raise up a new host.‚Äù He turned back to face her. ‚ÄúI have spoken with His High Holiness. He will not release you until you have atoned for your sins.‚Äù ‚ÄúI have confessed.‚Äù ‚ÄúAtoned, I said. Before the city. A walk‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúNo.‚Äù She knew what her uncle was about to say, and she did not want to hear it. ‚ÄúNever. Tell him that, if you speak again. I am a queen, not some dockside whore.‚Äù ‚ÄúNo harm would come to you. No one will touch‚Äî‚Äù ‚ÄúNo,‚Äù she said, more sharply. ‚ÄúI would sooner die.‚Äù Ser Kevan was unmoved. ‚ÄúIf that is your wish, you may soon have it granted. His High Holiness is resolved that you be tried for regicide, deicide, incest, and high treason.‚Äù ‚ÄúDeicide?‚Äù She almost laughed. ‚ÄúWhen did I kill a god?‚Äù ‚ÄúThe High Septon speaks for the Seven here on earth. Strike at him, and you are striking at the gods themselves.‚Äù

{:#Nagga-and-the-Grey-King}
**Nagga and the Grey King**

> On the crown of the hill four-and-forty monstrous stone ribs rose from the earth like the trunks of great pale trees. The sight made Aeron‚Äôs heart beat faster. Nagga had been the first sea dragon, the mightiest ever to rise from the waves. She fed on krakens and leviathans and drowned whole islands in her wrath, yet the Grey King had slain her and the Drowned God had changed her bones to stone so that men might never cease to wonder at the courage of the first of kings. Nagga‚Äôs ribs became the beams and pillars of his longhall, just as her jaws became his throne. For a thousand years and seven he reigned here, Aeron recalled. Here he took his mermaid wife and planned his wars against the Storm God. From here he ruled both stone and salt, wearing robes of woven seaweed and a tall pale crown made from Nagga‚Äôs teeth. But that was in the dawn of days, when mighty men still dwelt on earth and sea. The hall had been warmed by Nagga‚Äôs living fire, which the Grey King had made his thrall. On its walls hung tapestries woven from silver seaweed most pleasing to the eyes. The Grey King‚Äôs warriors had feasted on the bounty of the sea at a table in the shape of a great starfish, whilst seated upon thrones carved from mother-of-pearl. Gone, all the glory gone. Men were smaller now. Their lives had grown short. The Storm God drowned Nagga‚Äôs fire after the Grey King‚Äôs death, the chairs and tapestries had been stolen, the roof and walls had rotted away. Even the Grey King‚Äôs great throne of fangs had been swallowed by the sea. Only Nagga‚Äôs bones endured to remind the ironborn of all the wonder that had been.

{:#Cragorn}
**Cragorn**

> Sharp as a swordthrust, the sound of a horn split the air. Bright and baneful was its voice, a shivering hot scream that made a man‚Äôs bones seem to thrum within him. The cry lingered in the damp sea air: aaaaRREEEEeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeee. All eyes turned toward the sound. It was one of Euron‚Äôs mongrels winding the call, a monstrous man with a shaved head. Rings of gold and jade and jet glistened on his arms, and on his broad chest was tattooed some bird of prey, talons dripping blood. aaaaRRREEEEeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeee. The horn he blew was shiny black and twisted, and taller than a man as he held it with both hands. It was bound about with bands of red gold and dark steel, incised with ancient Valyrian glyphs that seemed to glow redly as the sound swelled. aaaaaaaRRREEEEEEEEEEEEeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeee. It was a terrible sound, a wail of pain and fury that seemed to burn the ears. Aeron Damphair covered his, and prayed for the Drowned God to raise a mighty wave and smash the horn to silence, yet still the shriek went on and on. It is the horn of hell, he wanted to scream, though no man would have heard him. The cheeks of the tattooed man were so puffed out they looked about to burst, and the muscles in his chest twitched in a way that it made it seem as if the bird were about to rip free of his flesh and take wing. And now the glyphs were burning brightly, every line and letter shimmering with white fire. On and on and on the sound went, echoing amongst the howling hills behind them and across the waters of Nagga‚Äôs Cradle to ring against the mountains of Great Wyk, on and on and on until it filled the whole wet world. And when it seemed the sound would never end, it did. The hornblower‚Äôs breath failed at last. He staggered and almost fell. The priest saw Orkwood of Orkmont catch him by one arm to hold him up, whilst Left-Hand Lucas Codd took the twisted black horn from his hands. A thin wisp of smoke was rising from the horn, and the priest saw blood and blisters upon the lips of the man who‚Äôd sounded it. The bird on his chest was bleeding too.

> ‚ÄúCragorn‚Äôs died, you know.‚Äù ‚ÄúWho?‚Äù ‚ÄúThe man who blew my dragon horn. When the maester cut him open, his lungs were charred as black as soot.‚Äù

> That night, for the first time, he brought forth the dragon horn that the Crow‚Äôs Eye had found amongst the smoking wastes of great Valyria. A twisted thing it was, six feet long from end to end, gleaming black and banded with red gold and dark Valyrian steel. Euron‚Äôs hellhorn. Victarion ran his hand along it. The horn was as warm and smooth as the dusky woman‚Äôs thighs, and so shiny that he could see a twisted likeness of his own features in its depths. Strange sorcerous writings had been cut into the bands that girded it. ‚ÄúValyrian glyphs,‚Äù Moqorro called them. That much Victarion had known. ‚ÄúWhat do they say?‚Äù ‚ÄúMuch and more.‚Äù The black priest pointed to one golden band. ‚ÄúHere the horn is named. ‚ÄòI am Dragonbinder,‚Äô it says. Have you ever heard it sound?‚Äù ‚ÄúOnce.‚Äù One of his brother‚Äôs mongrels had sounded the hellhorn at the kingsmoot on Old Wyk. A monster of a man he had been, huge and shaven-headed, with rings of gold and jet and jade around arms thick with muscle, and a great hawk tattooed across his chest. ‚ÄúThe sound it made¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýit burned, somehow. As if my bones were on fire, searing my flesh from within. Those writings glowed red-hot, then white-hot and painful to look upon. It seemed as if the sound would never end. It was like some long scream. A thousand screams, all melted into one.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd the man who blew the horn, what of him?‚Äù ‚ÄúHe died. There were blisters on his lips, after. His bird was bleeding too.‚Äù The captain thumped his chest. ‚ÄúThe hawk, just here. Every feather dripping blood. I heard the man was all burned up inside, but that might just have been some tale.‚Äù ‚ÄúA true tale.‚Äù Moqorro turned the hellhorn, examining the queer letters that crawled across a second of the golden bands. ‚ÄúHere it says, ‚ÄòNo mortal man shall sound me and live.‚Äô¬Ý‚Äù Bitterly Victarion brooded on the treachery of brothers. Euron‚Äôs gifts are always poisoned. ‚ÄúThe Crow‚Äôs Eye swore this horn would bind dragons to my will. But how will that serve me if the price is death?‚Äù ‚ÄúYour brother did not sound the horn himself. Nor must you.‚Äù Moqorro pointed to the band of steel. ‚ÄúHere. ‚ÄòBlood for fire, fire for blood.‚Äô Who blows the hellhorn matters not. The dragons will come to the horn‚Äôs master. You must claim the horn. With blood.‚Äù

{:#Nimble-Dick-Crabb}
**Nimble Dick Crabb**

> Brienne saw a sapling sway. From the bushes slid a man, so caked with dirt that he looked as if he had sprouted from the earth. A broken sword was in his hand, but it was his face that gave her pause, the small eyes and wide flat nostrils. She knew that nose. She knew those eyes. Pyg, his friends had called him. Everything seemed to happen in a heartbeat. A second man slipped over the lip of the well, making no more noise than a snake might make slithering across a pile of wet leaves. He wore an iron halfhelm wrapped in stained red silk, and had a short, thick throwing spear in hand. Brienne knew him too. From behind her came a rustling as a head poked down through the red leaves. Crabb was standing underneath the weirwood. He looked up and saw the face. ‚ÄúHere,‚Äù he called to Brienne. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs your fool.‚Äù ‚ÄúDick,‚Äù she called urgently, ‚Äúto me.‚Äù Shagwell dropped from the weirwood, braying laughter. He was garbed in motley, but so faded and stained that it showed more brown than grey or pink. In place of a jester‚Äôs flail he had a triple morningstar, three spiked balls chained to a wooden haft. He swung it hard and low, and one of Crabb‚Äôs knees exploded in a spray of blood and bone. ‚ÄúThat‚Äôs funny,‚Äù Shagwell crowed as Dick fell. The sword she‚Äôd given him went flying from his hand and vanished in the weeds. He writhed on the ground, screaming and clutching at the ruins of his knee. ‚ÄúOh, look,‚Äù said Shagwell, ‚Äúit‚Äôs Smuggler Dick, the one who made the map for us. Did you come all this way to give us back our gold?‚Äù ‚ÄúPlease,‚Äù Dick whimpered, ‚Äúplease don‚Äôt, my leg¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúDoes it hurt? I can make it stop.‚Äù ‚ÄúLeave him be,‚Äù said Brienne. ‚ÄúDON‚ÄôT!‚Äù shrieked Dick, lifting bloody hands to shield his head. Shagwell whirled the spiked ball once around his head and brought it down in the middle of Crabb‚Äôs face. There was a sickening crunch. In the silence that followed, Brienne could hear the sound of her own heart.

{:#Pyg-Timeon-and-Shagwell}
**Pyg, Timeon, and Shagwell**

> It may be that I will need to kill him, she told herself one night as she paced about the camp. The notion made her queasy. Her old master-at-arms had always questioned whether she was hard enough for battle. ‚ÄúYou have a man‚Äôs strength in your arms,‚Äù Ser Goodwin had said to her, more than once, ‚Äúbut your heart is as soft as any maid‚Äôs. It is one thing to train in the yard with a blunted sword in hand, and another to drive a foot of sharpened steel into a man‚Äôs gut and see the light go out of his eyes.‚Äù To toughen her, Ser Goodwin used to send her to her father‚Äôs butcher to slaughter lambs and suckling pigs. The piglets squealed and the lambs screamed like frightened children. By the time the butchering was done Brienne had been blind with tears, her clothes so bloody that she had given them to her maid to burn. But Ser Goodwin still had doubts. ‚ÄúA piglet is a piglet. It is different with a man. When I was a squire young as you, I had a friend who was strong and quick and agile, a champion in the yard. We all knew that one day he would be a splendid knight. Then war came to the Stepstones. I saw my friend drive his foeman to his knees and knock the axe from his hand, but when he might have finished he held back for half a heartbeat. In battle half a heartbeat is a lifetime. The man slipped out his dirk and found a chink in my friend‚Äôs armor. His strength, his speed, his valor, all his hard-won skill¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýit was worth less than a mummer‚Äôs fart, because he flinched from killing. Remember that, girl.‚Äù

> Pyg was edging closer. Shagwell took a hop toward her. She backed away from them. They will back me off the cliff if I let them. ‚ÄúStay away,‚Äù she warned them. ‚ÄúI think I‚Äôm going to fuck you up the nose, wench,‚Äù Shagwell announced. ‚ÄúWon‚Äôt that be amusing?‚Äù ‚ÄúHe has a very small cock,‚Äù Timeon explained. ‚ÄúDrop that pretty sword and might be we‚Äôll go gentle on you, woman. We need gold to pay these smugglers, that‚Äôs all.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd if I give you gold, you‚Äôll let us go?‚Äù ‚ÄúWe will.‚Äù Timeon smiled. ‚ÄúOnce you‚Äôve fucked the lot of us. We‚Äôll pay you like a proper whore. A silver for each fuck. Or else we‚Äôll take the gold and rape you anyway, and do you like the Mountain did Lord Vargo. What‚Äôs your choice?‚Äù ‚ÄúThis.‚Äù Brienne threw herself toward Pyg. He jerked his broken blade up to protect his face, but as he went high she went low. Oathkeeper bit through leather, wool, skin, and muscle, into the sellsword‚Äôs thigh. Pyg cut back wildly as his leg went out from under him. His broken sword scraped against her chainmail before he landed on his back. Brienne stabbed him through the throat, gave the blade a hard turn, and slid it out, whirling just as Timeon‚Äôs spear came flashing past her face. I did not flinch, she thought, as blood ran red down her cheek. Did you see, Ser Goodwin? She hardly felt the cut.

> ‚ÄúYour turn,‚Äù she told Timeon, as the Dornishman pulled out a second spear, shorter and thicker than the first. ‚ÄúThrow it.‚Äù ‚ÄúSo you can dance away and charge me? I‚Äôd end up dead as Pyg. No. Get her, Shags.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou get her,‚Äù Shagwell said. ‚ÄúDid you see what she did to Pyg? She‚Äôs mad with moon blood.‚Äù The fool was behind her, Timeon in front. No matter how she turned, one was at her back. ‚ÄúGet her,‚Äù urged Timeon, ‚Äúand you can fuck her corpse.‚Äù ‚ÄúOh, you do love me.‚Äù The morningstar was whirling. Choose one, Brienne told herself. Choose one and kill him quickly. Then a stone came out of nowhere, and hit Shagwell in the head. Brienne did not hesitate. She flew at Timeon. He was better than Pyg, but he had only a short throwing spear, and she had a Valyrian steel blade. Oathkeeper was alive in her hands. She had never been so quick. The blade became a grey blur. He wounded her in the shoulder as she came at him, but she slashed off his ear and half his cheek, hacked the head off his spear, and put a foot of rippled steel into his belly through the links of the chainmail byrnie he was wearing. Timeon was still trying to fight as she pulled her blade from him, its fullers running red with blood. He clawed at his belt and came up with a dagger, so Brienne cut his hand off. That one was for Jaime. ‚ÄúMother have mercy,‚Äù the Dornishman gasped, the blood bubbling from his mouth and spurting from his wrist. ‚ÄúFinish it. Send me back to Dorne, you bloody bitch.‚Äù She did.

> Shagwell was on his knees when she turned, looking dazed as he fumbled for the morningstar. As he staggered to his feet, another stone slammed him in the ear. Podrick had climbed the fallen wall and was standing amongst the ivy glowering, a fresh rock in his hand. ‚ÄúI told you I could fight!‚Äù he shouted down. Shagwell tried to crawl away. ‚ÄúI yield,‚Äù the fool cried, ‚ÄúI yield. You mustn‚Äôt hurt sweet Shagwell, I‚Äôm too droll to die.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou are no better than the rest of them. You have robbed and raped and murdered.‚Äù ‚ÄúOh, I have, I have, I shan‚Äôt deny it¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut I‚Äôm amusing, with all my japes and capers. I make men laugh.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd women weep.‚Äù ‚ÄúIs that my fault? Women have no sense of humor.‚Äù Brienne lowered Oathkeeper. ‚ÄúDig a grave. There, beneath the weirwood.‚Äù She pointed with her blade. ‚ÄúI have no spade.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou have two hands.‚Äù One more than you left Jaime. ‚ÄúWhy bother? Leave them for the crows.‚Äù ‚ÄúTimeon and Pyg can feed the crows. Nimble Dick will have a grave. He was a Crabb. This is his place.‚Äù The ground was soft from rain, but even so it took the fool the rest of the day to dig down deep enough. Night was falling by the time he was done, and his hands were bloody and blistered. Brienne sheathed Oathkeeper, gathered up Dick Crabb, and carried him to the hole. His face was hard to look on. ‚ÄúI‚Äôm sorry that I never trusted you. I don‚Äôt know how to do that anymore.‚Äù As she knelt to lay the body down, she thought, The fool will make his try now, whilst my back is turned. She heard his ragged breathing half a heartbeat before Podrick cried out his warning. Shagwell had a jagged chunk of rock clutched in one hand. Brienne had her dagger up her sleeve. A dagger will beat a rock almost every time. She knocked aside his arm and punched the steel into his bowels. ‚ÄúLaugh,‚Äù she snarled at him. He moaned instead. ‚ÄúLaugh,‚Äù she repeated, grabbing his throat with one hand and stabbing at his belly with the other. ‚ÄúLaugh!‚Äù She kept saying it, over and over, until her hand was red up to the wrist and the stink of the fool‚Äôs dying was like to choke her. But Shagwell never laughed. The sobs that Brienne heard were all her own. When she realized that, she threw down her knife and shuddered.

> Podrick helped her lower Nimble Dick into his hole. By the time they were done the moon was rising. Brienne rubbed the dirt from her hands and tossed two dragons down into the grave. ‚ÄúWhy did you do that, my lady? Ser?‚Äù asked Pod. ‚ÄúIt was the reward I promised him for finding me the fool.‚Äù Laughter sounded from behind them. She ripped Oathkeeper from her sheath and whirled, expecting more Bloody Mummers¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut it was only Hyle Hunt atop the crumbling wall, his legs crossed. ‚ÄúIf there are brothels down in hell, the wretch will thank you,‚Äù the knight called down. ‚ÄúElsewise, that‚Äôs a waste of good gold.‚Äù ‚ÄúI keep my promises. What are you doing here?‚Äù ‚ÄúLord Randyll bid me follow you. If by some freak‚Äôs chance you stumbled onto Sansa Stark, he told me to bring her back to Maidenpool. Have no fear, I was commanded not to harm you.‚Äù Brienne snorted. ‚ÄúAs if you could.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhat will you do now, my lady?‚Äù ‚ÄúCover him.‚Äù ‚ÄúAbout the girl, I meant. The Lady Sansa.‚Äù Brienne thought a moment. ‚ÄúShe was making for Riverrun, if Timeon told it true. Somewhere along the way she was taken by the Hound. If I find him¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚Äú‚Ä¶ he‚Äôll kill you.‚Äù ‚ÄúOr I‚Äôll kill him,‚Äù she said stubbornly. ‚ÄúWill you help me cover up poor Crabb, ser?‚Äù ‚ÄúNo true knight could refuse such beauty.‚Äù Ser Hyle climbed down from the wall. Together, they shoved the dirt on top of Nimble Dick as the moon rose higher in the sky, and down below the ground the heads of forgotten kings whispered secrets.

{:#Garin-the-Great}
**Garin the Great**

> ‚ÄúPrince Oberyn was full of stories.‚Äù Garin had been with them as well that day; he was Arianne‚Äôs milk brother, and they had been inseparable since before they learned to walk. ‚ÄúHe told about Prince Garin, I remember, the one that I was named for.‚Äù ‚ÄúGarin the Great,‚Äù offered Drey, ‚Äúthe wonder of the Rhoyne.‚Äù ‚ÄúThat‚Äôs the one. He made Valyria tremble.‚Äù ‚ÄúThey trembled,‚Äù said Ser Gerold, ‚Äúthen they killed him. If I led a quarter of a million men to death, would they call me Gerold the Great?‚Äù He snorted. ‚ÄúI shall remain Darkstar, I think. At least it is mine own.‚Äù He unsheathed his longsword, sat upon the lip of the dry well, and began to hone the blade with an oilstone.

> ‚ÄúThe conquerors did not believe either, Hugor Hill,‚Äù said Ysilla. ‚ÄúThe men of Volantis and Valyria hung Garin in a golden cage and made mock as he called upon his Mother to destroy them. But in the night the waters rose and drowned them, and from that day to this they have not rested. They are down there still beneath the water, they who were once the lords of fire. Their cold breath rises from the murk to make these fogs, and their flesh has turned as stony as their hearts.‚Äù The stump of Tyrion‚Äôs nose was itching fiercely. He gave it a scratch. The old woman may be right. This place is no good. I feel as if I am back in the privy again, watching my father die. He would go mad as well if he had to spend his days in this grey soup whilst his flesh and bones turned to stone. Young Griff did not seem to share his misgivings. ‚ÄúLet them try and trouble us, we‚Äôll show them what we‚Äôre made of.‚Äù ‚ÄúWe are made of blood and bone, in the image of the Father and the Mother,‚Äù said Septa Lemore. ‚ÄúMake no vainglorious boasts, I beg you. Pride is a grievous sin. The stone men were proud as well, and the Shrouded Lord was proudest of them all.‚Äù The heat from the glowing coals brought a flush to Tyrion‚Äôs face. ‚ÄúIs there a Shrouded Lord? Or is he just some tale?‚Äù ‚ÄúThe Shrouded Lord has ruled these mists since Garin‚Äôs day,‚Äù said Yandry. ‚ÄúSome say that he himself is Garin, risen from his watery grave.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe dead do not rise,‚Äù insisted Haldon Halfmaester, ‚Äúand no man lives a thousand years. Yes, there is a Shrouded Lord. There have been a score of them. When one dies another takes his place. This one is a corsair from the Basilisk Islands who believed the Rhoyne would offer richer pickings than the Summer Sea.‚Äù ‚ÄúAye, I‚Äôve heard that too,‚Äù said Duck, ‚Äúbut there‚Äôs another tale I like better. The one that says he‚Äôs not like t‚Äôother stone men, that he started as a statue till a grey woman came out of the fog and kissed him with lips as cold as ice.‚Äù

{:#Arys-Oakheart}
**Arys Oakheart**

> Ser Arys had come to Dorne to attend his own princess, as Areo Hotah had once come with his. Even their names sounded oddly alike: Areo and Arys. Yet there the likeness ended. The captain had left Norvos and its bearded priests, but Ser Arys Oakheart still served the Iron Throne. Hotah had felt a certain sadness whenever he saw the man in the long snowy cloak, the times the prince had sent him down to Sunspear. One day, he sensed, the two of them would fight; on that day Oakheart would die, with the captain‚Äôs longaxe crashing through his skull. He slid his hand along the smooth ashen shaft of his axe and wondered if that day was drawing nigh.

> Arianne Martell had crossed the Mander once, when she had gone with three of the Sand Snakes to visit Tyene‚Äôs mother. Compared to that mighty waterway, the Greenblood was scarce worthy of the name of river, yet it remained the life of Dorne. It took its name from the murky green of its sluggish waters; but as they approached, the sunlight seemed to turn those waters gold. She had seldom seen a sweeter sight. The next part should be slow and simple, she thought, up the Greenblood and onto the Vaith, as far as a poleboat can go. That would give her time enough to prepare Myrcella for all that was to come. Beyond Vaith the deep sands waited. They would need help from Sandstone and the Hellholt to make that crossing, but she did not doubt that it would be forthcoming. The Red Viper had been fostered at Sandstone, and Prince Oberyn‚Äôs paramour Ellaria Sand was Lord Uller‚Äôs natural daughter; four of the Sand Snakes were his granddaughters. I will crown Myrcella at the Hellholt and raise my banners there. They found the boat half a league downstream, hidden beneath the drooping branches of a great green willow. Low of roof and wide abeam, the poleboats had hardly any draft to speak of; the Young Dragon had disparaged them as ‚Äúhovels built on rafts,‚Äù but that was hardly fair. All but the poorest orphan boats were wonderfully carved and painted. This one was done in shades of green, with a curved wooden tiller shaped like a mermaid, and fish faces peering through her rails. Poles and ropes and jars of olive oil cluttered her decks, and iron lanterns swung fore and aft. Arianne saw no orphans. Where is her crew? she wondered. Garin reined up beneath the willow. ‚ÄúWake up, you fish-eyed lagabeds,‚Äù he called as he leapt down from the saddle. ‚ÄúYour queen is here, and wants her royal welcome. Come up, come out, we‚Äôll have some songs and sweet-wine. My mouth is set for‚Äî‚Äù The door on the poleboat slammed open. Out into the sunlight stepped Areo Hotah, longaxe in hand. Garin jerked to a halt. Arianne felt as though an axe had caught her in the belly. It was not supposed to end this way. This was not supposed to happen. When she heard Drey say, ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs the last face I‚Äôd hoped to see,‚Äù she knew she had to act. ‚ÄúAway!‚Äù she cried, vaulting back into the saddle. ‚ÄúArys, protect the princess‚Äî‚Äù Hotah thumped the butt of his longaxe upon the deck. Behind the ornate rails of the poleboat, a dozen guardsmen rose, armed with throwing spears or crossbows.
