His other hand was missing, severed at the wrist, the stump bound up in leather. Blood flowed thick and sluggish from the slash across his throat. The wolf lapped at it with his tongue, licked the ragged eyeless ruin of his nose and cheeks, then buried his muzzle in his neck and tore it open, gulping down a gobbet of sweet meat. No flesh had ever tasted half as good. When he was done with that one, he moved to the next, and devoured the choicest bits of that man too. Ravens watched him from the trees, squatting dark-eyed and silent on the branches as snow drifted down around them. The other wolves made do with his leavings; the old male fed first, then the female, then the tail. They were his now. They were pack. No, the boy whispered, we have another pack. Lady‚Äôs dead and maybe Grey Wind too, but somewhere there‚Äôs still Shaggydog and Nymeria and Ghost. Remember Ghost?

> Falling snow and feasting wolves began to dim. Warmth beat against his face, comforting as a mother‚Äôs kisses. Fire, he thought, smoke. His nose twitched to the smell of roasting meat. And then the forest fell away, and he was back in the longhall again, back in his broken body, staring at a fire. Meera Reed was turning a chunk of raw red flesh above the flames, letting it char and spit. ‚ÄúJust in time,‚Äù she said. Bran rubbed his eyes with the heel of his hand and wriggled backwards against the wall to sit. ‚ÄúYou almost slept through supper. The ranger found a sow.‚Äù Behind her, Hodor was tearing eagerly at a chunk of hot charred flesh as blood and grease ran down into his beard. Wisps of smoke rose from between his fingers. ‚ÄúHodor,‚Äù he muttered between bites, ‚Äúhodor, hodor.‚Äù His sword lay on the earthen floor beside him. Jojen Reed nipped at his own joint with small bites, chewing each chunk of meat a dozen times before swallowing. The ranger killed a pig. Coldhands stood beside the door, a raven on his arm, both staring at the fire. Reflections from the flames glittered off four black eyes. He does not eat, Bran remembered, and he fears the flames. ‚ÄúYou said no fire,‚Äù he reminded the ranger. ‚ÄúThe walls around us hide the light, and dawn is close. We will be on our way soon.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhat happened to the men? The foes behind us?‚Äù ‚ÄúThey will not trouble you.‚Äù ‚ÄúWho were they? Wildlings?‚Äù Meera turned the meat to cook the other side. Hodor was chewing and swallowing, muttering happily under his breath. Only Jojen seemed aware of what was happening as Coldhands turned his head to stare at Bran. ‚ÄúThey were foes.‚Äù Men of the Night‚Äôs Watch. ‚ÄúYou killed them. You and the ravens. Their faces were all torn, and their eyes were gone.‚Äù Coldhands did not deny it. ‚ÄúThey were your brothers. I saw. The wolves had ripped their clothes up, but I could still tell. Their cloaks were black. Like your hands.‚Äù Coldhands said nothing. ‚ÄúWho are you? Why are your hands black?‚Äù The ranger studied his hands as if he had never noticed them before. ‚ÄúOnce the heart has ceased to beat, a man‚Äôs blood runs down into his extremities, where it thickens and congeals.‚Äù His voice rattled in his throat, as thin and gaunt as he was. ‚ÄúHis hands and feet swell up and turn as black as pudding. The rest of him becomes as white as milk.‚Äù Meera Reed rose, her frog spear in her hand, a chunk of smoking meat still impaled upon its tines. ‚ÄúShow us your face.‚Äù The ranger made no move to obey. ‚ÄúHe‚Äôs dead.‚Äù Bran could taste the bile in his throat. ‚ÄúMeera, he‚Äôs some dead thing. The monsters cannot pass so long as the Wall stands and the men of the Night‚Äôs Watch stay true, that‚Äôs what Old Nan used to say. He came to meet us at the Wall, but he could not pass. He sent Sam instead, with that wildling girl.‚Äù Meera‚Äôs gloved hand tightened around the shaft of her frog spear. ‚ÄúWho sent you? Who is this three-eyed crow?‚Äù ‚ÄúA friend. Dreamer, wizard, call him what you will. The last greenseer.‚Äù The longhall‚Äôs wooden door banged open. Outside, the night wind howled, bleak and black. The trees were full of ravens, screaming. Coldhands did not move. ‚ÄúA monster,‚Äù Bran said. The ranger looked at Bran as if the rest of them did not exist. ‚ÄúYour monster, Brandon Stark.‚Äù ‚ÄúYours,‚Äù the raven echoed, from his shoulder. Outside the door, the ravens in the trees took up the cry, until the night wood echoed to the murderer‚Äôs song of ‚ÄúYours, yours, yours.‚Äù ‚ÄúJojen, did you dream this?‚Äù Meera asked her brother. ‚ÄúWho is he? What is he? What do we do now?‚Äù ‚ÄúWe go with the ranger,‚Äù said Jojen. ‚ÄúWe have come too far to turn back now, Meera. We would never make it back to the Wall alive. We go with Bran‚Äôs monster, or we die.‚Äù

{:#Daemon-Blackfyre}
**Daemon Blackfyre**

> ‚ÄúThe Golden Company marches toward Volantis as we speak, there to await the coming of our queen out of the east.‚Äù Beneath the gold, the bitter steel. ‚ÄúI had heard the Golden Company was under contract with one of the Free Cities.‚Äù ‚ÄúMyr.‚Äù Illyrio smirked. ‚ÄúContracts can be broken.‚Äù ‚ÄúThere is more coin in cheese than I knew,‚Äù said Tyrion. ‚ÄúHow did you accomplish that?‚Äù The magister waggled his fat fingers. ‚ÄúSome contracts are writ in ink, and some in blood. I say no more.‚Äù The dwarf pondered that. The Golden Company was reputedly the finest of the free companies, founded a century ago by Bittersteel, a bastard son of Aegon the Unworthy. When another of Aegon‚Äôs Great Bastards tried to seize the Iron Throne from his trueborn half-brother, Bittersteel joined the revolt. Daemon Blackfyre had perished on the Redgrass Field, however, and his rebellion with him. Those followers of the Black Dragon who survived the battle yet refused to bend the knee fled across the narrow sea, among them Daemon‚Äôs younger sons, Bittersteel, and hundreds of landless lords and knights who soon found themselves forced to sell their swords to eat. Some joined the Ragged Standard, some the Second Sons or Maiden‚Äôs Men. Bittersteel saw the strength of House Blackfyre scattering to the four winds, so he formed the Golden Company to bind the exiles together. From that day to this, the men of the Golden Company had lived and died in the Disputed Lands, fighting for Myr or Lys or Tyrosh in their pointless little wars, and dreaming of the land their fathers had lost. They were exiles and sons of exiles, dispossessed and unforgiven¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýyet formidable fighters still. ‚ÄúI admire your powers of persuasion,‚Äù Tyrion told Illyrio. ‚ÄúHow did you convince the Golden Company to take up the cause of our sweet queen when they have spent so much of their history fighting against the Targaryens?‚Äù Illyrio brushed away the objection as if it were a fly. ‚ÄúBlack or red, a dragon is still a dragon. When Maelys the Monstrous died upon the Stepstones, it was the end of the male line of House Blackfyre.‚Äù The cheesemonger smiled through his forked beard. ‚ÄúAnd Daenerys will give the exiles what Bittersteel and the Blackfyres never could. She will take them home.‚Äù

{:#Serra}
**Serra**

> The cheesemonger woke when they stopped to change the horses and sent for a fresh hamper. ‚ÄúHow far have we come?‚Äù the dwarf asked him as they stuffed themselves with cold capon and a relish made of carrots, raisins, and bits of lime and orange. ‚ÄúThis is Andalos, my friend. The land your Andals came from. They took it from the hairy men who were here before them, cousins to the hairy men of Ib. The heart of Hugor‚Äôs ancient realm lies north of us, but we are passing through its southern marches. In Pentos, these are called the Flatlands. Farther east stand the Velvet Hills, whence we are bound.‚Äù Andalos. The Faith taught that the Seven themselves had once walked the hills of Andalos in human form. ‚ÄúThe Father reached his hand into the heavens and pulled down seven stars,‚Äù Tyrion recited from memory, ‚Äúand one by one he set them on the brow of Hugor of the Hill to make a glowing crown.‚Äù Magister Illyrio gave him a curious look. ‚ÄúI did not dream my little friend was so devout.‚Äù The dwarf shrugged. ‚ÄúA relic of my boyhood. I knew I would not make a knight, so I decided to be High Septon. That crystal crown adds a foot to a man‚Äôs height. I studied the holy books and prayed until I had scabs on both my knees, but my quest came to a tragic end. I reached that certain age and fell in love.‚Äù ‚ÄúA maiden? I know the way of that.‚Äù Illyrio thrust his right hand up his left sleeve and drew out a silver locket. Inside was a painted likeness of a woman with big blue eyes and pale golden hair streaked by silver. ‚ÄúSerra. I found her in a Lysene pillow house and brought her home to warm my bed, but in the end I wed her. Me, whose first wife had been a cousin of the Prince of Pentos. The palace gates were closed to me thereafter, but I did not care. The price was small enough, for Serra.‚Äù ‚ÄúHow did she die?‚Äù Tyrion knew that she was dead; no man spoke so fondly of a woman who had abandoned him. ‚ÄúA Braavosi trading galley called at Pentos on her way back from the Jade Sea. The Treasure carried cloves and saffron, jet and jade, scarlet samite, green silk¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand the grey death. We slew her oarsmen as they came ashore and burned the ship at anchor, but the rats crept down the oars and paddled to the quay on cold stone feet. The plague took two thousand before it ran its course.‚Äù Magister Illyrio closed the locket. ‚ÄúI keep her hands in my bedchamber. Her hands that were so soft¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù Tyrion thought of Tysha. He glanced out at the fields where once the gods had walked. ‚ÄúWhat sort of gods make rats and plagues and dwarfs?‚Äù Another passage from The Seven-Pointed Star came back to him. ‚ÄúThe Maid brought him forth a girl as supple as a willow with eyes like deep blue pools, and Hugor declared that he would have her for his bride. So the Mother made her fertile, and the Crone foretold that she would bear the king four-and-forty mighty sons. The Warrior gave strength to their arms, whilst the Smith wrought for each a suit of iron plates.‚Äù ‚ÄúYour Smith must have been Rhoynish,‚Äù Illyrio quipped. ‚ÄúThe Andals learned the art of working iron from the Rhoynar who dwelt along the river. This is known.‚Äù ‚ÄúNot by our septons.‚Äù

{:#Maester-Kedry-Willam-Wells-and-Cletus-Yronwood}
**Maester Kedry, Willam Wells, and Cletus Yronwood**

> Their driver awaited them beside his hathay. In Westeros, it might have been called an oxcart, though it was a deal more ornate than any cart that Quentyn had ever seen in Dorne, and lacked an ox. The hathay was pulled by a dwarf elephant, her hide the color of dirty snow. The streets of Old Volantis were full of such. Quentyn would have preferred to walk, but they were miles from their inn. Besides, the innkeep at the Merchant‚Äôs House had warned him that traveling afoot would taint them in the eyes of foreign captains and the native-born Volantenes alike. Persons of quality traveled by palanquin, or in the back of a hathay¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand as it happened the innkeep had a cousin who owned several such contrivances and would be pleased to serve them in this matter. Their driver was one of the cousin‚Äôs slaves, a small man with a wheel tattooed upon one cheek, naked but for a breechclout and a pair of sandals. His skin was the color of teak, his eyes chips of flint. After he had helped them up onto the cushioned bench between the cart‚Äôs two huge wooden wheels, he clambered onto the elephant‚Äôs back. ‚ÄúThe Merchant‚Äôs House,‚Äù Quentyn told him, ‚Äúbut go along the wharves.‚Äù Beyond the waterfront and its breezes, the streets and alleys of Volantis were hot enough to drown a man in his own sweat, at least on this side of the river. The driver shouted something at his elephant in the local tongue. The beast began to move, trunk swaying from side to side. The cart lurched along behind her, the driver hooting at sailors and slaves alike to clear the way. It was easy enough to tell one from the other. The slaves were all tattooed: a mask of blue feathers, a lightning bolt that ran from jaw to brow, a coin upon the cheek, a leopard‚Äôs spots, a skull, a jug. Maester Kedry said there were five slaves for every free man in Volantis though he had not lived long enough to verify his estimate. He had perished on the morning the corsairs swarmed aboard the Meadowlark. Quentyn lost two other friends that same day‚ÄîWillam Wells with his freckles and his crooked teeth, fearless with a lance, and Cletus Yronwood, handsome despite his lazy eye, always randy, always laughing. Cletus had been Quentyn‚Äôs dearest friend for half his life, a brother in all but blood. ‚ÄúGive your bride a kiss for me,‚Äù Cletus had whispered to him, just before he died. The corsairs had come aboard in the darkness before the dawn, as the Meadowlark was anchored off the coast of the Disputed Lands. The crew had beaten them off, at the cost of twelve lives. Afterward the sailors stripped the dead corsairs of boots and belts and weapons, divvied up their purses, and yanked gemstones from their ears and rings from their fingers. One of the corpses was so fat that the ship‚Äôs cook had to cut his fingers off with a meat cleaver to claim his rings. It took three Meadowlarks to roll the body into the sea. The other pirates were chucked in after him, without a word of prayer or ceremony. Their own dead received more tender treatment. The sailors sewed their bodies up in canvas, weighed down with ballast stones so they might sink more quickly. The captain of the Meadowlark led his crew in a prayer for the souls of their slain shipmates. Then he turned to his Dornish passengers, the three who still remained of the six who had come aboard at the Planky Town. Even the big man had emerged, pale and greensick and unsteady on his feet, struggling up from the depths of the ship‚Äôs hold to pay his last respects. ‚ÄúOne of you should say some words for your dead, before we give them to the sea,‚Äù the captain said. Gerris had obliged, lying with every other word, since he dare not tell the truth of who they‚Äôd been or why they‚Äôd come. It was not supposed to end like that for them. ‚ÄúThis will be a tale to tell our grandchildren,‚Äù Cletus had declared the day they set out from his father‚Äôs castle. Will made a face at that, and said, ‚ÄúA tale to tell tavern wenches, you mean, in hopes they‚Äôll lift their skirts.‚Äù Cletus had slapped him on the back. ‚ÄúFor grandchildren, you need children. For children, you need to lift some skirts.‚Äù Later, in the Planky Town, the Dornishmen had toasted Quentyn‚Äôs future bride, made ribald japes about his wedding night to come, and talked about the things they‚Äôd see, the deeds they‚Äôd do, the glory they would win. All they won was a sailcloth sack filled with ballast stones. As much as he mourned Will and Cletus, it was the maester‚Äôs loss that Quentyn felt most keenly. Kedry had been fluent in the tongues of all of the Free Cities, and even the mongrel Ghiscari that men spoke along the shores of Slaver‚Äôs Bay. ‚ÄúMaester Kedry will accompany you,‚Äù his father said the night they parted. ‚ÄúHeed his counsel. He has devoted half his life to the study of the Nine Free Cities.‚Äù Quentyn wondered if things might not have gone a deal easier if only he were here to guide them.

{:#Raymun-Redbeard-and-Willam-Stark}
**Raymun Redbeard and Willam Stark**

> Giant was waiting in the armory. His real name was Bedwyck. At a hair and a half over five feet he was the smallest man in the Night‚Äôs Watch. Jon came directly to the point. ‚ÄúWe need more eyes along the Wall. Way-castles where our patrols can get out of the cold and find hot food and a fresh mount. I am putting a garrison in Icemark and giving you command of it.‚Äù Giant put the tip of his little finger in his ear to clean out the wax. ‚ÄúCommand? Me? M‚Äôlord knows I‚Äôm just a crofter‚Äôs get, on the Wall for poaching?‚Äù ‚ÄúYou‚Äôve been a ranger for a dozen years. You survived the Fist of the First Men and Craster‚Äôs Keep, and came back to tell the tale. The younger men look up to you.‚Äù The small man laughed. ‚ÄúOnly dwarfs look up to me. I don‚Äôt read, my lord. On a good day I can write my name.‚Äù ‚ÄúI‚Äôve sent to Oldtown for more maesters. You‚Äôll have two ravens for when your need is urgent. When it‚Äôs not, send riders. Until we have more maesters and more birds, I mean to establish a line of beacon towers along the top of the Wall.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd how many poor fools will I be commanding?‚Äù ‚ÄúTwenty, from the Watch,‚Äù said Jon, ‚Äúand half as many men from Stannis.‚Äù Old, green, or wounded. ‚ÄúThey won‚Äôt be his best men, and none will take the black, but they‚Äôll obey. Make what use of them you can. Four of the brothers I‚Äôm sending with you will be Kingslanders who came to the Wall with Lord Slynt. Keep one eye on that lot and watch for climbers with the other.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúWe can watch, m‚Äôlord, but if enough climbers gain the top o‚Äô the Wall, thirty men won‚Äôt be enough to throw them off.‚Äù Three hundred might not be enough. Jon kept that doubt to himself. It was true that climbers were desperately vulnerable whilst on the ascent. Stones and spears and pots of burning pitch could be rained down on them from above, and all they could do was cling desperately to the ice. Sometimes the Wall itself seemed to shake them off, as a dog might shake off fleas. Jon had seen that for himself, when a sheet of ice cracked beneath Val‚Äôs lover Jarl, sending him to his death. If the climbers reached the top of the Wall undetected, however, everything changed. Given time, they could carve out a toehold for themselves up there, throwing up ramparts of their own and dropping ropes and ladders for thousands more to clamber over after them. That was how Raymun Redbeard had done it, Raymun who had been King-Beyond-the-Wall in the days of his grandfather‚Äôs grandfather. Jack Musgood had been the lord commander in those days. Jolly Jack, he was called before Redbeard came down upon the north; Sleepy Jack, forever after. Raymun‚Äôs host had met a bloody end on the shores of Long Lake, caught between Lord Willam of Winterfell and the Drunken Giant, Harmond Umber. Redbeard had been slain by Artos the Implacable, Lord Willam‚Äôs younger brother. The Watch arrived too late to fight the wildlings, but in time to bury them, the task that Artos Stark assigned them in his wroth as he grieved above the headless corpse of his fallen brother. Jon did not intend to be remembered as Sleepy Jon Snow. ‚ÄúThirty men will stand a better chance than none,‚Äù he told Giant.

{:#Manly-Stokeworth-and-Janos-Slynt}
**Manly Stokeworth and Janos Slynt**

> Joffrey sat on his Iron Throne and dispensed what it pleased him to call justice. Nine cases out of ten seemed to bore him; those he allowed his council to handle, squirming restlessly while Lord Baelish, Grand Maester Pycelle, or Queen Cersei resolved the matter. When he did choose to make a ruling, though, not even his queen mother could sway him. A thief was brought before him and he had Ser Ilyn chop his hand off, right there in court. Two knights came to him with a dispute about some land, and he decreed that they should duel for it on the morrow. ‚ÄúTo the death,‚Äù he added. A woman fell to her knees to plead for the head of a man executed as a traitor. She had loved him, she said, and she wanted to see him decently buried. ‚ÄúIf you loved a traitor, you must be a traitor too,‚Äù Joffrey said. Two gold cloaks dragged her off to the dungeons. Frog-faced Lord Slynt sat at the end of the council table wearing a black velvet doublet and a shiny cloth-of-gold cape, nodding with approval every time the king pronounced a sentence. Sansa stared hard at his ugly face, remembering how he had thrown down her father for Ser Ilyn to behead, wishing she could hurt him, wishing that some hero would throw him down and cut off his head.

> ‚ÄúTrue enough,‚Äù the small man said. ‚ÄúIs it just to be Icemark, then, or will m‚Äôlord be opening t‚Äôother forts as well?‚Äù ‚ÄúI mean to garrison all of them, in time,‚Äù said Jon, ‚Äúbut for the moment, it will just be Icemark and Greyguard.‚Äù ‚ÄúAnd has m‚Äôlord decided who‚Äôs to command at Greyguard?‚Äù ‚ÄúJanos Slynt,‚Äù said Jon. Gods save us. ‚ÄúA man does not rise to command of the gold cloaks without ability. Slynt was born a butcher‚Äôs son. He was captain of the Iron Gate when Manly Stokeworth died, and Jon Arryn raised him up and put the defense of King‚Äôs Landing into his hands. Lord Janos cannot be as great a fool as he seems.‚Äù And I want him well away from Alliser Thorne. ‚ÄúMight be that‚Äôs so,‚Äù said Giant, ‚Äúbut I‚Äôd still send him to the kitchens to help Three-Finger Hobb cut up the turnips.‚Äù If I did, I‚Äôd never dare to eat another turnip. Half the morning passed before Lord Janos reported as commanded. Jon was cleaning Longclaw. Some men would have given that task to a steward or a squire, but Lord Eddard had taught his sons to care for their own weapons. When Kegs and Dolorous Edd arrived with Slynt, Jon thanked them and bid Lord Janos sit. That he did, albeit with poor grace, crossing his arms, scowling, and ignoring the naked steel in his lord commander‚Äôs hands. Jon slid the oilcloth down his bastard sword, watching the play of morning light across the ripples, thinking how easily the blade would slide through skin and fat and sinew to part Slynt‚Äôs ugly head from his body. All of a man‚Äôs crimes were wiped away when he took the black, and all of his allegiances as well, yet he found it hard to think of Janos Slynt as a brother. There is blood between us. This man helped slay my father and did his best to have me killed as well. ‚ÄúLord Janos.‚Äù Jon sheathed his sword. ‚ÄúI am giving you command of Greyguard.‚Äù That took Slynt aback. ‚ÄúGreyguard¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝGreyguard was where you climbed the Wall with your wildling friends¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúIt was. The fort is in a sorry state, admittedly. You will restore it as best you can. Start by clearing back the forest. Steal stones from the structures that have collapsed to repair those still standing.‚Äù The work will be hard and brutal, he might have added. You‚Äôll sleep on stone, too exhausted to complain or plot, and soon you‚Äôll forget what it was like to be warm, but you might remember what it was to be a man. ‚ÄúYou will have thirty men. Ten from here, ten from the Shadow Tower, and ten lent to us by King Stannis.‚Äù Slynt‚Äôs face had turned the color of a prune. His meaty jowls began to quiver. ‚ÄúDo you think I cannot see what you are doing? Janos Slynt is not a man to be gulled so easily. I was charged with the defense of King‚Äôs Landing when you were soiling your swaddling clothes. Keep your ruin, bastard.‚Äù I am giving you a chance, my lord. It is more than you ever gave my father. ‚ÄúYou mistake me, my lord,‚Äù Jon said. ‚ÄúThat was a command, not an offer. It is forty leagues to Greyguard. Pack up your arms and armor, say your farewells, and be ready to depart at first light on the morrow.‚Äù ‚ÄúNo.‚Äù Lord Janos lurched to his feet, sending his chair crashing over backwards. ‚ÄúI will not go meekly off to freeze and die. No traitor‚Äôs bastard gives commands to Janos Slynt! I am not without friends, I warn you. Here, and in King‚Äôs Landing too. I was the Lord of Harrenhal! Give your ruin to one of the blind fools who cast a stone for you, I will not have it. Do you hear me, boy? I will not have it!‚Äù ‚ÄúYou will.‚Äù Slynt did not deign to answer that, but he kicked the chair aside as he departed. He still sees me as a boy, Jon thought, a green boy, to be cowed by angry words. He could only hope that a night‚Äôs sleep would bring Lord Janos to his senses.

> The next morning proved that hope was vain. Jon found Slynt breaking his fast in the common room. Ser Alliser Thorne was with him, and several of their cronies. They were laughing about something when Jon came down the steps with Iron Emmett and Dolorous Edd, and behind them Mully, Horse, Red Jack Crabb, Rusty Flowers, and Owen the Oaf. Three-Finger Hobb was ladling out porridge from his kettle. Queen‚Äôs men, king‚Äôs men, and black brothers sat at their separate tables, some bent over bowls of porridge, others filling their bellies with fried bread and bacon. Jon saw Pyp and Grenn at one table, Bowen Marsh at another. The air smelled of smoke and grease, and the clatter of knives and spoons echoed off the vaulted ceiling. All the voices died at once. ‚ÄúLord Janos,‚Äù Jon said, ‚ÄúI will give you one last chance. Put down that spoon and get to the stables. I have had your horse saddled and bridled. It is a long, hard road to Greyguard.‚Äù ‚ÄúThen you had best be on your way, boy.‚Äù Slynt laughed, dribbling porridge down his chest. ‚ÄúGreyguard‚Äôs a good place for the likes of you, I‚Äôm thinking. Well away from decent godly folk. The mark of the beast is on you, bastard.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou are refusing to obey my order?‚Äù ‚ÄúYou can stick your order up your bastard‚Äôs arse,‚Äù said Slynt, his jowls quivering. Alliser Thorne smiled a thin smile, his black eyes fixed on Jon. At another table, Godry the Giantslayer began to laugh. ‚ÄúAs you will.‚Äù Jon nodded to Iron Emmett. ‚ÄúPlease take Lord Janos to the Wall‚Äî‚Äù ‚Äîand confine him to an ice cell, he might have said. A day or ten cramped up inside the ice would leave him shivering and feverish and begging for release, Jon did not doubt. And the moment he is out, he and Thorne will begin to plot again. ‚Äîand tie him to his horse, he might have said. If Slynt did not wish to go to Greyguard as its commander, he could go as its cook. It will only be a matter of time until he deserts, then. And how many others will he take with him? ‚Äú‚Äîand hang him,‚Äù Jon finished. Janos Slynt‚Äôs face went as white as milk. The spoon slipped from his fingers. Edd and Emmett crossed the room, their footsteps ringing on the stone floor. Bowen Marsh‚Äôs mouth opened and closed though no words came out. Ser Alliser Thorne reached for his sword hilt. Go on, Jon thought. Longclaw was slung across his back. Show your steel. Give me cause to do the same. Half the men in the hall were on their feet. Southron knights and men-at-arms, loyal to King Stannis or the red woman or both, and Sworn Brothers of the Night‚Äôs Watch. Some had chosen Jon to be their lord commander. Others had cast their stones for Bowen Marsh, Ser Denys Mallister, Cotter Pyke¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand some for Janos Slynt. Hundreds of them, as I recall. Jon wondered how many of those men were in the cellar right now. For a moment the world balanced on a sword‚Äôs edge. Alliser Thorne took his hand from his sword and stepped aside to let Edd Tollett pass.

> Dolorous Edd took hold of Slynt by one arm, Iron Emmett by the other. Together they hauled him from the bench. ‚ÄúNo,‚Äù Lord Janos protested, flecks of porridge spraying from his lips. ‚ÄúNo, unhand me. He‚Äôs just a boy, a bastard. His father was a traitor. The mark of the beast is on him, that wolf of his¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝLet go of me! You will rue the day you laid hands on Janos Slynt. I have friends in King‚Äôs Landing. I warn you‚Äî‚Äù He was still protesting as they half-marched, half-dragged him up the steps. Jon followed them outside. Behind him, the cellar emptied. At the cage, Slynt wrenched loose for a moment and tried to make a fight of it, but Iron Emmett caught him by the throat and slammed him back against the iron bars until he desisted. By then all of Castle Black had come outside to watch. Even Val was at her window, her long golden braid across one shoulder. Stannis stood on the steps of the King‚Äôs Tower, surrounded by his knights. ‚ÄúIf the boy thinks that he can frighten me, he is mistaken,‚Äù they heard Lord Janos said. ‚ÄúHe would not dare to hang me. Janos Slynt has friends, important friends, you‚Äôll see¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù The wind whipped away the rest of his words. This is wrong, Jon thought. ‚ÄúStop.‚Äù Emmett turned back, frowning. ‚ÄúMy lord?‚Äù ‚ÄúI will not hang him,‚Äù said Jon. ‚ÄúBring him here.‚Äù ‚ÄúOh, Seven save us,‚Äù he heard Bowen Marsh cry out. The smile that Lord Janos Slynt smiled then had all the sweetness of rancid butter. Until Jon said, ‚ÄúEdd, fetch me a block,‚Äù and unsheathed Longclaw. By the time a suitable chopping block was found, Lord Janos had retreated into the winch cage, but Iron Emmett went in after him and dragged him out. ‚ÄúNo,‚Äù Slynt cried, as Emmett half-shoved and half-pulled him across the yard. ‚ÄúUnhand me¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýyou cannot¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýwhen Tywin Lannister hears of this, you will all rue‚Äî‚Äù Emmett kicked his legs out from under him. Dolorous Edd planted a foot on his back to keep him on his knees as Emmett shoved the block beneath his head. ‚ÄúThis will go easier if you stay still,‚Äù Jon Snow promised him. ‚ÄúMove to avoid the cut, and you will still die, but your dying will be uglier. Stretch out your neck, my lord.‚Äù The pale morning sunlight ran up and down his blade as Jon clasped the hilt of the bastard sword with both hands and raised it high. ‚ÄúIf you have any last words, now is the time to speak them,‚Äù he said, expecting one last curse. Janos Slynt twisted his neck around to stare up at him. ‚ÄúPlease, my lord. Mercy. I‚Äôll¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝI‚Äôll go, I will, I¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù No, thought Jon. You closed that door. Longclaw descended. ‚ÄúCan I have his boots?‚Äù asked Owen the Oaf, as Janos Slynt‚Äôs head went rolling across the muddy ground. ‚ÄúThey‚Äôre almost new, those boots. Lined with fur.‚Äù Jon glanced back at Stannis. For an instant their eyes met. Then the king nodded and went back inside his tower.

{:#Urrax-and-Byron-Swann}
**Urrax and Byron Swann**

> ‚ÄúEvery dwarf is a bastard in his father‚Äôs eyes.‚Äù ‚ÄúNo doubt. Well, Hugor Hill, answer me this. How did Serwyn of the Mirror Shield slay the dragon Urrax?‚Äù ‚ÄúHe approached behind his shield. Urrax saw only his own reflection until Serwyn had plunged his spear through his eye.‚Äù Haldon was unimpressed. ‚ÄúEven Duck knows that tale. Can you tell me the name of the knight who tried the same ploy with Vhagar during the Dance of the Dragons?‚Äù Tyrion grinned. ‚ÄúSer Byron Swann. He was roasted for his trouble ‚Ä¶ only the dragon was Syrax, not Vhagar.‚Äù ‚ÄúI fear that you‚Äôre mistaken. In The Dance of the Dragons, A True Telling, Maester Munkun writes‚Äî‚Äù ‚Äú‚Äîthat it was Vhagar. Grand Maester Munkun errs. Ser Byron‚Äôs squire saw his master die, and wrote his daughter of the manner of it. His account says it was Syrax, Rhaenyra‚Äôs she-dragon, which makes more sense than Munken‚Äôs version. Swann was the son of a marcher lord, and Storm‚Äôs End was for Aegon. Vhagar was ridden by Prince Aemond, Aegon‚Äôs brother. Why should Swann want to slay her?‚Äù Haldon pursed his lips.

{:#Alester-Florent}
**Alester Florent**

> Lord Alester brushed the straw from his clothes. ‚ÄúThe king has sent for me. Or the queen, yes, Selyse would never let me rot here, her own blood.‚Äù Outside the cell, Lamprey appeared with a ring of keys in hand. Ser Axell Florent and four guardsmen followed close behind him. They waited beneath the torch while Lamprey searched for the correct key. ‚ÄúAxell,‚Äù Lord Alester said. ‚ÄúGods be good. Is it the king who sends for me, or the queen?‚Äù ‚ÄúNo one has sent for you, traitor,‚Äù Ser Axell said. Lord Alester recoiled as if he‚Äôd been slapped. ‚ÄúNo, I swear to you, I committed no treason. Why won‚Äôt you listen? If His Grace would only let me explain‚Äî‚Äù Lamprey thrust a great iron key into the lock, turned it, and pulled open the cell. The rusted hinges screamed in protest. ‚ÄúYou,‚Äù he said to Davos. ‚ÄúCome.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhere?‚Äù Davos looked to Ser Axell. ‚ÄúTell me true, ser, do you mean to burn me?‚Äù ‚ÄúYou are sent for. Can you walk?‚Äù ‚ÄúI can walk.‚Äù Davos stepped from the cell. Lord Alester gave a cry of dismay as Lamprey slammed the door shut once more. ‚ÄúTake the torch,‚Äù Ser Axell commanded the gaoler. ‚ÄúLeave the traitor to the darkness.‚Äù ‚ÄúNo,‚Äù his brother said. ‚ÄúAxell, please, don‚Äôt take the light¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýgods have mercy¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúGods? There is only R‚Äôhllor, and the Other.‚Äù Ser Axell gestured sharply, and one of his guardsmen pulled the torch from its sconce and led the way to the stair.

> The beer was brown, the bread black, the stew a creamy white. She served it in a trencher hollowed out of a stale loaf. It was thick with leeks, carrots, barley, and turnips white and yellow, along with clams and chunks of cod and crabmeat, swimming in a stock of heavy cream and butter. It was the sort of stew that warmed a man right down to his bones, just the thing for a wet, cold night. Davos spooned it up gratefully. ‚ÄúYou have tasted sister‚Äôs stew before?‚Äù ‚ÄúI have, my lord.‚Äù The same stew was served all over the Three Sisters, in every inn and tavern. ‚ÄúThis is better than what you‚Äôve had before. Gella makes it. My daughter‚Äôs daughter. Are you married, onion knight?‚Äù ‚ÄúI am, my lord.‚Äù ‚ÄúA pity. Gella‚Äôs not. Homely women make the best wives. There‚Äôs three kinds of crabs in there. Red crabs and spider crabs and conquerors. I won‚Äôt eat spider crab, except in sister‚Äôs stew. Makes me feel half a cannibal.‚Äù His lordship gestured at the banner hanging above the cold black hearth. A spider crab was embroidered there, white on a grey-green field. ‚ÄúWe heard tales that Stannis burned his Hand.‚Äù The Hand who went before me. Melisandre had given Alester Florent to her god on Dragonstone, to conjure up the wind that bore them north. Lord Florent had been strong and silent as the queen‚Äôs men bound him to the post, as dignified as any half-naked man could hope to be, but as the flames licked up his legs he had begun to scream, and his screams had blown them all the way to Eastwatch-by-the-Sea, if the red woman could be believed. Davos had misliked that wind. It had seemed to him to smell of burning flesh, and the sound of it was anguished as it played amongst the lines. It could as easily have been me.

> Ser Axell Florent stopped for a word. Queen Selyse‚Äôs uncle was a keg of a man with thick arms and bandy legs. He had the prominent ears of a Florent, even larger than his niece‚Äôs. The coarse hair that sprouted from his did not stop him hearing most of what went on in the castle. For ten years Ser Axell had served as castellan of Dragonstone while Stannis sat on Robert‚Äôs council in King‚Äôs Landing, but of late he had emerged as the foremost of the queen‚Äôs men. ‚ÄúSer Davos, it is good to see you, as ever,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúAnd you, my lord.‚Äù ‚ÄúI made note of you this morning as well. The false gods burned with a merry light, did they not?‚Äù ‚ÄúThey burned brightly.‚Äù Davos did not trust this man, for all his courtesy. House Florent had declared for Renly.

> Nearer the door, the Queen‚Äôs Hand was attacking a brace of capons, sucking the meat off the bones and washing down each bite with ale. When he espied Jon Snow, Axell Florent tossed a bone aside, wiped his mouth with the back of his hand, and sauntered over. With his bowed legs, barrel chest, and prominent ears, he presented a comical appearance, but Jon knew better than to laugh at him. He was an uncle to Queen Selyse and had been among the first to follow her in accepting Melisandre‚Äôs red god. If he is not a kinslayer, he is the next best thing. Axell Florent‚Äôs brother had been burned by Melisandre, Maester Aemon had informed him, yet Ser Axell had done little and less to stop it. What sort of man can stand by idly and watch his own brother being burned alive?

{:#Joramun-and-Rattleshirt}
**Joramun and Rattleshirt**

> Mance Rayder‚Äôs thick grey-brown hair blew about his face as he walked. He pushed it from his eyes with bound hands, smiling. But when he saw the cage, his courage failed him. The queen‚Äôs men had made it from the trees of the haunted forest, from saplings and supple branches, pine boughs sticky with sap, and the bone-white fingers of the weirwoods. They‚Äôd bent them and twisted them around and through each other to weave a wooden lattice, then hung it high above a deep pit filled with logs, leaves, and kindling. The wildling king recoiled from the sight. ‚ÄúNo,‚Äù he cried, ‚Äúmercy. This is not right, I‚Äôm not the king, they‚Äî‚Äù Ser Godry gave a pull on the rope. The King-Beyond-the-Wall had no choice but to stumble after him, the rope choking off his words. When he lost his feet, Godry dragged him the rest of the way. Mance was bloody when the queen‚Äôs men half-shoved, half-carried him to the cage. A dozen men-at-arms heaved together to hoist him into the air. Lady Melisandre watched him rise. ‚ÄúFREE FOLK! Here stands your king of lies. And here is the horn he promised would bring down the Wall.‚Äù Two queen‚Äôs men brought forth the Horn of Joramun, black and banded with old gold, eight feet long from end to end. Runes were carved into the golden bands, the writing of the First Men. Joramun had died thousands of years ago, but Mance had found his grave beneath a glacier, high up in the Frostfangs. And Joramun blew the Horn of Winter, and woke giants from the earth. Ygritte had told Jon that Mance never found the horn. She lied, or else Mance kept it secret even from his own. A thousand captives watched through the wooden bars of their stockade as the horn was lifted high. All were ragged and half-starved. Wildlings, the Seven Kingdoms called them; they named themselves the free folk. They looked neither wild nor free‚Äîonly hungry, frightened, numb. ‚ÄúThe Horn of Joramun?‚Äù Melisandre said. ‚ÄúNo. Call it the Horn of Darkness. If the Wall falls, night falls as well, the long night that never ends. It must not happen, will not happen! The Lord of Light has seen his children in their peril and sent a champion to them, Azor Ahai reborn.‚Äù She swept a hand toward Stannis, and the great ruby at her throat pulsed with light.
  
> ‚ÄúFREE FOLK!‚Äù cried Melisandre. ‚ÄúBehold the fate of those who choose the darkness!‚Äù The Horn of Joramun burst into flame. It went up with a whoosh as swirling tongues of green and yellow fire leapt up crackling all along its length. Jon‚Äôs garron shied nervously, and up and down the ranks others fought to still their mounts as well. A moan came from the stockade as the free folk saw their hope afire. A few began to shout and curse, but most lapsed into silence. For half a heartbeat the runes graven on the gold bands seemed to shimmer in the air. The queen‚Äôs men gave a heave and sent the horn tumbling down into the fire pit. Inside his cage, Mance Rayder clawed at the noose about his neck with bound hands and screamed incoherently of treachery and witchery, denying his kingship, denying his people, denying his name, denying all that he had ever been. He shrieked for mercy and cursed the red woman and began to laugh hysterically. Jon watched unblinking. He dare not appear squeamish before his brothers. He had ordered out two hundred men, more than half the garrison of Castle Black. Mounted in solemn sable ranks with tall spears in hand, they had drawn up their hoods to shadow their faces¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand hide the fact that so many were greybeards and green boys. The free folk feared the Watch. Jon wanted them to take that fear with them to their new homes south of the Wall. The horn crashed amongst the logs and leaves and kindling. Within three heartbeats the whole pit was aflame. Clutching the bars of his cage with bound hands, Mance sobbed and begged. When the fire reached him he did a little dance. His screams became one long, wordless shriek of fear and pain. Within his cage, he fluttered like a burning leaf, a moth caught in a candle flame. Jon found himself remembering a song. Brothers, oh brothers, my days here are done,    the Dornishman‚Äôs taken my life, But what does it matter, for all men must die,    and I‚Äôve tasted the Dornishman‚Äôs wife! Val stood on the platform as still as if she had been carved of salt. She will not weep nor look away. Jon wondered what Ygritte would have done in her place. The women are the strong ones. He found himself thinking about Sam and Maester Aemon, about Gilly and the babe. She will curse me with her dying breath, but I saw no other way. Eastwatch reported savage storms upon the narrow sea. I meant to keep them safe. Did I feed them to the crabs instead? Last night he had dreamed of Sam drowning, of Ygritte dying with his arrow in her (it had not been his arrow, but in his dreams it always was), of Gilly weeping tears of blood. Jon Snow had seen enough. ‚ÄúNow,‚Äù he said. Ulmer of the Kingswood jammed his spear into the ground, unslung his bow, and slipped a black arrow from his quiver. Sweet Donnel Hill threw back his hood to do the same. Garth Greyfeather and Bearded Ben nocked shafts, bent their bows, loosed. One arrow took Mance Rayder in the chest, one in the gut, one in the throat. The fourth struck one of the cage‚Äôs wooden bars, and quivered for an instant before catching fire. A woman‚Äôs sobs echoed off the Wall as the wildling king slid bonelessly to the floor of his cage, wreathed in fire. ‚ÄúAnd now his Watch is done,‚Äù Jon murmured softly. Mance Rayder had been a man of the Night‚Äôs Watch once, before he changed his black cloak for one slashed with bright red silk.

> ‚ÄúYou need a bigger gate,‚Äù Tormund complained to Jon with a sour look up at the sky, where a few clouds had blown in. ‚ÄúToo bloody slow this way. Like sucking the Milkwater through a reed. Har. Would that I had the Horn of Joramun. I‚Äôd give it a nice toot and we‚Äôd climb through the rubble.‚Äù ‚ÄúMelisandre burned the Horn of Joramun.‚Äù ‚ÄúDid she?‚Äù Tormund slapped his thigh and hooted. ‚ÄúShe burned that fine big horn, aye. A bloody sin, I call it. A thousand years old, that was. We found it in a giant‚Äôs grave, and no man o‚Äô us had ever seen a horn so big. That must have been why Mance got the notion to tell you it were Joramun‚Äôs. He wanted you crows to think he had it in his power to blow your bloody Wall down about your knees. But we never found the true horn, not for all our digging. If we had, every kneeler in your Seven Kingdoms would have chunks o‚Äô ice to cool his wine all summer.‚Äù Jon turned in his saddle, frowning. And Joramun blew the Horn of Winter and woke giants from the earth. That huge horn with its bands of old gold, incised with ancient runes¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýhad Mance Rayder lied to him, or was Tormund lying now? If Mance‚Äôs horn was just a feint, where is the true horn?

> The warhorn he had given to Sam. On closer examination the horn had proved cracked, and even after he had cleaned all the dirt out, Jon had been unable to get any sound from it. The rim was chipped as well, but Sam liked old things, even worthless old things. ‚ÄúMake a drinking horn out of it,‚Äù Jon told him, ‚Äúand every time you take a drink you‚Äôll remember how you ranged beyond the Wall, all the way to the Fist of the First Men.‚Äù

> The captain wanted Aemon‚Äôs chain as well, but there Sam had refused. It was a great shame for any maester to surrender his chain, he had explained. Xhondo had to go over that part three times before Quhuru Mo accepted it. By the time the dealing was done, Sam was down to his boots and blacks and smallclothes, and the broken horn Jon Snow had found on the Fist of First Men. I had no choice, he told himself. We could not stay on Braavos, and short of theft or beggary, there was no other way to pay for passage. He would have counted it cheap at thrice the price if only they had gotten Maester Aemon safe to Oldtown.

> The wildling wore a sleeveless jerkin of boiled leather dotted with bronze studs beneath a worn cloak mottled in shades of green and brown. No bones. He was cloaked in shadows too, in wisps of ragged grey mist, half-seen, sliding across his face and form with every step he took. Ugly things. As ugly as his bones. A widow‚Äôs peak, close-set dark eyes, pinched cheeks, a mustache wriggling like a worm above a mouthful of broken brown teeth. Melisandre felt the warmth in the hollow of her throat as her ruby stirred at the closeness of its slave. ‚ÄúYou have put aside your suit of bones,‚Äù she observed. ‚ÄúThe clacking was like to drive me mad.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe bones protect you,‚Äù she reminded him. ‚ÄúThe black brothers do not love you. Devan tells me that only yesterday you had words with some of them over supper.‚Äù ‚ÄúA few. I was eating bean-and-bacon soup whilst Bowen Marsh was going on about the high ground. The Old Pomegranate thought that I was spying on him and announced that he would not suffer murderers listening to their councils. I told him that if that was true, maybe they shouldn‚Äôt have them by the fire. Bowen turned red and made some choking sounds, but that was as far as it went.‚Äù The wildling sat on the edge of the window, slid his dagger from its sheath. ‚ÄúIf some crow wants to slip a knife between my ribs whilst I‚Äôm spooning up some supper, he‚Äôs welcome to try. Hobb‚Äôs gruel would taste better with a drop of blood to spice it.‚Äù Melisandre paid the naked steel no mind. If the wildling had meant her harm, she would have seen it in her flames. Danger to her own person was the first thing she had learned to see, back when she was still half a child, a slave girl bound for life to the great red temple. It was still the first thing she looked for whenever she gazed into a fire. ‚ÄúIt is their eyes that should concern you, not their knives,‚Äù she warned him. ‚ÄúThe glamor, aye.‚Äù In the black iron fetter about his wrist, the ruby seemed to pulse. He tapped it with the edge of his blade. The steel made a faint click against the stone. ‚ÄúI feel it when I sleep. Warm against my skin, even through the iron. Soft as a woman‚Äôs kiss. Your kiss. But sometimes in my dreams it starts to burn, and your lips turn into teeth. Every day I think how easy it would be to pry it out, and every day I don‚Äôt. Must I wear the bloody bones as well?‚Äù ‚ÄúThe spell is made of shadow and suggestion. Men see what they expect to see. The bones are part of that.‚Äù Was I wrong to spare this one? ‚ÄúIf the glamor fails, they will kill you.‚Äù The wildling began to scrape the dirt out from beneath his nails with the point of his dagger. ‚ÄúI‚Äôve sung my songs, fought my battles, drunk summer wine, tasted the Dornishman‚Äôs wife. A man should die the way he‚Äôs lived. For me that‚Äôs steel in hand.‚Äù

> Melisandre touched the ruby at her neck and spoke a word. The sound echoed queerly from the corners of the room and twisted like a worm inside their ears. The wildling heard one word, the crow another. Neither was the word that left her lips. The ruby on the wildling‚Äôs wrist darkened, and the wisps of light and shadow around him writhed and faded. The bones remained‚Äîthe rattling ribs, the claws and teeth along his arms and shoulders, the great yellowed collarbone across his shoulders. The broken giant‚Äôs skull remained a broken giant‚Äôs skull, yellowed and cracked, grinning its stained and savage grin. But the widow‚Äôs peak dissolved. The brown mustache, the knobby chin, the sallow yellowed flesh and small dark eyes, all melted. Grey fingers crept through long brown hair. Laugh lines appeared at the corners of his mouth. All at once he was bigger than before, broader in the chest and shoulders, long-legged and lean, his face clean-shaved and windburnt. Jon Snow‚Äôs grey eyes grew wider. ‚ÄúMance?‚Äù ‚ÄúLord Snow.‚Äù Mance Rayder did not smile. ‚ÄúShe burned you.‚Äù ‚ÄúShe burned the Lord of Bones.‚Äù Jon Snow turned to Melisandre. ‚ÄúWhat sorcery is this?‚Äù ‚ÄúCall it what you will. Glamor, seeming, illusion. R‚Äôhllor is Lord of Light, Jon Snow, and it is given to his servants to weave with it, as others weave with thread.‚Äù Mance Rayder chuckled. ‚ÄúI had my doubts as well, Snow, but why not let her try? It was that, or let Stannis roast me.‚Äù ‚ÄúThe bones help,‚Äù said Melisandre. ‚ÄúThe bones remember. The strongest glamors are built of such things. A dead man‚Äôs boots, a hank of hair, a bag of fingerbones. With whispered words and prayer, a man‚Äôs shadow can be drawn forth from such and draped about another like a cloak. The wearer‚Äôs essence does not change, only his seeming.‚Äù She made it sound a simple thing, and easy. They need never know how difficult it had been, or how much it had cost her. That was a lesson Melisandre had learned long before Asshai; the more effortless the sorcery appears, the more men fear the sorcerer. When the flames had licked at Rattleshirt, the ruby at her throat had grown so hot that she had feared her own flesh might start to smoke and blacken. Thankfully Lord Snow had delivered her from that agony with his arrows. Whilst Stannis had seethed at the defiance, she had shuddered with relief. ‚ÄúOur false king has a prickly manner,‚Äù Melisandre told Jon Snow, ‚Äúbut he will not betray you. We hold his son, remember. And he owes you his very life.‚Äù ‚ÄúMe?‚Äù Snow sounded startled. ‚ÄúWho else, my lord? Only his life‚Äôs blood could pay for his crimes, your laws said, and Stannis Baratheon is not a man to go against the law ‚Ä¶ but as you said so sagely, the laws of men end at the Wall. I told you that the Lord of Light would hear your prayers. You wanted a way to save your little sister and still hold fast to the honor that means so much to you, to the vows you swore before your wooden god.‚Äù She pointed with a pale finger. ‚ÄúThere he stands, Lord Snow. Arya‚Äôs deliverance. A gift from the Lord of Light¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýand me.‚Äù

{:#Black-Fist-Cetherys-Mossador-Duran-Eladon-Goldenhair-Loyal-Spear-and-Rylona-Rhee}
**Black Fist, Cetherys, Mossador, Duran, Eladon Goldenhair, Loyal Spear, and Rylona Rhee**

> What is it?‚Äù she cried, as Irri shook her gently by the shoulder. It was the black of night outside. Something is wrong, she knew at once. ‚ÄúIs it Daario? What‚Äôs happened?‚Äù In her dream they had been man and wife, simple folk who lived a simple life in a tall stone house with a red door. In her dream he had been kissing her all over‚Äîher mouth, her neck, her breasts. ‚ÄúNo, Khaleesi,‚Äù Irri murmured, ‚Äúit is your eunuch Grey Worm and the bald men. Will you see them?‚Äù ‚ÄúYes.‚Äù Her hair was disheveled and her bedclothes all atangle, Dany realized. ‚ÄúHelp me dress. I‚Äôll have a cup of wine as well. To clear my head.‚Äù To drown my dream. She could hear the soft sounds of sobs. ‚ÄúWho is that weeping?‚Äù ‚ÄúYour slave Missandei.‚Äù Jhiqui had a taper in her hand. ‚ÄúMy servant. I have no slaves.‚Äù Dany did not understand. ‚ÄúWhy does she weep?‚Äù ‚ÄúFor him who was her brother,‚Äù Irri told her. The rest she had from Skahaz, Reznak, and Grey Worm, when they were ushered into her presence. Dany knew their tidings were bad before a word was spoken. One glance at the Shavepate‚Äôs ugly face sufficed to tell her that. ‚ÄúThe Sons of the Harpy?‚Äù Skahaz nodded. His mouth was grim. ‚ÄúHow many dead?‚Äù Reznak wrung his hands. ‚ÄúN-nine, Magnificence. Foul work it was, and wicked. A dreadful night, dreadful.‚Äù Nine. The word was a dagger in her heart. Every night the shadow war was waged anew beneath the stepped pyramids of Meereen. Every morn the sun rose upon fresh corpses, with harpies drawn in blood on the bricks beside them. Any freedman who became too prosperous or too outspoken was marked for death. Nine in one night, though ‚Ä¶ That frightened her. ‚ÄúTell me.‚Äù Grey Worm answered. ‚ÄúYour servants were set upon as they walked the bricks of Meereen to keep Your Grace‚Äôs peace. All were well armed, with spears and shields and short swords. Two by two they walked, and two by two they died. Your servants Black Fist and Cetherys were slain by crossbow bolts in Mazdhan‚Äôs Maze. Your servants Mossador and Duran were crushed by falling stones beneath the river wall. Your servants Eladon Goldenhair and Loyal Spear were poisoned at a wineshop where they were accustomed to stop each night upon their rounds.‚Äù Mossador. Dany made a fist. Missandei and her brothers had been taken from their home on Naath by raiders from the Basilisk Isles and sold into slavery in Astapor. Young as she was, Missandei had shown such a gift for tongues that the Good Masters had made a scribe of her. Mossador and Marselen had not been so fortunate. They had been gelded and made into Unsullied. ‚ÄúHave any of the murderers been captured?‚Äù ‚ÄúYour servants have arrested the owner of the wineshop and his daughters. They plead their ignorance and beg for mercy.‚Äù They all plead ignorance and beg for mercy. ‚ÄúGive them to the Shavepate. Skahaz, keep each apart from the others and put them to the question.‚Äù ‚ÄúIt will be done, Your Worship. Would you have me question them sweetly, or sharply?‚Äù ‚ÄúSweetly, to begin. Hear what tales they tell and what names they give you. It may be they had no part in this.‚Äù She hesitated. ‚ÄúNine, the noble Reznak said. Who else?‚Äù ‚ÄúThree freedmen, murdered in their homes,‚Äù the Shavepate said. ‚ÄúA moneylender, a cobbler, and the harpist Rylona Rhee. They cut her fingers off before they killed her.‚Äù The queen flinched. Rylona Rhee had played the harp as sweetly as the Maiden. When she had been a slave in Yunkai, she had played for every highborn family in the city. In Meereen she had become a leader amongst the Yunkish freedmen, their voice in Dany‚Äôs councils.

> ‚ÄúWe have no captives but this wineseller?‚Äù ‚ÄúNone, this one grieves to confess. We beg your pardon.‚Äù Mercy, thought Dany. They will have the dragon‚Äôs mercy. ‚ÄúSkahaz, I have changed my mind. Question the man sharply.‚Äù ‚ÄúI could. Or I could question the daughters sharply whilst the father looks on. That will wring some names from him.‚Äù ‚ÄúDo as you think best, but bring me names.‚Äù Her fury was a fire in her belly. ‚ÄúI will have no more Unsullied slaughtered. Grey Worm, pull your men back to their barracks. Henceforth let them guard my walls and gates and person. From this day, it shall be for Meereenese to keep the peace in Meereen. Skahaz, make me a new watch, made up in equal parts of shavepates and freedmen.‚Äù ‚ÄúAs you command. How many men?‚Äù ‚ÄúAs many as you require.‚Äù Reznak mo Reznak gasped. ‚ÄúMagnificence, where is the coin to come from to pay wages for so many men?‚Äù ‚ÄúFrom the pyramids. Call it a blood tax. I will have a hundred pieces of gold from every pyramid for each freedman that the Harpy‚Äôs Sons have slain.‚Äù That brought a smile to the Shavepate‚Äôs face. ‚ÄúIt will be done,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúbut Your Radiance should know that the Great Masters of Zhak and Merreq are making preparations to quit their pyramids and leave the city.‚Äù Daenerys was sick unto death of Zhak and Merreq; she was sick of all the Meereenese, great and small alike. ‚ÄúLet them go, but see that they take no more than the clothes upon their backs. Make certain that all their gold remains here with us. Their stores of food as well.‚Äù ‚ÄúMagnificence,‚Äù murmured Reznak mo Reznak, ‚Äúwe cannot know that these great nobles mean to join your enemies. More like they are simply making for their estates in the hills.‚Äù ‚ÄúThey will not mind us keeping their gold safe, then. There is nothing to buy in the hills.‚Äù ‚ÄúThey are afraid for their children,‚Äù Reznak said. Yes, Daenerys thought, and so am I. ‚ÄúWe must keep them safe as well. I will have two children from each of them. From the other pyramids as well. A boy and a girl.‚Äù ‚ÄúHostages,‚Äù said Skahaz, happily. ‚ÄúPages and cupbearers. If the Great Masters make objection, explain to them that in Westeros it is a great honor for a child to be chosen to serve at court.‚Äù She left the rest unspoken. ‚ÄúGo and do as I‚Äôve commanded. I have my dead to mourn.‚Äù When she returned to her rooms atop the pyramid, she found Missandei crying softly on her pallet, trying as best she could to muffle the sound of her sobs. ‚ÄúCome sleep with me,‚Äù she told the little scribe. ‚ÄúDawn will not come for hours yet.‚Äù ‚ÄúYour Grace is kind to this one.‚Äù Missandei slipped under the sheets. ‚ÄúHe was a good brother.‚Äù Dany wrapped her arms about the girl. ‚ÄúTell me of him.‚Äù ‚ÄúHe taught me how to climb a tree when we were little. He could catch fish with his hands. Once I found him sleeping in our garden with a hundred butterflies crawling over him. He looked so beautiful that morning, this one¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝI mean, I loved him.‚Äù ‚ÄúAs he loved you.‚Äù Dany stroked the girl‚Äôs hair. ‚ÄúSay the word, my sweet, and I will send you from this awful place. I will find a ship somehow and send you home. To Naath.‚Äù ‚ÄúI would sooner stay with you. On Naath I‚Äôd be afraid. What if the slavers came again? I feel safe when I‚Äôm with you.‚Äù Safe. The word made Dany‚Äôs eyes fill up with tears. ‚ÄúI want to keep you safe.‚Äù Missandei was only a child. With her, she felt as if she could be a child too. ‚ÄúNo one ever kept me safe when I was little. Well, Ser Willem did, but then he died, and Viserys¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝI want to protect you but¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýit is so hard. To be strong. I don‚Äôt always know what I should do. I must know, though. I am all they have. I am the queen¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthe¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthe¬Ý‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚Äú‚Ä¶ mother,‚Äù whispered Missandei. ‚ÄúMother to dragons.‚Äù Dany shivered. ‚ÄúNo. Mother to us all.‚Äù Missandei hugged her tighter. ‚ÄúYour Grace should sleep. Dawn will be here soon, and court.‚Äù ‚ÄúWe‚Äôll both sleep, and dream of sweeter days. Close your eyes.‚Äù When she did, Dany kissed her eyelids and made her giggle. Kisses came easier than sleep, however. Dany shut her eyes and tried to think of home, of Dragonstone and King‚Äôs Landing and all the other places that Viserys had told her of, in a kinder land than this¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut her thoughts kept turning back to Slaver‚Äôs Bay, like ships caught in some bitter wind. When Missandei was sound asleep, Dany slipped from her arms and stepped out into the predawn air to lean upon the cool brick parapet and gaze out across the city. A thousand roofs stretched out below her, painted in shades of ivory and silver by the moon. Somewhere beneath those roofs, the Sons of the Harpy were gathered, plotting ways to kill her and all those who loved her and put her children back in chains. Somewhere down there a hungry child was crying for milk. Somewhere an old woman lay dying. Somewhere a man and a maid embraced, and fumbled at each other‚Äôs clothes with eager hands. But up here there was only the sheen of moonlight on pyramids and pits, with no hint what lay beneath. Up here there was only her, alone. She was the blood of the dragon. She could kill the Sons of the Harpy, and the sons of the sons, and the sons of the sons of the sons. But a dragon could not feed a hungry child nor help a dying woman‚Äôs pain.

{:#Kyra}
**Kyra**

> ‚ÄúHe‚Äôs been eating rats,‚Äù said the second boy. ‚ÄúLook.‚Äù The first boy laughed. ‚ÄúHe has. That‚Äôs funny.‚Äù I had to. The rats bit him when he slept, gnawing at his fingers and his toes, even at his face, so when he got his hands on one he did not hesitate. Eat or be eaten, those were the only choices. ‚ÄúI did it,‚Äù he mumbled, ‚ÄúI did, I did, I ate him, they do the same to me, please ‚Ä¶‚Äù The boys moved closer, the straw crunching softly under their feet. ‚ÄúTalk to me,‚Äù said one of them. He was the smaller of the two, a thin boy, but clever. ‚ÄúDo you remember who you are?‚Äù The fear came bubbling up inside him, and he moaned. ‚ÄúTalk to me. Tell me your name.‚Äù My name. A scream caught in his throat. They had taught him his name, they had, they had, but it had been so long that he‚Äôd forgotten. If I say it wrong, he‚Äôll take another finger, or worse, he‚Äôll ‚Ä¶ he‚Äôll ‚Ä¶ He would not think about that, he could not think about that. There were needles in his jaw, in his eyes. His head was pounding. ‚ÄúPlease,‚Äù he squeaked, his voice thin and weak. He sounded a hundred years old. Perhaps he was. How long have I been in here? ‚ÄúGo,‚Äù he mumbled, through broken teeth and broken fingers, his eyes closed tight against the terrible bright light. ‚ÄúPlease, you can have the rat, don‚Äôt hurt me ‚Ä¶‚Äù ‚ÄúReek,‚Äù said the larger of the boys. ‚ÄúYour name is Reek. Remember?‚Äù He was the one with the torch. The smaller boy had the ring of iron keys. Reek? Tears ran down his cheeks. ‚ÄúI remember. I do.‚Äù His mouth opened and closed. ‚ÄúMy name is Reek. It rhymes with leek.‚Äù In the dark he did not need a name, so it was easy to forget. Reek, Reek, my name is Reek. He had not been born with that name. In another life he had been someone else, but here and now, his name was Reek. He remembered. He remembered the boys as well. They were clad in matching lambswool doublets, silver-grey with dark blue trim. Both were squires, both were eight, and both were Walder Frey. Big Walder and Little Walder, yes. Only the big one was Little and the little one was Big, which amused the boys and confused the rest of the world. ‚ÄúI know you,‚Äù he whispered, through cracked lips. ‚ÄúI know your names.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou‚Äôre to come with us,‚Äù said Little Walder. ‚ÄúHis lordship has need of you,‚Äù said Big Walder. Fear went through him like a knife. They are only children, he thought. Two boys of eight. He could overcome two boys of eight, surely. Even as weak as he was, he could take the torch, take the keys, take the dagger sheathed on Little Walder‚Äôs hip, escape. No. No, it is too easy. It is a trap. If I run, he will take another finger from me, he will take more of my teeth. He had run before. Years ago, it seemed, when he still had some strength in him, when he had still been defiant. That time it had been Kyra with the keys. She told him she had stolen them, that she knew a postern gate that was never guarded. ‚ÄúTake me back to Winterfell, m‚Äôlord,‚Äù she begged, pale-faced and trembling. ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt know the way. I can‚Äôt escape alone. Come with me, please.‚Äù And so he had. The gaoler was dead drunk in a puddle of wine, with his breeches down around his ankles. The dungeon door was open and the postern gate had been unguarded, just as she had said. They waited for the moon to go behind a cloud, then slipped from the castle and splashed across the Weeping Water, stumbling over stones, half-frozen by the icy stream. On the far side, he had kissed her. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôve saved us,‚Äù he said. Fool. Fool. It had all been a trap, a game, a jape. Lord Ramsay loved the chase and preferred to hunt two-legged prey. All night they ran through the darkling wood, but as the sun came up the sound of a distant horn came faintly through the trees, and they heard the baying of a pack of hounds. ‚ÄúWe should split up,‚Äù he told Kyra as the dogs drew closer. ‚ÄúThey cannot track us both.‚Äù The girl was crazed with fear, though, and refused to leave his side, even when he swore that he would raise a host of ironborn and come back for her if she should be the one they followed. Within the hour, they were taken. One dog knocked him to the ground, and a second bit Kyra on the leg as she scrambled up a hillside. The rest surrounded them, baying and snarling, snapping at them every time they moved, holding them there until Ramsay Snow rode up with his huntsmen. He was still a bastard then, not yet a Bolton. ‚ÄúThere you are,‚Äù he said, smiling down at them from the saddle. ‚ÄúYou wound me, wandering off like this. Have you grown tired of my hospitality so soon?‚Äù That was when Kyra seized a stone and threw it at his head. It missed by a good foot, and Ramsay smiled. ‚ÄúYou must be punished.‚Äù Reek remembered the desperate, frightened look in Kyra‚Äôs eyes. She had never looked so young as she did in that moment, still half a girl, but there was nothing he could do. She brought them down on us, he thought. If we had separated as I wanted, one of us might have gotten away.

> The riders had been sixteen days on the hunt, with only hard bread and salt beef to eat, aside from the occasional stolen kid, so that night Lord Ramsay commanded that a feast be laid to celebrate his return to Barrowton. Their host, a grizzled one-armed petty lord by the name of Harwood Stout, knew better than to refuse him, though by now his larders must be well nigh exhausted. Reek had heard Stout‚Äôs servants muttering at how the Bastard and his men were eating through the winter stores. ‚ÄúHe‚Äôll bed Lord Eddard‚Äôs little girl, they say,‚Äù Stout‚Äôs cook complained when she did not know that Reek was listening, ‚Äúbut we‚Äôre the ones who‚Äôll be fucked when the snows come, you mark my words.‚Äù Yet Lord Ramsay had decreed a feast, so feast they must. Trestle tables were set up in Stout‚Äôs hall, an ox was slaughtered, and that night as the sun went down the empty-handed hunters ate roasts and ribs, barley bread, a mash of carrots and pease, washing it all down with prodigious quantities of ale. It fell to Little Walder to keep Lord Ramsay‚Äôs cup filled, whilst Big Walder poured for the others at the high table. Reek was chained up beside the doors lest his odor put the feasters off their appetites. He would eat later, off whatever scraps Lord Ramsay thought to send him. The dogs enjoyed the run of the hall, however, and provided the night‚Äôs best entertainment, when Maude and Grey Jeyne tore into one of Lord Stout‚Äôs hounds over an especially meaty bone that Will Short had tossed them. Reek was the only man in the hall who did not watch the three dogs fight. He kept his eyes on Ramsay Bolton. The fight did not end until their host‚Äôs dog was dead. Stout‚Äôs old hound never stood a mummer‚Äôs chance. He had been one against two, and Ramsay‚Äôs bitches were young, strong, and savage. Ben Bones, who liked the dogs better than their master, had told Reek they were all named after peasant girls Ramsay had hunted, raped, and killed back when he‚Äôd still been a bastard, running with the first Reek. ‚ÄúThe ones who give him good sport, anywise. The ones who weep and beg and won‚Äôt run don‚Äôt get to come back as bitches.‚Äù The next litter to come out of the Dreadfort‚Äôs kennels would include a Kyra, Reek did not doubt. ‚ÄúHe‚Äôs trained ‚Äôem to kill wolves as well,‚Äù Ben Bones had confided. Reek said nothing. He knew which wolves the girls were meant to kill, but he had no wish to watch the girls fighting over his severed toe.

> Ramsay had a new plaything to amuse him, one with teats and a cunny¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýbut soon Jeyne‚Äôs tears would lose their savor, and Ramsay would want his Reek again. He will flay me inch by inch. When my fingers are gone he will take my hands. After my toes, my feet. But only when I beg for it, when the pain grows so bad that I plead for him to give me some relief. There would be no hot baths for Reek. He would roll in shit again, forbidden to wash. The clothes he wore would turn to rags, foul and stinking, and he would be made to wear them till they rotted. The best he could hope for was to be returned to the kennels with Ramsay‚Äôs girls for company. Kyra, he remembered. The new bitch he calls Kyra.

{:#Ralf-Kenning}
**Ralf Kenning**

> ‚ÄúThey will shout your name as loud,‚Äù said Nute. Victarion nodded, though he did not share the Barber‚Äôs certainty. Balon had three sons, and a daughter he loved well. He had said as much to his captains at Moat Cailin, when first they urged him to claim the Seastone Chair. ‚ÄúBalon‚Äôs sons are dead,‚Äù Red Ralf Stonehouse had argued, ‚Äúand Asha is a woman. You were your brother‚Äôs strong right arm, you must pick up the sword that he let fall.‚Äù When Victarion reminded them that Balon had commanded him to hold the Moat against the northmen, Ralf Kenning said, ‚ÄúThe wolves are broken, lord. What good to win this swamp and lose the isles?‚Äù And Ralf the Limper added, ‚ÄúThe Crow‚Äôs Eye has been too long away. He knows us not.‚Äù Euron Greyjoy, King of the Isles and the North. The thought woke an old rage in his heart, but still¬Ý‚Ä¶ ‚ÄúWords are wind,‚Äù Victarion told them, ‚Äúand the only good wind is that which fills our sails. Would you have me fight the Crow‚Äôs Eye? Brother against brother, ironborn against ironborn?‚Äù Euron was still his elder, no matter how much bad blood might be between them. No man is as accursed as the kinslayer. But when the Damphair‚Äôs summons came, the call to kingsmoot, then all was changed. Aeron speaks with the Drowned God‚Äôs voice, Victarion reminded himself, and if the Drowned God wills that I should sit the Seastone Chair¬Ý‚Ä¶¬ÝThe next day he gave command of Moat Cailin to Ralf Kenning and set off overland for the Fever River where the Iron Fleet lay amongst the reeds and willows. Rough seas and fickle winds had delayed him, but only one ship had been lost, and he was home.

> The chamber at the top of the steps was dark, smoky, and oppressively hot. A ragged skin had been hung across the narrow window to keep the damp out, and a slab of peat smoldered in a brazier. The smell in the room was foul, a miasma of mold and piss and nightsoil, of smoke and sickness. Soiled rushes covered the floor, whilst a heap of straw in the corner passed for a bed. Ralf Kenning lay shivering beneath a mountain of furs. His arms were stacked beside him‚Äîsword and axe, mail hauberk, iron warhelm. His shield bore the storm god‚Äôs cloudy hand, lightning crackling from his fingers down to a raging sea, but the paint was discolored and peeling, the wood beneath starting to rot. Ralf was rotting too. Beneath the furs he was naked and feverish, his pale puffy flesh covered with weeping sores and scabs. His head was misshapen, one cheek grotesquely swollen, his neck so engorged with blood that it threatened to swallow his face. The arm on that same side was big as a log and crawling with white worms. No one had bathed him or shaved him for many days, from the look of him. One eye wept pus, and his beard was crusty with dried vomit. ‚ÄúWhat happened to him?‚Äù asked Reek. ‚ÄúHe was on the parapets and some bog devil loosed an arrow at him. It was only a graze, but¬Ý‚Ä¶¬Ýthey poison their shafts, smear the points with shit and worse things. We poured boiling wine into the wound, but it made no difference.‚Äù I cannot treat with this thing. ‚ÄúKill him,‚Äù Reek told the guard. ‚ÄúHis wits are gone. He‚Äôs full of blood and worms.‚Äù The man gaped at him. ‚ÄúThe captain put him in command.‚Äù ‚ÄúYou‚Äôd put a dying horse down.‚Äù ‚ÄúWhat horse? I never had no horse.‚Äù I did. The memory came back in a rush. Smiler‚Äôs screams had sounded almost human. His mane afire, he had reared up on his hind legs, blind with pain, lashing out with his hooves. No, no. Not mine, he was not mine, Reek never had a horse. ‚ÄúI will kill him for you.‚Äù Reek snatched up Ralf Kenning‚Äôs sword where it leaned against his shield. He still had fingers enough to clasp the hilt. When he laid the edge of the blade against the swollen throat of the creature on the straw, the skin split open in a gout of black blood and yellow pus. Kenning jerked violently, then lay still. An awful stench filled the room. Reek bolted for the steps. The air was damp and cold there, but much cleaner by comparison. The ironman stumbled out after him, white-faced and struggling not to retch.

{:#Dagon-Codd-and-Adrack-Humble}
**Dagon Codd and Adrack Humble**

> Two dozen ironborn sat drinking at the table. A few looked at him with dull, flat eyes when he entered. The rest ignored him. All the men were strangers to him. Several wore cloaks fastened by brooches in the shape of silver codfish. The Codds were not well regarded in the Iron Islands; the men were said to be thieves and cowards, the women wantons who bedded with their own fathers and brothers. It did not surprise him that his uncle had chosen to leave these men behind when the Iron Fleet went home. This will make my task that much easier. ‚ÄúRalf Kenning is dead,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúWho commands here?‚Äù The drinkers stared at him blankly. One laughed. Another spat. Finally one of the Codds said, ‚ÄúWho asks?‚Äù ‚ÄúLord Balon‚Äôs son.‚Äù Reek, my name is Reek, it rhymes with cheek. ‚ÄúI am here at the command of Ramsay Bolton, Lord of the Hornwood and heir to the Dreadfort, who captured me at Winterfell. His host is north of you, his father‚Äôs to the south, but Lord Ramsay is prepared to be merciful if you yield Moat Cailin to him before the sun goes down.‚Äù He drew out the letter that they‚Äôd given him and tossed it on the table before the drinkers. One of them picked it up and turned it over in his hands, picking at the pink wax that sealed it. After a moment he said, ‚ÄúParchment. What good is that? It‚Äôs cheese we need, and meat.‚Äù ‚ÄúSteel, you mean,‚Äù said the man beside him, a greybeard whose left arm ended in a stump. ‚ÄúSwords. Axes. Aye, and bows, a hundred more bows, and men to loose the arrows.‚Äù ‚ÄúIronborn do not surrender,‚Äù said a third voice. ‚ÄúTell that to my father. Lord Balon bent the knee when Robert broke his wall. Elsewise he would have died. As you will if you do not yield.‚Äù He gestured at the parchment. ‚ÄúBreak the seal. Read the words. That is a safe conduct, written in Lord Ramsay‚Äôs own hand. Give up your swords and come with me, and his lordship will feed you and give you leave to march unmolested to the Stony Shore and find a ship for home. Elsewise you die.‚Äù ‚ÄúIs that a threat?‚Äù One of the Codds pushed to his feet. A big man, but pop-eyed and wide of mouth, with dead white flesh. He looked as if his father had sired him on a fish, but he still wore a longsword. ‚ÄúDagon Codd yields to no man.‚Äù No, please, you have to listen. The thought of what Ramsay would do to him if he crept back to camp without the garrison‚Äôs surrender was almost enough to make him piss his breeches. Reek, Reek, it rhymes with leak. ‚ÄúIs that your answer?‚Äù The words rang feebly in his ears. ‚ÄúDoes this codfish speak for all of you?‚Äù The guard who had met him at the door seemed less certain. ‚ÄúVictarion commanded us to hold, he did. I heard him with my own ears. Hold here till I return, he told Kenning.‚Äù ‚ÄúAye,‚Äù said the one-armed man. ‚ÄúThat‚Äôs what he said. The kingsmoot called, but he swore that he‚Äôd be back, with a driftwood crown upon his head and a thousand men behind him.‚Äù ‚ÄúMy uncle is never coming back,‚Äù Reek told them. ‚ÄúThe kingsmoot crowned his brother Euron, and the Crow‚Äôs Eye has has other wars to fight. You think my uncle values you? He doesn‚Äôt. You are the ones he left behind to die. He scraped you off the same way he scrapes mud off his boots when he wades ashore.‚Äù Those words struck home. He could see it in their eyes, in the way they looked at one another or frowned above their cups. They all feared they‚Äôd been abandoned, but it took me to turn fear into certainty. These were not the kin of famous captains nor the blood of the great Houses of the Iron Islands. These were the sons of thralls and salt wives. ‚ÄúIf we yield, we walk away?‚Äù said the one-armed man. ‚ÄúIs that what it says on this here writing?‚Äù He nudged the roll of parchment, its wax seal still unbroken. ‚ÄúRead it for yourself,‚Äù he answered, though he was almost certain that none of them could read. ‚ÄúLord Ramsay treats his captives honorably so long as they keep faith with him.‚Äù He has only taken toes and fingers and that other thing, when he might have had my tongue, or peeled the skin off my legs from heel to thigh. ‚ÄúYield up your swords to him, and you will live.‚Äù

> ‚ÄúYou‚Äôre the one they call Turncloak. Why should we believe your promises?‚Äù He is drunk, Reek realized. The ale is speaking. ‚ÄúBelieve what you want. I have brought Lord Ramsay‚Äôs message. Now I must return to him. We‚Äôll sup on wild boar and neeps, washed down with strong red wine. Those who come with me will be welcome at the feast. The rest of you will die within a day. The Lord of the Dreadfort will bring his knights up the causeway, whilst his son leads his own men down on you from the north. No quarter will be granted. The ones that die fighting will be the lucky ones. Those who live will be given to the bog devils.‚Äù ‚ÄúEnough,‚Äù snarled Dagon Codd. ‚ÄúYou think you can frighten ironborn with words? Begone. Run back to your master before I open your belly, pull your entrails out, and make you eat them.‚Äù He might have said more, but suddenly his eyes gaped wide. A throwing axe sprouted from the center of his forehead with a solid thunk. Codd‚Äôs sword fell from his fingers. He jerked like a fish on a hook, then crashed face-first onto the table. It was the one-armed man who‚Äôd flung the axe. As he rose to his feet he had another in his hand. ‚ÄúWho else wants to die?‚Äù he asked the other drinkers. ‚ÄúSpeak up, I‚Äôll see you do.‚Äù Thin red streams were spreading out across the stone from the pool of blood where Dagon Codd‚Äôs head had come to rest. ‚ÄúMe, I mean to live, and that don‚Äôt mean staying here to rot.‚Äù One man took a swallow of ale. Another turned his cup over to wash away a finger of blood before it reached the place where he was seated. No one spoke. When the one-armed man slid the throwing axe back through his belt, Reek knew he had won. He almost felt a man again. Lord Ramsay will be pleased with me. He pulled down the kraken banner with his own two hands, fumbling some because of his missing fingers but thankful for the fingers that Lord Ramsay had allowed him to keep. It took the better part of the afternoon before the ironborn were ready to depart. There were more of them than he would have guessed‚Äîforty-seven in the Gatehouse Tower and another eighteen in the Drunkard‚Äôs Tower. Two of those were so close to dead there was no hope for them, another five too weak to walk. That still left fifty-eight who were fit enough to fight. Weak as they were, they would have taken three times their own number with them if Lord Ramsay had stormed the ruins. He did well to send me, Reek told himself as he climbed back onto his stot to lead his ragged column back across the boggy ground to where the northmen were encamped. ‚ÄúLeave your weapons here,‚Äù he told the prisoners. ‚ÄúSwords, bows, daggers. Armed men will be slain on sight.‚Äù It took them thrice as long to cover the distance as it had taken Reek alone. Crude litters had been patched together for four of the men who could not walk; the fifth was carried by his son, upon his back. It made for slow going, and all the ironborn were well aware of how exposed they were, well within bowshot of the bog devils and their poisoned arrows. If I die, I die. Reek only prayed the archer knew his business, so death would be quick and clean. A man‚Äôs death, not the end Ralf Kenning suffered. The one-armed man walked at the head of the procession, limping heavily. His name, he said, was Adrack Humble, and he had a rock wife and three salt wives back on Great Wyk. ‚ÄúThree of the four had big bellies when we sailed,‚Äù he boasted, ‚Äúand Humbles run to twins. First thing I‚Äôll need to do when I get back is count up my new sons. Might be I‚Äôll even name one after you, m‚Äôlord.‚Äù Aye, name him Reek, he thought, and when he‚Äôs bad you can cut his toes off and give him rats to eat. He turned his head and spat, and wondered if Ralf Kenning hadn‚Äôt been the lucky one.

> Reek swung down from his saddle and took a knee. ‚ÄúMy lord, Moat Cailin is yours. Here are its last defenders.‚Äù ‚ÄúSo few. I had hoped for more. They were such stubborn foes.‚Äù Lord Ramsay‚Äôs pale eyes shone. ‚ÄúYou must be starved. Damon, Alyn, see to them. Wine and ale, and all the food that they can eat. Skinner, show their wounded to our maesters.‚Äù ‚ÄúAye, my lord.‚Äù A few of the ironborn muttered thanks before they shambled off toward the cookfires in the center of the camp.
